packages.title: Academic Board - May 28, 2026
packages.summary: The Academic Board on May 28, 2026 approved three major capital projects: the Faculty of Dentistry Clinic 2 Renewal (2,945 sm renovated space, completion April 2028, funded by Major Capital Projects Reserves and donor contributions), the Department of Computer Science relocation to Schwartz Reisman Innovation Campus West (3,486 gsm, 1,588 nasm, occupancy 2027, funded by Faculty of Arts & Science Future Major Capital Projects Reserves), and the Build More Housing Initiative creating approximately 1,200 new student beds across Harbord Street and College Street sites (37,165 sm gross floor area, funded through joint venture partnership). The Board also received presentations on the UTSC Field House (6,306 nasm, occupancy targeted May 2029), the St. George 2040 Campus Plan vision, amendments to the Policy on Academic Continuity, and updates on SAMIH expansion, AI strategy, research leadership, and the Supportive Leave Policy review.
packages.gradRelevance: The Build More Housing Initiative directly addresses graduate student housing demand, targeting 1,200 new beds designed for graduate and upper-year students across two sites, with focus on affordability and diverse housing options. The Department of Computer Science relocation to Schwartz Reisman Innovation Campus West allocates 250 sm of graduate student space within the 1,588 nasm total, supporting AI research and graduate education. The UTSC Field House (occupancy May 2029) will expand recreation and wellness facilities for UTSC graduate students. The Supportive Leave Policy review consultation process included students and identified improvements to clarity, support information, and procedures that will affect graduate student access to leave provisions while maintaining academic standing.
keyItems: Faculty of Dentistry Clinic 2 Renewal approved in principle: 2,945 sm of renovated space in 3,168 sm total project scope area, with 1,128 nasm of program space; completion targeted April 2028; funded by Faculty of Dentistry Major Capital Projects Reserves, Future Faculty Reserves, Clinic 2 Capital Donations, Central University Matching, and ongoing fundraising/financing

Department of Computer Science relocation to Schwartz Reisman Innovation Campus West approved in principle: 3,486 gsm (1,588 nasm) including 328 sm research laboratories, 473 sm academic offices, 250 sm graduate student space, 520 sm office support space; occupancy planned 2027; funded by Faculty of Arts & Science Future Major Capital Projects Reserves

Build More Housing Initiative approved in principle: approximately 1,200 new student residence beds across 40-56 Harbord Street and 167-171 College Street sites; 37,165 sm gross floor area with 40,684 sm total gross construction area; funded entirely through joint venture partnership model without provincial government support; designed to improve affordability for graduate and upper-year students

UTSC Field House recommended for approval: 6,306 nasm including 6,004 sm athletic/recreation space, 79 sm study space, 180 sm student activity space; 7,194 gsm total; construction April 2027–May 2029 with occupancy May 2029; addresses enrolment growth and demand for recreation facilities comparable to St. George campus

St. George 2040 Campus Plan presented: comprehensive vision for campus transformation addressing 175,000 GSM completed buildings, 55,000 GSM acquisitions, 59,000 GSM in construction, projecting 350,000 NASM academic space shortfall and 2,700–4,700 bed student housing demand; eight strategic framework areas including Central Campus Core, Green Corridor, Innovation Corridor, and Ishpadinaa Placemaking

Amendments to Policy on Academic Continuity approved: clarifies roles of Provost, unit heads, and instructors during academic disruptions; adds reference to academic freedom; specifies that if disruption declared due to labour dispute, faculty and librarians will not be required to carry out duties of employees in legal work stoppage; deans support amendments

documents.title: Faculty of Dentistry Clinic 2 Renewal project
documents.summary: Detailed architectural and design documentation for the Faculty of Dentistry Clinic 2 Renewal project at 124 Edward Street. Contains floor plans for parking garage and patient lobby (mezzanine level), Level 1 (including multi-functional training space and Clinic 1 entrance), and Level 2 (including Clinic 2 entrance and operatories). Project scope covers 2,945 sm of renovated space within 3,168 sm total project area, with 1,128 nasm of program space. Includes implementation of Clinic Master Plan Phases 4 and 7b, featuring new AODA-compliant patient elevator and egress stair at Elm Street entrance, improved vertical connections and wayfinding, renovated Clinic 2 space with open and closed operatories including accessible operatory, and enhanced reception/waiting areas. Designed by Montgomery Sisam Architects and Kahler Slater Inc., with 100% construction documents issued January 9, 2026; project completion targeted April 2028.
documents.gradRelevance: Limited direct relevance to graduate students; primarily affects dental students and clinical education infrastructure. However, improved accessibility, modern infection control standards, and enhanced teaching environments support graduate dental education and clinical training.

documents.title: Department of Computer Science at Schwartz Reisman Innovation Campus - West presentation
documents.summary: Presentation on the Department of Computer Science relocation to Schwartz Reisman Innovation Campus West. Project includes 3,486 gsm (1,588 nasm) of renovated space with the following allocation: Research Laboratories & Support Space 328 sm, Academic Offices 473 sm, Research Office/Project Space 17 sm, Graduate Student Space 250 sm, Office Support Space 520 sm. Project purpose is to foster collaboration among AI researchers and the broader AI community through purpose-designed academic and research space. Municipal approvals ongoing; construction anticipated to begin 2026 with planned occupancy 2027. Building is University-owned with space leased to innovation and startup companies at market rates on self-sustaining basis.
documents.gradRelevance: Direct relevance: allocates 250 sm dedicated graduate student space within the relocation project, supporting graduate research and education in artificial intelligence and computer science. Provides purpose-designed collaborative research environment for graduate students in AI research, a key institutional priority.

documents.title: UTSC – Field House presentation
documents.summary: Presentation on the proposed UTSC Field House capital project by Dean Tim Tang. Project addresses growing demand for recreation, wellness, and student gathering space at UTSC as enrolment increases. Proposed facility includes 6,004 sm athletic/recreation space, 43 sm administrative space, 79 sm study space, 180 sm common use/student activity space, totaling 6,306 nasm and 7,194 gsm. Project timeline: Award of Design-Build Contract July 2026; 100% Design Development September–November 2026; Building Permit Application February–April 2027; Construction April 2027–May 2029; Occupancy May 2029. Incorporates sustainability features (low-energy consumption design), artificial turf, tensile roof system for natural lighting. Supports UTSC's Healthy Campus and Culture of Care priorities. Revenue-generating through external rentals while prioritizing student access.
documents.gradRelevance: Direct relevance: Field House addresses UTSC graduate and upper-year student demand for recreation and wellness facilities, currently at capacity. Expansion provides sports, recreation, and gathering spaces comparable to St. George campus facilities, supporting student experience, mental health, and sense of belonging for graduate students.

documents.title: St. George 2040 Campus Plan Overview
documents.summary: Comprehensive presentation of the St. George 2040 Campus Plan, a long-term vision for campus transformation toward 'a vibrant and connected campus where urban ecology meets public life.' Plan builds on 1999 Outdoor Space Master Plan and 2011 St. George Campus Master Plan. Identifies 175,000 GSM completed buildings over past 15 years and projects 350,000 NASM academic space shortfall and 2,700–4,700 student bed demand. Eight strategic framework areas: Central Campus Core (historic core extension), Green Corridor (Queen's Park North reconnection), Northeast Connection, Southwest Quadrant Hub, Ishpadinaa Placemaking (Spadina Avenue Indigenous corridor), Neighbourhood Gateway (northwest housing), Civic Corridor (Bloor Street engagement), and Innovation Corridor (research/health sciences connection). Includes three bold public realm gestures and campus-wide strategies for ecology, sustainability, Indigeneity, mobility, lighting, and accessibility. Public life study identified seven key takeaways on campus activation, connectivity, and shared street potential.
documents.gradRelevance: Moderate relevance: Campus Plan addresses student housing demand (2,700–4,700 beds projected need), commuter experience enhancement, and student engagement spaces. Graduate students will benefit from improved pedestrian connectivity, accessibility, public realm activation, and the Neighbourhood Gateway housing expansion in northwest quadrant, though specific graduate student allocations not detailed.

documents.title: Amendments to the Policy on Academic Continuity
documents.summary: Presentation by Vice-Provost Nicholas Rule on proposed amendments to the Policy on Academic Continuity. Amendments clarify scope of policy, roles during academic disruption (Provost, unit heads, instructors), and when disruption may be declared. Add explicit reference to academic freedom. Include new instruction that instructors should consider how to support or enhance academic continuity in course design and syllabi. Critical provision: if academic disruption is declared due to labour dispute, faculty members and librarians will not be required to carry out duties of employees legally engaged in work stoppage. Deans support the amendments.
documents.gradRelevance: Direct relevance: These amendments affect all graduate students during labour disputes or academic disruptions. Key provision clarifies that faculty and librarians cannot be required to perform work of striking unionized employees, which protects graduate student TAs and RAs and preserves labour dispute protections. Policy affects continuity of teaching and learning when disruptions occur.

packages.title: Academic Board - April 16, 2026
packages.summary: On April 16, 2026, the Academic Board approved two new academic programs effective September 1, 2027: a four-year honours Bachelor of Computer Science (BCS) offered by the Faculty of Arts & Science at St. George, UTM, and UTSC, replacing the Honours Bachelor of Science designation for computer science students; and a Doctor of Philosophy in Interdisciplinary and Applied Biology at UTSC, admitting approximately five students annually in four-year and five-year pathways with required practica and applied research components. The Board also reviewed follow-up reports on the Department of Art History and Institute of Medical Science, received information on twelve ongoing academic unit reviews highlighting strong program quality and faculty scholarship, approved the 2025-26 University Professors Selection Committee chaired by Provost Trevor Young, and received quarterly donation reports showing major gifts including $50 million from the Temerty Foundation for Temerty Building and $10 million from Google for an AI Chair.
packages.gradRelevance: The new PhD in Interdisciplinary and Applied Biology at UTSC directly affects graduate students, offering approximately five new positions annually (one to two direct-entry from bachelor's degree; four-year option from master's degree) beginning September 2027, with two full-time course equivalents covering coursework, four-month practicum placement, and thesis supervision. Graduate students in Department of Art History are affected by program improvements: revised PhD language requirements, comprehensive exam policies, and dissertation proposal procedures adopted December 3, 2025, plus updated PhD methods seminar launching 2026-27. The University Professors Selection Committee identifies distinguished scholars including six University Professors across disciplines; recipients receive $10,000 annual grants for five years. No tuition or funding changes for existing graduate programs announced.
keyItems: New Bachelor of Computer Science degree (four-year honours) approved for Faculty of Arts & Science, UTM, and UTSC, effective September 1, 2027, first conferral June 2028. Re-points existing Computer Science Specialist/Major programs and Data Science/Bioinformatics & Computational Biology/Information Security programs from Honours Bachelor of Science to new BCS designation; no changes to program requirements, admission standards, or enrolment. Transition period allows students admitted before September 2027 to graduate with either HBSc or BCS. OUAC application updates June 2026; Academic Calendars updated Spring/Summer 2027.

New PhD in Interdisciplinary and Applied Biology approved for UTSC Department of Biological Sciences, effective September 1, 2027. Program admits approximately five new students annually: four-year option from master's degree, five-year direct-entry from bachelor's degree. Curricular requirements: 2.0 FCEs coursework, elective coursework, seminar series, four-month external practicum placement with partner organizations, annual colloquium, qualifying examination, and original doctoral thesis. Five-year students complete additional 0.5 FCE electives. Makes Department of Biological Sciences a new graduate unit with appointed Graduate Chair and Coordinator.

Department of Art History completed curriculum-focused faculty retreat December 3, 2025 (five hours, Chestnut Conference Centre) adopting revised policies for MA/PhD language requirements, PhD comprehensive exams, and dissertation proposals. Mediated graduate faculty retreat September 5, 2025 (three hours, Art Gallery of Ontario) with professional facilitator addressing faculty climate and collegiality concerns. PhD methods seminar to be updated 2026-27. Department commits to continued implementation of review recommendations with Interim Monitoring Report due 2026-27.

Institute of Medical Science (IMS): Temerty Faculty of Medicine Dean's office consulting with Vice-Provost, Academic Programs on separate focused review of Graduate Diploma in Health Research (GDipHR). Internal assessment begins Spring 2026; findings to be included in Dean's Interim Monitoring Report due 2026-27.

Twelve external reviews of academic units and programs submitted to Committee on Academic Policy and Programs March 31, 2026, including provostial review of Leslie Dan Faculty of Pharmacy (March 3-5, 2025 site visit; external reviewers Michael Coughtrie, Jayanth Panyam, Cate Whittlesea) and eleven decanal reviews spanning Faculty of Arts & Science programs (Canadian Studies, Cognitive Science, Centre for Criminology and Sociolegal Studies, Spanish & Portuguese, Women & Gender Studies Institute), Faculty of Applied Science & Engineering (Electrical & Computer Engineering, Institute for Aerospace Studies), Temerty Faculty of Medicine (Physician Assistant program), UTM (Chemical & Physical Sciences, Historical Studies), and UTSC (Historical and Cultural Studies).

Leslie Dan Faculty of Pharmacy review identified research strengths: ranked top in Canada and within top 20 globally by citation index, publications, and extramural funding. PharmD provides balanced didactic, lab, and experiential education; PharmD for Pharmacists program delivering online with substantial experiential component and compressed from four years to three. Students benefit from wide experiential placements with engaged preceptors. PharmD for Pharmacists attracts approximately 60 applicants for upcoming intake.

documents.title: New Undergraduate Degree: Bachelor of Computer Science, Faculty of Arts and Science, University of Toronto Mississauga (UTM), University of Toronto Scarborough (UTSC)
documents.summary: Proposes four-year honours Bachelor of Computer Science degree effective September 1, 2027, offered by Faculty of Arts & Science (St. George), UTM, and UTSC. Addresses evolution of computer science discipline to include machine learning, quantum computing, cybersecurity, health informatics, and data privacy. Winter 2025 student survey across tri-campus showed overwhelming support for distinct BCS designation over general HBSc. Re-points 12 existing specialist/major programs (Computer Science, Data Science, Bioinformatics & Computational Biology, Information Security) from HBSc to BCS with zero changes to program objectives, learning outcomes, admission requirements, completion requirements, or enrolment plans. Degree-Level Expectations align with existing approved Bachelor's standards at each division. Transition period allows pre-September 2027 students choice of HBSc or BCS graduation. First students conferred June 2028. No resource changes; only divisional costs for communication plans.
documents.gradRelevance: No direct graduate-level implications, though approval demonstrates institutional commitment to discipline-specific credentialing that may influence graduate program positioning and employer perception of computer science expertise from U of T.

documents.title: New Graduate Program Proposal: Doctor of Philosophy in Interdisciplinary and Applied Biology, University of Toronto Scarborough (UTSC)
documents.summary: Proposes new PhD in Interdisciplinary and Applied Biology at UTSC effective September 1, 2027, creating graduate unit status for Department of Biological Sciences. Program offers two pathways: four-year option for master's degree holders and five-year direct-entry option for bachelor's degree holders. Curriculum: 2.0 FCEs coursework/seminars, 0.5 FCE additional for direct-entry students, four-month practicum with external partners (environmental, pharmaceutical, biotech, government agencies), annual colloquium, qualifying examination, original thesis. Admits approximately five new students annually (one to two direct-entry). External reviewers Silvia Cardona (University of Manitoba) and Laura Galloway (University of Virginia) strongly endorsed program, praising novel applied/interdisciplinary design, faculty expertise, excellent facilities (renovated open-concept labs, core facility with state-of-the-art microscopes, cell culture, molecular biology equipment, animal facilities, greenhouse). Department has 40 faculty members with impressive scholarship record and currently supervises 100 graduate students in tri-campus programs. Graduates positioned for roles as Biologist, Agricultural Chemist, Bioinformatician with employment prospects in government, consulting firms, pharmaceutical/biotech, hospitals, conservation authorities. Requires appointment of Graduate Chair Coordinator for day-to-day administration.
documents.gradRelevance: Directly creates ~25 new graduate student positions incrementally (5 per year for 5 years) in applied biology research with practicum requirement linking academic training to non-academic careers. Offers alternative pathway for bachelor's degree holders to pursue PhD without master's degree. Emphasizes interdisciplinarity and applied research, addressing student interest in linking graduate training to workforce readiness and diverse career pathways beyond academia.

documents.title: Follow-Up report on Reviews
documents.summary: Reports progress on two academic unit reviews. (a) Department of Art History (FAS) responded to February 2024 UTQAP review concerns by holding curriculum-focused faculty retreat December 3, 2025 (five hours) addressing graduate program policy: adopted revised MA/PhD language requirements, PhD comprehensive exam policies, and dissertation proposal procedures; discussed MA curriculum thesis option and methods seminar requirement. Also held mediated graduate faculty retreat September 5, 2025 (three hours) with professional facilitator addressing faculty climate concerns, resulting in shared principles and restored collegiality. PhD methods seminar to be updated 2026-27. (b) Institute of Medical Science (Temerty Faculty of Medicine) pursuing separate focused review of Graduate Diploma in Health Research (GDipHR); internal assessment begins Spring 2026 with findings to be included in Dean's Interim Monitoring Report (2026-27 academic year). Administrative understaffing previously delayed consultations.
documents.gradRelevance: Directly affects graduate students in Department of Art History through revised MA/PhD language requirements, comprehensive exam policies, dissertation procedures, and updated methods seminar launching 2026-27. IMS follow-up indicates separate review forthcoming for Graduate Diploma in Health Research, suggesting institutional commitment to program quality assessment.

documents.title: Semi-Annual Report on the Reviews of Academic Units and Programs
documents.summary: Compendium of 12 external reviews (1 provostial, 11 decanal) of academic units and programs submitted to Committee on Academic Policy and Programs March 31, 2026. Provostial review: Leslie Dan Faculty of Pharmacy (external reviewers Michael Coughtrie, Jayanth Panyam, Cate Whittlesea; site visit March 3-5, 2025). Decanal reviews span FAS (Canadian Studies, Cognitive Science, Centre for Criminology and Sociolegal Studies, Spanish & Portuguese, Women & Gender Studies Institute), Faculty of Applied Science & Engineering (Electrical & Computer Engineering, Institute for Aerospace Studies), Temerty Faculty of Medicine (Physician Assistant program), UTM (Chemical & Physical Sciences, Historical Studies), and UTSC (Historical and Cultural Studies). Reviews highlight strong program quality, excellent faculty scholarship and research (Leslie Dan ranked top in Canada and top 20 globally by citations/publications/extramural funding), high-calibre students, innovative curricula, interdisciplinary breadth, theory-to-practice connections through research and experiential learning. Common recommendations: strengthen coordination across cognate programs, ensure teaching delivery sustainability, enhance student/faculty mentorship and support, address enrolment pressures, strengthen career supports, ensure diversity/accessibility in curricula/hiring/opportunities, and strategic complement planning.
documents.gradRelevance: Leslie Dan Pharmacy review notes graduate programs (MSc, MScPhm, PhD Pharmaceutical Sciences) with recommendations to support graduate student progress to completion and improve income streams for graduate students. Reviews note need for expanded experiential placements and career development support, relevant to graduate research training and professional outcomes. Recommendations underscore importance of diversity and accessibility goals in student opportunities.

documents.title: Appointments: University Professors Selection Committee
documents.summary: Establishes 2025-26 University Professors Selection Committee chaired by Vice-President & Provost Trevor Young, comprising six internal University Professors (Dan Breznitz, Jutta Brunnée, Sheena Josselyn, Alison Keith, Frank Kschischang, Jeremy Quastel) representing broad disciplinary spectrum and two external members from peer institutions (Jacques Beauvais, University of Ottawa; Wanda Costen, Dalhousie University). University Professor designation recognizes unusual scholarly achievement and pre-eminence in field; limited to approximately 2% of tenured faculty, underscoring prestige. Recipients receive $10,000 annual grant for first five years of appointment. Selection procedures adhere to University of Toronto policy; recommendations submitted to Academic Board for approval.
documents.gradRelevance: University Professors often serve as doctoral thesis supervisors and mentors; presence of distinguished scholars across disciplines (Munk School Global Affairs, Law, Physiology, Classics, Mathematics, Electrical & Computer Engineering) signals institutional recognition of research excellence relevant to graduate research environment and supervision quality.

documents.title: Quarterly Report on Donations of $250,000 or more to the University of Toronto November 1, 2025 – January 31, 2026
documents.summary: Quarterly report of major gifts ($250,000+) received November 1, 2025 – January 31, 2026. Top gifts: Temerty Foundation $50 million for Temerty Building (Temerty Faculty of Medicine); Google $10 million for Hinton Chair in Artificial Intelligence (FAS); William Breen Family $5,056,980 for Irene Frances Breen Chair in Hemorrhagic Stroke (Temerty Faculty of Medicine); Anonymous Family Foundation $5 million for Big-Canoe Chair in Indigenous Health (Temerty Faculty of Medicine); Estate of Peter K. Strangway $4,260,822 for Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis Research Fund (Temerty Faculty of Medicine); Johnson & Johnson $1,500,000 for Chair in Advanced Data Analytics and Methods (Leslie Dan Faculty of Pharmacy); Younus Khan $1,500,000 for Khan Family Awards and Instruction Lab (UTSC); Paul Joseph Brown & Lisa Mennet $1,068,450 for Public History Engagement Fund (FAS); Michael Chai $1,000,000 for ESROP Award and five PhD Scholarships (Faculty of Applied Science & Engineering); multiple additional gifts supporting chairs, funds, fellowships, and awards across health sciences, applied engineering, public health, architecture, and management.
documents.gradRelevance: Graduate student funding sources include PhD scholarships (Michael Chai's five named PhD Scholarships in Engineering; Cedra G. Xie, Lennon Octavian Chai, Leo Zalucki, Luca Olivier Chai, Pomelo F. Xie scholarships); fellowships (Dalla Lana Fellowships in Health Impact $1 million, Anthony & Agnes Lee Distinguished Doctoral Fellowship); research funds supporting graduate thesis work (multiple medical research funds, data analytics/methods chair supporting graduate methodology training).

packages.title: Academic Board - April 16, 2026
packages.summary: On April 16, 2026, the Academic Board approved two major academic proposals: a new Bachelor of Computer Science honours degree across Faculty of Arts & Science, UTM, and UTSC, effective September 1, 2027, and a new Doctor of Philosophy in Interdisciplinary and Applied Biology at UTSC, also effective September 1, 2027, admitting ~5 students/year and using reallocated weighted graduate units (WGUs) with no new funded spaces. The Board received updates on student mental health supports confirming near-full implementation of the 2019 Task Force's 21 recommendations, including a stepped-care model, near-elimination of counselling wait times, and expanded CAMH partnerships. The University's $408 million student aid budget (via UTAPS and endowments) was highlighted as well above provincial average. Semi-annual UTQAP reviews of 12 academic units and programs were reported, with concerns raised about tri-campus consultation practices and engagement of cognate units during reviews.
packages.gradRelevance: The new PhD in Interdisciplinary and Applied Biology directly affects graduate students: it will enroll ~5 new students annually starting September 2027 via reallocation of existing weighted graduate units (WGUs) with no new government-funded positions, use paid practicums that do not conflict with scholarship regulations, and receive departmental administrative support without a dedicated graduate administrator. Graduate students benefit from the mental health initiative's near-elimination of counselling wait times and integrated mental health–accessibility services pilot. The $408 million student aid budget supports graduate bursaries and scholarships. Board discussion flagged concerns about faculty capacity on graduate committees; the Provost confirmed the small cohort would not create undue strain. Graduate-relevant policy reviews underway include the Supportive Leave Policy (recommendations expected next cycle) and Code of Student Conduct revisions (incorporating a mental health diversion process).
keyItems: Bachelor of Computer Science honours degree approved for Faculty of Arts & Science, UTM, UTSC, effective Sept 1, 2027; first graduates June 2028; existing Honours BSc students may choose either designation; motivated by computer science community recognition of distinct cohort, no resource/enrolment changes, extensive tri-campus consultation completed

PhD in Interdisciplinary and Applied Biology (UTSC) approved effective Sept 1, 2027; 4- and 5-year pathways; ~5 students/year admitted; research-intensive thesis plus interdisciplinary coursework and paid practicum; funded via reallocation of existing WGUs (no new government-funded positions)

Student mental health: near-full implementation of 2019 Task Force's 21 recommendations including stepped-care model, elimination of counselling wait times, expanded CAMH/hospital partnerships, integrated mental health–accessibility services pilot launched with promising results
Student aid: $408 million budget (UTAPS + endowments); support well above provincial average per Provost statement

Code of Student Conduct review completed with key recommendations: separate Code from procedures, clarify scope/administration, incorporate mental health diversion process; protest user guide introduced 18 months prior
Supportive Leave Policy review underway; revised recommendations expected next governance cycle

documents.title: 2019 Student Mental Health Task Force – Final Report & Recommendations (December 2019)
documents.summary: A comprehensive report from the Presidential & Provostial Task Force on Student Mental Health, chaired by Dr. Trevor Young (then Vice-Provost and Dean of Medicine), with assessors Joshua Barker (Vice-Provost, Graduate Research & Education, Dean of School of Graduate Studies) and Sandy Welsh (Vice-Provost, Students). The Task Force conducted 43 listening sessions (34 focus groups + 9 open sessions) across three campuses July–October 2019, plus Innovation Hub feedback, resulting in 21 recommendations across four mandates: mental health service delivery, tri-campus coordination, community partnerships (CAMH, hospitals), and physical spaces. Key findings identified a stepped-care model as essential, noted significant unmet demand (52% of Canadian post-secondary students reported depressing symptoms in 2019, up from 44% in 2016), and highlighted graduate student stress (36% of PhD students sought help for anxiety/depression per Nature study). Recommendations emphasize eliminating wait times, expanding community partnerships, and shifting campus culture toward 'Culture of Caring.' Report also addresses institutional systems, policies, and underlying culture issues affecting mental health.
documents.gradRelevance: Directly relevant to graduate students: 36% of PhD students reported seeking help for anxiety or depression related to their PhD; 33% of graduate students report stress impacting academic progress, 24% anxiety, 17% depression (NCHA data cited). Task Force recommended stepped-care model, elimination of counselling wait times, expanded services for students with complex mental illness, mental health diversion processes, integrated mental health–accessibility services, and culture change recognizing graduate student isolation in some disciplines. Report calls for comprehensive mental health strategy treating graduate students as high-risk population due to research-intensive environment and, in some fields, isolation.

documents.title: Tri-Campus Review: Academic Planning and Academic Change Working Group – Final Recommendations (February 2020)
documents.summary: A working group report from the Tri-Campus Review (2018–2021) examining academic planning, program change, unit structure, and the principle of a single tri-campus doctoral offering across UTM, UTSC, and St. George campuses. The report documents significant growth: undergraduate enrolment at UTSC rose from 5,564 (2002) to 11,083 (2018), at UTM from 5,657 to 12,332; graduate enrolment university-wide grew from 10,082 FTE (2002) to 18,319 (2018), with UTM/UTSC graduate students increasing from 168/70 FTE (2002) to 482/190 (2018). The report affirms strong support for unitary tri-campus PhD programs drawing on faculty strengths from all three campuses, notes challenges in tri-campus collaboration and inter-campus movement, and identifies concerns about insufficient consultation of cognate units during UTQAP reviews—a key theme echoed by the Academic Board Chair at the April 2026 meeting. Recommendations address balancing local autonomy with tri-campus coherence, justifiable duplication, and the need for better tri-campus engagement in external reviews.
documents.gradRelevance: Critical to graduate program governance: affirms the unitary tri-campus PhD model as central to U of T's research strength and international standing; documents growth in graduate enrolment (especially at UTM/UTSC); recommends strengthened engagement of graduate Chairs from all three campuses in UTQAP cyclical reviews, particularly where tri-campus doctoral programs are involved. Flags that insufficient tri-campus engagement during self-study and site visits can hinder implementation of review recommendations. The April 2026 Academic Board Chair's concerns about consultation practices directly reference this report's 2018–2021 findings and prior 2002–2012 strategic framework documents.

packages.title: Academic Board - February 26, 2026
packages.summary: On February 26, 2026, the Academic Board approved a $3.66 billion balanced budget for 2026-27 and long-range guidelines through 2030-31, featuring $39 million revenue growth but $101 million below prior projections due to recalibrated international enrolment and domestic tuition freeze. The Board approved a new four-year Bachelor of Information program at the Faculty of Information (starting September 1, 2027, with 400 planned students); recommended the Temerty Building (MSB West Wing Redevelopment) interim project scope for early works including electrical duct banks and interior demolition of MSB Block B; and approved Project LEAP 2.0, a climate infrastructure initiative eliminating legacy steam systems and investing in electrification, heat recovery, and solar installations. Provincial funding announced February 12, 2026, permits 2% domestic tuition increases starting 2026-27 and included health program corridor changes, STEM funding increases, and 40,000 competitive funded expansion seats across Ontario institutions.
packages.gradRelevance: Graduate students benefit directly from a committed minimum $40,000 base funding level for doctoral students (maintained in 2026-27 budget), representing the University's explicit affordability commitment. The budget allocates $408 million to financial aid (including increases to UTAPS) and $1.7 billion in endowed student support funds (43% of total endowment), benefiting graduate students with need-based bursaries. Incoming MA and MSc tuition will increase 3.1% and MScAC by 7.5% under government anomaly adjustment; however, the University Fund allocates $4.1 million to support emerging talent recruitment including postdoctoral scholars. Graduate enrolment remained stable at 2025-26 with 26% international graduate students. The new Temerty Building will provide research space for Faculty of Medicine graduate programs and the Department of Cell & Systems Biology, while Project LEAP 2.0 infrastructure improvements support research facilities in clinical spaces including Dentistry (relevant to graduate health sciences training).
keyItems: Budget Report 2026-27 and Long Range Budget Guidelines 2026-27 to 2030-31 approved: $3.66 billion balanced budget with $39 million increase over 2025-26 but $101 million less than previously planned; 1.1% revenue growth (below 5.6% historical average and below inflation); tuition and fees represent 65% of revenues, operating grants 20%, other revenues 15%

Provincial funding announcement February 12, 2026 permits 2% domestic tuition increases starting 2026-27, then 3% in 2027-28 and beyond; incoming MA and MSc tuition increase 3.1%, MScAC increases 7.5% under government anomaly adjustment; Strategic Mandate Agreement (SMA4) through 2029-30 with 25% of funding tied to metrics growing to 40% by Year 5; health programs moved to separate corridor and funded expansion added to base

Minimum $40,000 base funding commitment for doctoral students maintained; $408 million total financial aid budget for 2026-27 with increases to UTAPS program; $1.7 billion in endowed funds for student support (43% of total endowment); $88 million allocated to International Scholars and Pearson Scholarship programs in 2026-27

Cost containment measures include 5% base reduction to shared services, planned 2% staff complement reductions, moderated faculty hiring, and limits on discretionary spending; $1.0 million annually for Operational Excellence program; shared service costs held flat with efficiencies offsetting compensation, utilities, software, and service contract increases

Priority investments total $4.1 million for emerging talent salary support and postdoctoral recruitment; $4.2 million for research and innovation including research networks and federal defence funding strategy; $15 million for institutional digital strategy and responsible AI adoption

Undergraduate enrolment 2025-26 exceeded plan by 1,159 students (71,581 total); international undergraduate enrolment reduced by 1,083 below target (4.8% below 2024); planned international share increases from 28.6% (2025-26) to 29.8% (2030-31) but domestic students reduced by 990 to return to funded levels; 40+ new students from 15 separate countries in Fall 2025 with no single country exceeding 50%

documents.title: Budget 2026-27 and Long-Range Budget Guidelines 2026-27 to 2030-31
documents.summary: Comprehensive budget presentation detailing $3.66 billion balanced 2026-27 budget with $39 million revenue growth but $101 million below prior plans. Documents revenue sources (65% tuition/fees, 20% operating grants, 15% other), expenditures (65% faculty/staff compensation, 11% student aid, 6% capital/equipment, 6% occupancy), and cost containment measures (5% shared services reduction, 2% staff complement reductions, moderated faculty hiring). Details provincial funding changes announced February 12, 2026 permitting 2% domestic tuition increases 2026-27 and 3% thereafter; Strategic Mandate Agreement performance metrics (40% research, 60% teaching; 25-40% funding tied to metrics); domestic tuition freeze 2026-27; and planned international undergraduate enrolment moderation.
documents.gradRelevance: Directly addresses graduate student funding: minimum $40,000 base for doctoral students; $408 million financial aid budget with UTAPS increases; $1.7 billion endowed student support; MA/MSc tuition increase 3.1%, MScAC 7.5%; $88 million international scholarships; 26% international graduate students in 2025-26; University Fund allocates $4.1 million for emerging talent including postdoctoral recruitment and $4.2 million for research/innovation networks.

documents.title: University of Toronto - Temerty Building (MSB West Wing Redevelopment)
documents.summary: Project presentation for Temerty Building uniting Temerty Faculty of Medicine and Faculty of Arts & Science Department of Cell & Systems Biology. Proposes 8,293 net new assignable square meters (NASM) and 17,169 gross square meters (GSM), replacing existing MSB Block B 'West Wing' (8,806 NASM, 16,648 GSM). Space allocation: Arts & Science 7,483 NASM; Medicine 7,664 NASM; Institutional 1,952 NASM; Facilities & Services nodal plant 2,441 NASM; Building Services 5,786 GSM. Uses Integrated Project Delivery (IPD) model with open-book financials and Target Value Design. Early works (approved February 2026 governance) include site mobilization, electrical duct bank construction, utility relocations, and MSB west wing interior abatement/demolition. Full project cost approval anticipated September-December 2026. Anticipated occupancy July 2031.
documents.gradRelevance: Provides new state-of-the-art laboratory and teaching space for Faculty of Medicine and Department of Cell & Systems Biology graduate programs; supports interdisciplinary research and collaboration; includes active learning spaces and student support facilities relevant to graduate student research training and experience.

documents.title: LEAP 2.0: Climate Positive Campus Infrastructure Initiative
documents.summary: Project overview of LEAP 2.0 (Low Energy Acceleration Program Phase 2) addressing deferred maintenance and energy infrastructure renewal. Key components: eliminates legacy steam network and critical operational assets; renews ventilation, heating, cooling, and electrical systems; replaces obsolete controls; implements electrification of heating, hot water, lab sterilization; installs heat recovery, heat pumps, 150kW solar PV, LED lighting, improved insulation. Addresses $1.5 billion deferred maintenance liability with $350 million planned spend over three years. Supports program expansion in Dentistry. Provides increased electrical capacity, enhanced ventilation/humidification for clinical spaces (Clinic 1, pediatric), improved clinic resiliency, and emergency power connections for perimeter heating.
documents.gradRelevance: Infrastructure improvements support graduate research facilities in clinical and laboratory spaces, particularly for Dentistry and health sciences graduate programs; enhanced electrical capacity and resilient systems enable expanded research programming; energy efficiency and decarbonization align with institutional sustainability commitments affecting graduate student research environment.

packages.title: Academic Board - November 13, 2025
packages.summary: At its November 13, 2025 meeting, the Academic Board received a presentation on AI literacy initiatives for faculty from Professor Susan McCahan, heard from the Provost about the federal budget's positive impact on research funding (including $1.65 billion for research talent recruitment and announcement of a new Research Excellence Postdoctoral Fellows Program funding up to 100 two-year fellowships at $80,000 annual salary plus $10,000 research funding), approved amendments to the Part-Time Faculty employment policy allowing appointments up to three years (affecting ~430 part-time faculty, effective January 1, 2026), approved revisions to the Diploma Replacement and Reissuance Policy enabling remote declarations, reviewed eight academic unit program reviews across all three campuses with emphasis on experiential learning gaps, received the final report on the Statement on Prohibited Discrimination with 13 recommendations including creation of a Human Rights Office, received revisions to the Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment Policy (reviewed by Faye Mishna with consultations generating 150 feedback items), and heard discipline case statistics showing declining academic offence numbers from pandemic highs.
packages.gradRelevance: Graduate students are directly affected by multiple items: the new Research Excellence Postdoctoral Fellows Program ($20.9 million over five years for up to 100 fellows at $80,000/year plus $10,000 research funding) and Postdoctoral Competitive Research Awards ($3 million over three years) support early-career researchers; the AI literacy initiatives including new ChatGPT-EDU and Claude licenses and planned multi-model platform access (targeted for end of academic year) directly benefit grad researchers; School of Graduate Studies is leading an 'AI Anonymous' confidential focus-group initiative for graduate students to report AI use practices; guidance on appropriate AI use in graduate academic milestones is available; and the revised Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment Policy (with 12-month timeline clarification and respondent supports) applies to all students including graduate students.
keyItems: New Research Excellence Postdoctoral Fellows Program: $20.9 million investment over five years funding up to 100 two-year fellowships at $80,000 annual salary plus $10,000 research funding annually—exceeding current top federally funded post-doctoral programs
Postdoctoral Competitive Research Awards: $3 million over three years in awards valued at $10,000 or $30,000 to recognize current postdoctoral scholars and support independent research launches

Three leading scholars recruited: astrophysicist Sara Seager (from MIT, U of T alumna), business scholar Jacquelyn Pless (from MIT), and economist Mark Duggan (from Stanford, to become Director of Munk School)

Undergraduate enrolment: approximately 17,000 students with composition shift of ~300 fewer international students (1,100 below target) and ~200 more domestic students (900 above target); forecasted revenue shortfall of ~1.6% (approximately $60 million)
Federal budget provided $5 billion over three years in Health Infrastructure Fund; doctoral student permits exempted from provincial attestation letters and receiving expedited processing

AI Literacy Initiatives: Licensed ChatGPT-EDU available through library; protected Claude licenses being deployed to groups; multi-model platform providing protected AI models to students, staff, and faculty planned for end of academic year; CTSI offering fall series and workshops on adapting teaching and assessment with generative AI

documents.title: AI Initiatives for Faculty - Presentation
documents.summary: Professor Susan McCahan presented U of T's measured approach to generative AI for faculty, highlighting that the University is redesigning assessments to strengthen human critical thinking. The presentation outlined instructional supports available through the Centre for Teaching Support & Innovation (CTSI) including teaching examples, workshops, and a Fall Series on adapting teaching and assessment with generative AI, with parallel offerings at UTM. For faculty conducting AI research, resources include CRIS materials on generative AI for researchers, School of Graduate Studies guidance on appropriate AI use in graduate milestones, libraries' resources on copyright considerations, and CTSI materials on AI tools. The presentation confirmed ChatGPT-EDU licenses available through the library's licensing office and protected Claude licenses being deployed to interested groups, with the University expecting to expand access to additional protected models and launch a multi-model platform.
documents.gradRelevance: Graduate students benefit directly from School of Graduate Studies guidance on appropriate AI use in graduate academic milestones and the planned multi-model platform enabling access to multiple protected AI models by end of academic year. Resources for AI use in research are explicitly available to graduate researchers through CRIS, libraries, and CTSI.

documents.title: AI Task Force Report & Recommendations - Research Working Group
documents.summary: Delivered June 2025, this comprehensive report from the Research Working Group outlines AI's potential to augment research while managing risks including environmental costs, data sovereignty threats, bias, and unethical intellectual property use. The report makes three overarching recommendations: researchers should explore responsible AI use to advance research quality; U of T should support researchers in developing AI skills and infrastructure; and U of T should leverage its AI leadership to establish norms and principles across higher education. Specific recommendations for researchers include exploring AI potential aligned with disciplinary norms, reviewing AI output (authors remain responsible), disclosing substantive AI use, checking publisher/funder policies, avoiding AI for peer review, and faculty mentoring trainees in AI literacy. Institutional recommendations include reviewing research policies through an AI lens, investing in AI research infrastructure, developing training resources, and leading policy development. The report includes membership spanning diverse disciplines and university functions, consultation with VPRI, ROCO, Research Advisory Board, Research Administration Leaders Forum, librarians, and School of Graduate Studies leadership.
documents.gradRelevance: This report directly impacts graduate students as trainees engaged in research. Faculty are encouraged to mentor trainees in developing AI literacy as an academic competency, and those co-authoring with students must ensure students understand AI responsibilities. Graduate students are included in U of T researchers to whom these recommendations apply; the report emphasizes institutional support for researchers including students and trainees in developing AI skills and accessing necessary infrastructure.

documents.title: Semi-Annual Report: Academic Appeals Committee, Individual Cases, Fall 2025
documents.summary: This report details two academic appeals cases heard by the Academic Appeals Committee. Report #440 concerns a student in Faculty of Applied Science and Engineering seeking to retake a final exam in CIV332H1S (Transport II) following a car accident on April 19, 2024 that caused post-accident injuries (migraines and body pain) affecting exam performance. The Committee dismissed the appeal, finding that while the accident was documented by police report and hospital visit on April 19, the single medical verification from Dr. Rostas dated May 24, 2024 (over one month later) was insufficiently contemporaneous to establish the accident's effect on exam performance; the Committee emphasized that Division policy requires clear documentation close in time to the exam. Report #441 concerns a UTM student appealing denial of late withdrawal without academic penalty from three mathematics courses (MAT240H5S, MAT159H5S, MAT136H5Y) due to claimed impact from father's medical emergency; the student submitted one undated medical document, failed to attend the scheduled June 2, 2025 hearing via Zoom despite multiple notifications, did not submit additional materials, and did not respond to Notice of Intention to Dismiss, resulting in dismissal for lack of merit and failure to attend.
documents.gradRelevance: These cases illustrate academic regulations affecting all students including graduate students regarding exam deferrals, late withdrawals, and medical documentation requirements. The cases establish precedent that documentation must be contemporaneous (close in time) to the academic event and that medical verification alone from statements after the fact may be insufficient. Graduate students facing similar circumstances should understand these evidentiary standards.

documents.title: Semi-Annual Report: University Tribunal, Individual Cases, Fall 2025
documents.summary: This report documents 14 academic discipline cases heard under the Code of Behaviour on Academic Matters (CBAM) 2025 during Fall 2025. Cases involved serious academic offences including: forged Verification of Illness forms (suspensions of 2–5 years), unauthorized aids in exams including microphones with earpieces and button cameras (suspensions of 3–5 years or up to 5 years, with one expulsion and appeal dismissal), hiring someone to personate in exams (expulsion with appeal dismissal, or 5-year suspension), purchasing essays (5-year suspensions), and use of unauthorized aids across multiple exams including phone communication and shared documents with other students (one graduate student case resulted in degree recall and cancellation). Notable case outcomes include: student using unauthorized aids in 8 courses (graduate student, health care program) receiving degree recall/cancellation with 5-year transcript notation; student found not guilty of possessing smartwatch when items were discovered and removed before exam commencement; plagiarism case involving student with undiagnosed ADHD receiving 5-year suspension with five prior offences over three years. Panel considerations emphasized seriousness of premeditated offences, deterrence importance, commercial elements as aggravating factors, cooperation and remorse as mitigating factors, and alignment with existing case law.
documents.gradRelevance: This report directly affects graduate students subject to academic discipline. One case explicitly involves a graduate student in a health care program whose unauthorized aid use across 8 courses resulted in degree recall and cancellation, demonstrating the most severe consequences. Graduate students engaged in research should note the emphasis on integrity standards; cases show serious consequences for purchasing work, using outside assistance, and other misconduct. The report establishes disciplinary norms applicable to all students including graduate students.

documents.title: Membership of the Advisory Committee on the University of Toronto Library System for 2025-2026
documents.summary: The Advisory Committee on the University of Toronto Library System for 2025-26 was formed by the Chief Librarian on behalf of the Provost and submitted for information to Academic Board. The Committee consists of 19 members total: the Chief Librarian (Larry Alford), two Academic Board members (Catherine Chandler-Crichlow, Dean of Continuing Studies; Matt Ratto, Associate Dean of Faculty of Information), three professional librarians (Kathryn Barrett, Kathleen Scheaffer, Graeme Slaght), ten distinguished faculty from diverse disciplines, two student representatives (Damola Dina from UTSU, Nicholas Silver from UTGSU as Vice-President Academics), and two assessors serving as campus library chiefs. The Committee reviews library policies in light of the University's general and divisional objectives and makes recommendations to the office of the Vice-President & Provost; major policy matters are brought forward to appropriate Governing Council committees.
documents.gradRelevance: Nicholas Silver, Vice-President Academics of the University of Toronto Graduate Students' Union (UTGSU), serves as a student representative on this Committee, providing graduate student voice in library policy review and recommendations. This membership ensures graduate student perspectives inform library system policy development.

packages.title: Academic Board - October 09, 2025
packages.summary: At its October 9, 2025 meeting, the Academic Board received reports on the University of Toronto's 21st-place ranking in Times Higher Education global rankings, administrative return-to-campus plans beginning January 2026, and significant enrolment shifts: 17,000 new undergraduates (12,000 domestic, 5,000 international), representing a 5% year-over-year decline in international enrolment and 8% domestic enrolment above target, creating a projected $50M–$60M operating revenue shortfall for the fiscal year. The Board heard updates on the AI Task Force's six working groups and recommendations for responsible AI integration, including deployment of secure AI tools (ChatGPT.Edu, Microsoft Copilot, Claude) with 500+ licenses sold and 71 instructors onboarded. The Board also addressed campus safety following an October 7 incident at UTM affecting the Jewish Student Association's tabling event, confirmed the Policy on Social and Political Issues with Respect to Divestment, and approved 10 prioritized capital projects including MacMillan Theatre Remediation and the Temerty Faculty of Medicine West Wing.
packages.gradRelevance: Graduate students are affected by the $50M–$60M operating revenue shortfall, which may constrain funding and services; the University's plan to recalibrate enrolment targets and rely on reserves rather than program cuts; the AI Task Force initiatives, including secure AI tool access and integration into teaching, learning, and research; and the campus safety incident and discrimination policies affecting all students. The report mentions Graduate Education as a stakeholder in the AI Task Force but provides no specific funding, stipend, or TA/RA impacts.
keyItems: University of Toronto ranked 21st globally in Times Higher Education rankings; Provost emphasized exceptional faculty work and research excellence as drivers of ranking.
Administrative staff return-to-campus transition beginning January 2026 or earlier in some divisions, to be implemented over several months with HR and operations support.

Fall 2025 enrolment: 17,000 new undergraduates (12,000 domestic, 5,000 international); 5% year-over-year decline in international enrolment (approx. 300 fewer students) versus 200 more domestic students; domestic enrolment 8% above target, creating government funding gap.

Computer science enrolment down 25–30% across Ontario; UTSC's Scarborough Academic of Medicine and Integrated Health (SAMIH) performed strongly in domestic recruitment; engineering and business programs remained popular.

Projected operating revenue shortfall of $50M–$60M (1.5% below planned revenue) due to lower international tuition revenue and lack of additional government operating grant for above-funded domestic enrolment; University planning to draw on contingencies and reserves as short-term measure while recalibrating enrolment targets.

AI Task Force established Spring 2024 with six working groups (Teaching & Learning, Research, Student Services, People Strategy & Administration, Operations & Planning, Technology & Data Governance); delivered recommendations June 2025; 500+ ChatGPT.Edu and Microsoft Copilot licenses sold; 71 instructors onboarded across 10 divisions; 'AI Kitchen' secure multi-model platform pre-launched.

packages.title: Agenda Committee - April 09, 2026
packages.summary: On April 9, 2026, the Agenda Committee received follow-up reports from the Department of Art History (Faculty of Arts and Science) and Institute of Medical Science (Temerty Faculty of Medicine), plus a semi-annual compendium of 12 external reviews of academic units and programs. The Art History follow-up (due to 2024 UTQAP concerns) reported successful curriculum and faculty climate improvements: a five-hour retreat on December 3, 2025, produced revised MA/PhD language requirements, PhD comprehensive exam policies, and dissertation proposal policies; a three-hour mediated retreat on September 5, 2025, led by a professional facilitator, restored faculty collegiality. IMS's one-year follow-up reported that an internal assessment of the Graduate Diploma in Health Research (GDipHR) will commence in Spring 2026, with findings included in the Dean's Interim Monitoring Report for the 2026-27 academic year. The semi-annual compendium covered twelve reviews (one provostial: Leslie Dan Faculty of Pharmacy; eleven decanal across FAS, Engineering, Medicine, UTM, and UTSC), highlighting consistent themes: strong program quality and international reputation, student caliber, innovative curricula, equity/diversity/inclusion advances, and recommendations for strengthened program coordination, enrolment sustainability, experiential learning, career supports, and accessibility.
packages.gradRelevance: The Art History follow-up directly affects graduate students through newly revised MA and PhD language requirements, comprehensive examination policies, and dissertation proposal policies adopted December 3, 2025, plus an updated PhD methods seminar coming in 2026-27. The Leslie Dan Pharmacy review (spanning the compendium) identifies specific recommendations for graduate student support, including enhanced income streams for graduate students, better cross-disciplinary interaction across the two graduate fields (Biomolecular and Clinical/Social/Administrative Pharmaceutical Sciences), and addressing graduate student payment challenges. The compendium's broader themes note commitments to strengthening mentorship for students and faculty, enhancing career and experiential supports, and ensuring diversity and accessibility in student opportunities. No changes to stipends, TA/RA rates, fees, or unionized labour matters are documented in these materials.
keyItems: Department of Art History held five-hour curriculum-focused faculty retreat on December 3, 2025, at Chestnut Conference Centre, resulting in adopted revised policies for MA and PhD language requirements, PhD comprehensive examinations, and dissertation proposals; faculty agreed to update PhD methods seminar for 2026-27 academic year.

Department of Art History held three-hour mediated retreat for graduate faculty on September 5, 2025, at Art Gallery of Ontario, led by professional academic facilitator, addressing faculty climate concerns and restoring sense of shared principles and collegiality.

Institute of Medical Science (IMS) and Graduate Diploma in Health Research (GDipHR) Directors agreed a separate focused review of GDipHR should be conducted; internal assessment will commence Spring 2026, with findings included in Dean's Interim Monitoring Report due 2026-27 academic year.

Semi-annual compendium covering twelve external reviews (one provostial, eleven decanal) across Leslie Dan Pharmacy, FAS (Canadian Studies, Cognitive Science, Centre for Criminology, Spanish and Portuguese, Women and Gender Studies), Engineering (Electrical & Computer Engineering, Aerospace Studies), Medicine (Physician Assistant program), UTM (Chemical & Physical Sciences, Historical Studies), and UTSC (Historical and Cultural Studies).

Leslie Dan Pharmacy review (March 3-5, 2025; report received April 23, 2025; administrative response February 20, 2026) identified strengths in research intensity (ranked top in Canada, top 20 globally), PharmD curriculum redesign, student engagement, and alumni career placement; opportunities include supporting graduate student income streams, improving cross-disciplinary interaction between Biomolecular and Clinical/Social/Administrative Pharmaceutical Sciences fields, addressing graduate student payment challenges, and expanding experiential placements.

Cross-cutting themes in all twelve reviews: strong program quality and international reputation, high student calibre, innovative curricula with interdisciplinary breadth, commitments to equity/diversity/inclusion, and recommendations for strengthening program coordination, ensuring teaching sustainability, enhancing experiential and career supports, managing enrolment pressures, and ensuring accessibility in curricula and hiring.

documents.title: Semi-Annual Report on the Reviews of Academic Units and Programs
documents.summary: Compendium of twelve external reviews of academic units and programs (one provostial, eleven decanal) submitted to AP&P for discussion and feedback on March 31, 2026. Leslie Dan Pharmacy (provostial review, March 3–5, 2025 visit; report received April 23, 2025; administrative response February 20, 2026) reviewed PharmD, PharmD for Pharmacists, Certificate in Global Studies in Pharmacy, MSc Pharmaceutical Sciences, MScPhm, and PhD Pharmaceutical Sciences across two fields (Biomolecular; Clinical, Social, and Administrative). Reviewers (Michael Coughtrie, UBC; Jayanth Panyam, University of Washington; Cate Whittlesea, UCL) noted strengths including top-20-global research intensity, curriculum redesign aligned with best practices, accessibility initiatives for underserved communities, strong partnerships with precepting pharmacists, and diverse alumni career placements. Recommendations included supporting graduate student income streams, improving cross-disciplinary interaction between the two graduate fields, addressing graduate student payment challenges, expanding experiential placements, enhancing faculty mentoring and onboarding, addressing financial stability, and enrolment management. Eleven decanal reviews across FAS (Canadian Studies, Cognitive Science, Centre for Criminology, Spanish and Portuguese, Women and Gender Studies), Engineering (Electrical & Computer, Aerospace Studies), Medicine (Physician Assistant), UTM (Chemical & Physical Sciences, Historical Studies), and UTSC (Historical and Cultural Studies). Overall themes: strong program quality and reputation, high student calibre, innovative and relevant curricula, interdisciplinary breadth, theory-to-practice connection, equity/diversity/inclusion commitment, vibrant communities; areas for development include program coordination, teaching sustainability, enrolment management, experiential/career supports, and accessibility in curricula and faculty hiring.
documents.gradRelevance: Leslie Dan Pharmacy review specifically recommends supporting graduate student income streams, improving cross-disciplinary community across Biomolecular and Clinical/Social/Administrative Pharmaceutical Sciences graduate fields, and addressing graduate student payment challenges. Broader compendium themes emphasize enhanced mentorship and career supports for students, strengthening experiential learning opportunities, and ensuring diversity and accessibility in student opportunities and faculty hiring—all relevant to graduate students across multiple programs.

packages.title: Agenda Committee - April 09, 2026
packages.summary: The Agenda Committee met on April 9, 2026 to review academic program follow-ups, approve the April 16 Academic Board agenda, and approve 18 academic administrative appointments across multiple faculties. The Department of Art History and Institute of Medical Science submitted satisfactory follow-up reports with no concerns raised. Eleven decanal reviews and one Provostial review were discussed, revealing common themes around student support, time to completion, and program structure; a follow-up report was requested from the Faculty of Arts & Science Centre for Criminology and Sociolegal Studies. The Committee also discussed consultation processes for program reviews, specifically regarding Teaching Assistant distribution and graduate student recruitment in tri-campus contexts. Administrative appointments approved include Graduate Chairs in Mathematics (Mary Pugh, 2026–2029), Italian/Spanish/Portuguese/Latin American Studies (Susan Antebi, 2027–2032), and Department for the Study of Religion (Pamela Klassen renewable 2026–2032), along with Associate Deans and other senior posts across ten faculties/schools.

packages.title: Agenda Committee - November 04, 2025
packages.summary: The Agenda Committee of the Academic Board met on November 4, 2025, to review eight external academic program reviews commissioned by Deans across Arts & Science, UTM, and UTSC. The Semi-Annual Report on the Reviews of Academic Units and Programs, presented by Vice-Provost Nick Rule, covered reviews of the Digital Humanities Minor (Woodsworth College), Centre for Industrial Relations and Human Resources, International Relations program, Mark S. Bonham Centre for Sexual Diversity Studies, Department of Statistical Sciences, UTM Department of Management, UTM Department of Mathematical and Computational Sciences, and UTSC Department of Philosophy. All reviews highlighted strong program quality, innovative curricula, and student engagement, while noting themes around teaching position sustainability, enrolment pressures, experiential learning supports, and diversity/accessibility. The Digital Humanities review specifically found the program has reached enrolment capacity limits due to teaching positions secured only through 2026 and recommended permanent faculty appointments and dedicated administrative and physical space.
packages.gradRelevance: Graduate programs reviewed include the Industrial Relations and Human Resources MIRHR and PhD, and the Statistical Sciences MSc and PhD. The Digital Humanities review notes concerns about TA quality and availability (students praised 'the excellence of contributing faculty and TAs'). More broadly, reviewers across all eight units emphasized strengthening mentorship and support for both students and faculty, addressing enrolment pressures, and enhancing career supports—issues directly affecting graduate student experience. The Digital Humanities review's finding that teaching positions are 'secured only through 2026' signals potential staffing instability affecting both undergraduate and graduate instruction. No specific graduate stipend, fee, or unionized labour matters are addressed in this meeting.
keyItems: Semi-Annual Report on Reviews of Academic Units and Programs submitted for information covering eight external reviews: Digital Humanities Minor (Woodsworth), Centre for Industrial Relations and Human Resources (with MIRHR and PhD programs), International Relations, Mark S. Bonham Centre for Sexual Diversity Studies, Department of Statistical Sciences (with MSc and PhD), UTM Department of Management, UTM Department of Mathematical and Computational Sciences, and UTSC Department of Philosophy

Digital Humanities Minor review (October 25, 2024 visit, report received March 26, 2025) found program has 'reached the limits of its enrolment capacity' based on available teaching and administrative staffing; teaching positions 'secured only through 2026'; reviewers strongly recommend conversion to permanent teaching appointments and dedicated space allocation

Digital Humanities review commended program's positive learning environment for women in STEM, project-based learning, integration of creative work with rigorous research, and faculty/TA excellence; students praised effective instruction and cross-disciplinary collaboration

Common themes across all eight reviews: strong program quality and reputation, high-calibre students, innovative and relevant curricula with interdisciplinary breadth, commitment to equity/diversity/inclusion, but also need to strengthen coordination across cognate programs, ensure teaching sustainability, enhance experiential and career supports, address enrolment pressures, and embed diversity/accessibility in curricula and hiring

Digital Humanities review identified concerns: reliance on word-of-mouth recruitment limiting visibility, Woodsworth College staff capacity constraints while supporting multiple programs, lack of dedicated physical space for program community, and urgent need for stable staffing resources to maintain current success and enable growth

Digital Humanities Dean's response (September 12, 2025) endorsed reviewers' assessment of program as exemplary in North American digital humanities pedagogy, acknowledged strong faculty and student engagement, and implicitly committed to addressing staffing and resource recommendations

documents.title: Semi-Annual Report on the Reviews of Academic Units and Programs
documents.summary: Report submitted by Vice-Provost Nick Rule covering eight external reviews of academic programs and units across Arts & Science, UTM, and UTSC. Contains complete compendium of review summaries, external reviewers' findings, and Dean administrative responses. Reviews assessed Digital Humanities Minor, Centre for Industrial Relations and Human Resources (MIRHR and PhD), International Relations, Sexual Diversity Studies, Statistical Sciences (MSc and PhD), UTM Management, UTM Mathematical and Computational Sciences, and UTSC Philosophy. Key findings: all programs demonstrate strong quality and innovation; common concerns include teaching position sustainability, enrolment pressures, need for enhanced student supports and career services, and integration of diversity/accessibility goals.
documents.gradRelevance: Report includes reviews of programs with graduate components (Industrial Relations MIRHR and PhD, Statistical Sciences MSc and PhD) and emphasizes need to strengthen mentorship and support for students and faculty across all units. Digital Humanities review's concern that teaching positions are secured only through 2026 and urgent need for permanent staffing has implications for graduate instruction quality and TA availability.

packages.title: Agenda Committee - November 04, 2025
packages.summary: The Agenda Committee met on November 4, 2025 to review eight external UTQAP (University of Toronto Quality Assurance Process) academic program reviews covering units across Faculty of Arts & Science, UTM, and UTSC, including the Centre for Industrial Relations and Human Resources, Department of Statistical Sciences, Digital Humanities, International Relations, Mark S. Bonham Centre for Sexual Diversity Studies, UTM's Department of Management and Department of Mathematical and Computational Science, and UTSC's Department of Philosophy. Vice-Provost Nicholas Rule reported that external reviewers commended programs for academic excellence, quality students, and strong EDI commitments, while recommending improvements in program coordination, mentorship structures, enrolment management, experiential learning, and embedding diversity in curriculum and hiring. The Committee approved the November 13, 2025 Academic Board agenda and several academic administrative appointments effective January–September 2026, including Mark Duggan as Director of the Munk School (September 2026–June 2031), Janice Stein as Interim Director (January–August 2026), and appointments in Medicine, Architecture, and Education.

The review of the Department of Pharmacology and Toxicology is directly relevant as Leonardo Salmena was appointed Acting Graduate Chair (January–June 2026), giving him authority over graduate admissions, funding, and program oversight in that department. The review recommendations regarding accessibility and diversity in curriculum and hiring practices will shape graduate recruitment and student experience.
keyItems: Eight UTQAP external reviews completed and approved by Committee on Academic Policy and Programs on October 21, 2025, covering: Centre for Industrial Relations and Human Resources, Department of Statistical Sciences, Digital Humanities program, International Relations program, and Mark S. Bonham Centre for Sexual Diversity Studies (all Faculty of Arts & Science); Department of Management and Department of Mathematical and Computational Science (UTM); and Department of Philosophy (UTSC)
External reviewers identified academic excellence and strong EDI commitments as program strengths; commended integration of theoretical and practical learning and vibrant intellectual communities

Key improvement recommendations from reviewers: better coordination among related programs, enhanced mentorship and support structures, addressing enrolment challenges, expanding experiential and career development opportunities, embedding diversity and accessibility in curriculum design and hiring practices

Committee discussion focused on composition of external review teams (including international representation) and ensuring robust consultation with cognate units during site visits; request made for detailed summary of consultation process in draft FARIPs to be provided to Academic Board
Vice-Provost Rule clarified that divisional Deans determine external review team composition and develop site visit schedules, considering multiple factors in these decisions

documents.title: Terms of Reference
documents.summary: This document establishes the Agenda Committee's composition, quorum, functions, and procedures. Membership includes the Chair and Vice-Chair of the Academic Board (ex officio), chairs and senior assessors of the Committee on Academic Policy and Programs and Planning and Budget Committee (ex officio), the Vice-President and Provost (ex officio), 2 teaching staff members of the Academic Board, and 1 student member of the Academic Board, plus ex officio voting members from the Governing Council including the Chair, Vice-Chair, President, and Chancellor. The committee is chaired by the Chair of the Academic Board. Quorum is one-third of voting members (normally 5). The committee directs the flow of Academic Board business, determines readiness of agenda items, assigns unassigned issues to standing committees, establishes Special Committees and the Striking Committee, establishes the Nominating Committee for the University Tribunal and Academic Appeals Committee, and approves academic administrative appointments pursuant to the Policy on Appointment of Academic Administrators, with confirmation by the Chair of Governing Council, President, and Chair of Academic Board.
documents.gradRelevance: The committee's student member (1 of the Academic Board-appointed seats) provides graduate/student representation in directing academic business and setting Academic Board agenda; however, this terms of reference document does not specify whether the student member is graduate or undergraduate.
