packages.title: Audit Committee - April 15, 2026
packages.summary: The Audit Committee met on April 15, 2026, to review financial accounting changes, internal audit priorities, and institutional risks. Key decisions included accepting the Draft Notes to the Audited Financial Statements for April 30, 2026, which reflect the first year of Oak House Student Residence operations under proportionate consolidation accounting and adoption of AcG-20 for cloud computing arrangements. The Committee approved the Internal Audit Plan 2026-2027, focused on IT, process effectiveness, attitudes/behaviour/culture, and construction. Risk assessments were updated: Decline in Research Funding and Threats to Safety On Campus & Online downgraded from High to Major; Threats to Institutional Autonomy upgraded to Major. Deferred maintenance risk across all three campuses totals $1.5 billion ($1.3 billion at St. George), with Project RISE providing a $300 million investment approved by Governing Council.
packages.gradRelevance: Oak House Student Residence's integration into University financial statements directly affects graduate student housing availability and costs; the Committee discussed accounting treatment but no immediate fee or occupancy changes were decided. The Internal Audit Plan 2026-2027 includes process effectiveness audits and offboarding consistency reviews that could affect graduate student employment, TA/RA contracts, and access to systems—though specific audit timelines and recommendations were not detailed in this meeting. Deferred maintenance of $1.5 billion across campuses may influence future capital priorities and building conditions affecting graduate student facilities and research spaces. No specific stipend, tuition, or union matters were addressed in this meeting.
keyItems: Accounting treatment of Oak House Student Residence: University chose proportionate consolidation (rather than equity method) to account for its investment in the Spadina Sussex University Residence Co-tenancy joint venture, reflecting the degree of economic interest and operational integration with core University activities

Adoption of Accounting Guideline AcG-20 for cloud computing: University will capitalize directly attributable implementation expenditures for software services and expense them using straight-line method over expected period of access; applied retrospectively with no material expected impact, but will affect future student information system accounting

Internal Audit Plan 2026-2027 approved with four areas of focus: IT security and infrastructure, process effectiveness (including offboarding procedures for systems with financial or sensitive data access), attitudes/behaviour/culture patterns, and construction projects

Risk Dashboard updates: Decline in Research Funding Risk downgraded High→Major; Threats to Safety On Campus & Online downgraded High→Major; Threats to Institutional Autonomy upgraded Moderate→Major; Costas Catsaros (UPDC) and Nathan Corwin added to Risk Assessment Working Group

Deferred maintenance risk presentation: Tri-campus backlog totals $1.5 billion ($1.3 billion at St. George Campus); Project RISE provides $300 million investment approved by Governing Council; risk-based prioritization model used since 2019 to allocate spending; climate change and post-war/early-2000s construction booms create future cost peaks

Cybersecurity Annual Report highlights: AI rapidly increasing scale and sophistication of cyber attacks; Privacy Quest tool introduced; cybersecurity training rates reviewed; Committee expressed strong interest in receiving further updates from Chief Information Security & Digital Trust Officer on current vs. desired state of maturity and acceptable residual risk levels

documents.title: Draft Notes to the Audited Financial Statements – April 30, 2026
documents.summary: Presentation by Sanish Samuel (Controller and Director of Financial Services) on two major accounting changes: (1) First year of operations of Spadina Sussex University Residence Co-tenancy (Oak House Student Residence), which the University accounts for using proportionate consolidation based on economic interest and operational integration, rather than the equity method; (2) Adoption of Accounting Guideline AcG-20 for cloud computing arrangements, under which the University capitalizes directly attributable implementation costs for software services and expenses them on a straight-line basis over the expected service access period. AcG-20 applied retrospectively with no material impact expected, though future student information system investments may be significantly affected.
documents.gradRelevance: Oak House housing operations now consolidated in University financials, affecting graduate student housing resource allocation and potential cost impacts; future IT investments for student information systems will be capitalized differently, potentially affecting system modernization timelines and operational budgets.

documents.title: Internal Audit Plan 2026-2027
documents.summary: Presented by Alex Matos (Executive Director, Internal Audit), the Plan is strongly aligned to top risks identified by the University's Risk Assessment Working Group and higher education sector trends. Four areas of focus: IT (security, infrastructure); process effectiveness (including offboarding procedures for systems with financial impact or sensitive data access); attitudes/behaviour/culture patterns (reviewing data on outcome drivers rather than auditing culture per se); and construction. Plan includes time allocation for follow-up on previously issued action plans and mechanisms for ad-hoc triage and re-prioritization based on broader criteria including tri-campus impact.
documents.gradRelevance: Offboarding process audits directly affect graduate student TA/RA contract termination, system access removal, and financial reconciliation; IT audits may impact graduate research data security and access; construction audits affect graduate student facilities and research spaces.

documents.title: Annual Report: Information Security and Protection of Digital Assets
documents.summary: Presented by Donna Kidwell (Chief Information Officer) and Nathan Corwin (Chief Information Security & Digital Trust Officer), this annual report highlights that AI is rapidly increasing the scale and sophistication of cyber attacks against the University. Report covers sources of vulnerability, current state of cybersecurity assessments, cybersecurity training rates, introduction of the Privacy Quest tool, and cybersecurity implications of the University's interest in grants in high-security research areas (defense, nuclear). Committee discussed current vs. desired state of maturity, acceptable levels of residual risk, controls, compliance, and establishment of new metrics and baselines.
documents.gradRelevance: Cybersecurity training rates and Privacy Quest tool directly affect graduate student and researcher access to sensitive data and high-security research areas; cyber attacks threaten graduate research integrity and confidentiality; defense and nuclear research security requirements may affect graduate student participation in funded projects.

documents.title: Risk Dashboards / Risk Assessment Working Group Report
documents.summary: Reported by Kristin Taylor (University Counsel and Chief Legal Officer) on the Risk Assessment Working Group's updated risk assessments as of April 15, 2026. Three changes since February 25, 2026: Decline in Research Funding Risk downgraded from High to Major; Threats to Safety On Campus & Online downgraded from High to Major; Threats to Institutional Autonomy increased from Moderate to Major. New members added: Costas Catsaros (Executive Director, Project Development & Controls, UPDC) bringing construction and real estate expertise, and Nathan Corwin (Chief Information Security & Digital Trust Officer). Risk reporting will evolve as part of the ERM Program's evolution.
documents.gradRelevance: Research funding risk downgrade may affect graduate student funding availability and fellowship amounts; safety threats directly impact graduate student wellbeing on campus and online; institutional autonomy threats could affect graduate program autonomy and academic freedom.

documents.title: Deferred Maintenance Risk Presentation
documents.summary: Presented by Ron Saporta (Chief Operating Security Officer), the presentation details tri-campus deferred maintenance totaling $1.5 billion, with $1.3 billion attributable to St. George Campus. The delta between University investment and provincial average contributions led to Project RISE, a $300 million investment approved by the Governing Council. A risk-based prioritization model developed in 2019 triages and assesses how deferred maintenance funds are spent most impactfully to mitigate risk. Presentation discusses the risk horizon, challenges and cost impacts from climate change, and peaks in upcoming deferred maintenance costs from post-war and early-2000s construction booms. Committee discussed the assessed risk level and adequacy of funding over the short- and medium-term.
documents.gradRelevance: Deferred maintenance backlog of $1.5 billion affects graduate student facilities, research building conditions, lab infrastructure, and housing quality; Project RISE $300 million investment will influence which graduate research and residential facilities receive upgrades; climate change risk may affect laboratory and housing reliability.

packages.title: Audit Committee - February 25, 2026
packages.summary: The Audit Committee met on February 25, 2026, to review risk management and audit matters. The Committee learned that the University faces a new mandatory U.S. Single Audit requirement for health-related grants from American funding agencies, newly formalized in 2025, with completion expected in March 2026 (originally due end-January). Vice-President Leah Cowen presented on research funding risks, noting that while overall research funding increased in 2025, the University faces declining Tri-Agency market share and comparative disadvantage versus less research-intensive institutions. The Committee updated its risk inventory, reclassifying Decline in Domestic Enrolment and Talent Shortage to low severity, adding Workforce Disruption as a Major risk and Threats to Institutional Autonomy as Moderate. Professor Cowen's mitigation efforts (divisional strategic research plans, grant-writing support, Horizon Europe participation, Canada Impact+ Research Talent Initiative) successfully lowered research funding risk from High rating.
packages.gradRelevance: The research funding presentation directly affects graduate students: declining Tri-Agency funding threatens support for trainees, personnel, and equipment that core graduate research depends on. Cowen identified that reduced Tri-Agency funding negatively affects 'trainees' (including graduate students receiving research funding) and facility maintenance. The Committee noted that mitigation initiatives including the Canada Impact+ Research Talent Initiative and diversified funding sources (Horizon Europe, defence-related research) will shape graduate research funding availability. No specific stipend, TA/RA compensation, or fee changes were discussed in this meeting.
keyItems: U.S. Single Audit for health-related grants now mandatory annually (formerly sporadic spot audits); newly formalized regulatory requirement took effect 2025; audit completion delayed from January to expected March 2026 due to complexity.
Overall research funding increased in 2025, but University faces declining Tri-Agency market share and comparative disadvantage relative to less research-intensive Canadian institutions.

Key research funding risks identified: complex research security compliance, international partnership requirements, uncertain U.S. research funding landscape, variable faculty participation, and growing indirect costs (labour, inflation, tariffs).

Research funding supports trainees, personnel, equipment, and facility maintenance; declining Tri-Agency funding threatens these core institutional activities and commercialization/knowledge translation.

Mitigation strategies: divisional quantitative strategic research plans, targeted supports, strategic grant-writing team providing application services, Horizon Europe participation, Canada Impact+ Research Talent Initiative, increased defence-related research engagement.
Risk Assessment Working Group introduced low severity risk category and reclassified Decline in Domestic Enrolment and Talent Shortage to low severity.

packages.title: Audit Committee - September 17, 2025
packages.summary: The Audit Committee met on September 17, 2025, to review risk management, insurance coverage, and international enrolment trends. The University received $67,138 from CURIE's February 2025 surplus distribution and maintained a five-year average loss ratio of 29%, well below the 40% threshold. Coverage improvements for 2025 included new equipment breakdown insurance, removal of COVID-related exclusions, and an increase in the abuse sublimit from $15 million to $20 million. The Committee declined additional $10 million cyber insurance excess coverage, determining the current $2 million limit satisfies federal funding obligations. International enrolment presentations indicated modest declines in undergraduate international intake with mixed graduate program results, but 2025-26 financial impact remains manageable within the overall budget.
packages.gradRelevance: Graduate enrolment trends were explicitly discussed: forecasts indicate mixed results across graduate programs with international intake declines anticipated. The abuse sublimit increase from $15 million to $20 million is relevant to campus safety and student welfare. The Committee's risk management approach and cyber insurance adequacy affect institutional resilience that protects graduate student data and operations. No specific changes to graduate stipends, fees, TA/RA compensation, or housing were mentioned in this meeting.

The Board will also approve the University's Fighting Against Forced Labour and Child Labour in Canadian Supply Chains Annual Report for the 2024-25 fiscal year, which documents that U of T imported $29.4 million in goods (4.4% of total procurement spend of $665.5 million) and is not aware of any forced labour or child labour requiring remediation. Additionally, the Board will receive for information the Vice-President People, Strategy, Equity and Culture's 2025 annual report covering HR initiatives, equity programs, and labour relations; UTAM's semi-annual investment performance report showing LTCAP returned 10.8% in 2025 and 8.4% over ten years, and EFIP returned 4.6% in 2025; and quarterly compliance reports on health and safety, donations over $250,000, and debt status.
packages.gradRelevance: Graduate students are directly affected by Spaces & Experiences decisions: Oak House residence (opened September 2025 after delays) now houses students; Food Services reported first positive net income in six years, impacting meal plan pricing and availability; the Build More Housing Initiative targets 1,200 new beds on two on-campus sites to address housing demand. The Fighting Against Forced Labour report is relevant as it covers supply chain practices for goods U of T procures and imports. The PSEC report notes ten collective agreements ratified including potential impacts on unionized graduate student positions (CUPE 3902), and revisions to the Child Care Benefit for CUPE 3902 Unit 1 and UTFA members. Investment performance affects endowment-funded scholarships and awards for graduate students.
keyItems: Spaces & Experiences 2026-27 budget approval: Net income forecast at $4.6 million after debt service, depreciation and capital expenditures; all profit centres except Oak House budgeting positive net income; Oak House occupancy currently at 87% following September 2025 delayed opening due to construction delays.

Oak House residence: First new St. George campus residence in over 20 years; opened September 2025 after two-week delay; capital cost overruns and operating costs from delayed occupancy created major negative variance in S&E budget; has become major asset for student experience.

Build More Housing Initiative: Two RFPs completed in 2025 resulting in partnership currently in final negotiation stage for two on-campus sites delivering approximately 1,200 new beds; intended to address university's significant housing demand through cost-effective pipeline approach.

Food Services: Achieved first positive net income in more than six years in fiscal 2026; redesigned residential meal plan offering better value to students; reopening of Conference Centre contributed to strong performance; continues to adjust to labour market pressures and evolving consumer expectations.

Schwartz Reisman Innovation Campus West (SR West): Exceeded expectations with $1.5 million positive variance to budget; achieved virtually break-even status one year ahead of schedule; reached full activation of all event spaces.
Transportation Services: On track to retire final outstanding mortgage and accumulated deficit within 18 months, supporting return to strong annual cash flows.

documents.title: Spaces & Experiences: Annual Report 2025-2026 and Budget 2026-2027
documents.summary: Comprehensive annual report covering S&E's five business units (Real Estate Partnerships, University Family Housing, Student Residence Communities, Food/Beverage/Campus Events, Transportation) for fiscal 2026 with budget projections to 2031. Food Services achieved break-even for first time in six years; Schwartz Reisman Innovation Campus West exceeded budget expectations by $1.5 million. Long-range financial outlook projects net income of $44.4 million by F2031 against gross revenues of $164.2 million.

Transportation Services recovery affects campus mobility for graduate students.

documents.title: Annual Report: Fighting Against Forced Labour and Child Labour in Canadian Supply Chains
documents.summary: Mandated report under Federal Bill S-211 (effective January 1, 2024) documenting U of T's efforts to prevent forced labour and child labour in supply chains for fiscal year ended April 30, 2025. U of T imported $29.4 million in goods (4.4% of $665.5 million total procurement spend) from 9,700 active suppliers, primarily from Canada ($631.1M) with remaining from US, UK, Germany, Italy. Direct imports concentrated in scientific equipment, IT/audiovisual equipment, books, and apparel/textiles. Report documents Social Procurement Program connecting to 4,000 Canadian suppliers; Trademark Licensing Policy (in place since 2000) requiring humane production conditions and banning child labour under 15; developing Supplier Code of Conduct over 24-36 months; university not aware of any forced labour or child labour instances requiring remediation.
documents.gradRelevance: Ensures ethical sourcing practices in university procurement; relevant to graduate students through research equipment and materials sourcing; CUPE 3902 members may benefit from strengthened labour standards in supply chains; demonstrates institutional commitment to human rights in operations.

documents.title: Annual Report: Vice-President, People, Strategy, Equity and Culture
documents.summary: 2025 annual report presented in digital form covering five portfolios: University HR, Equity/Safety/High Risk, Labour and UTFA Relations, and Indigenous Initiatives. Highlights include: ten collective agreements successfully ratified; revisions to Child Care Benefit for CUPE 3902 Unit 1 and UTFA members; finalized revisions to UTFA Memorandum of Agreement Articles 7 & 13; created AI guidance suite for staff; Reference Group on Multi-Faith Spaces established; Indigenous Substantiation Recommendations Report finalized; National Indigenous Peoples Day allocated as Presidential Day; collaboration with VP and Provost on discrimination and harassment policy review resulting in 13 recommendations.
documents.gradRelevance: Significant for unionized graduate students (CUPE 3902 Unit 1): revised Child Care Benefit improves support for graduate students with dependents; collective agreement ratifications affect TA/RA terms and conditions; UTFA agreement revisions may affect graduate student program policies; AI guidelines relevant to graduate student research practices.

documents.title: Semi-Annual Update on Investment Performance and Responsible Investing Activities to December 31, 2025
documents.summary: UTAM semi-annual investment report to Business Board on portfolio performance and responsible investing activities. Long-Term Capital Appreciation Pool (LTCAP) returned 10.8% in 2025 (net of fees) and 8.4% over ten years, exceeding 10-year target of CPI+4% by 1.7 percentage points; outperformed Reference Portfolio over 5 and 10-year horizons (0.9 ppts and 0.3 ppts respectively) though underperformed by 1.0 ppt in 2025. Expendable Funds Investment Pool (EFIP) returned 4.6% in 2025, exceeding target by 0.6 percentage points. Total assets under management increased 5.2% to $9.341 billion. Carbon footprint of LTCAP Sub-Portfolio fell 71.9% from 2019 baseline (exceeding 50% by 2030 target); fossil fuel exposure decreased to 0.5% of LTCAP from 2.0% in 2021; sustainable and low carbon investments comprise 17.3% of LTCAP (exceeding 10% by 2025 target).
documents.gradRelevance: Investment performance directly affects endowment funding available for graduate student scholarships, awards, and fellowships; strong 10-year returns support long-term financial sustainability of graduate student funding; responsible investing practices align with values relevant to graduate student community.

documents.title: Status Report on Debt to March 31, 2026
documents.summary: Quarterly debt status report documenting University's borrowing position against approved Debt Strategy. Total debt policy limit for 2025-26 is $3.1 billion (based on 6% debt burden ratio); actual debt outstanding at March 31, 2026 is $1.2 billion comprising $774.8 million external direct debt (debentures due 2031-2051, LEAP loans) and $435.0 million internal loans. Debt burden ratio 2.3% (0.9% internal, 1.4% external), well below 6% policy maximum. Viability ratio 5.5, above 0.8 minimum threshold. Total allocations of $2.0 billion approved for capital projects with $1.1 billion remaining available under policy limit. All approved debt including projects in progress would yield debt burden ratio of 3.6% and viability ratio of 4.9.
documents.gradRelevance: Borrowing supports capital projects including housing (Oak House, Build More Housing Initiative), academic facilities, and infrastructure that directly benefit graduate student experience and research environments; strong financial position supports continued investment in graduate student spaces and services.

packages.title: Business Board - March 04, 2026
packages.summary: On March 4, 2026, the Business Board approved the 2026-27 operating budget of $3.66B (1.1% increase) and tuition fee schedules for both publicly-funded and self-funded programs. Ontario domestic tuition remained frozen for 2026-27 with a 2% increase taking effect Fall 2026; out-of-province domestic tuition increased 5%; international undergraduate tuition increased 4.1% and graduate tuition 3.6% (excluding PhD students at domestic rates). The Board approved 21 new ancillary fees (Category 6) including placement fees for UTM and Dalla Lana co-op/internship programs, and removed 8 existing fees. Student financial support totaled $408M for 2026-27 with the University spending 73% more per student on aid than the Ontario average. In response to February 2026 provincial OSAP changes capping non-repayable grants at 25% (down from 85%), the University committed to reinvesting unplanned operating revenues into its own aid programs to offset student impact. The Board also approved Project Catalyst, a cross-institutional administrative efficiency initiative, and in camera approved capital projects including the Temerty Building and Climate Positive Campus Project LEAP 2.0.
packages.gradRelevance: Graduate students face a 3.6% international tuition increase for 2026-27 (domestic PhD students pay domestic rates and are unaffected by the increase). The provincial OSAP changes will materially reduce non-repayable grant funding available to graduate students, though the University pledged to reinvest unplanned revenues into financial aid programs to partially offset this loss. Total student financial support of $408M covers both undergraduate and graduate students. No specific graduate student funding, stipend, TA/RA rates, or unionized labour matters were discussed at this meeting.

Compensation of $2.43B represents two-thirds of expenditures with 2% net growth and nine net new faculty positions planned. Shared services absorbed $23.5M cuts (5% reduction). Student financial support budgeted at $408M. Revenues fell approximately $100M below prior long-range projection due to international student intake recalibration. Tuition revenues reflect 2% Ontario domestic increase (Fall 2026), 5% out-of-province increase, 4.1% international undergraduate increase, and 3.6% international graduate increase. Pension risk contingency set at $50M annually. Project Catalyst administrative efficiency initiative approved. The budget remained balanced with strong credit rating.
documents.gradRelevance: Directly affects graduate students through compensation decisions (2% net growth partially offset by complement reductions), financial aid allocation ($408M total), and tuition increases for international and out-of-province graduate students (3.6% and 5% respectively). International student enrolment recalibration resulting in $100M revenue shortfall will impact graduate program availability and funding.

documents.title: Schedule of Ancillary Fees (Categories 5 and 6) 2026-27
documents.summary: Details all Category 5 (Cost-Recovery Ancillary Fees) and Category 6 (Administrative User Fees and Fines) for 2026-27. Approximately 1,500 total fees across the university. 13% of fees remain unchanged; two-thirds increase by less than 5%; 11% increase by more than 5% reflecting deferred adjustments. 21 new Category 6 fees approved including placement coordination fees for UTM co-op/internship programs and Dalla Lana School of Public Health. 8 existing fees removed. Going forward, a CPI-level increase will apply automatically annually unless divisions opt out in advance. Category 5 fees recover direct costs of goods and services (lab materials, case studies, field trips); Category 6 covers administrative and user services (placement coordination, external examination fees).
documents.gradRelevance: Graduate students will be subject to new placement coordination fees for co-op and internship programs at UTM and Dalla Lana School of Public Health, as well as increases to other ancillary fees (external examination fees, lab materials, field trips) averaging less than 5% for two-thirds of fees, with some fees increasing more than 5% to recover deferred costs.

packages.title: Business Board - January 28, 2026
packages.summary: On January 28, 2026, the Business Board received the Annual Report on Deferred Maintenance showing $1.5 billion in total liability across the University's building portfolio (replacement value $7.4 billion), with the St. George campus carrying $1.3 billion. The Board approved Project RISE, a $300 million investment across 68 buildings targeting critical systems like elevators and electrical infrastructure. The Debt Strategy Annual Review confirmed total debt of approximately $2 billion with a debt burden ratio of 2.4 percent (well below the 6 percent policy ceiling) and a viability ratio of 5.6 times, projecting sufficient capacity for the $4.4 billion five-year capital plan. The Financial Forecast for April 30, 2026 projected net income of $170 million despite a $70 million shortfall in international enrolment revenue, offset by stronger investment income. The Board approved amendments to three workplace policies (Health and Safety, Workplace Violence, Workplace Harassment) effective immediately, revised the Procurement Policy, and approved recommendations on a Recapitalization Strategy and Real Estate Partnership in camera.
packages.gradRelevance: The deferred maintenance report directly impacts graduate student facilities, as the $1.5 billion liability includes academic buildings where grad students study, conduct research, and attend classes; the $300 million Project RISE investment targets critical systems that affect building usability. The Build More Housing Initiative was presented in camera and warrants graduate student attention as student housing capacity and affordability directly affect graduate enrolment and living conditions. The workplace policies amendments (Health and Safety, Workplace Violence, Workplace Harassment effective January 28, 2026) apply to graduate student employees in TA and RA positions. However, the report contains no specific information on TA/RA stipends, grad fee changes, or unionized labour matters.
keyItems: Annual Report on Deferred Maintenance: $1.5 billion total liability across academic and administrative buildings; replacement value of $7.4 billion; St. George campus holds $1.3 billion of liability; Facility Condition Index stable at 20.2 percent

Project RISE initiative approved: $300 million investment across 68 buildings over three years targeting critical systems (elevators, electrical infrastructure) using multi-factor risk model for prioritization

Deferred maintenance provincial funding gap: McGill University receives approximately $150 million annually for a smaller campus compared to University of Toronto's significantly lower allocation; new partnership strategy proposed with government for joint real estate solutions related to Legislative Assembly renovation swing space

Debt Strategy Annual Review 2025: total debt $2 billion ($1.2 billion for completed projects, remainder for active developments); debt burden ratio 2.4 percent (below 6 percent policy ceiling); viability ratio 5.6 times (exceeds 0.8 minimum); sufficient capacity for $4.4 billion five-year capital plan

Debt management approach: internal borrowing currently offers lower cost of capital than external debentures; financial model uses conservative 5.5 percent borrowing cost assumption; stress tests confirm compliance with policy limits even if rates increase; credit rating agencies reaffirmed strong long-term credit standing

Forecast of University Financial Results (April 30, 2026): projected net income $170 million; international enrolment revenue down $70 million; offset by stronger investment income; Long-Term Capital Appreciation Pool conservatively assumes 7 percent return; budget environment tightening with expenses growing faster than revenues

packages.title: Business Board - November 26, 2025
packages.summary: At its November 26, 2025 meeting, the Business Board approved revised Procurement Policy effective November 27, 2025, raising the open competitive procurement threshold from $100,000 to $121,200 and delegating authority to the CFO to amend approval schedules for regulatory compliance. The Board received the Review of the Statement on Prohibited Discrimination and Discriminatory Harassment, which recommends transitioning to a formal policy, establishing a Human Rights Office as a centralized intake point for student complaints, and implementing an institutional tracking system to comply with Bill 166 (Strengthening Accountability and Student Supports Act, 2024). Fall enrolment showed 156 more domestic students but 240 fewer international students than prior year, pressuring finances as provincial funding does not cover full domestic education costs. Investment performance was strong: LTCAP returned 10.3% (one-year) and 7.8% (ten-year annualized), exceeding its 6.6% target, with total assets under management at $8.5 billion. In camera, the Board approved use of Faculty of Arts & Science operating reserves to establish a Third-Century Chair in Artificial Intelligence and received updates on capital projects including the Science and Applied Medicine Innovation Hub and Academic Wood Tower.
packages.gradRelevance: The Human Rights Office recommendation directly affects graduate students by establishing a new centralized intake point for harassment and discrimination complaints with enhanced enforcement authority and institutional tracking. The fall enrolment pressure on domestic student funding (which does not cover full education costs) may constrain graduate funding allocations and program resources. In camera, the Board received a collective bargaining update from the Vice-President, People Strategy, Equity & Culture—significant for graduate student unionized labour issues—though details remain confidential. The new AI Chair in Arts & Science may create research opportunities but the report does not specify graduate student funding or TA/RA implications.
keyItems: Procurement Policy revised and approved November 26, 2025, effective November 27, 2025: open competitive procurement threshold increased from $100,000 to $121,200 to align with Ontario Broader Public Sector Procurement Directive; Approval Authority Schedule removed from policy appendix and delegated to CFO for administrative management with commitment to report back to Board on any changes.

Review of Statement on Prohibited Discrimination and Discriminatory Harassment recommended: transition from 1994 Statement to formal policy with enhanced enforcement authority; establish Human Rights Office as centralized intake point for student harassment and discrimination complaints; implement institutional tracking system to fulfill Bill 166 reporting requirements on annual harassment and discrimination complaint categories.

Fall 2025 enrolment: 156 more domestic students (exceeding plan) but 240 fewer international students than prior year; domestic increase creates financial pressure as provincial funding does not cover full cost of educating domestic students; international enrolment returned to pre-pandemic levels despite University's superior performance versus other Ontario and Canadian institutions.

Long-Term Capital Appreciation Pool (LTCAP) returned 10.3% one-year and 7.8% ten-year annualized return, outperforming 6.6% target; Expendable Funds Investment Pool Medium-Term pool returned 8.7%, Short-Term pool returned 5.8%, both exceeding targets; total assets under management increased 6.5% to $8.5 billion as of June 30, 2025.

University of Toronto Communications generated over 39 million targeted impressions for integrated marketing campaigns and 108,000 global news media mentions in 2024-2025, with 78% favorable coverage; University ranked 1st in Canada and 4th globally among 1,000 institutions in visibility; secured coverage in The Guardian, New York Times, and The Economist.

Human Rights Office establishment recommended to consolidate harassment and discrimination complaint pathways, provide centralized support for complainants and respondents, and measure effectiveness through clarity of pathways, timelines, and periodic evaluation; office will address human rights violations, workplace investigations, and microaggressions.

documents.title: Review of the Statement on Prohibited Discrimination and Discriminatory Harassment
documents.summary: Review commissioned to update 1994 Statement and comply with Bill 166 (Strengthening Accountability and Student Supports Act, 2024), conducted through 45 closed consultation sessions, eight open sessions, online surveys, and email submissions with students, faculty, librarians, and staff across three campuses. Key recommendations: transition from Statement to formal policy with enhanced enforcement authority; establish Human Rights Office as centralized intake point for harassment and discrimination complaints with support for both complainants and respondents; implement institutional tracking system for annual reporting on harassment and discrimination complaints by category.
documents.gradRelevance: Directly affects graduate students by creating new Human Rights Office as centralized complaint intake point with clearer pathways and timelines for harassment and discrimination complaints; office will address human rights violations, workplace investigations, and microaggressions; institutional tracking system will enable better documentation and reporting of grad student complaints.

packages.title: Business Board - September 25, 2025
packages.summary: The Business Board meeting on September 25, 2025 reviewed the University of Toronto's advancement progress, financial health, and equity initiatives. Vice-President Advancement David Palmer reported that the Defy Gravity campaign reached $2.266 billion (56% of the $4 billion goal) with $361.49 million raised in FY 2024–2025, while new Provostial Guidelines on Gift Acceptance and Gift Administration were presented establishing frameworks for gifts of $50,000 and above. The 2024 Equity, Diversity & Inclusion Report showed 73.9% response rate (10,110 employees) on the Employment Equity Survey, with notable increases in faculty representation among racialized persons and modest staff representation changes. Debt status to August 31, 2025 stood at $1.196 billion in actual outstanding debt against a policy limit of $3.1 billion, with a debt burden ratio of 2.3%. Moody's maintained the university's Aa1 stable credit rating with CAD8.9 billion in cash and investments providing exceptional liquidity.
packages.gradRelevance: Graduate student funding and employment outcomes appear indirectly affected by several items. The Defy Gravity campaign shows $3.1 million raised on Giving Day (March 25, 2025) for 104 featured funds benefiting 'students and researchers,' though specific graduate support amounts are not itemized. The EDI Report indicates faculty hiring increases among racialized persons (+9.8% new hires) and women (+8.5%), which affects graduate supervision and research opportunities. The strong financial position (CAD8.9 billion in cash/investments, Aa1 credit rating, 2.3% debt burden ratio) provides stability for institutional commitments to student support and institutional aid, though no specific graduate stipend or tuition adjustments are announced at this meeting. No TA/RA wage changes or unionized labour negotiations are detailed.
keyItems: Defy Gravity campaign reached $2.266 billion (56% toward $4 billion goal) from January 1, 2019 to April 30, 2025, with FY 2024–2025 raising $361.49 million from 142,981 engagement instances.
Alumni Reunion 2025 achieved record 16,263 registrations (11% increase from prior year), with 53,198 graduates engaged in alumni events and 3,924 registered for career events.

Provostial Guidelines on Gift Acceptance and Gift Administration established formal requirements: gifts of $50,000+ require signed agreements; gifts of $250,000+ must be publicly disclosed to Business Board and Academic Board quarterly; anonymous gifts only permitted for individual/family donors (not corporate); Vice-President Advancement or ad hoc gift administrative committee reviews gifts for reputational/material risk.

2024 Employment Equity Survey achieved 73.9% response rate (10,110 of 13,675 employees); staff representation: women 63.6%, racialized persons 55.5%, persons with disabilities 19.2%, 2SLGBTQ+ 17.0%; faculty: women increased 4.6% in overall representation, racialized persons increased 3.7%; promotions of racialized staff increased 6.3%.

EDI initiatives delivered 70 total trainings/workshops in 2024: 15 on anti-racism, 33 on accessibility, 22 on sexual and gender diversity; Institutional Equity Office received 135,000+ website visits; 141 complaints resolution meetings provided.

Actual debt outstanding at August 31, 2025 totaled $1.196 billion (external $746.5 million in debentures/LEAP; internal $450.2 million), against policy limit of $3.1 billion; debt burden ratio 2.3% (well below 6% policy maximum); unallocated borrowing capacity $1.127 billion.

documents.title: Annual Report: Vice-President, Advancement
documents.summary: Covers FY 2024–2025 advancement activities. Defy Gravity campaign reached $2.266 billion (56% of $4 billion goal) with 176,855 newly engaged alumni (79% of 225,000 target) and 830,003 engagement instances (83% of 1 million target). FY 2024–2025 specifically raised $361.49 million from 142,981 engagement instances; 19,655 newly engaged alumni. Alumni Reunion 2025 achieved record 16,263 registrations (11% increase), with 134 events across three campuses; 14,510 alumni participated in 39 educational lectures; 3,924 registered for career events. Giving Day March 25, 2025 raised $3.1 million from 2,800 donors for 104 featured funds. Advancement division implemented 91% of 79 EDI Task Force recommendations as of spring 2025.
documents.gradRelevance: Reports student benefits from Defy Gravity campaign including scholarships and research support, though graduate-specific allocations not broken out. Career programming (6,719 alumni accessed career services; 3,321 mentored students) may support graduate career transitions. No specific graduate funding amounts or stipend changes disclosed.

documents.title: Provostial Guidelines on Gift Acceptance and Gift Administration
documents.summary: New guidelines establishing parameters for university gift acceptance and administration. All gifts must align with approved academic priorities and university principles (academic freedom, institutional autonomy, integrity). Gifts of $50,000+ require formal gift agreements signed by Vice-President Advancement; Office of Counsel reviews all gifts for compliance. Anonymous gifts permitted only for individuals/families (not corporations); three levels defined: anonymous gift (for single gift), anonymous donor (all giving), institutionally anonymous (rare, VP and Counsel approval). Gifts of $250,000+ require public disclosure to Business Board and Academic Board quarterly. Gift administrative committee may convene for gifts with unique circumstances, material/reputational risk; Vice-President Advancement may recommend exceptional cases to Governing Council.
documents.gradRelevance: Establishes clear governance for research gifts supporting graduate scholarships and research funding; ensures academic freedom protections for gift-funded positions; disclosure requirements provide transparency on major donor support for graduate programs.

documents.title: Annual Report: Equity, Diversity and Inclusion
documents.summary: 2024 EDI Report covering January 1–December 31, 2024 activities across tri-campus. Employment Equity Survey achieved 73.9% response rate (10,110 of 13,675 employees): women 63.6%, racialized/persons of colour 55.5%, persons with disabilities 19.2%, 2SLGBTQ+ 17.0%, other gender identity 6.4%, trans 1.5%, Indigenous 1.5%. Staff changes 2023–2024: promotions of racialized staff +6.3%, persons with disabilities exits -3.7%; faculty changes: women overall +4.6%, racialized persons +3.7%, new hires racialized +9.8% and women +8.5%. Institutional Equity Office delivered 70 EDI trainings (15 anti-racism, 33 accessibility, 22 sexual/gender diversity); 70 engagement sessions; 141 complaints resolution meetings. Initiatives include Faith and Anti-Racism Engagement Series, Queer Orientation (40 events, 32 partners), Employees with Disabilities Community Network launch.
documents.gradRelevance: Directly relevant to graduate students as faculty diversity metrics affect supervision representation; EDI trainings and community networks support graduate student belonging and accessibility; 141 complaints resolution supports available for discrimination issues; Sexual & Gender Diversity Office and Accessibility initiatives provide specific support services.

documents.title: Presentation: Equity, Diversity & Inclusion Report 2024
documents.summary: Presentation slide deck accompanying EDI Report. Highlights institutional response rate 73.9% (10,110 responses). Speaking Out! survey 2024 (November 6–24, 3,824 responses, 28.9% rate): 70.6% felt positive about belonging/acceptance, 74.3% felt motivated in work (+1% vs. 2022), 61.8% found salary competitive (+12% vs. 2022), 14.4% experienced burnout (-2%), 65.8% felt engaged/dedicated (-3%). 2025 priorities include returning to core principles (community, commitment, compassion), recognizing importance of will and resources, creating spaces for dialogue and trust.
documents.gradRelevance: Speaking Out! survey data directly measures graduate employee experience (survey captures all employees including graduate TAs); improvements in salary competitiveness perception (+12%) and motivation (+1%) indicate positive trends; burnout (-2%) and engagement trends (-3%) show areas for continued focus relevant to graduate student wellbeing.

documents.title: Status Report on Debt to August 31, 2025
documents.summary: Reports university debt position as of August 31, 2025. Total debt policy limit FY 2025–2026 is $3.1 billion (based on 6% debt burden ratio). Actual outstanding debt: $1.196 billion (external debentures/LEAP $746.5 million; internal loans $450.2 million). Debt burden ratio 2.3% (0.9% internal, 1.4% external), well below 6% policy maximum. Viability ratio 5.7 (minimum threshold 0.8). Net allocations approved by Business Board: $2 billion, leaving $1.127 billion unallocated. If all approved allocations issued, debt burden would increase to 3.7% and viability ratio to 4.9, both well within policy limits.
documents.gradRelevance: Strong debt position and substantial unallocated borrowing capacity ($1.127 billion) provide financial flexibility for capital projects and programs benefiting graduate students, though specific allocations to graduate facilities/support not detailed.

documents.title: Credit Rating Report Moody's Investors Service – Credit Opinion dated Aug 30, 2025
documents.summary: Moody's maintained Aa1 stable rating for University of Toronto. Exceptional wealth and liquidity: CAD8.9 billion in cash/investments (April 30, 2025), providing 8.2x coverage of total adjusted debt and 2.25x coverage of operating expenses. Endowment CAD3.9 billion (9% YoY growth). Annual debt service coverage 3.6x. Total FTE students 91,245 (up 10% over five years). EBIDA margin 4.7% (down from 18% in 2021–22) due to wage inflation (salaries/benefits ~60% of expenses; 3% increase 2024–25 following 13% increase 2023–24) and provincial tuition freezes. Federal cap on international undergraduates at 35% below 2023–24 levels affects revenue; 30% of enrollment is international. Fundraising exceeds projections; Defy Gravity campaign at CAD2.3+ billion. Governance strong; decentralized budgeting with centralized labour negotiation.
documents.gradRelevance: Credit rating stability (Aa1 stable) and strong financial position provide institutional stability for graduate programs and funding commitments. However, EBIDA margin decline (4.7%) due to wage inflation and tuition constraints may constrain future graduate stipend growth. International student cap (35% reduction from 2023–24) affects graduate international enrollment and associated tuition revenues; 3% wage increase noted but specific graduate TA/RA rates not disclosed.

packages.title: Business Board - September 25, 2025
packages.summary: On September 25, 2025, the Business Board approved the MacMillan Theatre Remediation project execution and received reports on a $50–60 million revenue shortfall (1.5% of operating budget) driven by decreased international student enrollment despite welcoming 8,000 new graduate students. The Board heard that the University raised $361.5 million in philanthropy in the past year, surpassing its goal and bringing the Defy Gravity campaign to $2.266 billion of its $4 billion target. New Provostial Guidelines on Gift Acceptance and Gift Administration were adopted, discontinuing anonymous corporate gifts and requiring public disclosure for donations of $250,000 or more. Capital projects totaling approximately $1+ billion are targeting governance approval, including the Temerty Building for health sciences, Academic Wood Tower, and a new Field House at UTSC. The 2024 Employment Equity Survey achieved 73.9% response rate among all employees, showing increased promotions for racialized staff but also increased exits among staff with disabilities.
packages.gradRelevance: Graduate student enrollment reached 8,000 new admissions in fall 2025, but the $50–60 million revenue shortfall due to decreased international student enrollment—a cohort that includes graduate students—will inform future enrolment recalibration and may affect funding availability. The Employment Equity Survey data on workforce diversity and the discussion of collective bargaining (mentioned in in camera session under Professor Hannah-Moffat's update) directly concern graduate student unions and unionized TA/RA labor. No specific changes to graduate funding, stipends, fees, housing, or accessibility services were announced at this meeting.
keyItems: Oak House, the first new student residence built on St. George Campus in 20 years, opened successfully; no specific graduate housing details provided.

Enrollment target miss: University enrolled approximately 100,000 students total (17,000 new undergraduates, 8,000 new graduate students) but created $50–60 million revenue shortfall (1.5% of operating budget) due to decline in international students and acceptance of ~900 domestic students above provincial funding corridor; long-term recalibration of enrollment targets underway.

Philanthropy: University raised $361.5 million in fiscal year 2024–25, surpassing annual goal; Defy Gravity campaign has reached $2.266 billion of $4 billion target; record alumni participation with 16,300 registrants at 2025 Alumni Reunion (11% increase); University ranked fourth globally in 2025 Global University Visibility Rankings.

New Provostial Guidelines on Gift Acceptance and Gift Administration approved: discontinue anonymous corporate gifts; formalize three levels of anonymity for individual donors; require public disclosure for gifts of $250,000 or more; internal task force established for implementation.

Capital projects targeting governance approval: Temerty Building (health sciences), Academic Wood Tower, multi-divisional building at 90 Queen's Park, new Field House at UTSC; renovations to MacMillan Theatre, Dentistry Clinic, and Astronomy & Astrophysics research labs; new Department of Computer Science space; LEAP 2.0 energy efficiency project; demolition of 215 Huron St. building.
MacMillan Theatre Remediation project execution approved on motion per Vice-President Operations & Real Estate Partnerships memorandum dated July 30, 2025.

packages.title: College of Electors - November 05, 2025
packages.summary: The College of Electors met on November 5, 2025, to address alumni governor recruitment for three seats opening June 30, 2026. Incumbents Amanda Bartley, Brian Madden, and Grace Westcott confirmed their intention to seek re-election. The College adopted desired attributes for 2026 alumni governors including industry experience in risk management, financial services, real estate, and government relations; governance expertise in complex organizations; human resources and succession planning knowledge; mental health awareness; and demonstrated volunteer commitment to the University. President Melanie Woodin outlined strategic priorities including an upcoming consultative Strategic Planning process, emphasizing AI, quantum computing, urban innovation, and climate action as leadership areas, with planned expansion of accessibility services and campus community-building initiatives.

packages.title: College of Electors - September 29, 2025
packages.summary: The College of Electors met on September 29, 2025, to hear presentations on international student recruitment and alumni relations. Vice-President Joseph Wong reported that U of T maintained stable international enrolment across 100+ countries despite geopolitical challenges, including a 70% reduction in Indian applications and Canada-China tensions, through diversification and strategic recruitment via social media, alumni networks, and regional guidance counsellor engagement. Assistant Vice-President Barbara Dick presented alumni engagement progress, noting virtual programming expanded reach post-pandemic and that the University analyzed engagement patterns among first-generation alumni over 15 years. The College approved May 12, 2025 meeting minutes and opened nominations for three seats on the 2025-2026 Nominating Committee for Alumni Governors (deadline October 6, 2025; selection at November 5 meeting).
packages.gradRelevance: The international student presentation directly affects graduate students: U of T's diversification strategy and study permit support systems serve international graduate students, and the presentation confirmed that most academic programs accept international applicants (with some regulatory exceptions). The alumni relations discussion has indirect relevance—the University's engagement of first-generation alumni may inform approaches to graduate student support and transition to alumni status, though no specific graduate funding, TA/RA, unionization, or housing matters were discussed at this meeting.
keyItems: Vice-President Joseph Wong presented international student enrolment strategy: U of T attracted students from 100+ countries and maintained stable enrolment despite geopolitical challenges including 70% reduction in Indian applications and Canada-China tensions
U of T invests in substantial financial aid for international students including International Scholar Awards and Pearson Scholarships; study permit approval rate significantly above national average
International student support services include orientation, mental health, academic advising, and study permit assistance; strong retention and graduation rates reported

U of T uses diversified recruitment: region-specific social media strategies (dedicated Chinese channels, U.S.-specific platforms), guidance counsellor outreach, direct student engagement, and global alumni network leverage

Geopolitical barriers identified: higher study permit rejection rates for African applicants; only countries with 40+ enrolled students were highlighted; African representation remains below threshold due to federal systemic barriers
International students eligible for most academic programs; some divisions have limited capacity due to regulatory or accreditation requirements

packages.title: Committee on Academic Policy and Programs - May 12, 2026
Committee on Academic Policy and Programs - May 12, 2026
packages.summary: On May 12, 2026, the Committee on Academic Policy and Programs approved three major items affecting graduate education: a new Master of Physician Assistant Studies (MPAS) at Temerty Faculty of Medicine effective September 1, 2027, delivered in hybrid format with clinical placements across Ontario and located at UTSC; amendments to the Policy on Academic Continuity effective June 1, 2026 that clarify decision-making criteria and communication expectations following 2018 mediation with UFTA; and revised Guidelines for the Assessment of Effectiveness of Teaching at UTSC effective immediately, incorporating explicit standards for hybrid and experiential learning with strengthened equity, diversity and inclusion commitments. The Committee also approved 24 new programs (13 undergraduate and 2 graduate from Arts & Science) for the Ontario Co-operative Education Tax Credit 2026-27 list, resolved academic regulation changes for return-from-absence procedures at UTM and UTSC effective September 1, 2026, and received consent approval of three annual reports on certificates, graduate collaborative specializations, and major modifications for 2025-26.
packages.gradRelevance: Graduate students are directly affected by approval of the new Master of Physician Assistant Studies program, a professional master's degree at the Institute of Medical Science that will accept graduates of the existing BScPA with advanced standing options. Two graduate-level programs from Faculty of Arts & Science were added to the Ontario Co-operative Education Tax Credit eligibility list for 2026-27, potentially expanding graduate work-integrated learning. The Policy on Academic Continuity amendments, effective June 1, 2026, provide clearer frameworks affecting graduate students during labour disputes or extraordinary circumstances, including instructor authority to modify delivery methods. The revised teaching assessment guidelines apply to all faculty members whose teaching is evaluated, including those who teach graduate courses, and emphasize hybrid and experiential learning modalities common in graduate programs.
keyItems: Master of Physician Assistant Studies (MPAS) approved for Institute of Medical Science, Temerty Faculty of Medicine, effective September 1, 2027: hybrid online and in-person format with extensive clinical placements across Ontario, including two required rotations in Northern Ontario; physically located at UTSC as part of Scarborough Academy of Medicine and Integrated Health (SAMIH); advanced standing option for BScPA graduates planned; research milestones established with students not penalized if research extends beyond two-year program completion

Policy on Academic Continuity amended effective June 1, 2026 following 2018 mediation between University Administration and University of Toronto Faculty Association (UFTA): amendments enhance clarity on application, precise definitions, decision-making criteria and communication expectations; Provost and Academic Board retain authority to declare academic disruption in strong consultation with academic leaders; instructors authorized to modify delivery methods but not content without leadership support

Ontario Co-operative Education Tax Credit: Approved List of Programs 2026-27 approved with 24 new program additions: 13 undergraduate programs from Faculty of Arts & Science, 9 from University of Toronto Mississauga, and 2 graduate-level programs from Faculty of Arts & Science

Guidelines for the Assessment of Effectiveness of Teaching approved for UTSC effective immediately: revised standards explicitly address hybrid teaching and experiential learning; strengthened commitment to equity, diversity and inclusion; incorporate diverse pedagogical perspectives; anticipated eight-year review cycle; resources include workshops and interactive web interface through Centre for Teaching Support & Innovation; student evaluations used as one contextual piece among multiple assessment criteria

Undergraduate Academic Regulation changes approved for University of Toronto Mississauga effective September 1, 2026: amendments to Return from Absence regulation improve clarity, transparency and consistency in re-registration and re-application processes; aligned with previous Faculty of Arts & Science changes and consulted with multiple divisions

Undergraduate Academic Regulation changes approved for University of Toronto Scarborough effective September 1, 2026: amendments to Return from Absence regulation standardize re-registration and re-application processes following 12-month absence with clear pathways based on academic standing and external study activity; consulted with Faculty of Arts & Science, Daniels Faculty of Architecture Landscape and Design, and UTM

documents.title: Master of Physician Assistant Studies Proposal, Temerty Faculty of Medicine, dated March 23, 2026
documents.summary: Proposal for new Master of Physician Assistant Studies (MPAS) offered by Institute of Medical Science, Temerty Faculty of Medicine, representing transition from existing Bachelor of Science Physician Assistant (BScPA) to professional master's degree. Program delivered in hybrid format combining online and in-person courses with extensive clinical placements across Ontario including two required rotations in Northern Ontario. Physically located at UTSC as part of Scarborough Academy of Medicine and Integrated Health (SAMIH) to leverage community outreach, hospital networks and community health partnerships. Supported by IMS (which already administers graduate health science research and biomedical communications professional master's), School of Graduate Studies and Temerty Faculty of Medicine.
documents.gradRelevance: This is a new professional master's program for graduate students. Establishes individual and group research milestones for all students over two-year program; students not completing research by end of program will not be penalized but must meet established milestones. Advanced standing option available to BScPA graduates creates pathway for existing graduates to enter master's level.

packages.title: Committee on Academic Policy and Programs - March 31, 2026
Committee on Academic Policy and Programs - March 31, 2026
packages.summary: On March 31, 2026, the Committee on Academic Policy and Programs approved three major academic initiatives: a new four-year Bachelor of Computer Science (BCS) degree across all three campuses effective September 1, 2027, addressing AI, quantum computing, cybersecurity, and data privacy; a new PhD in Interdisciplinary and Applied Biology at UTSC admitting ~5 students/year starting September 2027 with paid industry practicums and a graduate Chair position; and closure of the Specialist in French & Italian at UTM effective April 30, 2026 (no active students since admissions suspended November 23, 2016). The Committee also reviewed 12 program and unit assessments, requesting one-year follow-up reports on the Centre for Criminology and Sociolegal Studies (addressing understaffing that grew from 6 to 10 staff) and examining graduate student time-to-completion concerns in ECE and UTIAS. The Leslie Dan Faculty of Pharmacy reported research funding growth from $12.6 million (2023-24) to $17 million projected, with grant awards increasing from 117 to 159. The Physician Assistant Program will relocate entirely to UTSC in 2026 and transition to a master's degree program.
packages.gradRelevance: Graduate students are directly affected by the new PhD in Interdisciplinary and Applied Biology (admitting ~5/year with paid practicum placements) and the ongoing review of graduate time-to-completion issues in ECE and UTIAS, where external reviewers flagged concerns about degree completion timelines. The Centre for Criminology and Sociolegal Studies review identified understaffing impacting graduate supervision and program completion, with a one-year follow-up report requested to assess staffing improvements (grown from 6 to 10 staff). Leslie Dan Faculty of Pharmacy's increased research funding ($12.6M to $17M) may create additional RA opportunities. The Physician Assistant Program's transition to a master's degree will change the graduate student cohort composition at UTSC starting 2026.
keyItems: Approved four-year Bachelor of Computer Science degree across Faculty of Arts & Science, UTM, and UTSC, effective September 1, 2027, addressing AI, quantum computing, cybersecurity, health informatics, and data privacy

Approved new PhD in Interdisciplinary and Applied Biology at UTSC (Department of Biological Sciences), effective September 1, 2027: admits ~5 students/year; four-year option for master's holders, five-year direct-entry for bachelor's holders; includes research thesis, interdisciplinary coursework, required paid industry practicums, and professional development colloquium; will appoint graduate Chair and graduate Chair coordinator
Approved closure of Specialist in French & Italian at UTM, effective April 30, 2026; admissions suspended since November 23, 2016; no active students; Major and Minor programs continue

Centre for Criminology and Sociolegal Studies: Committee requested one-year follow-up report on staffing concerns; unit grew from 6 staff (2018) to 10 staff (2026), added Centre manager, graduate assistant, undergraduate assistant, and communications staff; external reviewers noted understaffing in 2018 and 2026; graduate student handbook and tracking of degree milestones to be implemented

Faculty of Applied Science & Engineering, Electrical & Computer Engineering: External reviewers raised concerns about graduate students' time to completion; department implementing dashboard with comprehensive data for supervisors; no follow-up report requested

University of Toronto Institute for Aerospace Studies (UTIAS): Review flagged concerns about graduate student communication, PhD qualifying exam standardization, course continuity (two sabbaticals and one departure created curriculum gap), and student housing; Director advised developing common expectations for exam difficulty and difficulty questions; Faculty taking steps to ensure sabbatical coverage; no follow-up report requested
