Committee on Academic Policy and Programs - October 21, 2025
packages.summary: The October 21, 2025 Committee on Academic Policy and Programs meeting will consider an amendment to the Policy on the Replacement and Reissuance of Diplomas (1985), sponsored by Vice-Provost Dwayne Benjamin and University Registrar Angelique Saweczko. The amendment will expand acceptable verification from 'notarized declarations' to 'notarized or commissioned declarations,' enabling virtual commissioning for overseas graduates, and will shift reissuance record-keeping from a separate annual list to the student information system. The committee will also receive for information eight external academic program reviews covering units across Faculty of Arts & Science, University of Toronto Mississauga, and University of Toronto Scarborough, including the Digital Humanities Minor (Woodsworth College), Centre for Industrial Relations and Human Resources, International Relations, Sexual Diversity Studies, Statistical Sciences, Management at UTM, Mathematical and Computational Sciences at UTM, and Philosophy at UTSC. No financial implications are associated with the diploma policy amendment.
packages.gradRelevance: Graduate programs directly reviewed include the Centre for Industrial Relations and Human Resources (MIRHR and PhD programs), Department of Statistical Sciences (Financial Insurance MFI, Statistics MSc and PhD), and the Centre for Industrial Relations and Human Resources programs. The diploma policy amendment affects all graduate students seeking replacement diplomas, particularly those overseas who now have accessible virtual commissioning options. The program reviews assess graduate program quality, student support, faculty stability, and curriculum—relevant to graduate student experience across multiple units.
keyItems: Amendment to Policy on Replacement and Reissuance of Diplomas 1985: change Section 2 and 3 from requiring 'notarized declaration' to 'notarized or commissioned declaration' to allow virtual commissioning for overseas graduates; change Section 6 to record reissued diplomas in student information system instead of maintaining separate annual list; effective immediately upon approval.

Digital Humanities Minor (Woodsworth College) external review: reviewers Giovanna Ceserani (Stanford) and David Schloen (University of Chicago) commended program as exemplary in North American digital humanities pedagogy, with growing enrolments and strong student engagement; identified concern that teaching positions secured only through 2026 and program at enrolment capacity; recommended permanent teaching positions and expansion of course sections to meet demand.

Centre for Industrial Relations and Human Resources (Faculty of Arts & Science) external review: programs reviewed include undergraduate MIRHR Specialist/Major, Work and Organizations streams (HBA), Certificate in Human Resource Management (CHRM); graduate MIRHR and PhD programs.

Department of Statistical Sciences (Faculty of Arts & Science) external review: programs reviewed include undergraduate Actuarial Science (HBSc) Specialist/Major, Data Science (HBSc) Specialist, Statistical Science variants (HBSc) Specialist/Major/Minor, Applied Data Science Minor; graduate Financial Insurance (MFI), Statistics (MSc, PhD).
International Relations program (Trinity College) external review: programs reviewed include undergraduate International Relations (HBA) Specialist/Major and Certificate in International Relations.
Mark S. Bonham Centre for Sexual Diversity Studies external review: program reviewed is Sexual Diversity Studies (HBA) Specialist/Major/Minor.

documents.title: Amendment to the Policy on the Replacement and Reissuance of Diplomas
documents.summary: This document proposes amendments to the 40-year-old Policy on the Replacement and Reissuance of Diplomas (December 1985). The amendment changes Section 2 and 3 to accept 'notarized or commissioned declarations' instead of only 'notarized declarations,' enabling virtual commissioning of statutory declarations for overseas alumni. Section 6 is revised to record reissued diplomas in the student information system rather than maintaining a separate annual list. Sponsored by Vice-Provost Dwayne Benjamin and University Registrar Angelique Saweczko; consulted with University Counsel in April 2025. The amendment has no budgetary implications and will be effective immediately upon approval; governance path includes AP&P recommendation (October 21, 2025) and Academic Board approval (November 13, 2025).
documents.gradRelevance: All graduate students seeking replacement diplomas benefit from increased accessibility, particularly those overseas who can now use virtual commissioning rather than requiring in-person notarization. The change addresses a concrete accessibility barrier for the global alumni community.

documents.title: Semi-Annual Report on the Reviews of Academic Units and Programs
documents.summary: This report provides a compendium of eight external reviews of academic programs and units commissioned by Deans, reviewed by external experts October 2024–September 2025, and submitted to AP&P for information and discussion. Reviews cover Faculty of Arts & Science programs (Digital Humanities Minor, Centre for Industrial Relations and Human Resources, International Relations, Sexual Diversity Studies, Department of Statistical Sciences), University of Toronto Mississauga programs (Department of Management, Department of Mathematical and Computational Sciences), and University of Toronto Scarborough programs (Department of Philosophy). Overall themes emphasize strong program quality, high-calibre students, innovative curricula, interdisciplinary breadth, research and experiential learning opportunities, and equity/diversity/inclusion commitments. Reviewers consistently identified areas for development: strengthening coordination across cognate programs, ensuring teaching and program delivery sustainability, enhancing student and faculty mentorship and supports, addressing enrolment pressures, strengthening experiential and career supports, and ensuring diversity and accessibility in curricula, hiring, and opportunities. Sponsored by Nick Rule, Vice-Provost, Academic Programs. No financial implications noted.
documents.gradRelevance: Multiple reviewed units directly house graduate programs: Centre for Industrial Relations and Human Resources (MIRHR and PhD); Department of Statistical Sciences (Financial Insurance MFI, Statistics MSc and PhD); and implicitly others with graduate components. Graduate students benefit from external validation of program quality and explicit reviewer recommendations for enhanced student mentorship, research opportunities, career supports, and diversity initiatives. Graduate program sustainability is directly addressed through reviewer concerns about teaching position stability and faculty complement adequacy.

packages.title: Committee on Academic Policy and Programs - October 21, 2025
packages.summary: On October 21, 2025, the Committee on Academic Policy and Programs approved an amendment to the Policy on the Replacement and Reissuance of Diplomas (last revised in 1985), enabling remote commissioning of declarations and updating reporting requirements to use the ROSI system. The Committee conducted semi-annual reviews of eight academic units and programs across FAS, UTM, and UTSC. Key discussions included the Mark S. Bonham Centre for Sexual Diversity Studies' potential transition from EDU-B to EDU-A status to enable majority faculty appointments; the International Relations program's decision to close the Certificate in International Affairs (created 2019) due to COVID-19 pandemic impacts on international opportunities; Digital Humanities' receipt of dedicated administrative staff and planned Woodsworth College space by 2027; UTM Management's expansion of co-op participation from 247 to a goal of 1,000 students annually and three-year hiring plan for 40 positions including five in Management; and UTM Mathematical and Computational Sciences' robotics research space reallocation project scheduled for completion by 2027.
packages.gradRelevance: Graduate programs directly affected include the International Relations program ending its Certificate in International Affairs, potentially impacting graduate international study options; UTM's Institute for Management and Innovation (IMI) graduate programs (Master of Management in Professional Accounting and Master of Management of Innovation) undergoing their own UTQAP review with admissions criteria under examination; and UTM's co-op program expansion involving graduate students. Part-time and contractually limited term (CLTA) faculty job stability was discussed, relevant to graduate student TAs and RAs; teaching stipend equity between divisions was noted as governed by collective bargaining agreements with room for divisional negotiation. The Digital Humanities program's administrative and space improvements may affect graduate student support and workspace.
keyItems: Amendment to Policy on the Replacement and Reissuance of Diplomas (1985) approved effective immediately, adding remote commissioning of declarations and updating ROSI-based tracking by Registrar's office

Mark S. Bonham Centre for Sexual Diversity Studies seeking transition from EDU-B to EDU-A status to hold majority or 100% faculty appointments; Deans Advisory Committee on Academic Change (DACAC) to review proposal and explore alternatives including partnerships

International Relations program discontinuing Certificate in International Affairs (established 2019) due to COVID-19 limitations on international travel and administrative burden; proposing curriculum changes including moving fourth-year methods seminar to second year

Digital Humanities program secured full-time dedicated administrative staff; Woodsworth College renovation underway with expected dedicated space and shared meeting areas by 2027; faculty appointments relying on contractually limited positions with cognate humanities departments contributing permanent tenure-stream involvement

UTM Department of Management expanding co-op program from 247 to targeted 1,000 students annually within 10 years; joined co-op this year with 10 students; experiential education portfolio includes expanding research opportunity programs (ROPs)

UTM launching three-year hiring plan to fill 40 faculty positions (including 5 in Management) lost due to reduced international enrollment; no new growth positions planned until financial stability improves

packages.title: Committee on Academic Policy and Programs - September 16, 2025
packages.summary: On September 16, 2025, the Committee on Academic Policy and Programs held an orientation meeting for new members. The meeting provided guidance on two key processes: (1) Academic Change pathways, which outline how proposals develop through collegial discussion, external peer review, divisional and university governance approval, and external Quality Council approval; (2) Cyclical Reviews under UTQAP, conducted through self-studies, external reviewer site visits, reading groups (four groups of 3-4 reviews each), and monitoring reports. For 2025-26, decanal reviews are scheduled in Faculty of Arts & Science (Centre for Industrial Relations and Human Resources, Department of Statistical Sciences, Digital Humanities, International Relations, and Sexual Diversity Studies programs), University of Toronto Mississauga (Department of Management, Department of Mathematical and Computational Sciences), University of Toronto Scarborough (Department of Philosophy), and one provostial review (Leslie Dan Faculty of Pharmacy). The next meeting is scheduled for October 21, 2025, 3:10–5:10 p.m.
packages.gradRelevance: While the agenda focuses primarily on academic governance structures, cyclical reviews may examine graduate program quality, admission standards, funding, and supervisory practices within departments and faculties under review. Graduate students should monitor reviews of their home departments or programs listed in the 2025-26 cycle: Centre for Industrial Relations and Human Resources, Department of Statistical Sciences, Digital Humanities program, International Relations program, Sexual Diversity Studies program, Department of Management (UTM), Department of Mathematical and Computational Sciences (UTM), Department of Philosophy (UTSC), University of Toronto Institute for Aerospace Studies, and Department of Electrical Engineering, as these reviews may surface issues affecting graduate education, TA/RA allocations, or program support. The meeting text itself does not reference specific stipend amounts, fee changes, unionized labour matters, or housing, but external reviewers will be consulting with students and staff during site visits.
keyItems: Academic Change process outlined: ideas emerge from collegial discussion, are developed with cognate programs through external peer review, receive divisional and university governance approval, then external Quality Council approval from Ontario colleagues

Cyclical Reviews process includes self-study (written by programs pre-site visit), external reviewer site visits (led by associate/full professors from peer institutions), reading group review (AP&P members assess accuracy of review summary and adequacy of Dean's administrative response), and automatic interim monitoring reports plus optional one-year follow-up reports for concerns requiring sustained response

Reading groups structured as four groups, each receiving 3-4 reviews, with a group leader acting as coordinator and spokesperson; each group receives self-study, full review report, review summary, Dean's administrative response and implementation plan, and program/unit response

Reading group assessment addresses three key questions: (1) Does the summary accurately capture the tone and content of the full review? (2) Does the Dean's administrative response adequately address all issues identified? (3) Is a one-year follow-up report needed?

2025-26 decanal reviews scheduled: Faculty of Arts & Science reviews Centre for Industrial Relations and Human Resources, Department of Statistical Sciences, Digital Humanities program, International Relations program, and Sexual Diversity Studies program
2025-26 decanal reviews scheduled: University of Toronto Mississauga reviews Department of Management and Department of Mathematical and Computational Sciences

documents.title: Orientation and Discussion: Academic Change
documents.summary: This presentation outlines the three-stage Academic Change process at the University of Toronto. Proposal Development involves ideas emerging from collegial discussion among colleagues, development in consultation with cognate programs/units, and external peer review. Proposal Approval occurs in two stages: first divisional governance approval by divisional colleagues, then university governance approval by U of T colleagues. External approval by Ontario's Quality Council provides the final stage of oversight.
documents.gradRelevance: Graduate students may propose new graduate programs or curriculum changes through this process; understanding the collegial discussion, external review, and governance stages is relevant for students advocating for changes to their programs.

documents.title: Orientation and Discussion: Cyclical Reviews and Reading Group Guidance 2025-26
documents.summary: This presentation details the cyclical review process under UTQAP for academic programs. Reviews follow a continuous improvement cycle with four phases: Self-Study (written by programs/units before site visit with critical self-analysis), External Reviewers (associate/full professors from peer institutions conduct site visit and write review report), Governance (AP&P members and reading groups assess reviews; four reading groups handle 3-4 reviews each), and Oversight (Quality Council reviews Final Assessment Reports and Implementation Plans). AP&P ensures reviews comply with University policy, the Provost manages appropriately, and all academic quality issues are addressed or have implementation plans. Reading groups assess whether the summary accurately captures the review, whether the Dean's administrative response adequately addresses identified issues, and whether a one-year follow-up report is needed. All reviews automatically receive Interim Monitoring Reports; one-year follow-up reports can be requested for concerns requiring sustained response. The 2025-26 review schedule includes decanal reviews of nine units: Centre for Industrial Relations and Human Resources, Department of Statistical Sciences, Digital Humanities program, International Relations program, Sexual Diversity Studies program (Faculty of Arts & Science); Department of Management and Department of Mathematical and Computational Sciences (UTM); Department of Philosophy (UTSC); and one provostial review of Leslie Dan Faculty of Pharmacy.
documents.gradRelevance: Graduate students in departments undergoing cyclical review in 2025-26 should be aware that external reviewers will conduct site visits including consultations with students and staff; the review process examines program quality, resource allocation, and implementation of recommendations. Graduate students are stakeholders in these reviews and may be consulted. Reviews may identify issues or recommendations affecting graduate education, funding, or supervision within their programs.

Vice-President Leah Cowen announced VPRI's new strategic grant writing team supporting three tri-agency competitions (NSERC Alliance Society grants up to $500,000 Spring 2026, CIHR Project grants $1M+ 2026, SSHRC Insight grants over $300,000), the launch of the Research Leadership Academy for mid-career tenure-stream faculty, the 2026 President's Impact Awards ($10,000/year for five years, nominations due February 13), and the September 17 U of T Acceleratorfest. The committee confirmed its next meeting for October 21, 2025.
packages.gradRelevance: Graduate students are directly affected by the updated generative AI guidelines covering comprehensive exams, candidacy requirements, language requirements, doctoral theses, and oral examinations—all mandatory graduate milestones. The new tri-agency grant writing support (NSERC Alliance up to $500,000, CIHR $1M+, SSHRC over $300,000) may fund graduate research positions and RA/TA opportunities. The President's Impact Awards ($10,000/year × 5 years) could support graduate supervisor research funding, indirectly benefiting graduate student research support.
keyItems: School of Graduate Studies updating 2023 generative AI guidelines beyond graduate theses to include comprehensive exams, candidacy requirements, language requirements, doctoral theses, and final oral examinations; feedback collected via graduate chair survey; updated guidance to be discussed at December tri-campus SGS session and published on graduate unit websites with discipline-specific amendments

packages.title: Elections Committee - November 27, 2025
packages.summary: On November 27, 2025, the Elections Committee will consider comprehensive revisions to the Election Guidelines 2026, which Chief Returning Officer Timothy Harlick proposes for approval effective January 1, 2026. The guidelines represent a structural overhaul reorganizing the document from an estate-based format (Administrative Staff, Teaching Staff, Students) to a process-based structure (Eligibility, Nominations, Elections, Expenses). Key changes include eliminating mail-in balloting in favour of exclusive web-based voting; implementing a universal $400 spending cap for all student candidates (replacing variable formula-based limits); moving campaign guidelines from appendices into the main body; consolidating all calendar dates into Appendix A to reduce administrative errors; and granting the CRO authority to address minor campaign matters informally and determine feasibility of by-elections within eight months of term's end. The recommendation is to approve these guidelines for effective implementation January 1, 2026, with subsequent Executive Committee approval scheduled for December 1, 2025.
packages.gradRelevance: The Election Guidelines 2026 directly affect graduate students eligible to vote in Governing Council elections and those considering candidacy. The universal $400 spending limit for all student candidates simplifies campaign finance rules for graduate student candidates and ensures consistent treatment across student constituencies regardless of size. The elimination of mail-in balloting and exclusive use of the web-based voting platform may impact graduate student participation, particularly those with accessibility needs or scheduling constraints, though the guidelines note enhanced accessibility features of the digital platform. Graduate students serving on Governing Council or its committees will operate under the new procedural rules for nominations, campaigning, and voting.
keyItems: Structural reorganization of Election Guidelines 2025 from estate-based structure (Administrative Staff, Teaching Staff, Students) to process-based structure (Eligibility, Nominations, Elections, Expenses) to eliminate redundancy and improve accessibility

Elimination of mail-in balloting as of 2026, with web-based voting platform established as exclusive voting method, reflecting zero mail-in participation in recent election cycles and citing improved security, real-time tabulation, and superior accessibility features

Implementation of universal $400 spending limit for all student candidates (replacing prior variable formula based on constituency size), aligning student spending limits with existing Teaching and Administrative Staff limits
Relocation of campaign guidelines and violation procedures from appendices into main body of guidelines (Sections 6.2 and 10) to ensure prominence rather than supplementary treatment

Removal of specific requirement for centralized Property Management approval for poster regulations; candidates now responsible for identifying and adhering to building-specific posting rules across all campuses
Consolidation of all time-sensitive calendar dates from main body into single Appendix A: Election Schedule to reduce administrative error and simplify future maintenance

documents.title: Election Guidelines 2026
documents.summary: The Election Guidelines 2026 document presents a comprehensive rewrite of the 2025 guidelines with six major changes: (1) structural reorganization from estate-based to process-based format eliminating redundancy; (2) elimination of mail-in balloting in favour of exclusive web-based voting platform; (3) financial standardization establishing a universal $400 spending limit for all student candidates; (4) relocation of campaign guidelines and enforcement procedures from appendices to main body sections 6.2 and 10, with streamlined poster regulations removing centralized Property Management approval requirement; (5) consolidation of calendar dates into Appendix A to reduce administrative error; and (6) streamlined by-election procedures empowering the CRO to determine feasibility. The document identifies no financial implications and recommends approval effective January 1, 2026, with governance path through Elections Committee (November 27, 2025) and Executive Committee (December 1, 2025).
documents.gradRelevance: The Election Guidelines 2026 establish rules governing graduate student participation in Governing Council elections, including the universal $400 spending cap for student candidates, web-based voting requirements, nomination procedures, campaign conduct, and expense enforcement. Graduate students eligible to vote or seek candidacy will operate under these revised rules, which simplify candidate spending limits and voting methods but eliminate mail-in alternatives that may have accommodated certain accessibility or scheduling needs.

documents.title: Election Guidelines 2025 (Current)
documents.summary: The Election Guidelines 2025 document (approved November 28, 2024, effective January 1, 2025) contains the current comprehensive election framework for Governing Council, Academic Board, University of Toronto Mississauga and University of Toronto Scarborough Campus Councils and their Standing Committees. It is organized by estate (Administrative Staff, Teaching Staff, Students) across 8 sections covering principles of governance, election roles (Chief Returning Officer, Deputy Returning Officer, Elections Committee), eligibility requirements, nomination processes, balloting (both web-based and mail), campaign guidelines, election expenses with spending limits, and procedures for appeals, recounts, and by-elections. The document establishes separate spending limits by constituency size for student candidates and includes provisions for both web-based and mail-in voting.
documents.gradRelevance: The Election Guidelines 2025 currently govern all aspects of graduate student participation in Governing Council elections, including nomination eligibility, campaign conduct, spending limits (which vary by student constituency size under current rules), and voting methods (both web-based and mail-in). Graduate students seeking candidacy or serving as voters operate under these guidelines, which the 2026 revision will substantially modify.

packages.title: Elections Committee - November 27, 2025
packages.summary: On November 27, 2025, the Elections Committee met to review major revisions to the Election Guidelines 2026, which Chair Vikram Chadalawada and Chief Returning Officer Timothy Harlick presented for recommendation to the Executive Committee. The 2026 Guidelines introduce three significant changes: structural reorganization by process rather than estate to reduce repetition, a move to web-based voting exclusively to improve security and eliminate paper ballots, and a standardized campaign spending limit of $400 for all candidates regardless of constituency size (replacing the previous variable formula). Minor changes include moving campaign rules and enforcement protocols from appendices into the main body, decentralizing poster approval to building-specific rules, delegating authority to the CRO to resolve minor campaign violations without escalation to Election Overseers, consolidating calendar dates into a single appendix, and standardizing by-election procedures with CRO discretion codified and reported back to the Committee. The Committee recommended approval of the Election Guidelines 2026, effective January 1, 2026, pending correction of two typographic errors identified by a member.
packages.gradRelevance: The Election Guidelines 2026 affect graduate students as voters and potential candidates in university elections. The standardized $400 campaign spending limit applies equally to all candidates, including those running for graduate student representative positions. The move to web-based voting exclusively may impact accessibility for some graduate students depending on technology access. The Guidelines' provisions on by-elections and nomination procedures may be relevant if graduate student constituency seats become vacant. However, this meeting contains no discussion of graduate student-specific electoral issues, stipend changes, TA/RA unionization matters, or services.
keyItems: Election Guidelines 2026 structurally reorganized by process rather than estate to reduce repetition and enhance user experience for candidates and administrators
Move to web-based voting exclusively, eliminating paper ballots to improve security and eliminate outdated processes
Standardized campaign spending limit of $400 applicable to all candidates regardless of constituency size, replacing previous variable formula
Campaign rules and enforcement protocols moved from appendices to main body of Guidelines; poster approval responsibilities decentralized to building-specific rules
Authority delegated to Chief Returning Officer Timothy Harlick to resolve minor or inadvertent campaign violations without escalation to Election Overseers
Calendar dates consolidated into single appendix to eliminate conflicting or incorrect timelines

documents.title: Overview of Mandate & Terms of Reference
documents.summary: The Elections Committee is a 9-member body reporting to the Executive Committee, with representation from administrative staff, alumni, Lieutenant-Governor-in-Council appointees, students, and teaching staff, plus ex officio positions for the Chancellor, Chair, Vice-Chair, and President. The committee is responsible for recommending election policy, reviewing and approving the annual Election Guidelines (forwarding major amendments to the Executive Committee), serving as Election Overseers, and receiving annual reports from the Chief Returning Officer. Quorum is 3 members with at least 2 non-ex officio members present. The committee normally meets in open session but may convene in camera for sensitive matters including violations of Election Guidelines.
documents.gradRelevance: The Elections Committee includes one student representative on a 9-member body, meaning graduate students have voting representation in the governance of all university elections including elections to the Governing Council and Academic Board where graduate students also have seats.

packages.title: Executive Committee - May 04, 2026
packages.summary: On May 4, 2026, the Executive Committee confirmed two new degree/program offerings effective September 1, 2027: a Bachelor of Computer Science (four-year honours degree across St. George, UTM, and UTSC campuses) and a PhD in Interdisciplinary and Applied Biology at UTSC. The Committee received Jodie Glean's presentation on the draft Policy on Human Rights Code Discrimination and Harassment, which will create a new Human Rights Office as a single point of intake, replace existing discrimination statements, include a 5-year review cycle, and implement non-disciplinary alternative dispute resolution pathways. Professor Cheryl Regehr presented recommendations from the Code of Student Conduct review (based on 350+ participants and 44 hours of consultation), proposing fundamental restructuring into user-friendly tiers of offences, creation of a central reporting portal under the Vice-Provost Students, and implementation of a mental health diversion pathway. The Committee confirmed fee increases for UTM and UTSC student societies, including MiWay U-Pass increases of $4.97/session at UTM and UTSC student society portion increases of $0.69–$1.03/session, plus proposed health and dental plan increases of up to $10.98–$13.88/session at UTSC.
packages.gradRelevance: Graduate students are affected by the new PhD in Interdisciplinary and Applied Biology at UTSC, which aligns with Towards 2030 initiatives for intensive graduate studies. The draft Human Rights Policy directly impacts graduate students as both potential complainants and respondents, establishing timelines, a centralized intake office, and a 12-month window to file complaints after departure. The Code of Student Conduct review addresses harassment and online abuse, with a proposed mental health diversion pathway relevant to graduate student conduct. Graduate student fees are not explicitly addressed in this report, though undergraduate fee increases at UTM ($0.88–$4.97/session depending on component) and UTSC ($0.69–$13.88/session) are confirmed.
keyItems: Bachelor of Computer Science degree confirmed as four-year honours program across St. George, UTM, and UTSC, effective September 1, 2027
PhD in Interdisciplinary and Applied Biology at UTSC confirmed effective September 1, 2027, aligned with Towards 2030 graduate research intensification

Draft Policy on Human Rights Code Discrimination and Harassment presented: creates new Human Rights Office as single intake point, replaces Statement on Prohibited Discrimination and Statement on Human Rights, includes 5-year review cycle, requires both complainant and respondent have University relationship, allows complaints within 12 months of departure

Code of Student Conduct review completed with four recommendations: (1) restructure Code into user-friendly document with tiered offences, (2) update scope to include online abuse and repeated harassment with progressive discipline, (3) create central reporting portal under Vice-Provost Students, (4) implement mental health diversion pathway for students unable to appreciate consequences of actions due to mental illness

Consultation on Code of Student Conduct involved 350+ participants, 44 hours of in-person/online sessions, landscape review of 22 Canadian universities, legal research conducted November 2025–February 2026

UTMSU fee increases confirmed for Fall 2026: MiWay U-Pass +$4.97/session, society portion +$0.88/session (full-time), Student Centre Levy +$0.26/session, CFS +$0.24/session (full-time only), and smaller increases across 10 other components

packages.title: Executive Committee - March 26, 2026
packages.summary: The Executive Committee held a special meeting on March 26, 2026, to restructure senior leadership. The Committee approved the elimination of two Vice-President positions (Vice-President, Operations and Real Estate Partnerships and Vice-President, People Strategy, Equity and Culture, both effective June 30, 2026) and creation of three new positions: Vice-President, People, Finance and Digital Services (July 1, 2026); Vice-President, Facilities and Real Estate (July 1, 2026); and Vice-President, Legal, Risk and Safety (May 1, 2026). Kristin Taylor was appointed as Vice-President, Legal, Risk and Safety and Chief Legal Officer for a six-year term (May 1, 2026 to April 30, 2032). All recommendations were forwarded to the Governing Council for approval.

The administrative restructuring affects senior leadership reporting lines and organizational structure but does not address graduate student-specific policies or support.

The President reported on $6.4 billion in provincial post-secondary funding announced February 12, 2026, the Impact+ Research Chairs program (24 nominees at $1M or $500K each), and antisemitism response initiatives including a new Human Rights Office and mandatory dean training, following the February 25 incident involving antisemitic cartoons used by the Dean of Dentistry who went on leave pending external investigation.
packages.gradRelevance: UTMAGS fees increase by $4.87 per fall/winter session for the MiWay U-Pass beginning Summer 2026, directly affecting grad students at Mississauga. The Student Mental Health Annual Report notes the Stepped Care Model, counselling wait time elimination (90% of requests scheduled same day), and mental health resources in 140 languages with 55,000+ portal page views and 10,000+ self-directed resource accesses—directly benefiting graduate students. The antisemitism response, including the new Human Rights Office as complaint entry point and mandatory Bill 166 training for Principals/Deans this spring, affects graduate student safety and complaint processes university-wide.

packages.title: Executive Committee - December 11, 2025
packages.summary: The Executive Committee held a special meeting on December 11, 2025, with 12 voting members present. The sole substantive item was an in-camera discussion resulting in approval of a motion to reappoint Professor Joseph Wong as Vice-President, International for a five-year term (spanning five and a half years including six months administrative leave) from July 1, 2026 to December 31, 2031. The recommendation was approved and forwarded to Governing Council for ratification. The meeting lasted 17 minutes.

packages.title: Executive Committee - December 01, 2025
packages.summary: On December 1, 2025, the Executive Committee approved the Election Guidelines 2026 (effective January 1, 2026), which eliminated mail-in ballots and introduced a universal campaign spending limit across all estates and constituencies to promote equity. The Committee endorsed revisions to the Policy on Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment (effective January 1, 2026) and amendments to the Policy and Procedures on Employment Conditions of Part-Time Faculty agreed between the University and the UTFA (effective January 1, 2026). The Committee received the Annual Report on Complaints of Prohibited Discrimination and Discriminatory Harassment covering July 1, 2024 to June 30, 2025, which reported 250 complaints (0.2% complaint rate across the 116,000-member community) and separately tracked complaints of antisemitism, anti-Black racism, Islamophobia, and anti-Indigenous racism per Bill 166 requirements. The administration will draft a new human rights policy to replace the Statement on Prohibited Discrimination and Discriminatory Harassment, to be brought for approval in governance cycle 5. President Melanie Woodin announced $24 million invested to create 100 new postdoctoral positions and reported three high-profile faculty recruits from MIT and Stanford for 2026-27.
packages.gradRelevance: Graduate students are directly affected by: (1) the $24 million investment creating 100 new postdoctoral positions announced by the President, which expands research funding and career pathways; (2) revisions to the Policy on Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment effective January 1, 2026, which applies to graduate students as community members; (3) amendments to Part-Time Faculty employment conditions effective January 1, 2026, which may affect graduate students employed as teaching assistants or part-time instructors; (4) the new human rights policy in development (cycle 5 approval) that will establish clearer complaint pathways and supports affecting all students including graduates. The discrimination complaint report (250 complaints over 12 months) provides baseline data on campus climate affecting all students.
keyItems: Election Guidelines 2026 approved effective January 1, 2026: eliminated mail-in ballots, reorganized content by process rather than estate, and implemented universal campaign spending limit for all candidates across estates and constituencies to promote equity principle

Policy on Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment revisions endorsed and forwarded to Governing Council for approval; effective January 1, 2026, following extensive consultation across all three campuses

Review of Statement on Prohibited Discrimination and Discriminatory Harassment completed by co-chairs Professor Brenda Cossman and Ms Caroline Rabbat with 13 recommendations; administration assembling working group to draft replacement policy for cycle 5 approval; new human rights office proposed as anchor for policy

New antisemitism training titled 'Understanding and Addressing Antisemitism at U of T: Rights, Responsibilities, and Inclusive Practices' launched by Institutional Equity Office, to be offered throughout academic year

documents.title: Reviews of Academic Programs and Units: Part 1 (Report Number 259 of the Academic Board, November 13, 2025)
documents.summary: Semi-annual report covering eight externally-commissioned reviews of academic programs and units across Faculty of Arts & Science, University of Toronto Mississauga, and University of Toronto Scarborough. Reviews examined Digital Humanities Minor (Woodsworth College), Centre for Industrial Relations and Human Resources, International Relations program, Mark S. Bonham Centre for Sexual Diversity Studies, Department of Statistical Sciences, Management and Mathematical/Computational Sciences at UTM, and Philosophy at UTSC. Reviewers from peer institutions (Stanford, University of Chicago, others) assessed program quality, curricula, faculty, student experience, and organizational structure. Common themes included strong program reputation and student quality, innovative curricula and experiential learning, and commitment to EDI; areas for development included coordination across cognate programs, teaching sustainability, enrolment management, career supports, and diversity in hiring and curricula.
documents.gradRelevance: Directly relevant to graduate students in reviewed programs: graduate-level programs reviewed include Industrial Relations and Human Resources (MIRHR, PhD), Financial Insurance (MFI), and Statistics (MSc, PhD). Recommendations address research opportunities, faculty mentorship, program sustainability, and student supports—factors affecting graduate student experience and career preparation. The Digital Humanities Minor review notes teaching positions secured only through 2026, affecting TA availability.

packages.title: Executive Committee - October 27, 2025
packages.summary: The October 27, 2025 Executive Committee meeting will confirm the MacMillan Theatre Remediation project scope (approximately 1,200 gross square metres, 440 net assignable square metres, 815-seat theatre), funded by Provostial Funds, with construction targeted for December 2025–June 2026 and reopening in fall 2026. The Committee will endorse the University Ombudsperson's 2024–25 annual report (covering July 1, 2024–June 30, 2025) for forwarding to Governing Council on November 6, 2025, noting 357 new requests for assistance and highlighting concerns about international student fee exemptions, graduate supervision, and student financial aid. No approvals were taken under summer executive authority. A motion for reconsideration regarding a proposed Governing Council resolution on antisemitism—bringing forward a statement that condemns acts of antisemitism on campus and extends apology to affected students, faculty, and staff for incidents since October 7, 2023—will be discussed, though the Executive Committee previously defeated a similar motion on December 3, 2024.
packages.gradRelevance: Graduate students are directly affected by the Ombudsperson report: 88 graduate students (up from 61 in 2023–24) sought assistance in 2024–25, with the most frequent concerns being graduate supervision and academic progress (14 cases), and financial aid, funding, and awards (12 cases). The report notes the School of Graduate Studies and Centre for Graduate Mentorship & Supervision are addressing supervision issues. The MacMillan Theatre project, while primarily a cultural asset for the Faculty of Music, will provide a hybrid lecture-performance venue that may benefit graduate instruction across campus. No other direct graduate-specific funding, fee, stipend, TA/RA labour, housing, or accessibility matters are substantively addressed in this meeting agenda.
keyItems: MacMillan Theatre Remediation project scope confirmed: approximately 1,200 gsm and 440 nasm theatre space (815 seats), to be funded by Provostial Funds; construction scheduled December 2025–June 2026 with fall 2026 opening target.

Project scope includes back-of-house improvements (rigging, fire curtain replacement, safety equipment, electrical/lighting upgrades) and front-of-house improvements (seat replacement with tablet arms, 14 barrier-free wheelchair spaces, AV upgrades for lecture capability, HVAC reinstatement to orchestra stage).

MacMillan Theatre conversion to hybrid lecture-performance venue will add 600+ seat lecture hall capacity to St. George campus to address growing enrolments and course capacity needs across academic units.

University Ombudsperson received 357 new requests for assistance (July 1, 2024–June 30, 2025); noted increase in graduate student cases (88 total, up from 61 previous year) with leading concerns being graduate supervision/academic progress (14 cases) and financial aid/funding/awards (12 cases).

Ombudsperson report identifies increased cases from international students (64 in 2024–25 vs. 50 in 2023–24) and doubled cases regarding International Fee Exemption decisions (9 cases); recommendations for enhanced communication resources to be developed by divisional registrars' offices.

Cases related to student fees increased to 27 in 2024–25 (from 21 in 2023–24), and cases on financial aid, graduate funding, and awards rose to 25 (from 15 in 2023–24); no systemic concerns identified warranting formal recommendations.

documents.title: Capital Project (Level 2): Report of the Project Planning Committee for the MacMillan Theatre Remediation – Project Scope and Sources of Funding
documents.summary: Detailed project planning report for MacMillan Theatre remediation within Edward Johnson Building (80 Queen's Park Crescent). Project totals approximately 1,200 gsm and 440 nasm; theatre is 815 seats and will be converted to hybrid lecture-performance venue. Back-of-house scope includes rigging equipment replacement, fire curtain, safety guards, fall arrest equipment, fireproofing, lighting/electrical upgrades, and demolition/abatement. Front-of-house scope includes seat replacement (excluding balcony) with foldable tablet arms, 14 barrier-free wheelchair spaces, new AV elements and teaching podium, new projection screens, HVAC reinstatement to orchestra stage, and demolition/abatement. Project funded by Provostial Funds. Construction timeline: October/November 2025 tender, December 2025–June 2026 construction, ready for takeover July 1, 2026, fall 2026 opening. Report includes sustainability considerations with Sustainability Office, including seat deconstruction/recycling and future BAS controls integration for HVAC optimization.
documents.gradRelevance: The hybrid lecture-performance conversion adds a 600+ seat lecture theatre to address increasing demand for large instructional spaces across St. George campus, supporting academic units' need to increase course capacity for growing enrolments. This benefits graduate-level instruction if large enrolment seminars or lectures utilize the space, though no specific graduate programming allocation is mentioned.

documents.title: Report of the University Ombudsperson for the period July 1, 2024 to June 30, 2025 and Administrative Response
documents.summary: Annual ombudsperson report covering 357 new requests for assistance (RFAs) from July 1, 2024–June 30, 2025. Key findings: 88 graduate students sought assistance (up from 61 in 2023–24), with top concerns being graduate supervision/academic progress (14 cases) and financial aid/funding/awards (12 cases). 64 international students contacted office (vs. 50 prior year); 9 cases involved International Fee Exemption decisions (vs. 4 in prior years), prompting recommendation for enhanced communication resources from divisional registrars. Student fees cases increased to 27 (from 21); financial aid/awards cases rose to 25 (from 15). Cases citing incivility declined to 27 (from 46 in 2023–24). Transfer credit cases increased to 7 (from 2 and 0 in prior years); petitions/appeals remained stable at 19 cases. Office notes Administration initiatives on policy currency, including reviews of Code of Behaviour on Academic Matters, Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment policy, and Prohibited Discrimination statement. Professor Faye Mishna appointed as incoming Ombudsperson for three-year term starting July 1, 2025.
documents.gradRelevance: Directly relevant to graduate students: 88 graduate student cases in 2024–25 (44% increase from prior year), with graduate supervision/academic progress and financial aid/funding/awards being leading concerns. School of Graduate Studies and Centre for Graduate Mentorship & Supervision commended for support and training. Report indicates ongoing monitoring of graduate funding processes and coordination with relevant offices to address student concerns; no formal systemic recommendations issued but continued monitoring planned.

documents.title: Report on Approvals under Summer Executive Authority
documents.summary: Report on actions taken under summer executive authority delegated to President by Governing Council on June 24, 2025. Authority covers appointments, curriculum changes, and urgent matters not deferrable until next regular meeting. Report indicates there were no approvals under summer executive authority within the purview of the Executive Committee; matters were either handled appropriately or deferred.

documents.title: Notice of Motion for Reconsideration
documents.summary: Executive Committee agenda item proposing reconsideration of a motion on antisemitism on campus. Governor proposes motion stating: 'Since October 7, 2023, there has been a reported 600% increase in antisemitic hate crimes in the City of Toronto, including numerous documented and undocumented incidents on our campuses. Be it resolved that the Governing Council of the University of Toronto unequivocally condemns acts of antisemitism on our campuses. We extend our sincere apology to the University's learners, faculty, and staff for all antisemitic incidents that have occurred at the University since October 7, 2023.' Similar motion was defeated December 3, 2024; Executive Committee consensus then was that Governing Council should exercise oversight role rather than make statements on its own behalf and that University's existing antidiscrimination initiatives already demonstrated commitment. Governor providing additional context of alleged antisemitic incidents involving U of T faculty/staff/students (including statements about 'Zionist funders,' blood libels, 'Jewish supremacists,' incidents on UTM campus). Motion for Reconsideration requires two-thirds Executive Committee majority; if approved, subsequent vote on endorsement requires simple majority. Document includes excerpts from December 3, 2024 and October 28, 2024 Executive Committee reports discussing prior consideration of similar antisemitism motions.

packages.title: Executive Committee - October 27, 2025
packages.summary: The Executive Committee met on October 27, 2025 to review the President's first report of the academic year, covering enrolment trends (17,000 new undergraduate students: 12,000 domestic, 5,000 international; 25-30% drop in computer science demand), a $60 million revenue shortfall (1.6% below planned), two major gifts ($80 million to name the Henry N. R. Jackman Faculty of Law; $2 million for the Commercialization Catalyst Prize), emergency research bridge funding from the contingency fund for faculty affected by U.S. funding cuts, and 260 complaints of prohibited discrimination and discriminatory harassment in 2024-2025 (consistent with prior year). The Committee confirmed the MacMillan Theatre Remediation project (1,200 gsm, 440 nasm theatre space, funded by Provostial Funds), endorsed the University Ombudsperson's Report for July 1, 2024–June 30, 2025 showing a 50% decline in incivility cases since 2022-2023 and 75% decline in communications complaints, and defeated a motion to reconsider whether Governing Council should make a statement condemning antisemitism.
packages.gradRelevance: The $60 million revenue shortfall and enrolment review directly affect graduate funding and TA/RA budgets. Emergency research bridge funding from the contingency fund supports affected faculty, which may include graduate supervisors and their funding. The University Ombudsperson Report notes improvements in academic misconduct decision timelines (long delays 'almost completely eliminated' post-COVID) directly benefiting graduate students in the misconduct process. The 260 discrimination complaints (up from 258 prior year, 193 in 2022-2023) and antisemitism data (12.6% of complaints) reflect campus climate affecting all students including graduate students.
keyItems: President Melanie Woodin reported 100,000+ total students enrolled across three campuses with 17,000 new undergraduates (12,000 domestic, 5,000 international); total enrolment similar to prior year but domestic proportion increased

Administrative response notes policy reviews underway for Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment Policy, Code of Conduct on Academic Matters, Statement on Discrimination and Discriminatory Harassment; Ombudsperson's Office consulted on major policy reviews and Supportive Leaves Policy cases.

packages.title: Governing Council - May 13, 2026
packages.summary: On May 13, 2026, the Governing Council approved the rescission of an honorary degree awarded to Buffy Sainte-Marie on June 17, 2019, effective immediately, following a review against the University's mission and values. The President reported on $482 million raised in the Defy Gravity campaign (exceeding previous records), highlighted U of T's #1 ranking in Canada and top-17 global standing across all five disciplinary fields in QS rankings, and presented updates on antisemitism action plan implementation (over half of recommendations already integrated through existing initiatives), a Quercus cybersecurity incident with no evidence of broader system compromise, and bicentennial planning. The Council also received presentations on UTM's Skills Accelerator program (employer-led experiential learning), an Indigenous Substantiation Framework (designed to prevent identity fraud in hiring, admissions, scholarships, and research opportunities), and a new draft Policy on Human Rights Code Discrimination and Harassment featuring a centralized Human Rights Office, clearer timelines, and alternative resolution pathways.
packages.gradRelevance: Graduate students are directly affected by: the Defy Gravity campaign's stated support for 'graduate student funding' and 'endowed chairs' contributing to the $482 million record; the new discrimination and harassment policy creating a centralized Human Rights Office with clearer complaint processes and timelines; and the Indigenous Substantiation Framework applying to 'scholarships and awards,' 'research funding,' and 'employment and hiring pathways' (with implementation beginning in employment/procurement, extending to admissions and student awards). The cybersecurity incident affecting Quercus temporarily suspended access to the learning management system used by graduate students. The Skills Accelerator program and antisemitism initiatives have indirect relevance to graduate student experience.
keyItems: Rescission of Buffy Sainte-Marie's honorary degree (conferred June 17, 2019) approved effective immediately following review against University mission and values; petitioners and Standing Committee on Prohibited Discrimination thanked for work; public announcement to follow

Defy Gravity campaign surpassed targets with $482 million raised in record-setting year, exceeding previous institutional and national records; commitments support undergraduate scholarships, graduate student funding, endowed chairs, and strategic academic initiatives

U of T ranked #1 in Canada in QS World University Rankings by Subject and among top 17 globally across all five broad disciplinary fields, reflecting strength in teaching, research, and graduate employability

Quercus cybersecurity incident caused temporary system suspension with no evidence of compromise to other University systems; graduating students received final grades on schedule; full debrief to be provided to Governing Council at future meeting

Antisemitism Action Plan: over half of recommendations already implemented through existing initiatives including expanded culturally sensitive mental health supports (Telus Health for students, EFAP for faculty/staff), additional education in equity/diversity/inclusion offices, and protocols for prompt removal of antisemitic materials; some recommendations deemed infeasible due to conflict with provincial regulations, legal standards, academic freedom, or collective agreements

Indigenous Substantiation Framework approved and already used in 50+ cases on interim basis; applies prospectively to employment, hiring, Indigenous admissions streams, scholarships/awards, procurement, commissioned projects, research funding, and honorary distinctions; community-led process with Online portal under development; phased implementation beginning with employment and procurement, later extending to admissions and student awards

packages.title: Governing Council - March 26, 2026
packages.summary: On March 26, 2026, Governing Council approved a balanced operating budget of approximately $3.66 billion for 2026-27, with projected revenue growth of $39 million (down from $140 million the previous year) due to declining international undergraduate enrollment and adjusted intake targets. Council passed the Tuition Fee Schedule for Publicly Funded Programs 2026-27 (Schedule 1) and approved Long-Range Budget Guidelines. President Melanie Woodin reported extensively on antisemitism efforts, including implementation of a new Policy on Human Rights, Discrimination and Harassment (governance consideration upcoming), establishment of a centralized Human Rights Office, mandatory antisemitism training for senior leaders developed with the Lab for the Global Study of Antisemitism, proposed Code of Student Conduct revisions (expected fall governance consideration), and a new research study led by Professor Ron Levi to document Jewish student experiences. The Council did not place a proposed Antisemitism Action Plan developed by four independent governors on the agenda, as the Executive Committee determined it lacked formal consultation and extended beyond governance functions into operational decision-making. New Governing Council members elected for 2026-27 include graduate students Daniel Ebrahimpour and Victoria Liu.
packages.gradRelevance: Graduate student Daniel Ebrahimpour and Victoria Liu were elected as graduate student governors for 2026-27. The budget report noted that student financial aid accounts for approximately 11% of expenditures ($408 million annually), with over 11,000 students receiving more than $42 million in UTAPS funding, though the report does not specify graduate-specific allocations. The proposed Code of Student Conduct revisions and Policy on Human Rights, Discrimination and Harassment (both with governance consideration expected) will directly affect graduate students, as will the new centralized Human Rights Office for complaint processing. No specific graduate student tuition fee amounts, TA/RA stipends, or graduate-specific fee changes are detailed in this meeting report.
keyItems: Operating budget of $3.66 billion approved for 2026-27 as balanced budget; revenue growth projected at $39 million (down from $140 million prior year) due to first-time decline in international undergraduate tuition revenue and recalibrated enrollment targets
Tuition Fee Schedule for Publicly Funded Programs 2026-27 (Schedule 1) passed on motion; full fee details not provided in this excerpt
Long-Range Budget Guidelines approved on recommendation of both Academic Board and Business Board

Proposed Antisemitism Action Plan developed by four independent governors not placed on agenda by Executive Committee after 90-minute discussion, citing lack of formal consultation, defined terms of reference, and administrative review; motion would have directed administration to implement all recommendations, extending beyond governance functions

President announced four current antisemitism initiatives: (1) consultation on new Policy on Human Rights, Discrimination and Harassment with governance consideration in upcoming cycle; (2) establishment of centralized Human Rights Office; (3) rollout of antisemitism workshops developed with Lab for Global Study of Antisemitism at Anne Tanenbaum Centre, including mandatory training for senior leaders; (4) proposed Code of Student Conduct revisions with governance consideration expected in fall 2026

Assistant Director, Faith and Anti-Racism (Dr. Shari Goldberg) appointed; Lab for Study of Global Antisemitism established at Anne Tanenbaum Centre for Jewish Studies; annual antisemitism research conferences hosted

documents.summary: This report presents data on student financial support disbursed during the 2024-25 academic year, including breakdown of aid by source (University funds, government grants, scholarships), distribution across student populations, and assessment of affordability outcomes. Specific figures on total aid distributed, number of aided students, and average aid per student are provided for analysis of the University's financial support effectiveness.
documents.gradRelevance: Report details student financial aid allocation and effectiveness, relevant to graduate students who may access University financial support programs; however, graduate-specific aid figures and allocations are not disaggregated in this meeting summary.

packages.title: Governing Council - December 11, 2025
packages.summary: On December 11, 2025, the Governing Council approved revisions to the Policy on Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment (effective January 1, 2026), which strengthened language on interim measures, reprisals, and confidentiality limits, incorporating feedback from 25 online submissions and six email responses plus external legal review by Gillian Hnatiw. Council also approved amendments to the Policy and Procedures on Employment Conditions of Part-Time Faculty, effective January 1, 2026, allowing units to offer longer initial appointments and renewals of up to three years for approximately 430 part-time faculty members, with a new right to appeal unsuccessful continuing-appointment reviews. The President reported $1.7 billion in federal funding through the Canada Global Impact Plus Research Talent Initiative and announced 100 new postdoctoral fellowships funded by $24 million institutional investment. Council received the inaugural Annual Report on Complaints of Prohibited Discrimination and Discriminatory Harassment (covering July 1, 2024–June 30, 2025) and an update on replacing the Statement on Prohibited Discrimination with a new centralized Human Rights Office, with a new policy to be brought for approval in a later cycle.
packages.gradRelevance: The Canada Global Impact Plus Research Talent Initiative ($1.7 billion over 12 years) and the $24-million investment in 100 new postdoctoral fellowships directly support graduate student and postdoctoral training and recruitment. The revised Policy on Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment (effective January 1, 2026) applies to all students, including graduate students, with strengthened protections on confidentiality limits and interim measures. The part-time faculty policy changes do not directly affect graduate students. Graduate student experience with international recruitment may improve through the IRCC pilot program committing to process new PhD student study permits within 14 days. No tuition fee, stipend, or TA/RA compensation changes were discussed.
keyItems: Canada Global Impact Plus Research Talent Initiative: $1.7 billion federal investment over 12 years for recruitment of leading scholars, research infrastructure, and graduate/postdoctoral training; U of T preparing rapid recruitment coordinated with deans
100 new postdoctoral fellowships launched with $24-million institutional investment

Policy on Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment: revised version approved effective January 1, 2026, with clarified definitions, 12-month timeline for completing processes, expanded respondent supports, strengthened interim measures and reprisals language, and new paragraph 32 clarifying when confidentiality cannot be maintained (imminent safety risk or legally required external disclosure)

Part-Time Faculty Employment Conditions Policy: amendments approved effective January 1, 2026, allowing units to offer longer initial appointments and renewals of up to three years for ~430 part-time faculty; new right to appeal unsuccessful continuing-appointment reviews; no mandatory implementation or financial implications

Statement on Prohibited Discrimination review: 13 key recommendations produced; central recommendation to replace Statement with new policy and create centralized Human Rights Office; new policy drafting underway with further consultations planned

Annual Report on Complaints of Prohibited Discrimination and Discriminatory Harassment: inaugural report covering July 1, 2024–June 30, 2025, to be submitted to MCURES by January 31, 2026; establishes baseline for future trend measurement

documents.title: Policy on Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment (Revised, Approved December 11, 2025)
documents.url: Not provided in source document list
documents.summary: The revised Policy on Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment incorporates 25 online submissions and six email responses, plus external legal review by Gillian Hnatiw. Policy becomes effective January 1, 2026.

Future reports will consider including informal concerns, training activities, and early resolution efforts.
