documents.title: Review of the Statement on Prohibited Discrimination and Discriminatory Harassment
documents.url: Not provided in source document list
documents.summary: Review commissioned in early 2025 by Provost and Vice-President Kelly Hannah-Moffat, co-chaired by Brenda Cossman (Faculty of Law) and Caroline Rabbat (former Faculty of Arts and Science). Involved broad engagement with students, faculty, librarians, and staff across all three campuses. Produced 13 key recommendations: replace existing Statement with new policy integrating relevant content from Statement on Human Rights (2012), create centralized Human Rights Office as single point of entry for complaints (modeled on sexual violence support structure), clarify confused complaint pathways particularly for students, improve communication and training, and develop systems to track complaints and meet legislative reporting requirements. Policy drafting underway with further consultations planned.
documents.gradRelevance: Directly affects graduate students as proposed Human Rights Office will serve as centralized entry point for discrimination and harassment complaints affecting students; improved pathways and respondent supports will benefit graduate students involved in complaints.

documents.title: International Students Presentation (December 11, 2025)
documents.url: Not provided in source document list
documents.summary: Associate Vice-Provost Mariana Mota Prado reported U of T's three-year average study permit approval rate of 88% vs. Canadian sector average 54%, attributed to 15 certified immigration advisors and high caliber of recruits. Federal reduction of national study permit target from 305,000 to 155,000 unlikely to impact U of T, as only 80,000–100,000 total new permits issued across Canada last year; U of T used all Provincial Attestation Letters (PALs) and received additional through redistribution. IRCC committed to processing study permits for new PhD students within 14 days in pilot program expected to expand. Two international students (Janhavi Pasricha, Political Science/Criminology third-year; Doyin Adeoya, Cell and Systems Biology fourth-year) described support from pre-orientation networks, residence life, Laidlaw Scholars Program, Centre for International Experience (CIE), and CIE immigration advising and ambassador roles.
documents.gradRelevance: Directly benefits graduate students: 14-day IRCC pilot for PhD study permit processing improves international graduate student recruitment and onboarding; 88% approval rate reflects strength of U of T's international graduate recruitment; 15 certified immigration advisors provide support through entire student lifecycle including postdoctoral work permit applications.

documents.title: University of Toronto Press: Overview and 2026 Impact Report (December 11, 2025)
documents.url: Not provided in source document list
documents.summary: Jessica Mosher, President/Publisher/CEO, reported UTP operates as independent not-for-profit affiliated with U of T. Since 2015, expanded science publishing from 176 to 250 books annually and journals from 37 four years ago to 80. 15% of authors from U of T, 15% from other Canadian institutions, 70% international. Strong investment in indexing and searchability across major research databases contributing to global citations and institutional rankings. Secured competitive federal export grant for expanded Asia and South America partnerships. Operates all four U of T bookstores. Incorporated as not-for-profit in 1991 with separate employees, unions, warehouse, and retail operations; Board ~50% U of T members; does not receive University financial support. Celebrating 125th anniversary with year-long exhibit at Robarts Library.
documents.gradRelevance: UTP provides publishing outlet for U of T research, including graduate student research; expansion to 80 journals and 250 books annually increases dissemination opportunities for graduate scholars; global reach (70% international authors) and strong indexing enhance visibility and impact of graduate research.

packages.title: Governing Council - November 06, 2025
packages.summary: The November 6, 2025 Governing Council meeting will consider industry partnerships, AI Task Force implementation, a 357-case Ombudsperson report, revisions to sexual violence policy, and routine approvals. Professor Leah Cowen will present U of T's work with over 600 industry partners generating $40M in funding through the BlueDoor initiative. Professor Susan McCahan will present the AI Task Force's June 2025 recommendations to establish AI literacy, an AI Adoption Table, secure AI infrastructure (an "AI kitchen"), and AI response teams. The Ombudsperson's office received 357 requests for assistance in 2024-25, with increases in graduate student cases (88, up from 61), international student fee exemption complaints (9), and student fee/funding concerns. Professor Sandy Welsh will present proposed revisions to the Policy on Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment based on community consultations and external legal review by Gillian Hnatiw, including new definitions, expanded Vice-Provost Students authority, added timelines, and facilitated resolution processes. Professor Simone Vigod was appointed Chair of Psychiatry at Temerty Faculty of Medicine (Sept 1, 2025–Aug 31, 2030) under Summer Executive Authority.
packages.gradRelevance: Graduate students are directly affected by four agenda items. The AI Task Force recommendations include language on "training for responsible AI use in graduate research" and establish infrastructure supporting graduate research. The Ombudsperson report highlights 88 graduate student requests for assistance (up from 61 in 2023-24), with the most frequent concerns being graduate supervision and academic progress (14 cases), and financial aid/funding/awards (12 cases); the Office notes the School of Graduate Studies provides a formal complaint resolution process for funding disputes. The Sexual Violence Policy revisions add trauma-informed training requirements and support for Respondents, which apply to graduate students and TAs. Industry partnerships offer work-integrated learning and funding opportunities for graduate researchers, with $40M in partner funding documented.
keyItems: Industry Partnerships presentation: U of T has partnerships with over 600 companies across 13 countries since 2020; BlueDoor initiative tracked 417 interactions, 350+ companies, 20 new multinational partnerships, 40 projects funded, $40M in partner funding in recent period; transformational partnerships strategy shifts from one-time collaborations to 5+ year agreements with framework agreements setting IP and licensing terms.

AI Task Force June 2025 report delivered; six working groups examined AI impact on teaching/learning, research, student services, administration, operations, and technology; cross-cutting recommendations include: (1) Build AI expertise through foundational literacy and professional development; (2) Create AI Adoption Table for decision-making and guidelines; (3) Develop AI infrastructure including secure 'AI kitchen' with data access and curated vetted tools; (4) Establish AI response teams for implementation support.

Ombudsperson 357 new requests for assistance July 1, 2024–June 30, 2025; graduate students up to 88 cases (from 61 previous year), with 77 from St. George, 8 from UTM, 3 from UTSC; top grad student concerns: supervision/academic progress (14 cases), financial aid/funding/awards (12 cases); international students increased to 64 cases with fee exemption complaints doubling to 9 cases; student fees/financial aid cases rose to 27 and 25 respectively.

Policy on Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment 2025 review conducted January–August 2025 with 200+ community members providing input across 45 consultation hours; external review by Gillian Hnatiw completed August 21, 2025; proposed revisions add definitions (gender-based violence, intimate partner violence, tech-facilitated sexual violence, coercive control), expand Vice-Provost Students decision-making authority to impose sanctions where expulsion/suspension not warranted, add timelines throughout, rename non-adjudicative resolution to facilitated resolution with expanded details.

Sexual Violence Policy revisions: new section on Safety, Reprisals, Interim Conditions (paras 48-54); clarified Complainant preference for investigation or facilitated resolution (para 79); expanded investigator requirements for trauma-informed experience; clarified that Complainant can withdraw from process; added support obligations even if Complainant declines to participate; added protocols for intimate image and document retention.

AI Task Force working group on Research recommends: streamline research administration through AI tools, develop protocols ensuring AI maintains research integrity, enhance cross-disciplinary collaboration, provide training for responsible AI use in graduate research; proposed 'AI kitchen' to provide secure environments for AI projects with appropriate data access.

documents.title: Presentation: Industry Partnerships
documents.summary: Presentation by Professor Leah Cowen (VP Research & Innovation) and Professor Joseph Wong (VP International) documenting U of T's research partnerships across community organizations, government, universities, and private companies. Data shows 600+ industry partners since 2020 across 13 countries; BlueDoor collective impact initiative tracked 417 interactions, 350+ companies, 20 new multinational partnerships, 40 projects funded, $40M partner funding. Highlights include transformational partnerships with BASF (self-driving labs), Nissan (EV/grid research), Siemens Energy (sustainable energy), Vale Energy Transition Metals (mining), and Konica Minolta (5-year renewal). Document explains partnership benefits: licensing, patents, income generation, researcher prominence, access to specialized resources, and work-integrated learning for students.
documents.gradRelevance: Graduate students benefit from work-integrated learning opportunities through industry partnerships, access to specialized equipment and facilities, and potential funding/in-kind contributions to research projects. Industry partnerships advance research knowledge and facilitate translation of discoveries, directly supporting graduate research expansion and career preparation.

documents.title: Presentation: Update on the AI Task Force Recommendations
documents.summary: Report from AI Task Force established Spring 2024, with final recommendations delivered June 2025. Six working groups examined AI impact on teaching/learning, research, student services, administration, operations, and technology. Key working group recommendations: Teaching & Learning focus on assessment redesign, AI literacy, tutoring standards, pedagogical communities; Research focus on streamlined administration, research integrity protocols, cross-disciplinary collaboration, training for responsible AI use in graduate research; Student Services focus on AI for administrative tasks, career exploration tools, forecasting, equitable access; Operations focus on planning/forecasting, document management, project management. Cross-cutting recommendations: (1) Build AI expertise through foundational literacy and professional development; (2) Create AI Adoption Table for guidelines and risk assessment; (3) Develop AI infrastructure including 'AI kitchen' with secure environments and vetted tools; (4) Establish AI response teams. Report reaffirms commitment to human expertise, academic freedom, institutional values.
documents.gradRelevance: Graduate students directly addressed: Research working group recommends training for responsible AI use in graduate research and protocols ensuring research integrity; Student Services recommendations include accessible AI tools and equitable access; AI infrastructure development supports graduate research projects with secure environments and data access.

documents.title: Report of the University Ombudsperson for the period July 1, 2024 to June 30, 2025 and Administrative Response
documents.summary: Office of Ombudsperson received 357 new requests for assistance July 1, 2024–June 30, 2025. Bruce Kidd's fourth and final report before Faye Mishna's July 1, 2025 appointment. Notable trends: international students up to 64 cases (50 in 2023-24), with fee exemption complaints doubling to 9 from 4; graduate students up to 88 from 61; student fee-related cases 27 (from 21); financial aid/funding/awards cases 25 (from 15); incivility cases declined to 27 (from 46). Recommendations acknowledged: transfer credit policy updated May 2024; petitions policy working group active; international student fee exemption decisions will have enhanced communication guidance. Office structure: Ombudsperson plus Ombuds Officers on each campus (St. George, UTM, UTSC) plus support staff. Office provides confidential informal resolution, impartial investigations, policy recommendations.
documents.gradRelevance: Graduate student cases increased 44% to 88 (77 St. George, 8 UTM, 3 UTSC). Primary concerns: graduation supervision and academic progress (14 cases, up from 8); financial aid/funding/awards (12 cases). Office notes School of Graduate Studies provides formal complaint resolution process for funding disputes. Ombudsperson highlights CGMS (Centre for Graduate Mentorship & Supervision) training and support efforts.

documents.title: Proposed Revisions to the Policy on Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment
documents.summary: 2025 review of Policy on Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment (last approved December 15, 2022, effective January 1, 2023) conducted January–August 2025. Two-part review: (1) community consultations January 15–March 28, 2025 chaired by Professor Faye Mishna with 200+ participants across 45 consultation hours, including student societies, UTFA, union leadership, academic administrators, staff, legal counsel, ombudsperson, equity directors; (2) external legal review by Gillian Hnatiw (Toronto-based lawyer, expert in gender-based violence, administrative law) completed August 21, 2025. Consultation input themes: (1) Clarity—add definitions, strengthen commitments, online navigation; (2) Process improvements—broaden VP Students authority, enhance Respondent supports, navigation guidance; (3) Timelines—provide process duration information; (4) Scope—clarify intimate partner violence coverage; (5) Education—mandatory training for key roles. Proposed revisions: new definitions (gender-based violence, intimate partner violence, tech-facilitated sexual violence, coercive control, procedural fairness, reprisal, gender expression/identity); renamed Statement of Commitments & Acknowledgements; new section V on Safety, Reprisals, Interim Conditions; expanded VP Students decision-making authority; timelines throughout; renamed facilitated resolution (previously non-adjudicative); mandatory trauma-informed training requirement (para 42); support for intimate partner violence (para 37); emergency procedures (para 72); intimate image/document retention protocols (para 26).
documents.gradRelevance: Graduate students and teaching assistants affected by expanded definition of sexual violence (now includes tech-facilitated and intimate partner violence), mandatory trauma-informed training for staff who receive disclosures, clarified Respondent supports (para 41), expanded interim measures authority, and formalized procedures. Policy applies to all Members of University Community including graduate students.

documents.title: Presentation: 2025 Review of the Policy on Sexual Violence & Sexual Harassment
documents.summary: Presentation by Professor Sandy Welsh (Vice-Provost, Students) summarizing 2025 Policy review process and proposed revisions. Community consultation input organized into five themes: (1) Clarity & Transparency—add definitions, strengthen Statement of Commitment, online navigation; (2) Process & Communication Improvements—online guides, broaden VP Students authority, non-adjudicative process details; (3) Timelines—add throughout policy; (4) Scope—clarify authority over intimate partner violence, anonymous reports, third-party reports; (5) Education, Training & Awareness—mandatory training, Respondent education programs. External expert (Gillian Hnatiw) recommendations: add/update definitions (gender-based violence, intimate partner violence, tech-facilitated violence, coercive control, consent, non-adjudicative resolution); update Statement of Commitment emphasizing Indigenous impact and myths; add new reporting avenues; rename/broaden non-adjudicative resolution; add timelines; broaden VP Students authority. Proposed revisions organized by section: Definitions (new terms added); Statement of Commitments (renamed, reordered, added trauma-informed commitment and accommodations language); Application and Scope (formalized former member and intimate partner violence practices); Confidentiality (moved up, clarified limitations, added intimate image protocols); Principles (added parallel processes and resolution timeline language); Education/Support (added intimate partner violence support, mandatory trauma training); Safety/Reprisals (new section with interim measures and reprisal response); Reporting (clarified process, components, emergency procedures, Complainant preference); Investigation (added trauma-informed requirement, timelines); Decision-making (expanded VP Students authority); Facilitated Resolution (renamed, expanded content). Other planned responses: online navigation tools, internal process review, trauma-informed navigation support.
documents.gradRelevance: Revisions directly affect graduate students and TAs as Respondents: expanded VP Students decision-making authority for sanctions; clarified Respondent supports (para 41); mandatory trauma-informed training for staff; expanded definition of sexual violence; formalized interim measures and safety planning; clarified appeal processes.

documents.title: Report on Approvals Under Summer Executive Authority
documents.summary: Report on actions taken under Summer Executive Authority delegation approved June 24, 2025 and effective through September 2, 2025. Under this authority, the President could approve appointments, curriculum changes, and urgent decisions not permitting deferral. All actions required Chair approval prior to implementation and reporting for information. One appointment reported: Professor Simone Vigod appointed as Chair of the Department of Psychiatry at the Temerty Faculty of Medicine, effective September 1, 2025 to August 31, 2030.

packages.title: Governing Council - November 06, 2025
packages.summary: At its November 6, 2025 meeting, the Governing Council heard from President Melanie Woodin on campus return-to-office plans, federal research funding ($1.65 billion talent attraction package), and the controversial October 7 UTMSU event at UTM, which the University decided not to cancel despite concerns about antisemitism. The Council received presentations on the University's approximately 300 active industry partnerships (7.34% of research funding) and the AI Task Force recommendations, including implementation of AI literacy resources and Cogniti tutoring tools across 90+ courses. The Ombudsperson's report documented 357 cases (9% increase) with declining incivility complaints, and Professor Simone Vigod was appointed Chair of Psychiatry at Temerty Faculty of Medicine through summer executive authority. The University enrolled approximately 17,000 new students this fall (12,000 domestic, 5,000 international) and faces a $60 million revenue shortfall (1.6% below plan), requiring recalibrated enrolment targets.
packages.gradRelevance: Graduate students are affected by the federal government's exemption of graduate students from Provincial Attestation Letter (PAL) requirements, supporting international grad recruitment. The AI Task Force recommendations include resources for the School of Graduate Studies and Cogniti AI tutors available in 90+ courses. The University's emergency bridging research funding for researchers losing U.S. grants mid-project may support graduate student-dependent research. International student visa policy changes will impact graduate student recruitment and domestic/international balance. The presentation on industry partnerships noted experiential learning opportunities and employer connections for students, though no graduate-specific figures were provided.
keyItems: President Woodin announced plans for critical mass of administrative staff on campus five days per week while honouring alternative work arrangements; shift driven by institutional priority to support students and research.

Federal budget allocated $1.65 billion for talent attraction package including accelerated research chairs, doctoral student recruitment, and post-doctoral fellow support; graduate students exempted from Provincial Attestation Letter (PAL) requirements following University advocacy.
Universities eligible for federal Build Community Strong Fund ($51 billion over 10 years) for infrastructure including Temerty Building renovation and Schwartz Reisman Innovation Campus Phase II.

University enrolled approximately 17,000 new undergraduate students (12,000 domestic, 5,000 international); current revenue forecast $60 million (1.6%) below plan requiring future enrolment target recalibration under Provost leadership.

UTMSU event on October 7, 2025 titled 'Honouring Our Martyrs' proceeded despite University concerns about perceived celebration of October 7, 2023 violence; no policy violation or credible security threat justified cancellation under University freedom of speech principles.
University provided emergency one-year bridging funding for researchers losing U.S. funding mid-project as temporary stop-gap measure while finding alternative sources.

documents.title: Report on Approvals Under Summer Executive Authority
documents.summary: This report documents actions taken by the President under delegated summer authority (effective until September 2, 2025) approved by the Governing Council on June 24, 2025. The delegation permitted appointments, curriculum changes, and urgent decisions that could not wait for regular Council meetings, all requiring prior approval from the Chair of the Governing Council.
documents.gradRelevance: The appointment of Professor Simone Vigod as Chair of Psychiatry affects graduate students in that department who will work under her leadership; psychiatry often involves graduate research and supervised training.

packages.title: Planning and Budget Committee - May 06, 2026
packages.summary: On May 6, 2026, the Planning and Budget Committee approved three major capital projects in principle: the Faculty of Dentistry Clinic 2 Renewal (2,945 square metres of renovated space, completion April 2028, funded by Faculty reserves, donations, and central matching); the Department of Computer Science relocation to Schwartz Reisman Innovation Campus West (3,486 gross square metres, 1,588 net assignable square metres, construction commencing July 2026, occupancy August 2027, funded by Faculty of Arts & Science Future Major Capital Projects Reserves); and the Build More Housing Initiative (approximately 1,200 new student residence beds across 40-56 Harbord Street and 167-171 College Street, 37,165 square metres gross floor area, funded through a joint venture partnership). The Committee also approved the St. George Campus Plan 2040 in principle and recommended the Academic Wood Tower revised project cost and funding. All financial details for projects 12-16 (in camera items) were approved on motions dated April 27-29, 2026.
packages.gradRelevance: The Build More Housing Initiative directly addresses graduate student housing demand, with Professor Mabury emphasizing 'particular pressure for graduate housing and upper-year students, with demand far exceeding available supply' from a population over 100,000 served by only approximately 11,000 existing beds. The proposed 1,200 new beds across Harbord Street and College Street sites will include a mix of unit types informed by demand analysis. The Department of Computer Science project supports graduate student research space with 250 square metres dedicated to graduate student areas and 328 square metres of research laboratories where graduate researchers conduct AI work. The Dentistry Clinic 2 project has minimal direct graduate relevance but supports clinical training infrastructure.
keyItems: Faculty of Dentistry Clinic 2 Renewal approved: 2,945 sm renovated space (3,168 sm total project scope), 1,128 nasm program space; completion April 2028; includes AODA-compliant elevator and stair, improved patient entrance, and enclosed operatories replacing open spaces; serves 60,000-70,000 patient visits annually; funded by Faculty Major Capital Projects Reserves, Future Faculty Reserves, Clinic 2 Capital Donations, Central University Matching, fundraising and financing

Department of Computer Science at Schwartz Reisman Innovation Campus West approved: 3,486 gsm (1,588 nasm) interior renovation on third and fourth floors; includes 250 sm graduate student space, 328 sm research labs, 473 sm academic offices; construction July 2026, occupancy August 2027; funded by Faculty of Arts & Science Future Major Capital Projects Reserves; supports AI research collaboration with Vector Institute

Build More Housing Initiative approved: approximately 1,200 new student residence beds planned for 40-56 Harbord Street and 167-171 College Street; 37,165 sm gross floor area of residence and amenity space (40,684 sm total construction area); funded entirely by joint venture partnership with private sector; addresses critical shortage where 100,000+ students served by only ~11,000 existing beds, with particular pressure on graduate and upper-year housing

Housing Initiative design informed by demand analysis supporting semi-private accommodations and shared facilities for community-building; Janice Johnson (Executive Director, Student Housing & Residence Experience) confirmed extensive research on student preferences; ongoing community consultation with Grange Community Association and local councillor's office; previous University-led development on Harbord site cancelled due to market costs exceeding budget

Dentistry Clinic 2 renovation achieves 15% more usable space through reconfiguration; nominal capacity slightly reduced but quality and safety significantly improved; new enclosed operatories meet current air exchange standards replacing open spaces; includes dedicated spaces for special needs patients not previously available; project cost details approved in camera via memorandum dated April 27, 2026

Computer Science project designed to co-locate AI research groups in purpose-designed environment fostering collaboration within Department and with broader AI community; municipal approvals in progress; Department confirmed enrolment exceeds previous peaks despite sector fluctuations; Professor Eyal de Lara emphasized AI emergence supporting continued demand for expertise; ongoing infrastructure and networking costs borne by Department

documents.title: Faculty of Dentistry Clinic 2 Renewal - Presentation
documents.summary: Technical presentation of Clinic 2 Renewal project showing floor plans and design scope. Project involves renovation of 2,945 square metres of existing space across mezzanine and two floors of Dentistry Building at 124 Edward Street. Includes implementation of Clinic Master Plan phases 7b, 4, and vertical connection improvements; addition of AODA-compliant passenger elevator and egress stair at Elm Street patient entrance; renovation of patient reception areas; and conversion of open operatory spaces to enclosed, individually controlled operatories meeting current air exchange standards. Floor plan documents show detailed room configurations including new patient lobby/reception, multi-functional training room, pediatrics clinic, multiple operatories, support labs (plaster, sterilizer), storage, and staff facilities on levels 1-2.
documents.gradRelevance: Graduate dentistry students use Clinic 2 spaces as primary training ground for clinical work; renovation improves pedagogical environment with modernized clinic spaces, new support facilities, and improved connections; enclosed operatories meet current standards while dedicated special needs patient spaces enable diverse clinical training scenarios not previously available.

documents.title: Department of Computer Science at Schwartz Reisman Innovation Campus West - Presentation
documents.summary: Presentation outlining Department of Computer Science relocation to Schwartz Reisman Innovation Campus (SRIC) West, third and fourth floors. Space program totals 1,588 net assignable square metres (3,486 gross) comprising: Research Laboratories & Support Space 328 sm, Academic Offices 473 sm, Research Office/Project Space 17 sm, Graduate Student Space 250 sm, Office Support Space 520 sm. Project purpose is to co-locate leading AI research groups in purpose-designed environment fostering collaboration within Department and with Vector Institute and broader AI community. Interior renovation with municipal approvals in progress; construction to commence July 2026 with occupancy targeted August 2027. Includes conference rooms and social lounge spaces for community-building and research collaboration.
documents.gradRelevance: Project allocates 250 square metres dedicated to graduate student space and 328 square metres of research laboratories and support space where graduate researchers in AI conduct work. Co-location in SRIC West facilitates collaboration between graduate students and faculty, and connection to Vector Institute ecosystem for AI research. Modern research lab and support facilities enhance graduate research environment.

documents.title: Build More Housing Initiative Project Development Report - On-Campus Housing
documents.summary: Project Development Report for Build More Housing Initiative presenting on-campus student residence projects at 40-56 Harbord Street and 167-171 College Street. Initial phase targets approximately 1,200 new beds across sites totaling 37,165 square metres gross floor area (40,684 square metres total construction area). Project funded entirely through joint venture partnership with private sector following competitive procurement process in 2025. Design incorporates mix of unit types informed by extensive demand analysis; emphasizes semi-private accommodations and shared facilities supporting community-building and academic success. Meets University long-term design standards for sustainability, durability, adaptability, and high energy efficiency. University undertook significant research on student preferences and engaged experienced residence development partners. Community consultation ongoing with Grange Community Association, local councillor's office, and dedicated community liaison on Harbord site.
documents.gradRelevance: Critical for graduate housing: Committee noted 'particular pressure for graduate housing and upper-year students, with demand far exceeding available supply' from student population over 100,000 served by only approximately 11,000 existing beds. Proposed 1,200 new beds directly address graduate housing shortage. Previous University-led Harbord development cancelled due to unviable costs; joint venture partnership model aims to improve financial feasibility and delivery. Design informed by demand analysis on student preferences for accommodation types.

packages.title: Planning and Budget Committee - March 25, 2026
packages.summary: On March 25, 2026, the Planning and Budget Committee received the Annual Report: Accessibility for Ontarians with Disabilities Act (AODA), 2025-2026 for information, presented by Jodie Glean-Mitchell, Executive Director of EDI, under sponsor Kelly Hannah-Moffat, Vice-President of People Strategy, Equity & Culture. The report, produced exclusively in digital format, highlights five thematic areas: supporting accessible teaching and learning, strengthening accessibility foundations, deepening accessibility practices, creating accessible physical spaces, and centring lived experience of disability. Key institutional initiatives include the Faculty of Information's Quercius Guidebook for neurodiversity, the Centre for Teaching Support & Innovation's Teaching with Universal Design for Learning microsite, the Jackman Faculty of Law's tactile stairs and handrail upgrades, and the creation of an Accessibility Advisory Committee as a key deliverable of the 2025-2030 Multi-Year Accessibility Plan. The report has no financial implications and will proceed to the University Affairs Board on April 23, 2026.
packages.gradRelevance: The AODA report directly affects graduate students through accessible teaching and learning initiatives, including the Faculty of Information's neurodiversity guidebook and the universal design for learning resources that serve graduate cohorts. Enhanced accessibility practices at U of T Mississauga's Career Centre provide support for graduate students with disabilities seeking career services. The creation of the Accessibility Advisory Committee and strengthened consultation processes on accessibility across campuses will shape the accessibility of graduate student spaces, services, and programs going forward.
keyItems: Annual Report: Accessibility for Ontarians with Disabilities Act (AODA), 2025-2026 presented for information only; report produced exclusively in digital format to satisfy AODA 2005 requirements and demonstrate institutional compliance with accessibility plan
Faculty of Information provided Quercius Guidebook for instructors on designing with neurodiversity, specifically for autistic students, directly supporting accessible graduate-level instruction

Centre for Teaching Support & Innovation launched 'Teaching with Universal Design for Learning at U of T' microsite as centralized hub for accessible and inclusive teaching practices across all campuses
U of T Mississauga enhanced referral process between Accessibility Services and Career Centre to ensure students with disabilities (including graduate students) receive comprehensive career support
Jackman Faculty of Law completed physical accessibility upgrades including tactile stairs indicators, handrail upgrades, improved elevator accessibility, wayfinding, and signage

Vice-Provost, Strategic Enrolment Management and University Registrar's Office updated 2012 classroom standards and design criteria using Pedagogy-Space-Technology (PST) Framework for accessible learning spaces

documents.title: Annual Report: Accessibility for Ontarians with Disabilities Act (AODA), 2025
documents.summary: The AODA 2025 report, produced exclusively in digital format, comprehensively documents U of T's accessibility initiatives across five thematic areas: supporting accessible teaching and learning, strengthening accessibility foundations, deepening accessibility practices, creating accessible physical spaces and structures, and centring lived experience of disability. The report situates local efforts within institutional context and identifies priorities for the 2025-2030 Multi-Year Accessibility Plan, including creation of an Accessibility Advisory Committee and encouragement of lived experience consultations across all faculties and divisions.
documents.gradRelevance: Graduate students benefit directly from accessible teaching initiatives (Quercius Guidebook for neurodiversity, Universal Design for Learning resources), improved physical accessibility in classrooms and facilities, and enhanced support services through Accessibility Services referral processes. The creation of the Accessibility Advisory Committee will shape future accessibility of graduate student programs, spaces, and services.

documents.title: Presentation: Accessibility for Ontarians with Disabilities Act (AODA) Report 2025
documents.summary: The presentation slides outline the AODA report's five key thematic areas with specific institutional examples: Faculty of Information's Quercius Guidebook for neurodivergent/autistic students; Centre for Teaching Support & Innovation's Teaching with UDL microsite; U of T Mississauga's enhanced Accessibility Services–Career Centre referral process; physical accessibility upgrades at Jackman Faculty of Law including tactile stairs and handrails; and updated classroom standards using the PST Framework. The presentation emphasizes consultation, collaboration, and capacity-building through workshops on accessibility practices, neurodiversity-affirming workplaces, and non-evident disabilities, with the 2025–2030 MYAP and Accessibility Advisory Committee launch planned for 2026 and beyond.
documents.gradRelevance: Graduate students directly benefit from accessible teaching resources, the UDL microsite, career support enhancements, improved physical spaces in law buildings, and neurodiversity-affirming workplace guidance. These initiatives directly enhance the graduate student experience, particularly for those with disabilities or neurodivergence.

packages.title: Planning and Budget Committee - March 25, 2026
packages.summary: The Planning and Budget Committee met on March 25, 2026, to receive the 2025-2026 AODA Annual Report from Jodie Glean (Executive Director, Equity, Diversity & Inclusion), which outlined accessibility initiatives including supports for neurodiverse learners, expanded disability consultations, campus infrastructure improvements, and the planned 2025-2030 Multi-Year Accessibility Plan with a new Accessibility Advisory Committee. The Committee approved the consent agenda and Report Number 219 from the February 11, 2026 meeting. Discussion clarified that neurodiversity encompasses variations in brain functionality and information processing (with or without formal diagnoses), that AODA work is proceeding within existing resources with ongoing divisional and central-level funding discussions, and that accessibility prioritization reflects a consultative balance of needs and resource constraints. The Committee also received (in camera) the Annual Report on Information Security and Protection of Digital Assets, affirming support for secure AI adoption, strengthened identity and access management, and enhanced network protections. The next meeting is scheduled for May 6, 2026.
packages.gradRelevance: The AODA presentation directly affects graduate students through expanded supports for students with disabilities and neurodiverse learners in teaching and learning environments; however, no specific funding amounts, fee changes, or TA/RA stipend impacts were discussed at this meeting. The Committee noted AODA work is proceeding within existing resources with ongoing funding discussions at divisional and central levels, but no dedicated graduate-level allocations or union-related labour matters were addressed. The information security report (reviewed in camera) has indirect relevance to graduate student data protection and AI tool usage guidelines but contains no specific graduate student funding or service changes.
keyItems: AODA 2025-2026 Annual Report presented by Jodie Glean highlighting four key themes: supporting accessible teaching and learning (including neurodiverse learner resources and expanded disability supports), deepening accessibility practices through community engagement, advancing accessible physical spaces and infrastructure, and centring lived experience of disability in decision-making.

Neurodiversity defined as an umbrella concept capturing variations in brain functionalities and different ways people process information and learn; clarified that demographic data is self-identification-based and does not isolate neurodiversity as a distinct category.
2025-2030 Multi-Year Accessibility Plan identified as key roadmap with planned launch of Accessibility Advisory Committee and implementation mechanisms for continued progress.

AODA initiatives currently proceeding within existing resources with ongoing discussions at divisional and central levels regarding governance structures and resource requirements; no new dedicated funding approved.
Accessibility of physical spaces continues to be addressed through collaboration with facilities and planning units, with accessibility integrated into broader campus planning efforts.

Prioritization of accessibility projects determined through consultative process involving multiple portfolios, reflecting complex balance of needs, resource constraints, and ongoing planning considerations.
