The 2025-2030 Multi-Year Accessibility Plan may establish ongoing accessibility infrastructure affecting graduate programs and services, though no specific funding allocations or graduate-level stipend/fee impacts were announced.

packages.title: Planning and Budget Committee - February 11, 2026
packages.summary: The Planning and Budget Committee on February 11, 2026 approved a $3.66 billion balanced budget for 2026-27 with only 1.1% revenue growth—$101 million less than previously projected due to recalibrated international enrolment targets. The Committee approved the Budget Report 2026-27 and Long Range Budget Guidelines 2026-27 to 2030-31, noting that while the University remains financially strong with significant reserves and a strong credit rating, it faces constrained revenue and significant expense pressures requiring cost containment measures including a 5% cut to shared services, workforce planning adjustments, and slowed hiring. The Committee also approved in principle the Temerty Building (MSB West Wing Redevelopment) interim project scope with funding from Temerty Faculty of Medicine redevelopment funds, Faculty of Arts & Science capital reserves, and institutional funding, as well as Project LEAP 2.0 infrastructure and energy renewal initiative to support climate positive goals by 2050. Additionally, the Faculty of Applied Science & Engineering's Strategic Academic Plan 2026-2031 was received for information.
packages.gradRelevance: Graduate students are directly affected by several budget items: minimum $40,000 base funding commitment for doctoral students is maintained; Teaching Assistants receive $112 million in 2026-27 compensation; the budget includes increases to UTAPS (University of Toronto Advanced Planning for Students) funding and continued donor-supported scholarship programs to support student affordability; international graduate students comprise 26% of the graduate population and will be impacted by recalibrated international enrolment plans reducing intake by 1,083 students; and the Build More Housing initiative plans approximately 1,200 new student beds on the St. George campus, with negotiations underway for additional off-campus graduate housing to address housing demand.
keyItems: Budget Report 2026-27 and Long Range Budget Guidelines 2026-27 to 2030-31 approved: $3.66 billion balanced operating budget with only $39 million growth (1.1% revenue growth rate), down $101 million from previously projected due to recalibrated international enrolment targets; revenue growth projected at 3% annually starting 2027-28

Cost containment measures implemented across divisions: 5% base expense reduction in shared services, planned 2% reduction in staff complement, slowed faculty hiring, reduced discretionary spending and capital renewal allocations

Graduate funding: minimum $40,000 base funding commitment for doctoral students maintained; Teaching Assistants budget $112 million; increases to UTAPS student financial support program; $408 million total financial aid budget for 2026-27 (+73% increase noted in comparative context)

International enrolment adjustments: undergraduate international intake reduced by 1,083 students to target; international graduate students comprise 26% of total graduate population; international scholarship programs budget $88 million for 2026-27

Student housing: Build More Housing initiative proposed to develop approximately 1,200 new student beds on St. George campus using leveraged private-sector expertise; negotiations underway for additional off-campus graduate housing; 7,900 beds at St. George, 1,500 at UTSC, 10,800 at UTM currently available

Temerty Building (MSB West Wing Redevelopment) interim project scope approved in principle: integrated research, teaching and institutional facility combining Temerty Faculty of Medicine and Faculty of Arts & Science; adds 8,293 net new assignable square metres; funded by Temerty MSB Redevelopment Fund, Faculty of Arts & Science Major Capital Projects Reserve Fund, and institutional/Facilities & Services interim funding; early works including electrical duct bank procurement, MSB West Wing interior abatement and demolition planned for June-August 2026; anticipated occupancy July 2031

documents.title: Budget 2026-27 and Long-Range Budget Guidelines 2026-27 to 2030-31 Presentation
documents.summary: Comprehensive budget presentation detailing the $3.66 billion balanced operating budget for 2026-27 with $39 million growth but $101 million less than previously planned due to recalibrated international enrolment. Documents revenue sources (65% tuition/fees, 20% operating grants, 15% other), expenditures (65% faculty/staff compensation, 11% student aid, 6% capital/equipment), and cost containment measures including 5% shared services cuts and 2% staff complement reductions. Details domestic tuition freeze, 3% annual growth assumptions starting 2027-28, and Strategic Mandate Agreement 4 performance metrics with 25% of funding tied to institutional metrics growing to 40% by year 5. Highlights priority investments in talent recruitment, student aid (UTAPS program increases), housing expansion (Build More Housing initiative for up to 5,500 additional beds with 1,200 on-campus beds planned), research infrastructure, and digital strategy. Shows international undergraduate enrolment reduction of 1,083 students to target with 28.6% planned international share of undergraduate enrolment in 2025-26. Provides detailed information on pension contingency planning, deferred maintenance ($1.5 billion liability with $350 million three-year spend plan), and capital projects totalling $4.4 billion over five years.
documents.gradRelevance: Critical for graduate students: $112 million Teaching Assistant compensation, minimum $40,000 base funding for doctoral students, 26% of graduate students are international, $408 million total 2026-27 financial aid budget, UTAPS program increases, Build More Housing initiative addressing student housing crisis, $88 million international scholarship programs budget, and graduate program enrolment corridor management showing University in-year enrolment 5.5% above funded midpoint with risk of exceeding ceiling by 2028-29.

documents.title: Temerty Building (MSB West Wing Redevelopment) Presentation
documents.summary: Project presentation for Temerty Building integrating Temerty Faculty of Medicine and Faculty of Arts & Science Department of Cell & Systems Biology into modernized life-science research and learning facility. Details space program: demolition of existing 8,806 NASM; proposed total 17,099 NASM including Faculty of Arts & Science (7,483 NASM, 10,570 GSM), Faculty of Medicine (7,664 NASM, 11,011 GSM), institutional spaces (1,952 NASM, 3,466 GSM), and Facilities & Services nodal plant (2,441 NASM, 2,983 GSM). Net new space addition of 8,293 assignable square metres and 17,169 gross square metres. Describes use of Integrated Project Delivery (IPD) model—collaborative relational contract with early contractor/consultant involvement, open-book financials, Target Value Design, and shared risk/reward through Risk Pool to reduce constructability risk and improve cost certainty. Early works include utility relocations, electrical duct bank construction, temporary exiting and shoring, and MSB West Wing interior abatement/demolition. Timeline includes site mobilization June 2026, interior abatement/demolition August 2026, new construction start November 2027, and anticipated occupancy July 2031. Emphasizes climate positive integration, Indigenous spaces, Convocation support, and interdisciplinary research collaboration.
documents.gradRelevance: Affects graduate students through creation of state-of-the-art laboratory and teaching space for research programs, flexible active learning spaces supporting interdisciplinary discovery, new institutional spaces for student learning and community engagement, and sustainable design supporting climate goals. Project timeline affects campus operations during multi-year construction period from 2026-2031.

documents.title: Project LEAP 2.0 Presentation
documents.summary: Infrastructure and energy renewal initiative advancing University's goal to become climate positive by 2050. Proposes three main goals: responsibly manage campus growth, renew aged utility infrastructure to support academic excellence, and build resilient energy systems. Project scope includes elimination of legacy steam network and critical operational assets, ventilation system renewal, replacement of obsolete controls in critical spaces, replacement of end-of-life electrical systems, increased electrical capacity for future expansion, decarbonization through electrification of heating/hot water and lab sterilization, heat recovery and heat pump implementation, LED lighting retrofits, 150kW solar photovoltaic installation, and insulation upgrades. Addresses deferred maintenance, supports program expansion in Dentistry, generates operational cost savings and energy savings with greenhouse gas emissions reductions. Specific improvements include enhanced ventilation and humidification to clinic spaces, improved heating/cooling connections to reduce single points of failure, and connection of perimeter heating to emergency power.
documents.gradRelevance: Project LEAP 2.0 benefits graduate students and researchers through improved campus infrastructure reliability and sustainability, enhanced laboratory ventilation and space conditions supporting research quality, increased electrical capacity supporting future program growth, and alignment with climate positive institutional goals affecting campus operations and priorities throughout multi-year implementation.

documents.title: Low Energy Acceleration Program (LEAP) Concept Report and Technical Documentation
documents.summary: Detailed concept report and technical submission for original LEAP project (predecessor to LEAP 2.0) dated April 8, 2022 and March 31, 2023. Comprehensive climate action plan targeting elimination of 48,300 metric tons of CO2 annually (55% of 2021 Scope 1 and 2 emissions, 53.2% of 1990 baseline). Key objectives include 40% minimum energy use reduction in energy-intensive buildings, energy system resiliency at building and district scale, addressing $30 million deferred maintenance in district energy infrastructure, and delivering project sustainably using carbon tax cost avoidance to fund investments. Projected $9 million annual carbon tax cost avoidance once carbon levy reaches $170/tonne CO2. Maximum total project cost $129 million. Scope includes central steam plant modernization (replacement of 1960 boiler with electric boiler, energy storage and demand response), deep energy retrofits at CCBR and Leslie Dan Pharmacy, and landmark geo-exchange integration. Uses performance-based design-build contracting with risk transfer to private sector. Schedule includes financial close and mobilization July 2023, construction through April 2026, commissioning May 2026-April 2027, with 25-year guarantee period May 2027-April 2053. Detailed technical analysis addresses existing conditions across multiple campus buildings and specific energy conservation measures.
documents.gradRelevance: LEAP infrastructure renewal directly affects graduate students and researchers through improved building systems supporting research activities, enhanced reliability and resilience of utility infrastructure supporting teaching and research, deferred maintenance reductions improving campus conditions, and demonstration of institutional commitment to climate positive goals aligning with many graduate students' sustainability values and research interests.

documents.title: Faculty of Applied Science & Engineering Strategic Academic Plan 2026-2031 Presentation
documents.summary: Five-year strategic framework for Faculty of Applied Science & Engineering guided by mission to be global leader in advancing transdisciplinary research, innovation and education. Organized around four strategic pillars: (1) Educating adaptive thinkers to address complex challenges with priorities including amplifying societal/global impact, expanding experiential learning, career readiness, training next-generation researchers, promoting equitable access; (2) Research excellence with priorities in innovation/impact, interdisciplinary collaboration, modernized research infrastructure; (3) Community, collaboration and resilience with priorities in community wellbeing, strengthened administrative services, EDIA integration, Indigenous knowledge systems integration; (4) Building transformative partnerships with priorities in strategic partnerships, novel partnership pathways, global engagement. Planning process included extensive consultation with students, staff, faculty, alumni, industry, and community partners. Addresses potential addition of 4,500 new provincially funded STEM spaces with Faculty planning for measured growth including proposed new biomedical engineering undergraduate program adding approximately 150 students. Institutional aspiration to add approximately 5,000 new undergraduate and 500 graduate STEM spaces across divisions if provincial funding materializes. Implementation mechanisms established to assess progress and outcomes over 2026-2031 period.
documents.gradRelevance: Graduate students affected through planned research infrastructure modernization supporting graduate-level research, emphasis on training next generation of researchers, graduate program growth planning including potential new graduate STEM space expansion of 500 students institutional-wide, EDIA integration affecting graduate student experience and support services, and career development priorities including leadership development.

packages.title: Planning and Budget Committee - September 18, 2025
packages.summary: On September 18, 2025, the Planning and Budget Committee approved the MacMillan Theatre Remediation project scope (approximately 1,200 gross square metres, 440 net assignable square metres of theatre space) to be funded by Provostial Funds, with construction beginning December 2025 and targeted completion July 2026. The Committee received reports on fall 2025 enrolment showing approximately 100,000 students across three campuses (17,000 new undergraduates, 8,000 new graduate students), but flagged a $50–60 million shortfall (1.5% of operating budget) due to international undergraduate enrolment 19% below target and domestic enrolment 8% above target, creating budgetary pressures under the provincial funding corridor. The Committee also reviewed the University's AI Task Force implementation, including 1,800+ visits to AI literacy modules, 700+ monthly AI Roundup registrants, institutional ChatGPT.Edu licenses (477 issued), and Claude pilot projects (300+ licenses issued across 16 projects). The Multi-Year Accessibility Plan 2025–2030 was presented, featuring an Institutional Accessibility Advisory Committee, updated Facility Accessibility Design Standard (August 14, 2025), and commitment to barrier-free institution status by 2030.
packages.gradRelevance: Graduate student enrolment reached 8,000 new entrants in fall 2025; doctoral and research-stream master's programs showed stabilization and slight growth, though professional master's programs (management, education) face market challenges. The AI initiatives directly affect graduate students through access to ChatGPT.Edu (477 licenses), Claude (300+ licenses for 16 projects across divisions), and Cogniti virtual tutors (70 instructors, 90 courses), which support teaching and research. The Multi-Year Accessibility Plan 2025–2030 and updated Facility Accessibility Design Standard (August 14, 2025) establish accessibility frameworks affecting graduate student spaces and services, with an Institutional Accessibility Advisory Committee to guide implementation. No specific funding changes, fee adjustments, or TA/RA labour matters were discussed.
keyItems: Fall 2025 enrolment: ~100,000 students across three campuses (17,000 new undergraduates, 8,000 new graduate students); enrolment numbers not finalized until November 2025

International undergraduate enrolment 19% below target with 5–6% decline from previous year (notable declines from China and India); domestic undergraduate enrolment 8% above target (~1,000 more than planned)

$50–60 million shortfall relative to revenue plan (1.5% of operating budget) due to international enrolment shortfall and domestic enrolment exceeding funding corridor capacity; manageable in-year via contingencies and reserves
Doctoral and research-stream master's programs showing stabilization and slight growth; professional master's programs (management, education) facing challenges due to market shifts
Strategic Mandate Agreement 4 (SMA4) signed with provincial government for next five-year period; funding formula review underway with government

AI Task Force recommendations implementation: 1,800+ visits to Open Educational Resource (OER) modules, 520 downloads; 700+ registrants in monthly AI Roundup sessions; 477 ChatGPT.Edu licenses issued; 300+ Claude licenses issued across 16 proposed projects; Cogniti virtual tutor pilots across 70 instructors, 10 divisions, 90 courses

documents.title: Multi-Year Accessibility Plan 2025–2030
documents.summary: The Multi-Year Accessibility Plan 2025–2030 outlines the University's strategic approach to meeting AODA obligations. Developed through a dedicated Consultation Committee with meaningful engagement from students, staff, faculty, and librarians with disabilities, the plan establishes five key themes and assigns each initiative to responsible University portfolios. Key initiatives include establishing an Institutional Accessibility Advisory Committee, creating a resource hub, improving feedback mechanisms, enhancing digital and physical accessibility, and updating training modules. The plan aims to achieve a barrier-free institution by 2030 and reflects over a decade of accessibility work.
documents.gradRelevance: Graduate students are directly affected through the plan's accessibility initiatives, including improved digital and physical accessibility across campus, enhanced feedback mechanisms, and the establishment of an Institutional Accessibility Advisory Committee that will guide strategic efforts. The plan's commitment to removing barriers in existing spaces addresses longstanding graduate student concerns about inaccessible classrooms and facilities.

documents.title: Facility Accessibility Design Standard (updated August 14, 2025)
documents.summary: The Facility Accessibility Design Standard, updated August 14, 2025, establishes mandatory accessibility requirements for all new and significantly renovated spaces at the University of Toronto. The standard is regularly updated to reflect current best practices and is applied across campus projects, including the MacMillan Theatre Remediation. It serves as the foundational framework for ensuring physical accessibility in University facilities.
documents.gradRelevance: Graduate students benefit from this standard's application to new and renovated spaces, including teaching facilities, libraries, and research spaces. The MacMillan Theatre Remediation project incorporates the standard in its design, ensuring the renovated 600+ seat lecture theatre and performance space meet current accessibility requirements.

packages.title: University Affairs Board - May 27, 2026
packages.summary: The University Affairs Board held its final cycle 5 meeting on May 27, 2026, approving a Hart House Capital Fee Levy referendum for St. George campus students ($15–$25 per term over 15–20 years depending on phase and full/part-time status) and recommending approval of the St. George Campus Plan 2040. The Board also established the Oak House Residents' Council society fee at $12 per term, received information on the WUSC Student Refugee Program fee increase approved at UTSC, and heard reports on equity initiatives, Indigenous programming, campus safety, and trademark licensing. Professor Sandy Welsh reported consultation on the Supportive Leaves Policy with 130+ individuals and will present a revised policy in Fall 2026.
packages.gradRelevance: Graduate students will pay the Hart House Capital Fee Levy alongside undergraduates (though the motion does not specify if graduate fees differ): $15/term in Phase 1 and $25/term in Phase 2 for full-time students, with 2% annual increases. Graduate Students' Union Vice-President Nicholas Silver raised concerns about referendum protocol equity and budget rules between Yes and No committees, recommending updates to Appendix D of the Compulsory Non-Tuition Related Fees Protocol before the referendum proceeds. The Supportive Leaves Policy review affects graduate student mental health supports and intersections with the Code of Student Conduct.
keyItems: Hart House Capital Fee Levy referendum APPROVED for St. George campus: $15/term full-time and $12/term part-time in Phase 1 (years 1–5, 2% annual increase); $25/term full-time and $5/term part-time in Phase 2 (years 6–15, 2% annual increase); scope narrowed and fee reduced 73% after COSS rejection in February 2026; Hart House Building Renewal Project addresses critical infrastructure (HVAC, roof, electrical, mechanical systems) with over 50% of infrastructure past intended lifespan as of 2018

St. George Campus Plan 2040 RECOMMENDED for approval in principle: 15-year vision addressing sustainable growth, projected shortfalls in academic spaces, student housing, family/faculty housing, and innovation spaces; includes Weston Family donation for Queen's Park North revitalization and ecological corridor; based on first-ever Public Space Public Life Study with three guiding priorities (transforming society, modelling urban future, leading in research/education)

Supportive Leaves Policy consultation completed (February–April 2026) with 130+ individuals, 25 online submissions, and student union/Ombudsperson meetings; feedback indicated policy functioning as intended but identified needs for improved clarity, transparency, accessibility, and administrator/educator tools for undergraduate mental health support; revised policy to be presented Fall 2026

Oak House Residents' Council society fee ESTABLISHED at $12 per fall and winter session for all Oak House residents beginning Fall 2026; new residence opened this year with resident referendum approving fee (low voter turnout noted but no minimum participation threshold exists)

WUSC Student Refugee Program fee increase APPROVED at UTSC (May 25, 2026) via student referendum in March 2026; program provides transition and financial support for refugee students through International Student Centre; no University financial implications

Campus Safety reports for all three campuses: 27 hate crimes reported at St. George (including postering incidents); new measures implemented for cybersecurity, protest management, de-escalation, autism spectrum disorder and mental health awareness, and trauma-informed care; St. George expanded Community Crisis Response Program for mental health calls and toxic drug overdose response; Falcon emotional support dog active since 2024

documents.title: Hart House Capital Fee Levy – Meeting Materials (Embedded in Report)
documents.summary: Hart House Warden David Kim presented the Building Renewal Project addressing critical infrastructure upgrades (HVAC, roof, accessibility, mechanical/electrical systems) with over 50% of infrastructure past intended lifespan as of 2018. The referendum proposal seeks approval for a 20-year levy beginning 2026–27, initially proposed at $60/term full-time and $12/term part-time but revised downward 73% to $15/term full-time and $12/term part-time (Phase 1, years 1–5, 2% annual increase) and $25/term full-time and $5/term part-time (Phase 2, years 6–15, 2% annual increase) after COSS rejection in February 2026. Student consultation since 2012 involved over 1,000 students in Fall 2025.
documents.gradRelevance: Graduate students at St. George campus will pay the Hart House Capital Fee Levy. The motion approved by the Board does not explicitly differentiate graduate from undergraduate fee amounts, implying all full-time St. George graduate students will pay $15/term in Phase 1 and $25/term in Phase 2.

documents.title: St. George Campus Plan 2040 – Meeting Materials (Embedded in Report)
documents.summary: The Plan replaces the 2011 Master Plan and 1999 Outdoor Space Master Plan over a 15-year horizon. Led by University Planning (began Fall 2024), it incorporates feedback from online surveys, open houses, and pop-ups, plus the first-ever Public Space Public Life Study revealing three guiding priorities: transforming society, modelling urban future, and leading in research/education. The Plan identifies projected shortfalls in academic spaces, student housing, student family and faculty housing, and innovation spaces. It integrates with the Weston Family donation for Queen's Park North revitalization.
documents.gradRelevance: Graduate students are affected by the identified shortfalls in student housing and faculty housing, which the Plan recognizes as future capacity challenges on the St. George campus.

documents.title: Supportive Leaves Policy Review – Senior Assessor Report (Embedded in Report)
documents.summary: Consultation conducted February–April 2026 with 130+ individuals through open sessions, stakeholder meetings, online submissions (25 received), and meetings with student unions and University Ombudsperson. Policy feedback was overwhelmingly positive regarding administration in practice, though consultation revealed policy applied only in extreme cases where all other measures exhausted. Areas for improvement: improved clarity and transparency, more details on supports/timelines/intersections with Code of Student Conduct, and enhanced accessibility in student communications. Concerns raised about administrator and educator tools for supporting undergraduate students with serious mental health issues.
documents.gradRelevance: Graduate students are affected by the Supportive Leaves Policy and its intersection with the Code of Student Conduct and other student support policies; revised policy will be presented Fall 2026.

packages.title: University Affairs Board - November 25, 2025
packages.summary: On November 25, 2025, the University Affairs Board approved revisions to the Policy on Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment, effective January 1, 2026, incorporating feedback from 31 submissions (approximately 150 individual items) received during the September 3–October 8, 2025 public consultation period. The Board also received reports on the Code of Student Conduct (which will undergo its first review since 2002, led by former Vice-President and Provost Cheryl Regehr, with consultations beginning in 2026) and the Supportive Leaves Policy (averaging 4.2 students per year across five years, with only one invocation in 2024–25 among 100,000+ enrolled students). Additionally, Vice-President Kelly Hannah-Moffat reported on the review of the Statement on Prohibited Discrimination and Discriminatory Harassment, which received 13 recommendations from consultations including 45 closed and 8 open sessions; the new Policy will be brought forward to governance in cycle 5. The Office of the Vice-Provost, Students, led the inaugural Food Security Strategy Jam on November 14, 2025, in collaboration with Hart House and all three student unions.
packages.gradRelevance: Graduate students are directly affected by the revised Policy on Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment (effective January 1, 2026), which applies to all students including graduate students, with enhanced clarity on definitions, procedures, reprisals, timelines, and support for respondents. The upcoming Code of Student Conduct review (consultations begin 2026) and the new Statement on Prohibited Discrimination and Discriminatory Harassment policy (governance cycle 5) will also apply to graduate students. The Food Security Strategy Jam (November 14, 2025) directly involved the University of Toronto Graduate Student Union as a partner, addressing food security issues affecting graduate students.
keyItems: Policy on Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment: revisions approved November 25, 2025, effective January 1, 2026; incorporated feedback from 31 submissions (25 online, 6 emailed) totalling approximately 150 individual items with enhanced clarity on definitions, procedures, reprisals, timelines, and support for respondents and interim measures

Code of Student Conduct: comprehensive review launched, first review since 2002; led by former Vice-President and Provost Cheryl Regehr; consultations expected to begin in 2026; decisions on discipline rest with division heads (Principals and Deans)

Supportive Leaves Policy annual report: 5-year average of 4.2 students per year placed on mandated leave; in 2024–25, one request from a division head, one student placed on leave; University had 100,000+ enrolled students and provided 52,000 mental health services appointments in 2024–25; review of Policy launching January 2026

Non-Academic Discipline report: annual statistics on concluded Code of Student Conduct cases over five years; three cases in 2024–25 took 21, 24, and 35 months to resolve; three cases in 2023–24 took 23, 25, and 28 months respectively; long timelines typically due to parallel criminal proceedings requiring investigation pause

Statement on Prohibited Discrimination and Discriminatory Harassment review: 13 recommendations received; review co-chaired by Professor Brenda Cosman and Caroline Rabbat; included 45 closed sessions and 8 open sessions across tri-campus; 3,200+ website visitors; key feedback: transform Statement into Policy for greater enforceability, create one point of entry for complaints, establish University-wide tracking system; full consultation report to be released within weeks; policy to be brought forward in governance cycle 5; reviews of Code and Statement proceeding collaboratively

Food Security Strategy Jam: inaugural event held November 14, 2025, hosted by Office of the Vice-Provost, Students in collaboration with Hart House, University of Toronto Student Union, Scarborough Campus Student Union, UTM Student Union, and University of Toronto Graduate Student Union; emerged from conversations between Vice-Provost Welsh and student leaders; described as first step in ongoing conversation

packages.title: University Affairs Board - October 23, 2025
packages.summary: On October 23, 2025, the University Affairs Board approved the MacMillan Theatre Remediation capital project (approximately 1,200 gsm, 440 nasm of theatre space, funded by Provostial Funds, opening fall 2026) and received updates on the university's AI Task Force completion (June 2025, with six working groups producing teaching/research/administration strategy), the revised Policy on Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment (due December 2025, with six new definitions including tech-facilitated sexual violence, renamed non-adjudicative to facilitated resolution, expanded scope to former members and intimate partner violence), the Sexual Violence Annual Report (July 1, 2024–June 30, 2025, showing increased reports and doubled case closures), Hart House Building Renewal (200,000+ sq. ft. landmark, six-phase infrastructure modernization with accessibility and community space upgrades), and the Multi-Year Accessibility Plan (reviewed every five years, next review in 2028, passed provincial AODA audit, guiding initiatives toward 2030).
packages.gradRelevance: Graduate students are directly affected by: (1) the Sexual Violence Policy revision expanding scope to former community members and adding tech-facilitated sexual violence protections; (2) the AI Task Force implementation, including ChatGPT Edu and virtual tutor pilots in 95 Quercus courses affecting teaching and research; (3) Hart House renewal creating flexible study and social spaces for 4,300+ annual student-led bookings; (4) MYAP accessibility enhancements including web accessibility, physical access, and an accessibility advisory committee. The MacMillan Theatre remediation has limited direct grad relevance unless graduate programs use performance space.
keyItems: MacMillan Theatre Remediation capital project approved: ~1,200 gsm (440 nasm theatre space), remediation of 1920s building with back-of-house upgrades (rigging, fire curtain, asbestos abatement, electrical/lighting) and front-of-house upgrades (612 new foldable tablet-arm seats, wheelchair accessibility, AV/lighting, HVAC restoration), funded by Provostial Funds, opening fall 2026

AI Task Force completed June 2025 with six working groups; implementation includes: open educational resource (OER) modules for AI learning, ChatGPT Edu and Co-pilot secure deployment, virtual tutor pilots in ~95 Quercus courses, guidelines for assessment/responsible use, and secure multi-model AI platform expected end of academic year

Policy on Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment revision presented for information (approval November governance cycle); due December 2025; adds six new definitions including tech-facilitated sexual violence; renames non-adjudicative to facilitated resolution; expands scope to reports from former community members and intimate partner violence; requires trauma-informed investigator training; allows Vice-Provost, Students to impose sanctions in non-expulsion cases; confirms timelines for assessments and investigations

Sexual Violence Annual Report (July 1, 2024–June 30, 2025): increased reports of sexual violence (interpreted as reduced barriers to reporting); case closures more than doubled from prior cycle; two case managers hired early 2024 improved resolution times; mandatory 'Building a Culture of Consent' violence prevention training for all new residence students

Hart House Building Renewal project approved: six-phase renewal of 200,000+ sq. ft. landmark building (opened 1919, hosts 1.5+ million visitors annually including 300,000 fitness centre visits); includes mechanical/electrical/HVAC modernization, roof/skylight/lighting repairs, accessibility improvements (ramps, lifts, barrier-free design), community spaces (reading rooms, cafe, maker spaces), supporting 4,300+ annual student-led bookings

Multi-Year Accessibility Plan (MYAP) presented: five-year plan to 2030; passed provincial AODA audit; developed via six-week multimodal consultation with students, faculty, librarians, and staff with disabilities; includes accessibility advisory committee, central resource hub, web accessibility enhancements, physical access measures, and barrier-free institutional systems

packages.title: UTM Academic Affairs Committee - April 28, 2026
packages.summary: On April 28, 2026, the UTM Academic Affairs Committee approved six new academic programs effective September 1, 2026: a Certificate in Economic Data Analytics (3 credits, projected 20–30 students annually), a Certificate in Ancient Languages (2 credits in Greek/Latin plus classical civilization), and a Certificate in Artificial Intelligence and Society (2 credits, interdisciplinary). The Committee also approved a new freestanding Minor in Health, Environments, and Society (4 credits, led by Geography, Geomatics and Environment) and recommended repointing three Computer Science programs (Specialist, Major, Information Security Specialist) from Honours Bachelor of Science to the new Bachelor of Computer Science degree, effective September 1, 2027, with no curriculum or cost changes. Four academic regulations took effect September 1, 2026: clarified Return from Absence pathways (distinguishing re-registration from reapplication, requiring reapplication for students who studied elsewhere without authorization), a new 10-of-20 credit minimum completion requirement at UTM (aligning with other divisions), and shortened remarking windows for term work from one month to two weeks. The library presented a five-year strategic plan emphasizing inclusive learning spaces, digital innovation, AI ethics, and expansion of South Asian and Sikh studies collections, supported by 130+ curriculum sessions and 15,000+ service interactions per semester.
packages.gradRelevance: Graduate student relevance is minimal in this report. Ron Buliung holds the title Interim Vice-Dean Graduate & Postdoctoral Affairs and attended, but no graduate funding, stipend, TA/RA rates, fee changes, or graduate-specific policies were discussed or modified. The new academic programs (certificates and minors) are explicitly undergraduate offerings with no stated graduate component. The Return from Absence and Degree Requirements policies apply to undergraduates and do not address graduate student pathways, leaves of absence, or regulations. The library's strategic plan mentions supporting 'undergraduate, graduate, and faculty needs' generically but provides no specific graduate services improvements, collection investments, or accessibility enhancements targeted to graduate researchers.
keyItems: Certificate in Economic Data Analytics approved effective September 1, 2026 (Department of Economics): 3 credits combining programming, econometrics, and applied economic analysis; draws on existing courses from economics, computer science, statistics, management, geography; projected 20 students initial enrolment growing to 30 annually; no additional resources required.

Certificate in Ancient Languages approved effective September 1, 2026 (Department of Historical Studies): 2 credits total (1.5 in ancient Greek and/or Latin, 0.5 in classical civilization); supports primary text engagement; title chosen to allow future expansion to additional languages such as Sanskrit.

Certificate in Artificial Intelligence and Society approved effective September 1, 2026 (Department of Philosophy): 2 credits, interdisciplinary drawing on philosophy, computer science, sociology, English, linguistics, communication/culture/technology; responds to strong student demand in philosophy courses at capacity; designed as flexible pilot that could evolve into minor.

Minor in Health, Environments, and Society approved effective September 1, 2026 (Department of Geography, Geomatics and Environment): 4 credits, place-based approach to health equity, includes foundational coursework, geographic methods training, upper-year electives on global health/migration/Indigenous health/pandemics; integrates experiential and community-engaged learning.

Computer Science programs repointing recommended effective September 1, 2027: Computer Science Specialist, Major, and Information Security Specialist repointed from Honours Bachelor of Science to Bachelor of Computer Science (subject to Executive Committee confirmation); no curriculum, admissions, learning outcomes, resources, or tuition changes; students admitted before Fall 2027 may graduate under either designation.

Return from Absence regulation revised effective September 1, 2026: clarifies two pathways—re-registration (for students with prior standing in at least one course) and reapplication (for students without standing); new clause requires reapplication for students who pursued studies at another institution during absence without prior authorization (letter of permission or exchange); students must disclose outside study; non-disclosure may trigger Academic Integrity Unit referral.

documents.title: Certificate in Economic Data Analytics Proposal (March 13, 2026)
documents.summary: Proposal for new 3-credit certificate in Department of Economics responding to demand for data-driven skills. Combines coursework in programming, econometrics, and applied economic analysis using existing courses across economics, computer science, statistics, management, and geography. Aligned with program review recommendations and student feedback for stronger programming and empirical methods training. Projected initial enrolment 20 students, steady state 30 annually. No additional resources required. Supports preparation for graduate study and data-intensive careers.
documents.gradRelevance: No direct graduate relevance; described as pathway for undergraduate preparation for graduate study but contains no graduate-specific coursework, funding, or opportunities.

documents.title: Certificate in Ancient Languages Proposal (April 10, 2026)
documents.summary: Proposal for new 2-credit certificate in Department of Historical Studies. Requires 1.5 credits in ancient Greek and/or Latin and 0.5 credits in classical civilization courses. Recognizes student achievement in classical languages, strengthens linguistic proficiency and contextual understanding. Responds to demonstrated student interest in classical studies. Supports students preparing for graduate work in disciplines requiring language proficiency. Uses existing courses and faculty resources. Title intentionally broad to enable future expansion to languages such as Sanskrit.
documents.gradRelevance: Explicitly notes support for students preparing for graduate work where language proficiency is required, though no graduate funding or specific graduate benefits detailed.

documents.title: Certificate in Artificial Intelligence and Society Proposal (March 27, 2026)
documents.summary: Proposal for new 2-credit interdisciplinary certificate in Department of Philosophy addressing technical and social dimensions of artificial intelligence. Draws on existing courses from philosophy, computer science, sociology, English, linguistics, and communication/culture/technology. Reflects strong student demand, particularly in philosophy courses at capacity. Designed to develop AI literacy and critical engagement with AI across disciplines. Flexible and accessible to all degree programs. Deliverable through existing resources. Framed as initial step toward more extensive AI programming, potentially evolving to minor status.

documents.title: Minor in Health, Environments, and Society Proposal (April 9, 2026)
documents.summary: Proposal for new 4-credit freestanding minor in Department of Geography, Geomatics and Environment addressing environmental and social determinants of health. Emphasizes place-based approaches to health equity with interdisciplinary perspectives from geography, anthropology, sociology. Includes foundational coursework, methods training in geographic approaches, and upper-year electives covering global health, migration, Indigenous health, pandemics. Integrates experiential learning and community-engaged research. Responds to student demand and gap in undergraduate offerings. Accessible to broad range of disciplines; prepares for public health, policy, and research careers.

documents.title: Computer Science Programs Repointing Proposal (April 7, 2026)
documents.summary: Proposal to repoint three existing programs (Computer Science Specialist, Computer Science Major, Information Security Specialist) from Honours Bachelor of Science to new Bachelor of Computer Science degree. Implementation of previously approved tri-campus degree. No changes to curriculum, course requirements, admissions, learning outcomes, or resources. Change responds to student demand, employer recognition, sector trends; improves visibility without cost increases. Students admitted before Fall 2027 may graduate under either designation. Coordinated communication strategy for academic calendar, recruitment, prospective/current student notification.

documents.title: Return from Absence Regulation Revision Proposal (April 9, 2026)
documents.summary: Proposal to revise Return from Absence policy governing students absent 12+ consecutive months. Clarifies and formalizes existing practices. Distinguishes two pathways: re-registration (for students with prior standing in at least one course) via Office of Registrar, and reapplication (for students without standing) via Ontario Universities' Application Centre. New clause addresses students who studied at other institutions during absence without authorization—requires reapplication and assessment through internal process. Students must disclose outside study; non-disclosure may trigger Academic Integrity Unit review. Aligns with similar changes at Faculty of Arts and Science and UTSC.
documents.gradRelevance: Applies to undergraduate students; no specific impact on graduate student returns from absence or graduate-specific pathways mentioned.

documents.title: Degree Requirements Regulation Amendment Proposal (April 3, 2026)
documents.summary: Proposal to introduce minimum 10-of-20 credit completion requirement at UTM for undergraduate degrees. Requires students complete at least 50% of degree at UTM granting the degree. Aligns with policies at Faculty of Arts and Science and UTSC. Preserves flexibility for courses at other divisions via letters of permission or transfer credits. Addresses program integrity, academic coherence, institutional planning for course offerings and resource allocation. Affects relatively small number of students. Communication through academic calendar, advising channels, and Degree Explorer.
documents.gradRelevance: Applies to undergraduate degree requirements; no impact on graduate students or programs.

documents.title: Re-marking of Term Work Regulation Amendment Proposal (March 30, 2026)
documents.summary: Proposal to reduce remark request timeframe for term work from one month to two weeks after work returned. Intended to align UTM regulations with other divisions and support timely academic feedback. Shorter window allows students to incorporate feedback into subsequent assignments. Supports administrative efficiency by ensuring remarking completed within academic term, reducing end-of-term overlap. Effective September 1, 2026.

packages.title: UTM Academic Affairs Committee - March 11, 2026
packages.summary: On March 11, 2026, the UTM Academic Affairs Committee approved closure of the Minor in Francophone Studies (effective April 30, 2026) and the Specialist in Italian and French (effective April 30, 2026), both programs with zero active students and minimal recent enrolment. The committee approved nine new streams in the UTM Co-op Internship Program (UTMCIP) spanning Biology, Management, Mathematical and Computational Sciences, and Forensic Sciences, effective September 1, 2026, after 70% of surveyed students indicated co-op as their preferred experiential learning option. A Focus in Business Data Analytics was approved for the Department of Management (effective September 1, 2026) with 91% student support in a 87-response survey. Graduate curriculum changes in the Institute for Management and Innovation were approved, including a new required course BTC1843H in the Master of Biotechnology Digital Health Technology stream and retirement of BTC1842H, plus calendar corrections for the Master of Forensic Accounting's hybrid format. For information only, a Bachelor of Computer Science degree (non-Honours) was presented, taking effect September 1, 2027 with first graduates in June 2028; currently enrolled students may opt into the new designation.
packages.gradRelevance: Graduate students will be directly affected by the Institute for Management and Innovation curriculum changes: Master of Biotechnology students entering the Digital Health Technology stream must now complete BTC1843H: Relational Database Access for Cybersecurity and Health as a new required course effective September 1, 2026, while BTC1842H: Medical Device Reimbursement is retired; Master of Forensic Accounting students' program delivery mode (hybrid format) was clarified in calendar corrections. The new UTMCIP streams may provide paid work-integrated learning opportunities for graduate students in participating programs, though the primary focus appears to be undergraduate degree completion extension (to five years with 12–16 month placements). No changes to graduate stipends, fees, TA/RA positions, or housing were discussed.
keyItems: Minor in Francophone Studies closure approved with April 30, 2026 effective date; program had zero enrolment as of 2017, with admissions administratively suspended September 1, 2017, and external UTQAP review recommended against reinstatement

Specialist in Italian and French closure approved with April 30, 2026 effective date; admissions suspended November 23, 2016 due to zero enrolment by 2016 (declining from eight students in 2013 to five in 2014–15 to zero in 2016)

Nine new streams approved for UTM Co-op Internship Program (UTMCIP) effective September 1, 2026, spanning Biology, Management, Mathematical and Computational Sciences, and Institute of Forensic Sciences; program includes four preparatory modules, 12–16 month paid placement, and reflective module; first cohort placements expected May 2026 with tracking via Simplicity platform; report back requested next year on outcomes

Focus in Business Data Analytics approved for Department of Management effective September 1, 2026, requiring 1.5 credits core (Introduction to Data Analysis, Applied Econometrics, Data Analytics for Management or Financial Modeling in Data Analytics) and 1.5 elective credits; 91% of 87 surveyed students supported the focus

Master of Biotechnology Digital Health Technology stream: new required course BTC1843H (Relational Database Access for Cybersecurity and Health) added effective September 1, 2026 to address healthcare data systems industry demand; BTC1842H (Medical Device Reimbursement) retired due to limited internship partner interest
Master of Forensic Accounting calendar corrections approved for 2025–26 to accurately reflect hybrid delivery format; no resource implications

The enrolment report, curriculum changes, and new undergraduate program proposals concern only undergraduate education, fees, and programming. The meeting documents contain no discussion of graduate program offerings, graduate funding, TA/RA stipends, graduate housing, or unionized labour matters relevant to UTGSU members.

documents.title: UTM Undergraduate Enrolment Update (January 2026)
documents.summary: Registrar Renu Kanga Fonseca presented comprehensive enrolment data for UTM showing total full-time headcount of 15,215 in 2025 with 4,093 new intake and 11,122 returning students. International enrolment declined from 4,292 in 2023 to 3,617 in 2025 (24% of total). Ontario high school new intake admission average was 86.6% in 2025. Graduation rates show 73.9% of students from 2012-2018 cohorts graduated within 7 years.

documents.title: New Undergraduate Program: Specialist in Accounting and Finance (BBA), UTM
documents.summary: Department of Management proposes new Specialist in Accounting and Finance conferred as Bachelor of Business Administration degree, requiring 16.5 full course equivalents, effective September 1, 2026. Program utilizes existing course offerings with no additional faculty hiring required. External appraisers Professor Catherine Shakespeare (University of Michigan) and Professor Regan Schmidt (University of Saskatchewan) conducted review October 2, 2025, endorsing the program as innovative for merging accounting and finance with potential to distinguish university as leader in field. Reviewers recommended comprehensive communication strategy for current students, prospective students, advisors, faculty, staff, employers, and alumni to clarify distinction from existing BCom accounting and BCom finance programs. First cohort begins September 2026. No financial implications to operating budget.

documents.title: Minor Modification: Minor in Criminology, Law and Society
documents.summary: Department of Sociology proposes new Minor in Criminology, Law & Society (CLS) to complement existing Major and Specialist programs, effective September 1, 2026. Student survey of 940 respondents (75% first-year, 25% second-year or higher) showed 80% of upper-year students expressed interest; 54% of first-year respondents intended to pursue CLS major or specialist, with vast majority indicating CLS minor would not deter major/specialist pursuit. Minor will introduce new courses SOC212H5 ('So, you want to be a lawyer?') and SOC368H5 ('Global Perspectives on Gang Culture') resourced with existing faculty. CLS programs currently account for ~59% of departmental major and specialist enrolments. Student qualitative feedback highlighted engaging content, labour market benefit, and appeal to law school aspirants. No financial implications to operating budget.

documents.title: Minor Modification: Undergraduate Curriculum Changes, UTM (Humanities, Sciences, Social Sciences)
documents.summary: Comprehensive curriculum report compiled from November 2025 divisional curriculum committee meetings across three academic areas, effective September 1, 2026. Humanities division (27 programs): 25 new courses, 225 course modifications, 6 course retirements across 256 total course changes. Sciences division (42 programs): 12 new courses, 72 course modifications, 6 course retirements across 90 total changes. Social Sciences division (22 programs): 19 new courses, 64 course modifications, 8 course retirements across 91 total changes. Changes include new courses such as DRE381H5 (Creative Devising at Shaw Festival), DRE389H5 (Theatre and Drama Internship), ENG411H5 (Game Studies Internship), LIN280H5 (Introduction to Language Teaching, Learning, and Assessment), and extensive modifications to linguistics, communication, English, drama, and other disciplinary programs. All resource implications reviewed by Resource Implications Committee within Dean's Office; library resources approved by Hazel McCallion Academic Learning Centre. No net operating budget implications.

packages.title: UTM Academic Affairs Committee - January 27, 2026
packages.summary: On January 27, 2026, the UTM Academic Affairs Committee approved three new academic programs and curriculum changes effective September 1, 2026: a Specialist in Accounting and Finance (BBA degree, using existing courses and faculty), a Minor in Criminology, Law & Society (drawing on existing department courses), and Humanities, Sciences, and Social Sciences undergraduate curriculum revisions. The Committee received enrolment data showing total full-time undergraduate headcount of 15,215 students (Fall 2025), with domestic students comprising 76% and international students 24%; Fall 2025 admitted 3,118 domestic and 975 international new intake students. The Committee also noted Vice-Principal France Gagnon's appointment of Michael Manley to a new research finance and operations role in the VPRI office.
packages.gradRelevance: This meeting reports primarily undergraduate academic affairs with minimal direct graduate student impact. Ron Buliung, Interim Vice-Dean Graduate & Postdoctoral Affairs, was present but no graduate-specific funding, stipend, TA/RA policy, or service matters were discussed or decided. Graduate students should note enrolment pressures (international student cap reductions due to federal permit policy) may affect campus budget allocations, though the Committee confirmed the campus operates within a balanced multi-year budget using administrative efficiencies rather than academic program cuts.
keyItems: Specialist in Accounting and Finance (BBA) approved effective September 1, 2026, using 16.5 full-course equivalents of existing courses and faculty resources; modest initial enrolment planned for demand monitoring; external appraisal in October 2025 was supportive with recommendation to clarify distinction from existing programs

Minor in Criminology, Law & Society approved effective September 1, 2026 in Department of Sociology; responds to sustained student interest in criminology/law coursework; uses existing and planned courses, requires no additional faculty or sessional appointments; consultation with anthropology, philosophy, and political science completed

Undergraduate curriculum changes across Humanities, Sciences, and Social Sciences approved effective September 1, 2026; revisions address course content updates, sequencing, prerequisites, and program structure refinements; all resource implications reviewed and approvals obtained
