Ontario secondary school admission averages for Fall 2025 new intake: 86.6%, consistent with recent years (high-80s range); 2,867 Ontario high school students enrolled; entrance awards distributed including XL ($1,000), XQ ($2,000), XN ($3,000), XM ($3,000 renewable), UQ International Scholar ($25,000 × 4 years), and UR International Scholar ($45,000 × 4 years)

Graduation rate data: approximately 70% of students graduated within six years; 2012–2018 entry cohorts (18,542 students) produced 13,703 U of T graduates within 7 years; variation in graduation outcomes across programs and pathways noted as ongoing focus

documents.title: UTM Undergraduate Enrolment Update
documents.summary: Comprehensive enrolment data presented by Renu Kanga Fonseca covering 2019–2025 trends. Total full-time headcount Fall 2025: 15,215 (domestic 11,598, international 3,617; 24% international). New intake Fall 2025: 3,118 domestic, 975 international. International student top source country: China (524), India (42), Hong Kong (40), Pakistan (31). Ontario high school admission average Fall 2025: 86.6%. Entrance awards distributed: XL (85.0%–89.9%, $1,000), XQ (90.0%–91.9%, $2,000), XN (92.0%–94.9%, $3,000), XM (95.0%+, $3,000 renewable), UQ International Scholar ($25,000 × 4 years), UR International Scholar ($45,000 × 4 years). Graduation rates: 35.7% within 4 years, 62.4% within 5 years, 70.8% within 6 years, 73.9% within 7 years (2012–2018 entry cohorts, 18,542 students producing 13,703 graduates). Program distribution: Computer Science/Math/Statistics 18.4%, Social Sciences 17.1%, Life Sciences 13.4%, Commerce 13.4%. Fee structure: 51.0% in regulated programs only, 29.7% not yet registered, 13.1% combined deregulated/regulated, 6.2% deregulated only.
documents.gradRelevance: No direct graduate student relevance; report covers only undergraduate enrolment. However, international enrolment decline (31% in 2021 to 24% in 2025) due to federal study permit restrictions may affect campus budget and indirectly impact graduate program resources and TA/RA funding.

packages.title: UTM Academic Affairs Committee - October 15, 2025
packages.summary: The UTM Academic Affairs Committee will approve on October 15, 2025 a major modification to the Specialist in Theatre and Drama Studies (ERSPE1880), transitioning it from a 35-year joint program with Sheridan College to full UTM delivery beginning Fall 2026, phased through 2028-29. The program will hire three Adjunct Lecturers or Professors in voice, movement, and acting to replace Sheridan faculty. Four Drama Studio (DRS) courses will be re-weighted from half-credit (H) to full-credit (Y) courses, increasing required credits from 12.0 to 14.0 for students entering Fall 2027 onward. Sheridan will continue serving in-progress students through 2028-29, and existing students will graduate with both UTM's Honours BA and Sheridan's Advanced Diploma. The committee also received presentations on Academic Change Processes at UTM and Experiential Education initiatives including ROPs (427 students in 2024-25), Academic Internships (391 students across humanities, sciences, and social sciences in 2024-25), and the new UTM Co-op Internship Program (247 students in Fall 2024, expanding to 770 by 2034-35).
packages.gradRelevance: The Theatre and Drama Studies modification has no direct graduate student implications regarding funding, fees, or labour. However, the Experiential Education presentation is relevant: it outlines that ROPs, Academic Internships, and UTMCIP are available to undergraduates, and the presentation does not address graduate student involvement in these programs or graduate research assistantships. Graduate students seeking teaching or mentoring roles should note that faculty eligibility for ROPs excludes Adjunct Faculty and Sessional Lecturers (the positions being created for TDS), limiting mentoring opportunities in those roles.
keyItems: Major Modification: Specialist in Theatre and Drama Studies (ERSPE1880) approved effective September 1, 2026, transitioning from joint UTM-Sheridan delivery to full UTM delivery, phased 2026-27 through 2028-29.

UTM will hire three Adjunct Lecturers or Professors in voice, movement, and acting to replace Sheridan faculty before early February 2026 auditions; positions are for established theatre professionals with continuing careers.

Four 100- and 200-level Drama Studio courses (DRS) re-weighted from half-credit (H) to full-credit (Y), increasing program requirements from 12.0 to 14.0 credits for students entering Fall 2027 or later.

Sheridan College will continue delivering DRS courses for in-progress students through 2028-29 academic year; students unable to complete by then will be accommodated at UTM; all current students graduating by 2028-29 receive both UTM Honours BA and Sheridan Advanced Diploma.
First cohort of students under modified program enters Fall 2026-27 at UTM; no net budget implications for campus operating budget.

Academic Change Processes presentation (Vice-Dean Bryan Stewart) outlined that AAC is the sole governance approval step for new majors/specialists where one exists, new minors, freestanding minors, new streams, program focuses, and Category 2 certificates; other major proposals (new degrees, new majors/specialists where none exists, new units) require additional approvals through AP&P, Governing Council, Quality Council, and potentially Ministry.

documents.title: Major Modification: Theatre and Drama Studies Specialist – Arts (Approval Document)
documents.summary: Formal approval document for the Theatre and Drama Studies Specialist (ERSPE1880) major modification. Outlines the transition from joint UTM-Sheridan delivery to full UTM delivery effective September 1, 2026, phased through 2028-29. Details the hiring of three Adjunct Lecturers or Professors in voice, movement, and acting; re-weighting of four DRS courses from H to Y credits, increasing program requirements from 12.0 to 14.0 credits; and the transition plan ensuring in-progress students complete at Sheridan through 2028-29. The proposal emphasizes maintaining conservatory-level professional actor training, existing course-level learning outcomes, and strong industry consultation; TDS graduates regularly win Dora Mavor Moore Awards and work in major Canadian theatres. No net budget implications. Consulted with current Sheridan and UTM faculty, Erindale Theatre staff, industry leaders, and students in town hall; consensus that professional-academic integration would distinguish TDS among Ontario programs.
documents.gradRelevance: No direct graduate student funding, fee, or labour implications. However, the three Adjunct positions created will be ineligible to supervise Research Opportunity Programs (based on ROP eligibility criteria), limiting mentoring opportunities for graduate students in those areas.

documents.title: Presentation: Pulling Back the Curtain on Academic Change
documents.summary: Vice-Dean Bryan Stewart's presentation mapping academic governance pathways at UTM. Explains that the Academic Affairs Committee is the sole governance approval step for new majors/specialists (where a major or specialist already exists), new minors (where specialist/major exists), freestanding minors, new streams within programs, Program Focuses, and Category 2 certificates. For larger changes (New Degrees, New Major or Specialist where none exists, New EDU A or B, New Units), AAC is the first of many approval steps, followed by VPAP review, external review, VPAP approval, PAG response, and then Committee on Academic Policy & Programs (AP&P), Governing Council (GC), Quality Council (QC), and Ministry. Diagram shows consultation and EOI phases feeding into proposal development and VPAP review cycles.

documents.title: Presentation: Beyond the Classroom – Curricular Experiential Learning at UTM
documents.summary: Presentation by Professor Vivienne Luk (Interim Associate Dean, Academic Experience) and Rena Banwait (Director, Experiential Education & Community Engagement) detailing UTM's three core curricular experiential learning programs. Research Opportunity Program (ROP): 427 registered students and 231 course offerings in 2024-25; faculty-mentored research for high-achieving students; eligible instructors include Full-time, Part-time, CLTA, LTA, Associate Faculty, and Faculty Emeriti; ineligible are Adjunct Faculty, Sessional Lecturers I-III, Visiting Scholars, Post-Docs, Graduate Course Instructors, and Librarians. Academic Internships: 391 students in 2024-25 (51 humanities in 7 courses, 90 sciences in 6 courses, 250 social sciences in 9 courses); unpaid placements of 100-200 hours over 4-8 months at organizations including ROM, Blackwood Gallery, Riverwood Conservancy, Peel Regional Police, and international sites in Brazil, China, Taiwan, France, and USA. UTM Co-op Internship Program (UTMCIP): launched Fall 2024 with 247 students (215 from Computer Science = 87%); mandatory Work-Readiness modules plus 12-16 month paid full-time work term; Department of Management joined Fall 2025 with 5 streams; 10-year growth plan to 770 students by 2034-35 with 50 students added annually; graduates receive transcript acknowledgement; participating units include Mathematical & Computational Sciences, Chemical & Physical Sciences, Economics, Institute of Communication/Culture/Information/Technology, and Biology.
documents.gradRelevance: These experiential programs are designed for undergraduate students and do not explicitly include graduate student participation. The presentation does not address graduate research assistantships, graduate internships, or graduate co-op opportunities, though graduate students may work as ROP instructors if they hold eligible teaching appointments (though Graduate Course Instructors are explicitly ineligible). Graduate students should consult the Experiential Education Unit if seeking involvement in supervision or coordination of these programs.

packages.title: UTM Academic Affairs Committee - October 15, 2025
packages.summary: On October 15, 2025, the UTM Academic Affairs Committee approved the major modification to the Specialist in Theatre and Drama Studies program, effective September 1, 2026, moving full delivery from the joint UTM–Sheridan partnership to UTM alone due to Sheridan's financial constraints. The program will increase from 12 to 14 credits to reflect actual workload in intensive studio courses, hire three adjunct professors in voice, movement, and acting, and retain tuition and provincial grants previously directed to Sheridan to fund these costs. The Committee also received comprehensive presentations on experiential learning at UTM: the Research Opportunity Program (ROP) enrolled 427 students in 231 projects in 2024–25; academic internships engaged 391 students across humanities, sciences, and social sciences in 2024–25; and the new University of Toronto Mississauga Co-op Internship Program (UTMCIP), launched Fall 2024 as a two-year pilot with 247 students, plans to grow to 770 students by 2034–35 through annual increases of 50 students per cohort.
packages.gradRelevance: The Theatre and Drama Studies modification has minimal direct grad relevance as it affects undergraduate specialists. However, the experiential learning presentation is highly relevant: graduate students may supervise Research Opportunity Program projects as faculty mentors (though tenure-track and contractual faculty are eligible mentors, not graduate students directly), and the UTMCIP's expansion into paid, full-time 12–16-month co-op placements across disciplines may indirectly affect graduate hiring and departmental research capacity if undergraduates delay entry to graduate programs. The presentation noted 427 ROP students and 391 academic internship participants in 2024–25, all competing for mentorship and positions that might otherwise flow to graduate researchers.
keyItems: Theatre and Drama Studies Specialist major modification approved effective September 1, 2026: program transitions from joint UTM–Sheridan delivery to full UTM delivery due to Sheridan's financial withdrawal; program credits increase from 12 to 14 to reflect actual workload of intensive studio courses; UTM will hire three adjunct professors (voice, movement, acting) as active theatre professionals; students will receive only U of T degree (no Sheridan diploma); costs fully funded through tuition and provincial grants previously allocated to Sheridan; consultation with students, faculty, Sheridan, and theatre professionals demonstrated broad support.

Research Opportunity Program (ROP): 427 students enrolled in 231 ROP projects in 2024–25; faculty mentors (full-time, part-time, CLTAs, LTAs, associate faculty, and faculty emeriti) guide high-achieving students through research; ineligible mentors include adjunct faculty, visiting scholars, postdocs, graduate course instructors, librarians, and sessional lecturers; programs offered in summer, fall, winter, and full-year formats.

Academic Internships: 391 students engaged in 2024–25 across three domains—51 in humanities (7 distinct courses), 90 in sciences (6 distinct courses), 250 in social sciences (9 distinct courses); senior-level students (third and fourth year) complete 100–200 hours with external organizations over 4 or 8 months and earn course credit; placements include Blackwood Gallery, Royal Ontario Museum, Peel Institution on Violence Prevention, Riverwood Conservancy, Peel Regional Police Forensic Identification Services, MP constituency offices, and international placements in Brazil, China, Taiwan, Italy, and France.

University of Toronto Mississauga Co-op Internship Program (UTMCIP) launched Fall 2024 as two-year pilot with 247 students: structured five-year program integrating paid full-time work terms (12–16 months) with academic study; students complete mandatory asynchronous work-readiness modules in years two and three, recruit for work term in year four, and complete final year of study post-placement; initial cohort comprised 215 from Computer Science (87%), 10 each from Chemical & Physical Sciences, Economics, and ICCIT, and 2 from Biology; Department of Management joined Fall 2025.

UTMCIP 10-year growth plan: annual addition of 50 new students per entering cohort; entering cohort to grow from 247 (2024–25) to 770 (2034–35); expansion includes strategic staffing increases and infrastructure investment; Department of Management streams began Fall 2025; students access U of T tri-campus Co-op Community job postings; placement is competitive based on student recruitment performance; concrete outcome data to be available by August 2026.

Academic change governance clarified: UTM Academic Affairs Committee is sole approval step for new major/specialist within existing major/specialist, new minor with existing specialist/major, freestanding minor, new stream, program focus, and Category 2 certificates; new degrees, new units, and new EDU A/B programs require additional approvals from Committee on Academic Policy & Programs, Academic Board, Executive Committee, and external review (two to three year timeline including four-month external review); minor modifications (course title/description changes) managed by Vice-Dean Undergraduate with departmental planning and program curriculum unit review.

documents.title: Bryan Stewart presentation: Pulling Back the Curtain on Academic Change
documents.summary: Vice-Dean Bryan Stewart presented UTM's academic change processes, distinguishing between minor modifications (course title/description changes, course retirements) and substantial proposals. Minor modifications follow a streamlined timeline managed by the Vice-Dean Undergraduate. Substantial proposals undergo a multi-step process: expressions of interest (EOI) and consultation, proposal development with templates, Office of Vice-Provost and Academic Dean (OVPAD) review, VPAP approval, and Committee approval. The UTM Academic Affairs Committee serves as the sole governance approval step for new majors/specialists within existing programs, new minors with existing specialists/majors, freestanding minors, new streams, program focuses, and Category 2 certificates. For complex proposals (new degrees, new units, new EDU A/B programs), the AAC is the first of multiple approval steps also requiring Committee on Academic Policy & Programs, Academic Board, Executive Committee review, with external review and Provostial Advisory Group involvement extending timelines to two to three years.
documents.gradRelevance: Graduate students may serve as academic advisors or mentors in programs affected by governance changes, and understanding approval timelines is relevant if graduate programs undergo modifications. The presentation's emphasis on VPAP coordination could affect graduate program proposals, though the specific content targets undergraduate program structure.

documents.title: Vivienne Luk and Rena Banwait presentation: Beyond the Classroom: Curricular Experiential Learning at UTM
documents.summary: This comprehensive presentation detailed three curricular experiential learning (EL) programs at UTM. Research Opportunity Program (ROP): 427 students in 231 ROP projects in 2024–25; faculty-mentored research for high-achieving students developing foundational research and analytical skills; eligible mentors are full-time/part-time faculty, CLTAs, LTAs, associate faculty, and faculty emeriti (if active within 2–3 years); offered in summer, fall, winter, and full-year formats. Academic Internships: 391 students in 2024–25 across humanities (51, 7 courses), sciences (90, 6 courses), and social sciences (250, 9 courses); senior students complete 100–200 hours over 4 or 8 months earning course credit; examples include Blackwood Gallery, Royal Ontario Museum, Peel Institution on Violence Prevention, and international placements in Brazil, China, Taiwan, Italy, and France. University of Toronto Mississauga Co-op Internship Program (UTMCIP): launched Fall 2024 as two-year pilot with 247 students; five-year program with mandatory work-readiness modules (years 2–3), recruitment, 12–16-month paid work term (year 4), and final year study; first cohort: 215 from Computer Science (87%), 10 each from CPS, Economics, ICCIT, 2 from Biology; Department of Management joined Fall 2025; 10-year growth targeting 770 students by 2034–35 (50 new students annually); tri-campus U of T Co-op Community access; concrete placement outcomes available August 2026.
documents.gradRelevance: Highly relevant: ROP program directly competes for mentorship slots and research funds that might otherwise support graduate students; 427 undergraduate ROP participants represent potential pipeline to graduate study; graduate students may be instructors for ROP courses but are ineligible as faculty mentors; UTMCIP expansion may delay undergraduate entry to graduate programs (five-year degree pathway vs. four years), affecting graduate program enrollment and TA/RA availability; 247 co-op students in year one expanding to 770 will reduce undergraduate availability for graduate research positions and departmental teaching assistantships.

packages.title: UTM Academic Affairs Committee - September 10, 2025
packages.summary: The UTM Academic Affairs Committee met on September 10, 2025 to review academic priorities, research initiatives, and program expansions. William Gough (Interim Vice-Principal Academic & Dean) outlined the 2025-26 academic priorities including advancing the academic plan, championing Indigenous curricula, expanding co-op internships, and forging health professions pathways; the committee will consider eight UTQAP reviews and new graduate programs including a PhD in Media Communications Technology and PhD in Quantitative Biosciences and Translational Sciences. France Gagnon (Vice-Principal Research & Innovation) presented the OVPRI's three pillars of support—strategic grant development, core research facilities, and partnerships/innovation—and identified goals to increase Tri-Council funding, particularly for humanists/social scientists applying for SSHRC grants and physical/life scientists pursuing NSERC Alliance and CIHR grants. The university announced the expansion of Bloomberg Nursing's BScN program to UTM starting Fall 2026, adding 30 seats funded by MCURES with simulation and clinical education resources, targeting students from Mississauga and the Peel Region with practicum placements at Trillium Health Partners. Enrolment data showed 16,191 total undergraduates (12,395 domestic, 3,796 international) and 379 graduate students (306 domestic, 73 international) as of September 2, 2025.
packages.gradRelevance: Ron Buliung (Vice-Dean, Graduate and Postdoctoral Affairs) oversees graduate programs, governance, curriculum, admissions, enrolment, and fellowship/prize awards at UTM; the committee will consider graduate program changes including the PhD in Media Communications Technology and PhD in Quantitative Biosciences and Translational Sciences (both in early stages), plus modifications to graduate curriculum scheduled for Cycle 3. France Gagnon's research briefing identifies federal graduate student funding through Canada Research Training Awards Suite linked to Tri-Council funding, making increased research grant success directly relevant to graduate funding allocations. The nursing program expansion to UTM starting Fall 2026 does not appear to include graduate-level offerings—it remains a 2-year second-entry undergraduate BScN program—so it has minimal direct graduate student impact beyond potential campus infrastructure changes.
keyItems: Interim Vice-Principal William Gough announced organizational chart changes including new Associate Dean for Faculty, Anna Korteweg, who oversees academic hiring/salary decisions, CUPE and SLII/III advancement, tenure/promotion reviews, and faculty planning

Academic priorities for 2025-26: advance academic plan, strategic enrolment plan, and faculty complement plan; champion Indigenous curricula including completion of Indigenous studies program proposal; expand co-op internship program; streamline academic integrity through Advocate by Symplicity platform; forge pathways into medicine, nursing, and occupational therapy

Eight UTQAP reviews proceeding in 2025-26: five advancing to governance (MCS, Management, Historical Studies, CPS, IMI) and three site visits planned (ICCIT, Anthropology, Political Science); four additional reviews launching for 2026-27

New programs approved/under consideration: Specialist in Accounting and Finance; PhD in Media Communications Technology; MFA in Creative Theatre Practice; PhD in Quantitative Biosciences and Translational Sciences (early stage); Major in Language Teaching & Learning (early stage)
Program closures: Specialist in French & Italian; Minor in Francophone Studies; Major modifications to Specialist in Computational Physics and Theatre & Drama Studies
Graduate enrolment as of September 2, 2025: 379 total students (306 domestic including 138 newly admitted FT and 23 PT; 73 international including 29 newly admitted FT and 3 PT)

documents.title: Portfolio Overview and Priorities for 2025-26 — William Gough, Interim Vice-Principal Academic & Dean
documents.summary: Gough presented the OVPAD organizational structure with five Vice-Deans (Margarida Duarte for Undergraduate, Bryan Stewart for Academic Programs, Alison Syme for Faculty, Ron Buliung for Graduate and Postdoctoral Affairs) and three Associate Deans plus new Associate Dean for Faculty Anna Korteweg. He outlined five academic priorities: charting purposeful pathways through academic/strategic enrolment/faculty complement plans; championing Indigenous curricula with completion of Indigenous studies program/EDU:B proposal; diversifying experiential learning by expanding co-op internships; streamlining academic integrity case resolution via Advocate by Symplicity; forging undergrad pathways into health professions. The report lists eight UTQAP reviews (five to governance: MCS, Management, Historical Studies, CPS, IMI; three site visits planned for ICCIT, Anthropology, Political Science) and four reviews launching for 2026-27. New programs include Specialist in Accounting and Finance, PhD in Media Communications Technology, and MFA in Creative Theatre Practice; closures include Specialist in French & Italian and Minor in Francophone Studies. As of September 2, 2025, enrolment totalled 16,191 undergraduates (12,395 domestic, 3,796 international) and 379 graduate students (306 domestic, 73 international).
documents.gradRelevance: Ron Buliung's Vice-Dean portfolio for Graduate and Postdoctoral Affairs oversees grad program development, governance, curriculum, admissions, enrolment management, and fellowship/prize awards. The report confirms new graduate programs under development (PhD in Media Communications Technology proceeding; PhD in Quantitative Biosciences and Translational Sciences in early stages) and graduate curriculum modifications scheduled for Cycle 3, directly affecting graduate student programming and support infrastructure.

documents.title: UTM Office of the Vice-Principal, Research and Innovation — France Gagnon
documents.summary: Gagnon presented OVPRI's three pillars of research support: strategic research grant development, core research facilities, and partnerships/innovation. The organizational chart identifies 7 animal technicians (CUPE-unionized), 1 manager (USW-unionized), multiple grant-funded SRAs, and vacant positions including Director of Partnerships & Innovation, Manager of Finance & Research Operations, and a 2-year limited-term Senior Communications Officer. The report identifies four strategic goals: identify and remove barriers for humanists/social scientists applying for SSHRC grants; increase support for physical/life scientists pursuing high-value NSERC Alliance and CIHR project grants; align internal funding programs with strategic priorities; leverage SpinUp and partnerships to increase industry/NFP/government-funded research. Gagnon emphasizes that increased Tri-Council funding at UTM directly correlates with federal allocations of graduate student funding through Canada Research Training Awards Suite and factors into Ontario's performance-based funding formula for operating grants.
documents.gradRelevance: Tri-Council research funding held at UTM directly influences federal graduate student funding allocations through Canada Research Training Awards Suite; OVPRI initiatives to increase SSHRC, NSERC, and CIHR grants will expand research funding available to support graduate research assistantships and scholarships. The OVPRI staff structure includes unionized positions relevant to research infrastructure supporting graduate research activities.

documents.title: BScN Program Expansion to UTM
documents.summary: The presentation outlined the expansion of Bloomberg Nursing's 2-year full-time BScN program to UTM beginning Fall 2026, adding 30 additional seats funded by MCURES. The program requires applicants to have completed at least 10 university full course equivalents prior to admission and requires 13.5 FCEs for degree completion, including 1,216 hours of clinical learning beginning in week 2 of term 1. Currently, 231 students enrol annually (462 total in program at St. George). The UTM cohort will have courses delivered on the UTM campus with simulation learning at UTM/St. George and clinical practicum placements focused at Trillium Health Partners in the Peel Region. Goals include facilitating access for diverse students from Mississauga and surrounding communities, increasing BScN awareness among UTM undergraduates, placing graduates in Mississauga healthcare workforce, expanding collaboration with regional health providers, and addressing healthcare workforce shortages in the region.
documents.gradRelevance: The BScN expansion to UTM is an undergraduate second-entry program with no graduate-level component; it does not directly affect graduate students, though infrastructure investments in simulation labs and clinical education resources may benefit the broader campus.

documents.title: Calendar of Business, 2025-26 — UTM Academic Affairs Committee
documents.summary: The calendar organizes academic business across five cycles for the 2025-26 academic year. Cycle 1 includes Portfolio Annual Action items. Cycle 2 addresses major modifications to Specialist in Computational Physics and Specialist in Theatre and Drama Studies. Cycle 3 covers graduate curriculum changes, undergraduate curriculum changes (Humanities, Sciences, Social Sciences), new PhD in Communication and Technology (requiring Governing Council approval), new Specialist in Accounting and Finance, and closures of Minor in Francophone Studies and Specialist in French & Italian. Cycle 4 includes major modifications to new Minors in Criminology/Law/Society and Health Geography, plus new co-op streams in BIO, MGT, MCS, and FSC. Cycle 5 covers annual reports (Academic Appeals, Academic Integrity, Committee on Standing, Library, Student Awards, Vice-Principal Research) and the UTM Academic Plan and UTQAP reviews.
documents.gradRelevance: Cycle 3 specifically includes graduate curriculum changes and the PhD in Communication and Technology program (requiring Governing Council approval), both directly affecting graduate academic offerings and governance.

packages.title: UTM Academic Affairs Committee - September 10, 2025
packages.summary: On September 10, 2025, the UTM Academic Affairs Committee heard presentations on academic priorities and a major nursing program expansion. Prof. William Gough (Interim Vice-Principal Academic & Dean) reported that UTM had 16,191 students as of September 2, 2025 (4,275 newly admitted, 11,916 returning), with domestic enrollment exceeding targets despite international shortfalls. New programs in development include a specialist in accounting and finance, UTM's first locally administered PhD in media communications technology, and an MFA in creative theater practice. Dean Robyn Stremler announced the BScN (Bachelor of Science in Nursing) program expansion to UTM beginning Fall 2026, adding 30 new student seats funded through MCURES provincial funding and a one-time $500,000 allocation for a simulation and clinical education laboratory. The Research Opportunity Program (ROP) maintained its $280,000 annual funding envelope, supporting approximately 168 of 179 eligible projects in the most recent cycle. Graduate enrollment at UTM was 379 students total (193 newly admitted, 186 returning).
packages.gradRelevance: Graduate students are directly affected by several initiatives: Ron Buliung's role as Interim Vice-Dean, Graduate & Postdoctoral Affairs oversees all graduate programs, governance, quality assurance, curriculum, admissions, and distribution of graduate fellowships and prizes at UTM. The committee noted graduate enrollment increased marginally to 379 students (306 domestic, 73 international), though the paused MMPA (professional accounting) program is being retooled for relaunch. New graduate programs under development include a PhD in Media Communications Technology and a PhD in Quantitative Biosciences and Translational Sciences, though no funding amounts or timelines for these programs were specified. The presentation on nursing expansion did not explicitly address graduate-level nursing programming or funding for graduate students in that program.
keyItems: UTM enrollment as of September 2, 2025: 16,191 total students (4,275 newly admitted domestic/international; 11,916 returning); graduate enrollment 379 total (193 newly admitted, 186 returning) with marginal increases except MMPA program paused for retooling

BScN (Bachelor of Science in Nursing) expansion to UTM starting Fall 2026: 30 new student seats, $500,000 one-time provincial (MCURES) funding for simulation and clinical education laboratory with high- and low-fidelity mannequins; simulcast technology to connect courses across St. George and UTM campuses; clinical placements focused on Trillium Health Partners in Peel Region

documents.title: Professor William Gough Portfolio Report - OVPAD
documents.summary: Prof. Gough's presentation details the Office of the Vice-Principal, Academic & Dean (OVPAD) organizational structure and leadership appointments including new Associate Dean for Faculty Anna Korteweg (appointed January 2025). The report specifies five Vice-Dean portfolios (Undergraduate: Margarida Duarte; Academic Programs: Bryan Stewart; Faculty: Alison Syme; Graduate & Postdoctoral Affairs: Ron Buliung) and two Associate Dean portfolios (Academic Experience: Vivienne Luk; Teaching & Learning: Sanja Hinic-Frlog). Academic priorities for 2025-26 focus on the academic plan 'Realizing Our Potential,' Indigenous curricula, co-op expansion, academic integrity case resolution via Advocate by Symplicity, and health professions pathways. Eight UTQAP reviews are underway with five proceeding to governance (MCS, Management, Historical Studies, CPS, IMI) and three site visits planned. New programs include specialist in accounting and finance, PhD in media communications technology, MFA in creative theater practice, and micro-credentialing certificates. Enrollment data: UTM 16,191 total students as of September 2, 2025 (4,275 newly admitted, 11,916 returning); graduate 379 total (193 newly admitted, 186 returning).
documents.gradRelevance: Ron Buliung as Interim Vice-Dean, Graduate & Postdoctoral Affairs directly oversees all graduate program governance, planning, quality assurance, curriculum, admissions, enrolment, and distribution of graduate fellowships and prizes. Graduate enrollment increased marginally to 379 students; new graduate programs under development include PhD in Media Communications Technology and PhD in Quantitative Biosciences and Translational Sciences, though no funding details or completion timelines provided. The report does not specify funding allocations for graduate student support or stipends.

documents.title: BScN Program Expansion to UTM Presentation
documents.summary: Dean Robyn Stremler presents the expansion of the Bachelor of Science in Nursing (BScN) program to UTM, effective Fall 2026. The program adds 30 new student seats funded through MCURES provincial funding and a one-time $500,000 allocation to establish a simulation and clinical education laboratory on UTM campus with high- and low-fidelity mannequins. The BScN is a 2-year full-time second-entry program with 231 students enrolled annually (462 total in program at St. George); applicants must have completed 10+ university full course equivalents; students complete 1,216 hours of clinical learning. The UTM cohort will use simulcast technology to connect courses across St. George and UTM campuses and focus clinical placements at Trillium Health Partners facilities and community organizations in Peel Region. Goals include creating diverse pathways for Mississauga/Peel Region students, increasing awareness among UTM undergraduates, retaining graduates in the region, and expanding collaboration with regional healthcare providers.
documents.gradRelevance: No graduate-level programming or graduate student funding addressed in this presentation. The nursing expansion targets second-entry undergraduates and does not specify whether graduate-level nursing specializations or master's tracks will be offered at UTM or how graduate nursing students may be affected.

packages.title: UTM Campus Affairs Committee - April 29, 2026
packages.summary: The April 29, 2026 UTM Campus Affairs Committee meeting reviewed four major areas: (1) Recreation, Athletics & Wellness operations reporting 240,000+ tracked engagements and 9,300 unique students served through DRAW programming including inclusive drop-in/registered programs and Move Mentors; (2) Student groups growth from 213 (2024-25) to 254 recognized groups in 2025-26 with 54 additional applications under review (308 total active), totaling 16,059 cumulative members at an average of 63 members per group, supported by Centre for Student Engagement through early recognition portal launch on July 31, 2025; (3) Campus Safety 2025 operations with 16 Special Constables and 5 Building Patrollers reporting 208 total crime occurrences (up from 118 in 2024) driven primarily by theft increases, plus introduction of new Safety & Crisis Response Coordinator Melanie Fujs; (4) Capital projects including Residence Phase IX at 65% completion for September 2026 opening (400-bed dormitory), Campus Heart & Indigenous Build with schematic design 100% complete targeting 2029 opening, and Central Utilities Plant II pre-design underway with CaPS approval targeted for September 2026.
packages.gradRelevance: Graduate students are directly affected by: (1) Residence Phase IX 400-bed housing project opening September 2026, providing additional on-campus accommodation; (2) UTM Association of Graduate Students (UTMAGS) listed as recognized group #181, eligible for funding/space via Centre for Student Engagement; (3) Chemical and Physical Sciences Graduate Student Association (CPS GSA) recognized as group #32, indicating grad-specific programming support; (4) Campus Safety mental health training expansion (28 hours of UTM Living Works Safe Talk training for 7 officers, 40 hours of Mental Health Awareness training, new trauma-informed Safety & Crisis Response Coordinator) improving support services; (5) Capital projects for academic spaces including DV1015 Bat Research Facility and GGE Academic Workshop potentially supporting graduate research.
keyItems: Department of Recreation, Athletics & Wellbeing (DRAW) reported 240,000+ tracked engagements and 9,300 unique students engaged in 2025-26 through sports, aquatics, fitness, and wellness programs including Move Mentors (in 2nd year), BIPOC/PRIDE/Adaptive programming, and special events like Bhangra for Wellness and Elevate UTM

Student groups recognized increased from 213 in 2024-25 to 254 in 2025-26; 54 additional applications under review for total of 308 active applications; 16,059 cumulative members at average 63 per group; no groups denied recognition or had recognition withdrawn in 2025-26

Centre for Student Engagement accelerated Student Organization Portal launch to July 31, 2025 (from traditional August) to increase early recognition and participation in September 2025 Orientation programming with expanded group participation

Campus Safety reported 208 total crime occurrences in 2025 (up from 118 in 2024); significant increases in theft under $5,000 (+51 cases to 86 total), mischief/damage (+22 to 40), and other offences (+24 to 32); decreases in assaults (-2 to 9), sexual assaults (-3 to 2), and criminal harassment (-9 to 21)

Campus Safety staffing maintained at 16 Special Constables across 4 platoons with no new hires in 2025; 5 Building Patrollers (licensed security guards) employed; new Safety & Crisis Response Coordinator position created and filled by Melanie Fujs (first coordinator at UTM) providing trauma-informed mental health crisis response

Campus Safety completed extensive training: mandatory training including 96 hours of use of force recertification (12 officers via TNT Consulting) and 48 hours first aid/CPR (3 officers via St. John Ambulance); additional mental health training totaling 280 hours of Mental Health Awareness (7 officers via Peel Police), 64 hours violence/mental health risk assessment (4 officers), and 40 hours trauma-informed transfer of care (10 officers via HCC)

documents.title: Strategic Presentation: Recreation, Athletics & Wellness on Campus
documents.summary: Department of Recreation, Athletics & Wellbeing (DRAW) presented by Melissa Gallo (Interim Assistant Dean Student Support and Success) and Peter Mumford (Director Recreation & Wellbeing) reporting 240,000+ total tracked engagements, 9,300 unique students served, and 13,000+ total unique users including staff/faculty/community. Programs include sports, aquatics, fitness, wellbeing, UTM Moves, community outreach; inclusive programming features BIPOC/PRIDE/inclusive sport, adaptive swimming, low-sensory fitness, Learn 2 Play. Move Mentors initiative in 2nd year targets equity-deserving audiences with low-barrier beginner-friendly programming. Special events include Bhangra for Wellness, Elevate UTM (Black Wellness), Adaptive Sport Day, Unified Sport. New initiatives launched: Strategic Plan, 5-year operating budget, Capital Lifecycle plan, Student Advisory Committee, Rec Zone.
documents.gradRelevance: Recreation facilities and programs available to graduate students through DRAW; Move Mentors and inclusive programming (adaptive, 2SLGBQT+, BIPOC, women-identified) support wellbeing for all student populations; capital lifecycle planning suggests facility improvements that may benefit grad student access to athletics/wellness services.

documents.title: Annual Report Recognized Student Groups
documents.summary: Report from Tim Fricker (Interim Dean of Student Experience & Wellbeing) on student group recognition for 2025-26 academic year. Recognized groups increased from 213 in 2024-25 to 254 in 2025-26 with 54 applications under review (308 total active); 16,059 cumulative members at average 63 per group; no denials or withdrawals. Centre for Student Engagement (CSE) accelerated Student Organization Portal launch to July 31, 2025 (versus August previously), increasing early recognition and Orientation participation. CSE provides on-campus space booking and group funding. Policy recognizes 254 groups granted recognition until July 31, 2026. Groups must commit to democracy, accountability, open membership (with exceptions for non-voting status). University provides right to use U of T name, facility access at reduced/no cost, office space eligibility, directory listings, verification letters.
documents.gradRelevance: UTM Association of Graduate Students (UTMAGS) and Chemical and Physical Sciences Graduate Student Association (CPS GSA) listed as recognized groups; graduate students benefit from expanded student group ecosystem (16,059 members across 254 groups); Centre for Student Engagement funding and space privileges accessible to grad-led organizations; increased student belonging and engagement supports graduate student experience.

documents.title: Student Groups Report 2025/26 Presentation
documents.summary: Presentation by Tim Fricker (Interim Dean, Student Experience & Wellbeing) highlighting 3-year student group growth trend. Identified four core values: contributes to student sense of belonging, develops leadership skills, builds community amongst peers, provides co-curricular engagement. Graph shows 3-year growth trajectory. Contact info: studentgroup.utm@utoronto.ca; website: utm.utoronto.ca/utm-engage/student-groups.
documents.gradRelevance: Graduate students benefit from student group co-curricular engagement opportunities; leadership development and community-building particularly relevant to graduate student sense of belonging and integration into UTM campus community.

documents.title: Annual Report: UTM Campus Safety (2025)
documents.summary: Campus Safety annual report from Deborah Fritz (Director, Campus Safety) for 2025 operations. Staffing: 16 Special Constables across 4 platoons (Platoons A-D led by Ruben Sidhu, Jen Cowan, Parveen Kambo, Noel Hall); 5 Building Patrollers; new Safety & Crisis Response Coordinator position filled by Melanie Fujs (first in this role). Crime statistics 2025: 208 total occurrences (up from 118 in 2024); theft under $5,000 increased +51 to 86 cases; mischief/damage +22 to 40; other offences +24 to 32; assaults -2 to 9; sexual assaults -3 to 2; criminal harassment -9 to 21. Other activity: alarms +94 to 246; medical assistance +24 to 181; mental health calls +4 to 53. Mandatory training: 12 officers completed 96 hours use of force via TNT Consulting; 3 officers completed 48 hours first aid/CPR. Additional training totaled 750+ hours including 280 hours mental health awareness (7 officers via Peel Police), 64 hours violence/mental health risk assessment (4 officers via Protect International), 40 hours trauma-informed transfer of care (10 officers via HCC). Campus Safety serves on Wellbeing & Belonging Committee, Comprehensive Student Support Committee; collaborates with Health & Counselling Centre, Recreation Athletics & Wellness on events (UTM Walks, UTM Defends self-defence), local law enforcement agencies. Community engagement initiatives: In Power Self Defense, Cram the Cruiser, raising Pride flag, orientation, departmental audits, active attacker training, de-escalation workshops, therapy dog sessions, UTM Walks, grad school garden party, Be Well Fair, Party with Plan, residence training. Zero complaints against Special Constables in 2025.
documents.gradRelevance: Campus Safety mental health training expansion (280 hours mental health awareness, trauma-informed approaches, 40 hours HCC transfer of care) directly supports graduate student mental health crisis response; new Safety & Crisis Response Coordinator Melanie Fujs provides individual-centered, trauma-informed crisis support; Crime statistics show increased theft (+51 cases under $5k) affecting graduate students; Community Safety participates in Comprehensive Student Support Committee identifying at-risk students; collaboration with Health & Counselling Centre and therapeutic services relevant to graduate wellbeing.

documents.title: Capital Projects Update
documents.summary: Presentation by Luke Barber (Executive Director, Digital & Physical Infrastructure) on capital projects status as of April 29, 2026. Major projects: (1) Residence Phase IX: 400-bed, 6-story dormitory-style residence, 65% construction complete as of March 2026, on track for September 2026 opening. (2) Campus Heart & Indigenous Build: schematic design 100% complete by Smoke Architecture and Moriyama Teshima; phased project with Indigenous Build on-track for full governance approval in 26/27 cycle, 2029 opening. (3) Central Utilities Plant II: CaPS Approval for Terms of Reference received November 2025; pre-design (output specification and DB procurement) underway; on track for CaPS design-build services approval September 2026, October 2029 opening. Level 1 capital projects include new spaces (Bat Research Facility DV1015, Indigenous Creation Studio, GGE Academic Workshop, Oscar Peterson Hall pre-engineered building) and deferred maintenance (P4/P8 parking restoration, Davis air handling upgrade, Davis research block UPS, CCT parking garage fiber upgrades, master services upgrades).
documents.gradRelevance: Residence Phase IX (400-bed) provides significantly expanded on-campus housing for graduate students targeting September 2026 opening; Bat Research Facility (DV1015) and GGE Academic Workshop support graduate research infrastructure; Indigenous Build component of Campus Heart project supports Indigenous graduate student programming/spaces (2029 opening); Central Utilities Plant II (October 2029 opening) ensures campus infrastructure supports expanded graduate student population.

packages.title: UTM Campus Affairs Committee - March 10, 2026
packages.summary: The UTM Campus Affairs Committee met on March 10, 2026, to review a balanced $372M operating budget for UTM's 2026-27 fiscal year with only 1% growth, down from a projected $22M revenue shortfall compared to prior year's long-range plan. The committee approved a comprehensive compulsory non-academic fee increase package for UTMSU students, including a $0.88/session increase to the society fee (full-time), a $4.97/session spike in the MiWay U-Pass fee, and smaller increases across 11 additional fees (Student Centre Levy $0.26, CFS $0.24, Regenesis UTM $0.11, HOUSE UTM $0.11, WUSC Levy $0.05, among others), resulting in total full-time UTMSU fee increases of $6.77/session (from $500.80 to $507.57). The committee also considered revisions to its Terms of Reference to replace specific job titles (Chief Administrative Officer, Dean of Student Affairs) with generic functional descriptions ("senior officer responsible for financial matters," "senior officer responsible for student affairs and services") to avoid future revisions when organizational structures change.
packages.gradRelevance: For graduate students at UTM, the fee increases directly raise compulsory non-academic incidental fees beginning Fall 2026, though most increases are modest (2.1% inflation-indexed). The budget document indicates student financial aid increased 85% over the past seven years and projects continued investment in student success and mental health services. However, graduate-specific impacts are limited in these materials; professional graduate students see a projected 3.2% decline in total continuing enrollment (from 402 to 353 students), and international graduate intake is projected down 42.9% (from 91 to 52 students). The budget mentions $408M in institutional student financial support across all programs, but does not break out graduate-specific scholarship amounts or TA/RA funding changes.
keyItems: UTM's balanced 2026-27 operating budget is $372M net revenue (after $86M U of T contributions and $55M university-wide cost deductions), representing only 1.1% growth over 2025-26 due to international enrolment softening caused by federal immigration policy changes (35% reduction in international student permits announced in 2024, with additional 10% and 7% cuts in 2025-2026)

International fee revenue projected to decrease by $43M in 2026-27; international undergraduate direct-entry intake reduced by 648 students to 1,032 from prior targets; risk to budget of approximately $29M if Fall 2026 international intake remains at Fall 2025 levels

UTMSU full-time undergraduate students face combined compulsory non-academic fee increase of $6.77 per session (fall/winter) from $500.80 to $507.57; part-time students increase by $5.35 per session from $184.49 to $189.84; Mississauga Academy of Medicine students increase by $9.26 per fall session and $9.25 per winter session

MiWay U-Pass fee increases by $4.97/session (largest single component of the fee increase package), justified as inflationary adjustment approved by previous referendum allowing up to 9% increases per UTMSU Board of Directors resolution at January 30, 2026 meeting

Academic division investment increased 7% year-over-year for 2026-27 (up 58% over past seven years); 26 faculty growth hires since 2022-23 achieved 1:27 faculty-to-student ratio improved from 1:36 over ten years

UTM allocating 6% of international undergraduate tuition revenue toward merit-based scholarships for international students from diverse regions; scholarship tiers set at $25K, $45K, and new third tier of $15K annually, renewable for three years

documents.title: Overview of the UTM Campus Operating Budget: 2026-27
documents.summary: Official presentation document describing UTM's 2026-27 operating budget context, highlighting a balanced $372M net revenue budget (after deductions) representing only 1% growth due to reduced international enrolment from federal immigration policy changes. Document identifies $22M revenue shortfall versus prior year long-range plan projections, notes Ontario's recent 10% domestic tuition cut and freeze, declining real value of provincial operating grants (fallen from 40% to 20% of institutional revenues over 20 years), and rising compensation costs post-Bill 124. Outlines budget tactics of maintaining streamlined capital projects, drawing on reserves judiciously, deferring new strategic initiatives, and reducing administrative shared services expenses by 5% through discretionary spending cuts and workforce planning.
documents.gradRelevance: Graduate students affected by enrolment planning showing projected 3.2% decline in professional graduate continuing student enrollment and 42.9% decline in international graduate intake. Budget indicates investment in student mental health services and academic advising as strategic priorities, though no specific grad student funding amounts provided.

documents.title: Presentation: U of T Mississauga 2026-27 Operating budget overview
documents.summary: Visual slide presentation providing detailed enrolment targets, revenue composition, expense breakdown, and budget tactics for UTM's 2026-27 fiscal year. Gross revenue $458M composed of 62% operating grants, 15% tuition & fees, 8% UF allocation, 7% self-funded student services, 4% divisional revenue & recoveries, and 4% other; net revenue $372M after $86M U of T contributions and $55M university-wide costs. Major expenses: 45% faculty & librarian compensation, 28% staff compensation, 11% facilities & maintenance, 10% deferred maintenance & renovations. Enrolment targets: 3,694 total undergraduate intake (1,032 domestic, 1,180 international, down 248 from prior projection); 15,079 continuing undergraduates; 353 professional graduate students (down from 402). Student scholarships prioritize 6% of international tuition revenue allocation toward merit scholarships at $15K, $25K, and $45K annual tiers.
documents.gradRelevance: Shows professional graduate continuing student enrollment declining 3.2% year-over-year; international graduate new students projected down 42.9% due to immigration policy restrictions. Document identifies student mental health fostering and accommodations access as strategic investment priorities.

documents.title: Compulsory Non-Academic Incidental Fees: UTM Student Societies, UTMSU - Proposals for Fee Increases for 2026-27
documents.summary: Detailed governance document with recommendation for approval of comprehensive UTMSU fee increase package effective Fall 2026. Full-time undergraduate fees increase total $6.77/session (from $500.80 to $507.57); part-time increase $5.35/session (from $184.49 to $189.84); Mississauga Academy of Medicine students increase $9.26/$9.25 per fall/winter session. Component increases: society fee $0.88 (full-time)/$0.03 (part-time); MiWay U-Pass $4.97; Student Centre Levy $0.26 (full-time)/$0.08 (part-time); Academic Societies $0.03; Blind Duck $0.07; CFS $0.24 (full-time); Downtown Legal Services $0.03 (full-time); Food Centre $0.01; HOUSE UTM $0.11 (full-time)/$0.06 (part-time); First Aid Emergency Response $0.01; Regenesis UTM $0.11 (full-time)/$0.06 (part-time); WUSC Levy $0.05 (full-time fall/winter) and $0.02 (full-time summer). All increases comply with Policy for Compulsory Non-Academic Incidental Fees; those at or below 2.1% Ontario December 2025 CPI require prior referendum approval of cost-of-living principle; MiWay increases up to 9% permitted under original referendum authorization.

Graduate students affiliated with UTM (School of Graduate Studies and Mississauga Academy of Medicine) likely pay subset of these fees and would be affected by increases to shared services they utilize (student centre, accessibility services, health/dental).

documents.title: Revisions to the UTM Campus Affairs Committee Terms of Reference
documents.summary: Governance document proposing minor revisions to UTM Campus Affairs Committee Terms of Reference approved for information. Changes replace specific job titles in the Presidential Assessor roster with generic functional descriptions to align with UTM administrative restructuring: "Chief Administrative Officer" becomes "senior officer responsible for financial matters" and "Dean of Student Affairs" becomes "senior officer responsible for student affairs and services." These changes avoid need for future TOR revisions when organizational titles or structures change. Committee maintains 34-member composition with quorum at one-third of voting members (normally 10). Document outlines full committee responsibilities for budget, campus security, capital projects, student societies, compulsory non-academic fees, and campus master plans, with approval levels specified for governance council, UTM council, or committee itself.
documents.gradRelevance: No direct grad student impact; procedural governance matter ensuring appropriate administrative roles maintain oversight of matters affecting student life and campus resources, including those affecting graduate student constituencies.

documents.title: Budget Report 2026-27 and Long-Range Budget Guidelines 2026-27 to 2030-31
documents.summary: Comprehensive institutional budget report presenting University of Toronto's balanced $3.66B operating budget for 2026-27 with only 1.1% growth over prior year and approximately $100M less revenue than anticipated in prior long-range plan. Driven by recalibration of international enrolment targets following federal immigration policy reductions (35% cut in 2024, additional 10% in 2025, 7% in 2026) and continued domestic tuition freeze (adjusted for inflation, domestic fees are 30% lower than 2018-19). Enrolment-related revenues (tuition and operating grants) represent 85% of operating budget at $3.1B. Document identifies key challenges: continued decline in real value of provincial operating grants (from 40% to 20% of revenues over 20 years), rising compensation costs from Bill 124 repeal, post-pandemic inflation on construction. Proposes institutional responses including 100 new emerging scholar positions, digital strategy investments for AI-readiness, and capital investments in student housing and advanced research space. Compensation costs represent 65% of university expenses; pension risk contingency stable at $50M annually over planning period.
documents.gradRelevance: University-wide document mentioning $408M total institutional student financial support (UTAPS and divisional programs) but not specifying graduate amounts. Notes Federal government global talent recruitment initiative capitalizing on $4.1M Provost allocation; indicates investments in U of T Co-op and student mental health ($4.2M); but graduate student support, TA/RA funding, and graduate-specific scholarship commitments not detailed. Graduate recruitment affected by international permit reductions and recalibrated international enrolment plans.

packages.title: UTM Campus Affairs Committee - March 10, 2026
packages.summary: On March 10, 2026, the UTM Campus Affairs Committee approved the 2026-27 operating budget of approximately $372 million in campus-specific operating revenue, with no cuts to the 19 academic departments and a 7% year-over-year increase in academic budgets. To address a projected $22 million revenue shortfall, UTM will defer select expenditures, increase summer enrollment targets, and reduce campus shared services by 5% through vacancy management. Student financial support will total $7.1 million, stabilized after 85% growth since 2019, with international students receiving new renewable scholarship tiers of $15,000, $25,000, and $45,000 annually. The Committee also approved multiple UTMSU fee increases for Fall 2026, including a $4.97 increase in the MiWay U-Pass fee per session, along with smaller increases across 12 other fee categories such as student society membership ($0.88), student centre levy ($0.26), and CFS ($0.24 for full-time). The Committee noted CAC terms of reference revisions to align with new administrative titles, including replacing 'Chief Administrative Officer' with 'senior officer responsible for financial matters' and 'Dean of Student Affairs' with 'Dean of Student Experience and Well-being.'
packages.gradRelevance: Graduate students are minimally affected by this meeting's decisions. The student financial support figure of $7.1 million is institutional and does not break out graduate-specific aid or TA/RA funding. The UTMSU fee increases apply only to undergraduate students represented by UTMSU and do not directly affect graduate students. No graduate housing, graduate stipend adjustments, graduate union matters (UTGSU), or graduate program-specific policies were discussed. Graduate students may be indirectly affected by academic budget increases of 7% year-over-year and the addition of 26 net new faculty positions, which could influence research funding and academic supervision opportunities.
keyItems: 2026-27 campus operating budget set at approximately $372 million in campus-specific revenue, with gross revenue of $458 million before $86 million allocated to tri-campus student aid and shared services

Academic department and decanal budgets will increase by 7% year-over-year, with no cuts to 19 academic departments; 26 net new faculty positions added over the past five years with $50 million in base funding growth since 2019

To address $22 million revenue shortfall: no academic cuts; instead prudent use of reserves, deferral of select planned expenditures, 5% reduction in campus shared services through vacancy management, and increased summer enrollment targets

Student financial support stabilized at $7.1 million for 2026-27 after 85% growth since 2019; international students now eligible for renewable scholarships of $15,000, $25,000, and $45,000 annually; UTM contributing 6% of international tuition to scholarships
$2 million in annual one-time-only investment over two years for teaching, research, academic advising, recruitment, student mental health, accommodations, and digital infrastructure

Domestic intake intentionally exceeded target for third consecutive year: 3,118 domestic students registered in Fall 2024 versus 2,662 target; Fall 2026 target remains at 2,662 until provincial Strategic Mandate Agreement (SMA4) corridor guidance received

packages.title: UTM Campus Affairs Committee - January 26, 2026
packages.summary: On January 26, 2026, the UTM Campus Affairs Committee approved 2026-27 ancillary budgets and fee increases effective May 1, 2026: residence rates adjusted by type (with family housing unchanged to preserve affordability), meal plans increasing 2.5% for required plans and 5.6% for optional plans, parking permits rising 2.5–3.0% with no hourly/daily increases, and a new 265-unit residence building (400 beds, LEED-certified, opening Fall 2026) projected to expand capacity to 2,000 beds. The Committee also approved a Health Services Fee increase from $79.95 to $85.81 (7.33%), Recreation & Athletics Fee from $223.01 to $234.16 (5.00% including 3.00% temporary), and Student Services Fee from $307.70 to $320.48 (4.15%), while Quality of Service to Students endorsed Health and Student Services fees but not Recreation & Athletics due to concerns about student funding of capital costs. UTMAGS fees increased $4.87 per fall/winter session and $2.44/$2.43 per fall/winter for MiWay U-Pass costs. Fall 2025 enrolment showed a shift to 24% international students (down from 26% due to federal study permit policy changes) and Computer Science/Math/Statistics became the largest admission category (18.4%) with 70.8% graduation rates within six years.
packages.gradRelevance: Graduate students at UTM pay UTMAGS fees, which increased by $4.87 per fall/winter session plus $2.44–$2.43 for summer U-Pass due to MiWay transit cost increases (effective Summer 2026). Graduate students also pay the approved Health Services Fee (now $85.81 sessional), Recreation & Athletics Fee (now $234.16, 5.00% increase), Student Services Fee (now $320.48, 4.15% increase), plus cross-divisional fees for UTGSU and Hart House. The new residence building opening Fall 2026 will expand on-campus housing capacity from approximately 1,600 to 2,000 beds, potentially affecting graduate housing availability and demand. No TA/RA stipends, teaching assistant contracts, or graduate-specific housing policies were discussed in this meeting.

packages.title: UTM Campus Affairs Committee - October 16, 2025
packages.summary: On October 16, 2025, the UTM Campus Affairs Committee received presentations on experiential education programs, student health services, strategic priorities, and first-year student engagement data. The Experiential Education Unit presented three programs: Research Opportunity Programs (427 students in 231 ROP offerings in 2024-25), Academic Internships (391 students across humanities, sciences, and social sciences), and the newly expanded UTM Co-op Internship Program (UTMCIP), which launched in Fall 2024 with 247 registered students across 17 programs in 5 departments, projecting growth to 770 students by 2034-35. The Health & Counselling Centre reported 15,968 total appointments in 2024-25 (26% increase from prior year), including 9,352 mental health and 6,616 physical health visits, with 43% growth in counselling appointments and 87% satisfaction with wait times. UTM's 2025-26 strategic priorities include completing program proposals for an MFA in creative theatre practice, PhD in media communication technologies, and a specialist in accounting and finance; expanding research funding; launching a UTM-based nursing program in 2026; and advancing Indigenous building design and Indigenous studies curriculum. The Before College Survey of Student Engagement (BCSSE) for 2025-26 incoming cohort showed 33% first-generation students, 69% selected UTM as first choice, 77% relying on parental support, and 56% expecting A grades (compared to 43% earning A's in high school).
packages.gradRelevance: The presentations have limited direct graduate relevance. The Experiential Education Unit's Research Opportunity Program may provide pipeline pathways for high-achieving undergraduates to pursue graduate study, but the program itself targets undergraduates. The Health & Counselling Centre expansion (15,968 total appointments, 43% growth in counselling) serves all students including graduates, though no graduate-specific enrollment or utilization data was provided. The strategic priority for a PhD in media communication technologies represents new graduate programming. The BCSSE data concerns incoming first-year undergraduates only and does not address graduate student demographics, funding, housing, TA/RA matters, or unionized labour concerns.
keyItems: Research Opportunity Program (ROP): 427 registered students across 231 course offerings in 2024-25, faculty-mentored research for high-achieving undergraduates developing skills for graduate study or research careers; eligible faculty include full-time, part-time, CLTAs, LTAs, and associate faculty; ineligible are adjunct, visiting scholars, postdocs, graduate course instructors, librarians, and sessional lecturers

Academic Internships: 391 total student enrolments in 2024-25 (51 humanities, 90 sciences, 250 social sciences); 100–200 hour placements over 4 or 8 months earning course credit; placements included Blackwood Gallery, Royal Ontario Museum, Peel Regional Police Forensic Identification, Crossroads International, and MP constituency offices

UTM Co-op Internship Program (UTMCIP) launched Fall 2024 with 247 registered students across 17 programs in 5 departments; 215 students (87%) enrolled in Computer Science programs (replacing U of T's PEY Co-op); 12- or 16-month paid full-time work terms with 5-year time-to-degree; 10-year growth projection to 770 students by 2034-35, adding 50 new students annually; Department of Management joined in Fall 2025 with 5 new program streams

Remote/Global Experiential Learning: ITA370H5 Machiavelli course includes virtual tours of Italian cities; EDS388Y5 Education Studies Practicum placed students in Brazil, China, Taiwan; FSC481Y5 Forensic Science Internship in France; UTMCIP offers paid work in USA with major tech companies

Health & Counselling Centre: 15,968 total appointments in 2024-25 (26% increase from 2023-24); 9,352 mental health appointments; 6,616 physical health appointments; 43% increase in counselling appointments; same-day appointments available; evening hours 2 nights/week; 87% of respondents satisfied with wait times; one-at-a-time counselling model with statistically significant reduction in distress post-appointment

Health Promotion Initiatives: 15 events with 4,000+ participants; 86 partner organizations; 15 Peers Supporting Peers (PSP) mentors offering 1:1 meetings, tabling, and drop-ins with 650+ total interactions; 1,704 student visitors (326% increase from 2023-24); expanded Ask the Nurse operational improvements; 2 grant-funded pilot projects (UofT ISEF international student peer mentorship, Peel Public Health digital health initiative)

documents.title: Beyond the Classroom: Curricular Experiential Learning at UTM
documents.summary: Presentation by Professor Vivienne Luk and Rena Banwait detailing three core experiential education programs at UTM. Research Opportunity Programs (ROPs) involve faculty-mentored research for high-achieving students, with 427 registered students in 231 offerings in 2024-25; eligible faculty include full-time, part-time, CLTAs, LTAs, and associate faculty, while adjunct, visiting scholars, postdocs, graduate course instructors, librarians, and sessional lecturers are ineligible. Academic Internships serve 391 students (51 humanities, 90 sciences, 250 social sciences) in 100–200 hour, unpaid course-integrated placements at organizations like Blackwood Gallery, Royal Ontario Museum, Peel Regional Police Forensic Identification, and MP constituency offices. UTM Co-op Internship Program (UTMCIP) launched Fall 2024 with 247 students across 17 programs in 5 departments (215 Computer Science, 10 Chemical & Physical Sciences, 10 Economics, 10 Institute of Communication/Culture/IT, 2 Biology); features mandatory work-readiness modules plus 12- or 16-month paid full-time work terms; projects 10-year enrollment growth to 770 students by 2034-35; Department of Management joined Fall 2025. Remote/global opportunities include virtual Italian city tours, teaching placements in Brazil/China/Taiwan, internships in France, and paid co-op positions in USA.

documents.title: Student Health Promotion Initiatives
documents.summary: Presentation by Tim Fricker (Interim Dean, Student Experience & Wellbeing) and Dr. Erin Kraftcheck (Medical Director) describing Health & Counselling Centre services and outcomes. Staff includes family physicians, registered nurses, psychiatrists, personal counsellors, dietitian, wellness navigation advisor/mental health care coordinator, health promotion professionals, student wellness ambassadors, and Peers Supporting Peers (PSP) mentors. In 2024-25, the centre provided 15,968 total appointments (26% increase from 2023-24): 9,352 mental health, 6,616 physical health, with 43% growth in counselling appointments; 87% satisfaction with wait times; same-day appointments available; evening hours 2 nights/week. Health Promotion delivered 15 events with 4,000+ participants and 86 partner organizations; 15 PSP mentors provided 650+ interactions via 1:1 meetings, tabling, and weekly drop-ins; 1,704 student visitors (326% increase). New initiatives include 2 grant-funded pilots (UofT ISEF international student peer mentorship, Peel Public Health digital health), expanded Ask the Nurse, operational improvements, and partnership with THP.
documents.gradRelevance: Health & Counselling Centre serves all students including graduate students, but presentation provides no graduate-specific enrollment, utilization, or outcomes data. The 43% growth in counselling and expansion of mental health services is relevant to all student populations, but no breakdown by student type (undergraduate vs. graduate) was provided.

documents.title: Status Report on UTM Campus Strategic Priorities

Strategic Framework aims to promote truth, openness, and reciprocity; foster student success; empower research discovery and impact; encourage collaboration and belonging; build efficient and sustainable operations; and embrace campus place. Presentation represents first step in governance, with previews of budget initiatives; Institution Operating Budget to follow in Cycle 3; Campus Operating Budget presentation in Cycle 4 covering enrolment, staff/faculty complement, expense priorities, and capital plans.

Research priorities: grow tri-council and other major research funding, increase SSHRC participation, facilitate high-value science collaborations, build research facilities including pre-engineered building. Collaboration and belonging: exceed anti-racism commitments through Black flourishing framework and campus equity network; publish wellbeing and belonging plan. Efficient operations: deliver balanced budget prioritizing research and teaching investment and streamlining administrative services; implement Project SHIFT sustainability and climate positive plans. Place-based connections: expand regional co-op, plan 2026 nursing program launch with BFoN and THP, develop locally-grounded fundraising for South Asian studies. documents.title: Before College Survey of Student Engagement (BCSSE): Student Preparedness for Learning
documents.summary: Presentation by Jonathan Davis (Analyst, Student Experience Research & Assessment) on BCSSE results for 2025-26 incoming cohort. BCSSE measures prior academic experiences, expectations for engagement in educationally purposeful activities, and links to learning, personal development, persistence, and graduation outcomes. 2025-26 cohort: 33% first-generation, 67% non-first-gen; 19% international, 13% permanent residents, 68% Canadian citizens; 69% selected UTM as first choice, 24% second choice, 7% third choice; funding sources: 77% parental support, 55% grants/scholarships, 46% personal savings, 41% loans, 28% employment; housing: 61% in residence farther than walking distance, 28% campus housing, 11% within walking distance, 0.4% homeless/in transition; grade expectations: 56% expect B's, 43% expect A's, 1% expect C's (vs. 0% A's, 7% B's, 93% C's in high school). Institutional expectations: 81% want academic support, 60% social opportunities and learning services, 59% diverse student interaction, 60% campus activities, 49% non-academic help, 26% challenging academics. BCSSE-NSSE comparison showed students overestimated work hours: expected 0 hrs (31%), 10-20 hrs (33%), 20+ hrs (35%) vs. actual first-year 0 hrs (62%), 10-20 hrs (30%), 20+ hrs (9%). Uses include personalized invitations, program triaging, data-informed communications, and data-informed programming.
documents.gradRelevance: BCSSE data exclusively concerns incoming first-year undergraduates. No graduate student demographic, engagement, or preparedness data provided. Not relevant to graduate student funding, TA/RA concerns, housing, or unionized labour matters.

packages.title: UTM Campus Affairs Committee - October 16, 2025
packages.summary: On October 16, 2025, UTM Campus Affairs Committee discussed experiential learning expansion, student health services, strategic priorities, and first-year student engagement. Professor Vivienne Luk presented three curricular programs: Research Opportunity Program (ROP) with 427 students in 231 projects in 2024–25; Academic Internships across Humanities, Sciences, and Social Sciences with 391 students; and the new University of Toronto Co-op Internship Program (UTMCIP) launched Fall 2024 with 247 first-year students, planned to grow 50 students annually to 770 by 2034–35. Dr. Erin Kraftcheck reported Health and Counselling Centre served nearly 16,000 appointments (60% mental health), with 90% student satisfaction on wait times, and two new grant-funded initiatives: International Student Experience Fund peer mentorship and Peel Public Health digital wellness programming. Vice-Principal Gillespie highlighted UTM's $22.3 million external research funding (70% increase), new nursing program launch in 2026 with $500,000 provincial funding and 30 funded seats via Bloomberg Faculty of Nursing partnership, and balanced budgets for five consecutive years with 49% academic investment growth since 2019–20. Jonathan Davis presented Beginning College Survey of Student Engagement data showing 33% first-generation students, 69% chose UTM as first choice, 50% had no existing UTM friendships, and 61% live beyond walking distance.
packages.gradRelevance: The ROP program directly supports graduate student pipelines, with faculty-mentored research particularly noted as ideal for students exploring graduate studies. UTMCIP expansion and academic internships provide professional development relevant to graduate career preparation. Health and Counselling Centre's mental health services expansion (60% of 15,968 appointments) and two new grants for peer mentorship and digital wellness support graduate student wellbeing. The nursing program launch in 2026 with 30 funded seats may create graduate-level opportunities. However, no specific graduate student funding increases, TA/RA rates, unionized labour matters, housing subsidies, or fees affecting graduate students were discussed or decided at this meeting.

documents.summary: Presentation by Jonathan Davis (October 16, 2025) analyzing BCSSE data from 1,798 incoming students (43% response rate), representing demographics consistent with institutional data. BCSSE data used for trend-tracking (grants/scholarships utilization tracked 2019–2025), personalized advising reports, program triaging, and comparison with end-of-year NSSE data showing expectations vs. reality gaps (e.g., 31% first-year students expected to work 0 hrs/week but 68% actually did not work).

packages.title: UTM Campus Affairs Committee - September 11, 2025
packages.summary: The September 11, 2025 UTM Campus Affairs Committee meeting presented portfolio overviews from senior administrators. Interim Vice-Principal Academic William Gough reported 379 total graduate students (306 domestic, 73 international) with eight UTQAP reviews underway; new graduate programs approved include a PhD in Media Communications Technology and an MFA in Creative Theatre Practice, while the PhD in Quantitative Biosciences and Translational Sciences is in early stages. Chad Nuttall presented the reimagined UTM Orientation model: the 2025 orientation engaged 2,500 new students across 7 days with 130+ programs, 38 departmental/student group events funded at $56K, and 158 student volunteers, replacing traditional academic sessions with three 'Academic Garden Parties' that attracted nearly 700 attendees.
packages.gradRelevance: Graduate student enrollment at UTM stands at 379 total (306 domestic, 73 international, with 193 newly admitted and 186 returning). Graduate students will benefit indirectly from the new 400-bed residence opening Fall 2026, the Career & Work program and Skills Accelerator rollout, new multipurpose rooms (DV1103, DV1104), and ongoing faculty recruitment and development under new Associate Dean for Faculty Anna Korteweg.
