documents.title: UTM Orientation: The Journey After Reimagining
documents.summary: Chad Nuttall, Assistant Dean of Students & International Initiatives, presented the reimagined UTM Orientation model implemented in 2024 in response to the term now beginning Tuesday after Labour Day. The reimagining process (Fall 2023) included literature review, CAS Standards review, Beginning College Survey of Student Engagement (BCSSE), themed focus groups (non-attendees, international, identity-based, commuter), post-orientation surveys, staff consultations, and benchmarking of 36 North American peers. Recommendations shifted from content-focused to community-focused programming with one unified schedule/website and concurrent departmental/student group events. UTM Orientation 2024 engaged 2,600 new students building their own schedules with 120+ programs over 8 days and positive student feedback. UTM Orientation 2025 (second year) registered 2,500 new students across 7 days with 130+ programs, 38 departmental and student group events funded with $56K, 152 student groups and campus partners in fairs, 158 student volunteers, and 'Academic Garden Parties' replacing traditional sessions (nearly 700 attended across three days). Future goals include continuous improvement to eventually reach all incoming students with expanded concurrent and larger-scale events.
documents.gradRelevance: While orientation primarily targets new undergraduate students, graduate students may participate as volunteers (158 volunteers in 2025 included upper-year students), through departmental/student group events (38 events with $56K funding), and in campus partner fairs (152 groups participated). Graduate orientation content and engagement strategies are not separately detailed.

documents.title: UTM Orientation 2025 Presentation
documents.summary: Chad Nuttall, Assistant Dean of Students & International Initiatives, presented UTM's reimagined orientation model implemented in response to a 2023 university decision to start classes the Tuesday after Labour Day. The redesign was informed by Council for the Advancement of Standards (CAS) review, Beginning College Survey of Student Engagement (BCSSE), student focus groups (international, commuters, students with disabilities, identity-based, non-attendees), post-orientation surveys, staff consultations, and benchmarking of 36 North American peers including Cornell, McMaster, Queen's, and Waterloo. Key student insights showed 50% of incoming students knew no one at UTM, one-third were first-generation university students, and 70% lived off-campus, necessitating in-person connection opportunities. The new model shifted from content-heavy to community-focused programming, with one centralized schedule/website, concurrent departmental and student group events, and inclusive accessibility. In 2024, 2,600 new students participated in 120+ programs over 8 days with very positive feedback. In 2025 (second year), 2,500 students participated in 130+ programs over 7 days, departments/student groups hosted 38 events with $56,000 financial support, 152 groups participated in fairs, 158 volunteers (upper-year students, alumni, staff/faculty) supported the program, and 'Academic Garden Parties' replaced academic sessions and attracted nearly 700 attendees.
documents.gradRelevance: Upper-year graduate students serve as volunteers (158 volunteers in 2025 included upper-year students) in the orientation program. Graduate student groups may participate in the departmental/student group events receiving funding support. International graduate students benefit from orientation's inclusive programming designed for diverse student populations including international attendees.

packages.title: UTM Campus Council - May 26, 2026
packages.summary: The UTM Campus Council meeting on May 26, 2026 will receive two major information presentations: (1) the UTM Library Strategic Plan 2026-2030, developed over 10 months by Chief Librarian Dany Savard's working group, outlining six strategic directions including student flourishing, digital innovation, special collections integration, equity and indigenization, adaptive culture, and regional partnerships—with no net budget implications; and (2) a Capital Projects Update from Luke Barber covering Level 1 projects ($100K–$10M) including a Bat Research Facility, Indigenous Creation Studio, and Oscar Peterson Hall, plus Major Projects including Residence Phase IX (400-bed dormitory 65% complete, opening September 2026), Campus Heart & Indigenous Build (schematic design complete, opening 2029), and Central Utilities Plant II (design-build approval expected September 2026, opening October 2029). The consent agenda includes three committee reports: Campus Affairs Committee Report 76 (April 29), Academic Affairs Committee Report 63 (April 28), and Agenda Committee Report 77 (May 12).
packages.gradRelevance: Graduate student relevance is limited but present in two areas: (1) Residence Phase IX's 400-bed dormitory opening September 2026 may affect graduate housing availability and costs; and (2) the UTM Library Strategic Plan's Goal A under Strategic Direction 1 explicitly commits to expanding 'co-curricular opportunities and embedded program-specific student experiences that facilitate deep learning' and 'research capacities' through workshops, which directly support graduate research and learning. The plan also establishes an AI literacy framework (Goal B) and enhanced research support services. No specific funding amounts, fee changes, TA/RA provisions, or unionized labour matters are discussed in this meeting.
keyItems: UTM Library Strategic Plan 2026-2030 presented by Chief Librarian Dany Savard; developed by working group of eight librarians and staff (Nga Foster, Christopher Long, Catherine MacGregor, Andrew Nicholson, Kathleen Scheaffer, Mike Serafin, Dorota Swieton) over 10 months in 2025; grounded in external review recommendations and tri-campus priorities; no net budget implications

Library Strategic Direction 1: Inspire student flourishing through spaces/services prioritizing academic attainment, career readiness, wellbeing; Goal A expands co-curricular library opportunities and program-specific experiences; Goal B creates AI literacy service framework with Institute for Study of University Pedagogy and Robert Gillespie Academic Skills Centre

Library Strategic Direction 3: Integrate special collections into curricula and research partnerships; supports curriculum development in Game Studies and South Asian Studies; establishes tri-campus solutions for archival access and preservation

Library Strategic Direction 4: Equity, diversity, inclusion and belonging; removes systemic barriers to physical/digital library access; embeds Indigenizing initiatives aligned with Wecheehetowin calls to action on Treaty 13A territory
Residence Phase IX major capital project: 400-bed, 6-storey dormitory-style residence; construction 65% complete as of March 2026 report; on track for September 2026 opening

Campus Heart & Indigenous Build major capital project: schematic design 100% complete by design team Smoke Architecture and Moriyama Teshima; Indigenous Build component on track for full governance approval in 2026-27 cycle; opening 2029

documents.title: UTM Library Strategic Plan Update
documents.summary: Cover memo from Vice-Principal Prof. Alexandra Gillespie and Chief Librarian Dany Savard presenting the 2026-2030 Library Strategic Plan for information. Indicates plan developed over 10 months in 2025 by all UTM librarians and staff through working groups; draws on 2023-24 external review recommendations and tri-campus library system priorities. Emphasizes that the library is at the heart of campus life and aligns with UTM's six strategic priorities. No net budget implications. Recommends information-only status.
documents.gradRelevance: Establishes library commitments to graduate student research support, expanded co-curricular opportunities, AI literacy programming, and research discovery acceleration over 2026-2030.

documents.title: UTM Library Strategic Plan 2026-2030: Outlining future goals and initiatives for growth (Report)
documents.summary: Full strategic plan document containing mission (proactive guidance, innovative instruction, seamless information access), vision (library as catalyst for student success and research excellence), and nine core values (resiliency, readiness, stewardship, indigenization, sustainability, equity, anti-oppression). Establishes six strategic directions with specific goals and outcomes: (1) inspire student flourishing through academic support and wellness; (2) accelerate learning through digital innovation and AI literacy; (3) integrate special collections into Game Studies and South Asian Studies curricula; (4) advance equity, diversity, inclusion, and indigenization; (5) build adaptive culture with staff investment; (6) strengthen partnerships with Peel Region and tri-campus system. Implementation via annual workplans 2026-2030 with meaningful metrics developed with partners.
documents.gradRelevance: Directly supports graduate student learning, research capacity, and academic success through expanded information literacy instruction, experiential learning, research support workshops, special collections access, AI literacy programming, and removal of systemic access barriers to library services and spaces.

documents.title: UTM Library Strategic Plan 2026-2030: Outlining future goals and initiatives for growth (Slides)
documents.summary: Presentation slides by Dany Savard summarizing library strategic plan overview. Details mission focus on guidance/instruction/access; vision positioning library as catalyst for student success and intellectual freedom; core values framework. Outlines six strategic directions: (1) student flourishing via academic attainment, career readiness, wellbeing; (2) digital innovation through tech-enhanced spaces and ethical AI use; (3) special collections improving access/infrastructure for Game Studies and South Asian Studies; (4) equity, inclusion, and indigenization removing barriers and amplifying diverse voices; (5) adaptive culture and staff development; (6) partnerships with community/heritage organizations and tri-campus cooperation. Notes moving forward via annual workplans, progress metrics, transparency, and continuous improvement.
documents.gradRelevance: Emphasizes library's role in supporting graduate-level research through special collections, digital innovation, AI literacy, and research support services integrated with campus partners.

packages.title: UTM Campus Council - May 26, 2026
packages.summary: On May 26, 2026, UTM Campus Council approved the appointment of William Gough as Vice-Principal, Academic and Dean for a five-year term beginning July 1, 2026. Vice-President & Principal Alexandra Gillespie reported record external research funding of $29.4 million in 2025–26 (130% increase since 2019-20), improved faculty-to-student ratio from 1:30 to 1:28, and approximately 100 additional graduates above historical averages. Council received updates on new academic programs (specialist in Accounting and Finance, minors in Logic and Health and Environment, certificates in AI and Society), expanded co-op programming with nine new streams, and a new Bachelor of Science in Nursing partnership with 30 funded seats. Capital projects included a six-storey, 400-bed residence opening Fall 2026 (75% complete), a Campus Heart and Indigenous Building project targeting 2029 completion pending governance approval, and a Central Utility Plant II geothermal project for decarbonization targeting 2029. Chief Librarian Dany Savard presented the UTM Library Strategic Plan 2026–2030 with six strategic directions including AI literacy support and expanded special collections access.
packages.gradRelevance: Research funding growth to $29.4 million affects graduate student research opportunities and funding availability. The new residence opening August 2026 with 25% increased capacity provides housing for graduate and undergraduate students. Student financial aid has increased 85% since 2019-20, benefiting graduate students receiving support. The new Bachelor of Science in Nursing program with 30 funded seats and expanded co-op programming with nine new streams may create TA/RA positions and experiential learning opportunities. Mental health services achieving 100% same-day or next-day access rate applies to all students including graduates. No mention of graduate stipend changes, TA/RA wage adjustments, or unionized labour matters.
keyItems: William Gough appointed Vice-Principal, Academic and Dean, UTM for five-year term effective July 1, 2026, following 20 months in interim role

Record external research funding of $29.4 million in 2025–26, representing 130% increase since 2019-20 and third consecutive year of record-setting performance; SSHRC Insight competition success rates now exceed tri-campus and national averages

Faculty-to-student ratio improved from 1:30 to 1:28 over past six years; 17 faculty searches underway with progress toward filling six Canada Research Chairs and two Canada Impact+ Research Chair positions

New academic programs approved: specialist in Accounting and Finance, specialist in Theatre and Drama (revised), minors in Logic, Health and Environment, and Criminology Law and Society; certificates in Economic Data Analytics, Ancient Languages, and AI and Society
Co-op programming expanded with nine new streams; enrolment in experiential education courses increased 143%; co-op enrolment projected to triple over next eight years
Bachelor of Science in Nursing partnership with Lawrence S. Bloomberg Faculty of Nursing launched with 30 newly funded seats and clinical placements within Trillium Health Partners

documents.title: UTM Library Strategic Plan, 2026–2030
documents.summary: Chief Librarian Dany Savard presented a ten-month consultation-developed strategic plan with six strategic directions: inspiring student flourishing through academic support and career readiness; accelerating learning through digital innovation and ethical AI engagement; expanding special collections and archives access; embedding equity, diversity, inclusion, belonging, and Indigenization; building adaptive organizational culture supporting staff development; strengthening Peel Region and tri-campus partnerships. Plan incorporates 2023-24 external review recommendations and tri-campus priorities. Implementation framework described as living and responsive process with measurable initiatives and annual workplan reporting.
documents.gradRelevance: AI literacy and ethical engagement programming through ISUP and Robert Gillespie Academic Skills Centre supports graduate student research and learning; expanded digital scholarship initiatives and special collections access directly benefit graduate research; equity and Indigenization initiatives align with graduate student support priorities.

packages.title: UTM Campus Council - April 13, 2026
packages.summary: On April 13, 2026, UTM Campus Council approved a balanced $372 million operating budget for 2026–27 despite a $22 million projected revenue shortfall caused by Ontario's 2019 tuition cut (reducing real revenues by 25%), pandemic costs, inflation, and federal immigration policy changes. The budget increases academic department funding by 7% year-over-year (adding to $50 million in base growth since 2019 and 26 new net faculty positions) while avoiding cuts to the 19 academic departments; savings come through reserve use, deferral of discretionary spending, and a 5% reduction in campus service portfolios requiring nine staff layoffs (less than 1% of workforce). Council also approved UTMSU compulsory non-academic incidental fee increases totaling $6.57 per full-time session (led by a $4.97 MiWay U-Pass increase), and heard that a comprehensive review of UTM's budget model and organizational structure will be launched in spring 2026 with findings due in January 2027. Additionally, Council received an information report on the ongoing Code of Student Conduct review, which engaged 350+ participants and recommends establishing a centralized Office of Student Conduct, dividing the Code into separate policy and procedures documents, expanding scope to explicitly address online harassment and AI-related misconduct, and introducing a mental health diversion pathway.
packages.gradRelevance: Graduate student Kelly Cheung was newly elected to Campus Council with a term beginning July 1, 2026. The budget maintains base funding growth in academic departments (up $50 million since 2019) that supports graduate programs, though international graduate enrollment targets fell 19% to 1,032 students due to federal immigration policy changes. The Code of Student Conduct review directly affects graduate students' conduct obligations and disciplinary processes, particularly the new mental health diversion pathway and centralized reporting model. However, no specific TA/RA wage information, graduate funding amounts, housing allocations, or other graduate-specific fee changes appear in this meeting; the $7.1 million student financial support increase benefits undergraduate and graduate students collectively but is not disaggregated by level.
keyItems: UTM 2026–27 operating budget: $372 million in campus-specific revenue; $458 million gross revenue (62% tuition, 15% provincial grants, 8% self-funded services, 7% university fund allocation, 4% divisional revenue, 4% other). UTM makes net positive contribution to university-wide budget (7% allocation of 14% retained institutional revenue).

Budget response to $22 million revenue shortfall: zero cuts to 19 academic departments or decanal/registrarial offices; academic budgets increased 7% year-over-year; savings through reserve use, deferral of discretionary spending, 5% reduction in campus service portfolios via vacancy management, and nine staff layoffs (eight full-time, one part-time).
Academic investment: $50 million+ in base funding growth to 19 academic departments and institutes since 2019; 26 new net faculty positions added.

International enrollment: intake target reduced 19% to 1,032 students (from 1,280 prior target); domestic target held at 2,662 despite actual 3,118 enrollment in fall 2024; overall 2026–27 intake target set at 3,694 (6% lower than previous years).

Student financial aid: $7.1 million allocated in 2026–27, stabilized after 85% growth since 2019; international scholarships include renewable awards of $25,000 and $45,000 annually, plus new $15,000 renewable tier (funded from previously withheld international tuition revenue).

UTMSU compulsory non-academic incidental fee increases approved for fall 2026: MiWay U-Pass +$4.97/session; society fee +$0.88/session; student centre levy +$0.26/session; CFS fee +$0.24/session (full-time only); Blind Duck +$0.07; WUSC levy +$0.05/fall–winter session; other smaller increases totaling approximately $6.57 full-time per session.

packages.title: UTM Campus Council - February 24, 2026
packages.summary: At the February 24, 2026 UTM Campus Council meeting, the university presented enrollment data showing 15,200 total full-time undergraduates with international students comprising one quarter of the population, alongside a balanced $3.66B institutional operating budget (1.1% increase) constrained by $2.43B in compensation spending that has grown 32% since 2021. Council approved 2026-27 service ancillary operating plans and fee increases effective May 1, 2026, including residence fees (varying by type to preserve affordability), hospitality/meal plan increases reflecting inflation, and parking fee adjustments. Three student fees were approved: Health Services Fee increasing 7.33% from $79.95 to $85.81 (full-time), Recreation & Athletics Fee increasing 5.00% from $223.01 to $234.16 (full-time), and Student Services Fee increasing 4.15% from $307.70 to $320.48 (full-time). Graduate student fees under UTMAGS were increased for MiWay U-Pass by $4.87 per fall/winter session and $2.44–$2.43 for summer sessions beginning Summer 2026.
packages.gradRelevance: Graduate students are directly affected by the UTMAGS fee increases totaling $4.87 per fall/winter session plus $2.44–$2.43 per summer session for MiWay U-Pass transit costs, effective Summer 2026. Although Rama Edaibis (UTMAGS graduate student representative) was present, the report does not detail graduate-specific funding, stipend changes, TA/RA labor matters, or dedicated graduate housing—suggesting limited graduate-targeted allocation in this cycle. Graduate students will also be subject to the approved Health Services Fee increase (7.33%), Recreation & Athletics Fee increase (5.00%), and Student Services Fee increase (4.15%) if registered or affiliated with UTM.
keyItems: International applications increased 13% year-over-year to 14,381; international intake target revised downward to 1,032 students (from previous 1,280) to reflect federal visa cap impacts and 70% decline in Indian applications; domestic intake target remains 2,662 students with domestic applications at 17,324 and first-choice listings up 11%.

Total full-time undergraduate enrollment stands at approximately 15,200 students; Fall 2025 domestic enrollment exceeded targets with 3,100 domestic and 975 international students; seven-year graduation rate at 74% (up 4+ points since 2012–13); international graduation rates reached 82.5%.

Institutional operating budget of $3.66B represents 1.1% increase; compensation spending of $2.43B grew 32% since 2021, outpacing enrollment and revenue growth; domestic tuition increase of 2% expected to provide ~$10M near-term benefit; $408M planned for student financial support with 85% increase over six years.

Residence fee increases approved for 2026-27 (effective May 1) varying by residence type and room configuration; family housing fees unchanged; major capital renewal projects and new residence building preparations underway; demand continues to exceed on-campus housing supply.

Health Services Fee increased 7.33% from $79.95 to $85.81 full-time ($15.99 to $17.16 part-time); priorities include enhanced clinical access, expanded wellness programming, and support for international and complex-needs students.

Recreation & Athletics Fee increased 5.00% from $223.01 to $234.16 full-time ($44.60 to $46.83 part-time); comprised of permanent 2.00% increase and temporary 3.00% increase; priorities include facility maintenance and critical repairs.

packages.title: UTM Campus Council - November 12, 2025
packages.summary: At the November 12, 2025 UTM Campus Council meeting, the university presented four information items: a cyber security update reporting $6.32M average cost per data breach for Canadian organizations and 4,356 weekly attacks per organization in the education sector; a status report on UTM's strategic priorities including proposed programs (MFA in creative theatre practice, PhD in media communication technologies, Specialist in accounting and finance) and a 400-bed residence; proposed revisions to the Policy on Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment incorporating feedback from 31 submissions (25 online, 6 written) received September 23–October 8, 2025, with changes to definitions, timelines, respondent supports, and reprisal language; and the 2024-25 Ombudsperson Annual Report documenting 357 new requests for assistance, including 88 graduate student cases (up from 61 in 2023-24).
packages.gradRelevance: Graduate students are directly affected by: (1) the proposed UTM programs including the PhD in media communication technologies; (2) the Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment Policy revisions, which establish procedures affecting all members of the university community; (3) the Ombudsperson report showing 88 graduate student requests for assistance in 2024-25, with 14 cases concerning graduate supervision and academic progress and 12 cases concerning financial aid, funding, and awards. The cyber security update is relevant to all students' data protection but contains no graduate-specific measures.
keyItems: Cyber Security Update: Organizations in the education sector face 4,356 weekly attacks on average; Canadian data breaches cost $6.32M on average; U of T's ChatGPT usage grew from ~1M sessions in June 2025 to 24M in September 2025. Windows 10 devices require upgrades; password managers and endpoint protection are being deployed; security training available to 15,000+ staff and faculty.

UTM Strategic Priorities 2025-26: Program proposals to be completed for MFA in creative theatre practice, PhD in media communication technologies, and Specialist in accounting and finance. Campus plans 400-bed residence and pre-engineered research building. Enrolment management and research funding growth (SSHRC participation) are priorities. UTM nursing program planned for 2026 launch in partnership with BFoN and THP.

Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment Policy Revisions: Policy review conducted January 15–March 28, 2025 (chaired by Professor Faye Mishna) and September 23–October 8, 2025, yielding 25 online submissions, 6 written submissions, and ~150 individual comments. Proposed changes add clarity to definitions (consent, procedural fairness, reprisal, intimate partner violence, gender-based violence), update timelines (paragraph 33), expand respondent supports (paragraphs 40 & 44), clarify interim conditions/measures (paragraph 47), and strengthen reprisal protections (paragraphs 52 & 53). Policy scheduled for Governing Council approval December 11, 2025.

Ombudsperson Annual Report 2024-25: 357 new requests for assistance received; 88 graduate students sought assistance (up from 61 in 2023-24), primarily from St. George campus (77 cases). Graduate supervision and academic progress generated 14 cases; financial aid, funding, and awards generated 12 cases. Professor Faye Mishna appointed as new Ombudsperson effective July 1, 2025 for three-year term.

Ombudsperson Case Trends—International Students: 64 cases in 2024-25 (vs. 50 in 2023-24); International Fee Exemption complaints doubled from 4 to 9 cases. Enhanced communication guidance materials will be developed for divisional registrar offices.

Ombudsperson Case Trends—Petitions and Appeals: 19 cases in 2024-25 (consistent with 20 in 2023-24 and 21 in 2022-23). Working groups in Faculty of Arts and Science, UTM, and UTSC are improving clarity and fairness of petition regulations.

documents.title: Cyber Security Update (November 2025)
documents.summary: Presentation on institutional cyber security posture. Reports that Canadian organizations average $6.32M in costs per data breach; education sector organizations face 4,356 weekly attacks on average; Canadian Anti-Fraud Center reported $638M in fraud losses. U of T ChatGPT usage surged from ~1M sessions in June 2025 to 24M in September 2025. Outlines threat actors (financially-motivated crime and nation-state espionage), security initiatives (virtual crisis simulation platform, password manager, endpoint protection, security training for 15,000+ staff/faculty), and priorities: upgrading Windows 10 devices, conducting privacy impact assessments, addressing cyber fraud, and enabling secure AI adoption.
documents.gradRelevance: Cyber security affects graduate student research data protection, especially those handling sensitive information, health data, or intellectual property. No graduate-specific policies or funding allocations are mentioned.

documents.title: Status Report on UTM Campus Strategic Priorities (November 2025)
documents.summary: Document establishing governance process for budget review. Reports that UTM's strategic framework priorities include promoting truth/openness/reciprocity, fostering student success, empowering research discovery, encouraging collaboration/belonging, building sustainable operations, and embracing place-based connections. This status report is the first step; Institutional Operating Budget will follow in Cycle 3; Campus Operating Budget in Cycle 4. No specific financial figures or detailed initiatives are provided in this cover document.
documents.gradRelevance: Establishes governance timeline for budget decisions affecting graduate programs and services; research discovery is an explicit priority but no specific graduate funding levels or TA/RA allocations are detailed here.

documents.title: Status Report on Campus Strategic Priorities—Presentation Slides (October 16, 2025)
documents.summary: Detailed presentation on UTM's 2025-26 strategic priorities. U of T ranked 16th in US News and 4th in NTU rankings; first among North American public universities for graduate employability, first among Canadian universities in research/innovation, top 20 globally in all five QS teaching/research fields. UTM 2025-26 priorities: complete program proposals for MFA in creative theatre practice, PhD in media communication technologies, and Specialist in accounting and finance; advance strategic enrolment management; strengthen student spaces (400-bed residence); grow tri-council and SSHRC research funding; build exceptional research facilities (pre-engineered building); exceed anti-racism commitments; deliver balanced budget prioritizing research and teaching; expand co-op with regional employers; plan 2026 launch of UTM nursing program with BFoN and THP; design Indigenous building and propose Indigenous studies programs.
documents.gradRelevance: Directly relevant: PhD in media communication technologies is proposed for 2025-26 completion. Research discovery emphasizes growing SSHRC participation and tri-council funding, affecting graduate research funding. No specific graduate stipend amounts, TA/RA wages, or funding line items are disclosed.

documents.title: Proposed Revisions to the Policy on Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment (Cover Document)
documents.summary: Governance document describing review process and timeline for proposed policy revisions. University launched review January 15, 2025 comprising two consultation phases (January 15–March 28, 2025 chaired by Professor Faye Mishna; September 23–October 8, 2025 via online form) and external legal review by Gillian Hnatiw. Second consultation yielded 25 online submissions, 6 written submissions, ~150 individual comments from 47% staff, 34% students, 13% faculty, 6% staff/students across three campuses. Feedback addressed definitions, procedural fairness, reprisals, intimate partner violence, confidentiality, timelines, respondent supports, mandatory training, and interim conditions. Governance path: UTSC Campus Council (November 11), UTM Campus Council (November 12), Academic Board (November 13), University Affairs Board (November 25), Executive Committee (December 1), Governing Council (December 11, 2025).
documents.gradRelevance: Policy directly applies to all graduate students and addresses sexual violence, harassment, and procedural fairness. Graduate students comprise significant portion of feedback providers. Policy will be binding on graduate population when approved.

documents.title: Presentation on Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment Policy Revisions (Fall 2025)
documents.summary: Presentation by Professor Sandy Welsh, Vice-Provost, Students, summarizing community feedback and substantive improvements. Fall 2025 feedback (September 23–October 8) included 25 online and 6 written submissions (~150 individual comments) from staff (47%), students (34%), faculty (13%), staff/students (6%) across all three campuses. Proposed changes add clarity to definitions (consent, facilitated resolution process, members of university community, procedural fairness, reprisal, gender-based violence); update timelines (paragraph 33); strengthen respondent supports (paragraphs 40 & 44) and mandatory training (paragraph 42); clarify interim conditions/measures (paragraph 47) and reprisal protections (paragraphs 52 & 53); address when reports engage multiple policies (paragraph 89) and when reports fall outside policy scope (paragraph 93).
documents.gradRelevance: Directly affects graduate students as members of the university community subject to the policy. Proposed changes strengthen procedural fairness, clarify respondent supports, and enhance reprisal protections—all material to graduate students involved in complaints or processes.

documents.title: Policy on Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment (Current Policy, December 15, 2022 effective January 1, 2023)
documents.summary: Full text of current institutional policy establishing comprehensive framework for sexual violence and harassment. Applies to all members of university community (students, faculty, librarians, postdocs, employees) regardless of campus location or whether event occurred on/off campus or via electronic media. Defines sexual violence, sexual assault, sexual harassment, gender-based harassment, consent, cyber sexual violence, and intimate images. Establishes centralized reporting through the Sexual Violence Prevention and Support Centre or Campus Safety; provides for disclosure, reporting, assessment, investigation, decision-making, non-adjudicative resolution, and appeals. Requires policy review every three years with community consultation. Notes commitment to procedural fairness, confidentiality (with exceptions), support for complainants and respondents, and zero tolerance for reprisals.
documents.gradRelevance: Foundational policy governing sexual violence and harassment involving all graduate students. Establishes reporting procedures, investigation processes, respondent and complainant supports, and appeal mechanisms binding on graduate population.

documents.title: Annual Report: Office of the Ombudsperson, and the Administrative Response, 2024-25
documents.summary: Annual report from Ombudsperson Bruce Kidd (final year of appointment; Professor Faye Mishna appointed effective July 1, 2025 for three-year term through June 30, 2028). 357 new requests for assistance received between July 1, 2024 and June 30, 2025. Tri-campus office structure includes Ombuds Officers at each campus and one support staff member. Key case trends: incivility cases declined to 27 (from 46 in 2023-24 and 56 in 2022-23); transfer credit cases increased to 7 (from 2 in 2023-24 and 0 in 2022-23); petitions/appeals stable at 19 (vs. 20 in 2023-24 and 21 in 2022-23); fees cases rose to 27 (from 21 in 2023-24 and 17 in 2022-23); financial aid/funding/awards cases increased to 25 (from 15 in 2023-24 and 9 in 2022-23); academic integrity cases rose to 35 (from 24 in 2023-24 and 26 in 2022-23); international student cases increased to 64 (from 50 in 2023-24); International Fee Exemption complaints doubled to 9 (from 4); student services cases decreased significantly to 16 (from 69 in 2023-24); communications cases continued decline to 21 (from 53 in 2023-24 and 82 in 2022-23); privacy cases to 10 (from 5 in 2023-24 and 9 in 2022-23); student conduct to 16 (from 7 in 2023-24). Highlights policy reviews underway on Code of Behaviour on Academic Matters, Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment Policy, and Statement on Prohibited Discrimination. Notes Administration's acceptance of all recommendations from Professor Randy Boyagoda's May 2025 Report on Civil Discourse. Commends Faculty of Arts and Science, UTM, and UTSC Petitions Policy Working Group and tri-campus Transfer Credit Working Group.
documents.gradRelevance: Directly relevant: 88 graduate students sought assistance in 2024-25 (up from 61 in 2023-24), with 77 on St. George, 8 on UTM, 3 on UTSC. Leading graduate concerns: supervision and academic progress (14 cases, up from 8 in 2023-24 but below 19 in 2022-23) and financial aid, funding, and awards (12 cases). Office supports Centre for Graduate Mentorship & Supervision. Report highlights increased graduate cases related to funding and fees, with no systemic unfairness identified but continued monitoring planned.

packages.title: UTM Campus Council - November 12, 2025
packages.summary: At its November 12, 2025 meeting, UTM Campus Council received updates on cybersecurity risks (Canadian institutions face $6.32 million average cost per breach and 4,356 weekly attacks), campus strategic priorities including a new 400-bed residence opening in 2026 and record research funding of $22.3 million in 2024-25, and final revisions to the Policy on Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment incorporating 150 individual comments from fall 2025 community consultation. The council also heard the Ombudsperson's 2024-25 annual report showing 357 university-wide requests for assistance (56 from UTM), with doubled complaints about international fee exemptions. President Melanie Woodin addressed the council for the first time, emphasizing the university's 200th anniversary and need to address global challenges through collaboration.
packages.gradRelevance: Graduate students are directly affected by: (1) complaints about graduate student financial aid, awards, and other fees increasing slightly per the Ombudsperson's report, with doubled complaints about international fee exemptions; (2) the new Policy on Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment revisions affecting all students including grad students, with clarified timelines, procedural fairness protections, and expanded definitions; (3) cybersecurity initiatives impacting research-intensive graduate work, particularly concerning AI tool usage (24 million ChatGPT sessions by September) and intellectual property protection; (4) record research funding of $22.3 million (70% increase) supporting graduate research opportunities; (5) the nursing program launching in 2026 with 30 funded seats though details on graduate-level involvement unclear.

documents.title: Cyber Security Update - November 2025
documents.summary: Presentation by Chief Information Officer Dr. Donna Kidwell and Acting Chief Information Security Officer Deyves Fonseca outlining that Canadian organizations face $6.32 million average cost per breach and education sector experiences 4,356 weekly attacks. Current phishing/scam email blocking rate is 98-99%.

documents.title: 2025 Review of the Policy on Sexual Violence & Sexual Harassment - Proposed Policy Revisions
documents.summary: Presentation by Vice-Provost Sandy Welsh on final revisions to university Policy on Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment. Fall 2025 community consultation (September 23-October 8) generated 25 online submissions, 6 written submissions, and approximately 150 individual comments from 47% staff, 34% students, 13% faculty, 6% staff/students across all three campuses. Key revisions include: clarified definitions (consent, facilitated resolution process, members of university community, procedural fairness, reprisal, gender-based violence); updated intimate partner violence language; clarified 12-month timeline for concluding sexual violence process; explicit confirmation interim measures are non-disciplinary; expanded respondent supports and mandatory training language; clarified reprisal protections.

documents.title: Update on the Review of the Policy on Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment
documents.summary: Comprehensive policy update on the 2025 review of the Policy on Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment (originally approved December 2016, revised December 2019, 2021, 2022). Review launched January 15, 2025, with two components: (1) broad consultation January 15–March 28, 2025 chaired by Professor Faye Mishna involving 200+ community members across 45 hours of engagement including student societies (UTSU, UTGSU), staff, faculty, union leadership, and identity-based community sessions (racialized, Indigenous, 2SLGBTQ+, persons with disability); (2) external legal expert review by Gillian Hnatiw (Toronto-based expert in gender-based violence). Draft revisions posted for public feedback September 23–October 8, 2025. Consultation input identified five themes: Clarity & Transparency, Process & Communication Improvements, Timelines, Scope of Policy, Education/Training/Awareness. Proposed revisions include new definitions, expanded Vice-Provost Students authority, online navigation tools, mandatory trauma-informed training, timelines throughout policy, and new section on Safety, Reprisals, Interim Conditions.
documents.gradRelevance: Graduate students are directly affected as Members of the University Community covered by the Policy. Specific graduate-relevant revisions: UTGSU submitted formal feedback during consultation; new mandatory trauma-informed training for staff likely to receive disclosures; expanded support for Respondents including educational programs; clearer reporting processes and online navigation tools; new provisions addressing intimate partner violence, tech-facilitated violence; protection against reprisals; access to supports regardless of investigation participation.

documents.title: 2025 Review of the Policy on Sexual Violence & Sexual Harassment: Presentation by Vice-Provost Sandy Welsh
documents.summary: Sandy Welsh (Vice-Provost, Students) presented the 2025 policy review progress and proposed revisions. Review mandate: assess impact of 2022 changes and identify further improvements. Two-part review: consultations with University community and external expert review. Community consultation produced five key input themes: (1) Clarity & Transparency—add definitions, strengthen Statement of Commitment, create online navigation guides; (2) Process & Communication Improvements—broaden Vice-Provost Students decision-making, enhance non-adjudicative process details; (3) Timelines—add timelines throughout; (4) Scope—clarify University authority re: anonymous/third-party reports, intimate partner violence; (5) Education/Training/Awareness—enhance staff and Respondent training. External expert Hnatiw recommended: add/update definitions (gender-based violence, intimate partner violence, tech-facilitated violence, coercive control, consent); update Statement of Commitment emphasizing Indigenous impact and dispelling myths; add new reporting avenues; rename and broaden non-adjudicative resolution; add timelines; broaden Vice-Provost decision-making. Other planned responses: policy navigation tools, internal process review, trauma-informed case management.
documents.gradRelevance: Graduate students directly affected as these revisions apply to all Members of the University Community. Key graduate-relevant changes: clearer reporting processes with online navigation tools; expanded supports for Respondents; new definitions covering tech-facilitated violence and intimate partner violence (relevant to student safety); mandatory trauma-informed training; explicit interim measures and safety planning provisions; clarified confidentiality protocols.

documents.title: 2025 Review of the Policy on Sexual Violence & Sexual Harassment
documents.summary: Update on review and revision of Policy on Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment, which applies to all students, staff, faculty, and librarians. Original policy effective January 1, 2017; provincial requirements mandate review every three years. 2025 review built on 2022 recommendations. Community consultation (Jan-Mar 2025) involved 200+ participants across three campuses; external expert review by lawyer Gillian Hnatiw. Key themes: improved definitions and clarity, enhanced communication tools, process timelines, expanded non-adjudicative resolution, increased training, broader scope including gender-based and intimate partner violence. Proposed revisions: added seven new definitions (gender-based violence, gender expression, gender identity, intimate partner violence, procedural fairness, reprisal, tech-facilitated sexual violence); updated statement of commitments emphasizing trauma-informed process and accommodations; formalized practices for reports from former community members and university response to intimate partner violence; expanded Vice-Provost Students decision-making authority; added timelines for key processes; mandatory trauma-informed training for staff and respondents; renamed and expanded non-adjudicative resolution section; added safety planning and reprisal response language. Community input period Sept 23-Oct 8, 2025; final approval targeted for Cycle 2.
documents.gradRelevance: Policy applies to all students including graduate students. Mandatory trauma-informed training requirements and expanded support mechanisms affect graduate students. Expanded scope to address intimate partner violence, tech-facilitated sexual violence, and gender-based violence may address concerns affecting graduate student populations, though policy revisions do not specify graduate-specific provisions or changes to graduate student protections.

packages.title: UTSC Academic Affairs Committee - April 29, 2026
packages.summary: The UTSC Academic Affairs Committee met on April 29, 2026 to approve major curriculum and academic regulation changes. Key approvals included: revised Guidelines for Assessment of Teaching Effectiveness (effective July 1, 2026); Dean's List assessment methodology realignment with St. George and UTM campuses (effective September 1, 2026); Return from Absence policy changes clarifying re-enrollment vs. re-application requirements (effective September 1, 2026); repointing of Computer Science programs to a new Bachelor of Computer Science degree (effective September 1, 2027, pending Executive Committee approval); a new Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning stream in Computer Science (effective September 1, 2026); modifications to the Joint Paramedicine Specialist program with Centennial College (effective September 1, 2026); and a new freestanding Minor in Social Data Science replacing the Certificate in Computational Social Science (effective September 1, 2026). Research funding data showed UTSC's 2025-26 success rates exceeded national and tri-campus averages across CIHR (33%), SSHRC Insight (59%), and NSERC Discovery (85.7%), with total awards of $3.896 million.
packages.gradRelevance: Graduate students are affected by the revised Teaching Effectiveness Guidelines (effective July 1, 2026), which may impact TA/RA evaluation standards and expectations for teaching-stream faculty who supervise graduate work. The new AI and Machine Learning stream and Social Data Science minor create potential research collaboration and supervised learning opportunities for graduate students in those areas. Research funding outcomes totaling $3.896 million (CIHR $1.9M, SSHRC $946K, NSERC $1.049M) directly support graduate student funding, stipends, and RA positions, though the committee noted funding amounts remain relatively small nationally and deeper analysis of participation rates by discipline (Humanities 57%, Social Sciences 60%, Life Sciences 90%, Physical Sciences 94%) suggests variance in graduate research support.
keyItems: Revised UTSC Guidelines for Assessment of Teaching Effectiveness approved effective July 1, 2026; no changes to core teaching standards but clarified how effectiveness is interpreted, documented, and assessed; reflected contemporary online/hybrid teaching models; emerged from UTSC Teaching Stream Working Group recommendations (2022-2023)

Dean's List eligibility changed from CGPA 3.50+ with minimum 1.50 credits previous session to CGPA 3.50+ assessed at end of session when students complete fifth, tenth, fifteenth, and twentieth credits; aligns UTSC with UTM and St. George campuses; effective September 1, 2026

Return from Absence policy clarified: students with any leave without studying elsewhere need only re-enroll; students absent 12+ months who studied at another institution must re-apply; policy excludes pre-approved Letters of Permission, study abroad, and joint programs; effective September 1, 2026

Computer Science Specialist and Major programs repointed from Honours Bachelor of Science to new Bachelor of Computer Science degree, subject to Executive Committee approval; no curriculum, structure, enrollment, or resource changes; effective September 1, 2027

New Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning stream added as fifth option within Computer Science Specialist and Specialist (Co-op) programs; responds to growing student demand; effective September 1, 2026
Joint Paramedicine Specialist program with Centennial College modified: updated learning outcomes, removed overlapping courses, added new required courses; effective September 1, 2026

documents.title: Minor Modifications: Undergraduate Curriculum Changes, UTSC: Humanities
documents.summary: Professor Katie Larson presented curriculum changes for Department of Philosophy including addition of three new courses in AI; committee noted that interpretive social sciences and humanities needed dialogue with technical advances in AI; approved effective September 1, 2026.
documents.gradRelevance: New philosophy courses in AI may create teaching assistant opportunities for graduate students and generate interest in graduate-level philosophy work at intersection of humanities and technology.

documents.title: Minor Modifications: Undergraduate Curriculum Changes, UTSC: Social Sciences
documents.summary: Professor Katie Larson presented curriculum changes for Departments of Health and Society and Sociology; committee discussed intersectionality between new Sociology courses and Political Science, committing to future interdepartmental coordination; discussed experiential learning components of 'Sociology of Neighbourhoods' course; approved effective September 1, 2026.
