packages.title: UTSC Academic Affairs Committee - March 10, 2026
packages.summary: At the March 10, 2026 UTSC Academic Affairs Committee meeting, members approved a new PhD in Interdisciplinary and Applied Biology (effective September 1, 2027) featuring a research thesis, flexible coursework, 4-month practicum, and culminating colloquium. Graduate curriculum changes were approved for Physical and Environmental Sciences (effective September 1, 2026) and Management (effective May 1, 2027). Multiple undergraduate curriculum changes across humanities, social sciences, and sciences were approved for 2026-27. The Office of Graduate & Postdoctoral Studies reported renovations to the Meeting Place lounge (opening May 2026) and transitioned four new Education Centres for Inquiry (EDU-Cs) from Research & Innovation to GPS oversight, with three proposals in tri-campus consultation. UTSC received $1.5 million in federal Canada Impact Plus funding to recruit three new research chairs, allocated by tri-agency research funding market share.
packages.gradRelevance: The new PhD in Interdisciplinary and Applied Biology directly affects graduate student recruitment and training with its structured four-component design including practicum and colloquium. Graduate curriculum changes in Physical and Environmental Sciences and Management affect existing grad students' course requirements. The Office of GPS's expansion of EDU-Cs and new lounge facilities (May 2026) improve graduate student spaces and professional development. The $1.5 million Canada Impact Plus Chair allocations will create new faculty positions potentially offering RA/postdoctoral opportunities, though no specific stipend or fee changes were announced.
keyItems: Approved new PhD in Interdisciplinary and Applied Biology, Department of Biological Sciences, UTSC, effective September 1, 2027, featuring four key components: research thesis, flexible coursework, 4-month practicum with partner organization, and culminating colloquium
Approved graduate curriculum changes for Department of Physical and Environmental Sciences, effective September 1, 2026
Approved graduate curriculum changes for Department of Management, effective May 1, 2027
Approved undergraduate curriculum changes for BBA and Psychology double degree programs, effective September 1, 2026
Approved humanities undergraduate curriculum changes (Department of Arts, Culture, and Media), effective September 1, 2026
Approved social sciences undergraduate curriculum changes for Department of Anthropology and Department of Human Geography, effective September 1, 2026, including new cultural heritage course

packages.title: UTSC Academic Affairs Committee - January 26, 2026
packages.summary: On January 26, 2026, the UTSC Academic Affairs Committee will approve five packages of undergraduate curriculum changes effective Fall 2026: Humanities (5 new courses including GASB17H3 Global Tamil Cinema, HISB77H3 Revolution and Revolutionaries, HISC72H3 Imperialism and Anti-Imperialism, HISD74H3 East African South Asians, and ECTD71H3 Bridging Cultures with AI); Management (10 co-op program modifications and 8 new courses including COPC71H3–COPC80H3 co-op work term courses, and MGFD35H3 Fixed Income Securities); Social Sciences (1 modification to POLB30H3 Law, Justice and Rights, shifting from political theory to social science focus and broadening Major/Minor access); Sciences (13 Psychology program modifications and 4 new statistics courses PSYA06H3, PSYB06H3, PSYC06H3, plus PSYD12H3 Me and I: The Self in Mind and Brain, increasing overall program credits from 7.0 to 7.5); and a major modification approving a new freestanding Minor in Artificial Intelligence, Mind, and Society (Arts, Department of Philosophy) effective September 1, 2026, designed for non-technical humanistic study of AI ethics and societal impacts. Additionally, the Committee will receive for information the 2025 Campus Curriculum Review Working Circle Milestone Report showing 49% of new courses reference curriculum review priorities (up from 29% in 2020–21) with doubled courses engaging Black and Indigenous knowledges, and the Annual Report from the Office of the Vice-Principal Research & Innovation documenting $22.72 million in total research funding (2024–25), up from $14.28 million in 2019–20 (59% increase), with 411 research grants, 271 tenure-stream faculty (up 59% in five years), and 10 Canada Research Chairs.
packages.gradRelevance: These curriculum changes predominantly affect undergraduate programs and have minimal direct impact on graduate students. However, the new Minor in Artificial Intelligence, Mind, and Society may eventually enable graduate-level expansion in AI ethics and philosophy; the Psychology program restructuring introduces a revised statistics sequence (PSYA06H3, PSYB06H3, PSYC06H3) that could inform graduate-level quantitative training; and the Research Office report shows continued growth in research funding and grants (now $22.72M), which indirectly benefits graduate funding and RA/TA opportunities. The management co-op program modifications and course renamings do not directly affect graduate compensation or services.
keyItems: Humanities minor modifications approved: 5 new courses including GASB17H3 Global Tamil Cinema, HISB77H3 Revolution and Revolutionaries, HISC72H3 Imperialism and Anti-Imperialism, HISD74H3 East African South Asians, and ECTD71H3 Bridging Cultures with AI; effective Fall 2026.

Management minor modifications approved: 10 co-op program changes and 8 new courses (COPC71H3–COPC80H3 work term courses, COPC74H3 and COPC75H3 additional work terms, COPC79H3 entrepreneurial term, COPC80H3 international term, MGFD35H3 Fixed Income Securities); effective Fall 2026.

Social Sciences curriculum change approved: POLB30H3 (Law, Justice and Rights) redesigned from political theory focus to social science course; breadth requirement changed from History/Philosophy/Cultural Studies to Social & Behavioural Sciences; priority expanded to include both Major and Minor in Public Law; effective Fall 2026.

Sciences program modifications approved: 13 Psychology programs restructured; new statistics sequence introduced (PSYA06H3 Introduction to Data Analysis for Scientific Literacy, PSYB06H3 Applied Statistical Analysis, PSYC06H3 Advanced Statistical Analysis); overall program credit requirement for Mental Health Studies Major increased from 7.0 to 7.5; new course PSYD12H3 Me and I: The Self in Mind and Brain added as capstone option; effective Fall 2026.

Major modification approved: New freestanding Minor in Artificial Intelligence, Mind, and Society (Arts), Department of Philosophy; non-technical humanistic approach to AI ethics, bias, fairness, autonomy, privacy, and societal impacts; effective September 1, 2026; forms part of coordinated AI cluster with proposed Minor in Social Data Science and new AI/ML streams in Computer Science.

2025 Campus Curriculum Review Working Circle Milestone Report presented for information: 49% of new courses (2022–2025) reference curriculum review priorities, up from 29% in 2020–21; courses engaging Black and Indigenous knowledges more than doubled across all divisions; implementation supported by targeted Pedagogies of Inclusive Excellence (PIE) fund; challenges remain in sustainability and staffing.

documents.title: 2025 UTSC Campus Curriculum Review Working Circle Milestone Report
documents.summary: Accountability report on UTSC's 2020-launched campus curriculum review assessing progress on 56 recommended actions from the 2022 report. Documents measurable progress: 49% of new courses (2022–2025) reference curriculum priorities vs. 29% baseline in 2020–21; courses engaging Black and Indigenous knowledges more than doubled across all academic divisions. Identifies ongoing challenges: uneven uptake, staffing constraints, short-term funding, and sustainability. Organized around seven implementation areas: curriculum development, pedagogical supports, academic homes for Indigenous/Black excellence, hiring, community engagement, institutional structures, and future role of Working Circle. Led by cross-campus Working Circle guided by Elder Josh Eshkawkogan and Giidaakunadaad (Nancy Rowe); over 100 contributors since 2020.

documents.title: Annual Report: Office of the Vice-Principal Research & Innovation, UTSC, 2024-2025
documents.summary: Comprehensive report demonstrating UTSC research ecosystem growth. Key figures: $22,720,631 total research funding (2024–25) up from $14,283,138 (2019–20), representing 59% increase; 411 research grants; 260 funding applications; 90 funding programs; 42 funding organizations; 9 private sector partners. Faculty growth: 271 tenure-stream faculty (59% increase over five years). Recognition highlights: Professors Myrna Simpson and Karina Vernon elected to Royal Society of Canada; Assistant Professor Walter Tavares awarded King Charles III's Coronation Medal for paramedicine leadership. Strategic initiatives include Community of Practice for Research Leaders, Harvest Walls arrival, Clusters of Scholarly Prominence Program expansion (three new clusters recognized), Design Thinking and Digital Research Storytelling workshops, knowledge mobilization RV, and Sam Ibrahim Centre for Inclusive Excellence in Entrepreneurship opening in new Sam Ibrahim building (donor gift of $25 million).
documents.gradRelevance: Research funding increase from $14.28M to $22.72M (59%) over five years supports expanded graduate funding, RA/TA positions, and research capacity. Nine private sector partnerships may create graduate-level internship and funding opportunities. Connaught Fund awarded $820,000 across nine research initiatives (exceeding $300,000 allocation), including three to UTSC researchers (Élyse Caron-Beaudoin, Matthew Hoffman, Laura Risk), potentially supporting graduate research.

documents.title: Minor Modifications: Undergraduate Curriculum Changes, UTSC: Humanities
documents.summary: Approval of five new undergraduate courses in Humanities effective Fall 2026: GASB17H3 Global Tamil Cinema (0.5 credit, in-person, supports Global Asian Studies program, includes community engagement with Tamil filmmakers); HISB77H3 Revolution and Revolutionaries (0.5 credit, examines modern revolutions, revolutionary lives and ideas across nineteenth–twentieth centuries); HISC72H3 Imperialism and Anti-Imperialism (0.5 credit, intellectual history of imperialism, reads imperialist and critical accounts); HISD74H3 East African South Asians: From India to Africa to Canada (0.5 credit, 15-student enrollment limit, examines history from twelfth-century trade to 1960s–80s diaspora to Canada, addresses racism, citizenship, Islamophobia). All courses approved by Departmental Curriculum Committee, Registrar's Office, and relevant departments; no significant financial implications.

documents.title: Minor Modifications: Undergraduate Curriculum Changes, UTSC: Management
documents.summary: Approval of 10 program modifications and 8 new courses for Management co-op programs effective Fall 2026. Programs modified: Specialist (Co-op) in Economics for Management Studies, Management, Management & Accounting, Management & Finance, Management & HR, Management & IT, Management & International Business, Management & Marketing, Strategic Management (Entrepreneurship and Management Strategy streams). New courses: COPC71H3–COPC75H3 (Management Co-op Work Terms 1–5, with 4 & 5 designated additional), COPC79H3 (Entrepreneurial Work Term), COPC80H3 (International Work Term), MGFD35H3 (Fixed Income Securities). Key changes: clarified co-op eligibility (2.5 CGPA minimum for all terms); students must complete three required 4-month work terms totaling 12 months; alternative term lengths approved by Co-op Office; students required to take summer courses; critical reflection assignments after each work term; Advancing Your Career Exploration (AYCE) course mandatory.

documents.title: Minor Modifications: Undergraduate Curriculum Changes, UTSC: Social Sciences
documents.summary: Approval of one course modification effective Fall 2026: POLB30H3 (Law, Justice and Rights). Previously a lecture course focused on theoretical justifications for rule of law and classical theories (natural law, legal positivism, critical theories); redesigned as introductory course to law, society, and politics grounding study in Public Law major/minor programs. Learning outcomes now emphasize understanding how social scientists study law and politics, defining key concepts (origins of law, court function, access to justice), evaluating competing arguments on inequality, and critiquing judicial impact. Breadth requirement changed from History/Philosophy & Cultural Studies to Social & Behavioural Sciences. Exclusion of PHLB11H3 removed. Priority enrollment expanded to include both Major and Minor in Public Law (previously Minor only).

documents.title: Minor Modifications: Undergraduate Curriculum Changes, UTSC: Sciences
documents.summary: Approval of 13 program modifications, 4 new courses, and 2 course modifications in Psychology effective Fall 2026. Major changes: Mental Health Studies Major program credits increased from 7.0 to 7.5; new statistics sequence introduced replacing retired PSYB07H3 with PSYA06H3 (Introduction to Data Analysis for Scientific Literacy, 0.5 cr) and PSYB06H3 (Applied Statistical Analysis, 0.5 cr); new capstone course PSYD12H3 (Me and I: The Self in Mind and Brain) added to Neuroscience Major capstone options; PSYC06H3 (Advanced Statistical Analysis) created for Specialist programs. Neuroscience Major program restructured with 3.5 credits Scientific Foundations (including new statistics requirement), 3.5 credits Neuroscience Foundations, 1.5 credits Advanced Foundations, 0.5 credit capstone. Students who completed retired PSYB07H3 directed to contact Program Administrator; exception allows PSYB07H3 in place of both PSYA06+B06 with additional PSY course credit makeup.
documents.gradRelevance: Psychology program restructuring introduces comprehensive statistics course sequence (PSYA06H3, PSYB06H3, PSYC06H3) that may inform graduate-level quantitative training and could indicate future graduate program alignment; 0.5 credit increase in overall program requirements.

documents.title: Major Modification: New Freestanding Minor in Artificial Intelligence, Mind, and Society (Arts) (Undergraduate), Department of Philosophy, UTSC
documents.summary: Major modification approved: new freestanding Minor in Artificial Intelligence, Mind, and Society (AIMS, Arts), Department of Philosophy, effective September 1, 2026. Non-technical humanistic and philosophical approach to AI combining foundational issues (nature of mind, knowledge, language, science) with ethics and critical thinking. Designed to complement Computer Science AI/ML streams and proposed Social Data Science minor; unique in philosophical focus addressing conceptual and societal implications of AI, including large language models, bias, fairness, autonomy, privacy, creativity, employment, environmental impact. Interdisciplinary structure enables students from diverse backgrounds to develop rigorous AI understanding and transferable analytical/ethical skills. Aligns with UTSC Strategic Plan (Inspiring Inclusive Excellence), supports Scarborough Academy of Medicine and Integrated Health (SAMIH, opening 2026), and advances UN Sustainable Development Goals. Extensive consultation with faculty, students, cognate units, and tri-campus partners confirmed strong demand. No significant financial implications.
documents.gradRelevance: New Minor in AI, Mind, and Society may eventually expand into graduate-level offerings in AI ethics and philosophy; demonstrates institutional investment in AI-related curriculum across humanistic and technical disciplines.

documents.title: Minor Modifications: Undergraduate Curriculum Changes, UTSC (Consent Agenda)
documents.summary: Consent agenda approval of minor modifications effective Fall 2026 across six departments: Global Development Studies (6 program modifications including Certificate in Global Development Environment and Health, Major/Minor/Specialist in International Development Studies; 8 course modifications replacing IDSB11H3 with IDSB06H3); Historical and Cultural Studies (2 program modifications to Major in Women's & Gender Studies and Minor in Classical Studies; 35 course modifications; 14 course retirements); Language Studies (2 program modifications to Major/Specialist in Linguistics and Specialist in Psycholinguistics; 30 course modifications); Management (3 program modifications to Major/Minor in Economics for Management Studies and Minor in Global Leadership; 8 course modifications; 1 retirement); Political Science (3 program modifications to Major/Minor/Specialist in Political Science; 17 course modifications; 9 retirements); Psychology (1 Minor in Psychology modification; 97 course modifications; 5 retirements). Changes include title updates, retired course housekeeping (adding round brackets), and course number adjustments maintaining program integrity without altering learning outcomes.

packages.title: UTSC Academic Affairs Committee - January 26, 2026
packages.summary: The UTSC Academic Affairs Committee met on January 26, 2026 to address campus grief following the December 23, 2025 killing of third-year life sciences student Shivank Avasthi, approve four sets of minor undergraduate curriculum modifications (Humanities, Management, Social Sciences, Sciences), and approve a new freestanding Minor in Artificial Intelligence, Mind, and Society effective Fall 2026. Professor Alison Mountz reported that UTSC research funding grew from approximately $14 million five years ago to just over $22 million in 2024-25 (59% increase), with tri-agency funding comprising 64% of external funding and tenure-stream faculty growth of 20% over the same period. Dean Karin Ruhlandt reported a 5% increase in overall applications with a notable 18% surge in joint degree applications with Centennial College, offset by a 2% decline in domestic applications and 14% increase in international applications, while computer science applications declined due to reduced entry-level job availability from AI.
packages.gradRelevance: This meeting contains minimal direct graduate student relevance. The research funding growth to $22 million may create increased RA opportunities, though no specific graduate funding, stipend amounts, or TA allocations are mentioned. The campus mourning acknowledgment and counselling support services available through the Office of Student Experience and Wellbeing apply to all students including graduate students. No unionized-labour, fee, housing, or graduate-specific policy matters were addressed in this meeting.
keyItems: Acting Principal Alison Mountz acknowledged the campus community's grief following the killing of Shivank Avasthi, a third-year life sciences student, on December 23, 2025 on a campus trail, and highlighted counselling and support services through the Office of Student Experience and Wellbeing

Campus Curriculum Review Working Circle Milestone Report (November 2025) assessed progress on 56 recommended actions from the 2022 report, centred Indigenous and Black knowledge with intersectional lens, but identified challenges including uneven departmental uptake, staffing constraints, short-term funding, and long-term sustainability concerns

UTSC research funding increased from approximately $14 million (five years prior) to just over $22 million in 2024-25, representing 59% growth; tri-agency funding constituted approximately 64% of external funding; tenure-stream faculty grew 20% over five years

Committee approved Minor in Artificial Intelligence, Mind, and Society (Arts) freestanding undergraduate minor from Department of Philosophy, effective Fall 2026 for 2026-27 academic year, designed as interdisciplinary program pairing with Computer and Mathematical Sciences, Psychology, Management, Media Studies, Linguistics, Political Science, Sociology, and health programs

Committee approved four sets of minor undergraduate curriculum modifications effective 2026-27: Department of Historical and Cultural Studies and Department of Language Studies (Humanities); Department of Management (Management); Department of Political Science (Social Sciences); Department of Psychology (Sciences)
Overall applications to UTSC increased 5% compared to UTM (6%) and Faculty of Arts & Science (7%); UTSC listed as first choice by 8% of applicants versus 12% for UTM and 13% for FAS

packages.title: UTSC Academic Affairs Committee - October 16, 2025
packages.summary: At the October 16, 2025 UTSC Academic Affairs Committee meeting, the committee was presented with the 2024-25 Annual Report on Academic Appeals showing a dramatic spike in undergraduate petitions (6,034 filed in 2024-25, up from 4,599 the prior year) and appeals heard by the Subcommittee on Academic Appeals (7 appeals in 2024-25, up from 0 in 2023-24), though the Dean's Advisory Committee early-resolution process continues to manage most cases before escalation. The committee approved a new Combined Degree Program pairing UTSC's Honours Bachelor of Arts Major in Music with OISE's Master of Teaching, effective Fall 2026, which will allow music students without classical audition backgrounds to pursue teacher licensure in Ontario and earn two degrees (HBA and MT) in six years with 1.0 FCE credit counting toward both programs. The proposal aligns with UTSC's inclusive excellence mandate and the Black Future Educators' Path initiative, with no net financial implications to the campus budget.
packages.gradRelevance: This meeting contains minimal direct relevance to graduate students. The Academic Appeals report concerns only undergraduate petitions and appeals processes. The Combined Degree Program is an undergraduate-to-graduate pathway linking UTSC's HBA Music major to OISE's Master of Teaching; while graduate students may benefit if the program increases diversity in the teacher education pipeline, the immediate impact is on undergraduate admission and progression. Neither item addresses graduate stipends, TA/RA compensation, unionized labour matters, graduate student housing, or graduate-specific policies or services.
keyItems: 2024-25 Academic Appeals Annual Report presented for information: petitions filed increased to 6,034 (from 4,599 in 2023-24); Dean's Advisory Committee reviewed 49 petition decisions (up from 22 in 2023-24); Subcommittee on Academic Appeals heard 7 appeals (up from 0 in 2023-24), showing increased student regulatory exemption requests but early DAC resolution preventing most escalations.

Combined Degree Program (CDP) for UTSC HBA Major in Music with OISE Master of Teaching approved for Fall 2026 entry, subject to OISE Faculty Council approval, allowing students to earn both an HBA and MT degree in six years with 1.0 FCE credit counting toward both degrees.

CDP targets music students without classical audition training, removing barriers to teacher licensure pathways and supporting diverse future music educators in Ontario; aligns with UTSC's inclusive excellence strategy and OISE/UT's Black Future Educators' Path initiative.

First students apply to CDP in September 2026; first cohort conditionally registers in CDP and MT in Fall 2027; first cohort unconditionally registers in MT in Fall 2028; Memorandum of Agreement renewal scheduled for Fall 2028.

CDP requires I/S division applicants to have 6.0 FCEs in Music major (Instrumental or Vocal focus) plus prerequisites for second teaching subject; J/I division requires 6.0 FCEs in Music plus prerequisites for one teaching subject; P/J division requires only Music major completion with no teaching subject prerequisites.

Students receive early (conditional) graduate admission to MT in Year 3 of undergraduate study; successful completion leads to Ontario College of Teachers Certificate of Qualification certifying eligibility to teach in Ontario schools.

documents.title: Annual Report: 2024-25 Subcommittee on Academic Appeals
documents.summary: This report documents undergraduate petition and appeals activity at UTSC for 2024-25. Petitions filed surged to 6,034 (compared to 4,599 in 2023-24), though only 49 reached the Dean's Advisory Committee for review (up from 22 in 2023-24) and just 7 reached the Subcommittee on Academic Appeals (up from 0 in 2023-24). The report shows a 31% year-over-year increase in petitions, demonstrating growing student requests for exemptions from academic regulations. The Dean's Advisory Committee's early-resolution process continues to significantly reduce appeals volume before they escalate to the SAA. Students are referred to campus services for guidance on the appeals process.

documents.title: Undergraduate Major Modification: Combined Degree Program for UTSC Major Program in Music (HBA) with OISE Master of Teaching
documents.summary: This 3-page approval document and 8-page detailed proposal introduce a new Combined Degree Program linking UTSC's Honours Bachelor of Arts Major in Music (formerly Music and Culture) with OISE's Master of Teaching (MT). The CDP allows students to earn both degrees in six years with 1.0 FCE credit counting toward both programs. It targets music students who may lack classical audition training required by traditional music performance programs but possess strong musicianship and educator potential. Students receive conditional MT admission in Year 3 of undergraduate study. Upon completion, graduates earn HBA and MT degrees and qualify for Ontario College of Teachers certification. The proposal removes barriers to music teacher licensure, aligns with UTSC's inclusive excellence mandate and the Black Future Educators' Path initiative, and requires no net financial outlay from the campus budget. First applications open September 2026; first cohort conditionally registers Fall 2027; full MT registration Fall 2028. OISE Faculty Council approval is required by October 29, 2025.
documents.gradRelevance: While this CDP creates a pathway from undergraduate to graduate study in teacher education, it does not directly affect current graduate student funding, compensation, housing, or unionized labour matters. However, by diversifying the pipeline of students entering OISE's MT program and supporting underrepresented cohorts in music teacher education, the program may indirectly influence graduate enrolment patterns and the composition of future cohorts within OISE's graduate programs.

packages.title: UTSC Academic Affairs Committee - October 16, 2025
packages.summary: The UTSC Academic Affairs Committee met on October 16, 2025, with four vacant seats (2 teaching staff, 2 graduate student) remaining unfilled after a by-election that filled one position. The Registrar reported that appeals submissions to the Dean's Advisory Committee tripled from 15 in 2017-18 to 49 in 2024-25, with deferred examinations as the most frequent subject and international students disproportionately represented; the office is managing increased caseload without additional staff resources. The Committee approved a new Combined Degree Program pairing UTSC's Music (HBA) Major with OISE's Master of Teaching, effective Fall 2026, targeting students with strong musicianship but without traditional performance qualifications. The Vice-Dean reported that UTSC's Curriculum Review Milestone Report, implementing 56 actions from the 2022 review centered on Indigenous, Black, racialized, international, and intercultural perspectives, has supported over 250 initiatives through the Pedagogies of Inclusive Excellence Fund, though uptake remains uneven across disciplines.
packages.gradRelevance: Two of the four vacant seats on the Committee are graduate student positions that remain unfilled as of October 2025, limiting grad student representation in UTSC academic governance. The appeals data is relevant to graduate students: while the report focuses on undergraduate petitions, the tripled caseload reflects systemic issues with exam administration and student mental health that affect all student levels. The Pedagogies of Inclusive Excellence Fund's 250+ supported initiatives may benefit graduate student experiences, though specific graduate programming impacts are not detailed in this report.
keyItems: Four vacant seats on UTSC Academic Affairs Committee remain unfilled: 2 teaching staff seats and 2 graduate student seats; by-election held September 15–October 3, 2025 filled only 1 seat

Academic Appeals data 2024-25: Dean's Advisory Committee appeals increased from 15 in 2017-18 to 49 in 2024-25; petitions to Registrar increased from 2,812 in 2017-18 to 6,034 in 2024-25; increase outpaced enrolment growth
Registrar's office identified deferred examinations as most frequent appeals subject; noting increased exam weighting by instructors in response to generative AI use as possible cause

International students disproportionately represented in petitions cases; Registrar's office noting high number of cases related to student mental health and some referrals to Health & Wellness Centre

Registrar's office reported increasing but small number of fraudulent supporting documents in petitions applications; office managing increased caseload with no additional staff resources, focusing on workflow streamlining and policy adjustments

UTSC Curriculum Review Milestone Report (to be released in coming weeks) implements 56 recommended actions from 2022 review centered on Indigenous, Black, racialized, international, and intercultural perspectives using intersectional approach

documents.title: UTSC Academic Affairs Committee Report Number 81 (October 16, 2025)
documents.summary: Meeting minutes from October 16, 2025 covering four vacant governance seats, Registrar's annual appeals report showing tripling of Dean's Advisory Committee cases from 2017-18 to 2024-25, approval of the UTSC Music/OISE Master of Teaching Combined Degree Program effective Fall 2026, and Vice-Dean report on Curriculum Review Milestone Report implementing 56 actions centered on inclusive excellence with 250+ supported initiatives through the Pedagogies of Inclusive Excellence Fund.
documents.gradRelevance: Two unfilled graduate student seats on Committee limit grad representation; appeals data and curriculum review initiatives affect all student levels; Combined Degree Program is undergraduate-focused.

packages.title: UTSC Academic Affairs Committee - September 11, 2025
packages.summary: The UTSC Academic Affairs Committee met on September 11, 2025 to consider curriculum quality assurance processes, three cyclical program reviews, and one graduate curriculum modification. The meeting presented the University of Toronto Quality Assurance Process (UTQAP) framework and 2025-26 strategic priorities emphasizing equity, anti-racism, Indigeneity, and accessibility alongside new health sciences initiatives including the Scarborough Academy of Medicine and Integrated Health (SAMIH). The Committee received for information reviews of the Specialist in Paramedicine (joint with Centennial College), the Department of Health and Society and its undergraduate programs, and the Department of Physical and Environmental Sciences and its undergraduate/graduate programs. The Committee approved a minor graduate curriculum modification to course EES1125H (Contaminated Site Remediation) in the Department of Physical and Environmental Sciences, effective September 2, 2025, with revised course description emphasizing real-world case studies and stakeholder perspectives in environmental remediation.
packages.gradRelevance: The meeting affects graduate students through: (1) approval of the modified EES1125H course description for Master of Environmental Science (MEnvSc) students, which now emphasizes practical application, stakeholder engagement, and complex scenarios; (2) the Health and Society Department review identifying opportunities for graduate curriculum development and research mentorship in emerging health areas; (3) the Physical and Environmental Sciences review noting enhanced support structures for graduate students and increased TA hours for courses requiring extra support; (4) strategic curriculum priorities that will shape graduate programming through SAMIH expansion and cross-departmental health sciences collaborations.
keyItems: Presentation on UTQAP and 2025-26 Strategic Priorities outlined Ontario's provincial quality assurance framework, UTSC's commitment to equity/anti-racism/Indigeneity/accessibility, and announced a new Expression of Interest process for academic units to propose future programs emphasizing cross-departmental collaboration and interdisciplinary/health sciences programming

Specialist in Paramedicine (joint UTSC/Centennial College) review found strong program outcomes (near 100% employment post-graduation, high certification exam success rates) but recommended major modifications to improve student integration, clearer communication about lift test physical fitness requirements, and consideration of administrative transfer to Department of Health and Society (implemented July 1, 2023); new course HLTB80H3 approved in 2024-25; next review scheduled Fall 2029

Department of Health and Society cyclical review (March 20-21, 2024 site visit) commended unique multidisciplinary approach and universal design implementation; recommended strategic directions for Population Health and Health Policy programs, equitable faculty workload distribution, research space for dry lab researchers, and focus on Indigenous student recruitment; Department introduced new courses including HLTB27H3 (Applied Statistics for Public Health), HLTC53H3 (Creative Research Practices in Aging), HLTD82H3 (Black Community Health); launched first departmental Research Day; next review scheduled 2031-32

Department of Physical and Environmental Sciences review (March 27-28, 2024 site visit) praised strong research culture, experiential learning opportunities, and 18 net faculty hires since 2017; recommended enhanced graduate student support structures, Physics coverage prioritization, lab space upgrades for accessibility and SAMIH cohort accommodation; Department reassessed TA responsibilities and increased TA hours; next review scheduled 2031-32

Graduate Curriculum Changes: EES1125H (Contaminated Site Remediation) course description revised from technical focus on remediation processes to integrated approach including science, business considerations, and stakeholder perspectives; approval effective September 2, 2025; submitted out-of-cycle due to sessional instructor unfamiliarity with timelines

Strategic curriculum development priorities for 2025-26 include launching SAMIH, expanding health sciences programming, opening Indigenous House, opening Conservation Centre at Toronto Zoo, and extending 2020-25 Strategic Plan 'Inspiring Inclusive Excellence'; process enhancements include new Expression of Interest mechanism and three supporting committees: UTSC Campus Curriculum Committee (CCC), Health Across Disciplines Working Group (formerly HESA), and UTSC Campus Curriculum Review Working Circle

documents.title: Presentation: Overview of University of Toronto Quality Assurance Process and 2025-26 Strategic Priorities for Curriculum Development at UTSC
documents.summary: This presentation outlines Ontario's provincial Quality Assurance Framework (QAF) and UTSC's Quality Assurance Process (UTQAP), which governs protocols for new programs, major modifications, program closures, and cyclical reviews. UTQAP incorporates institutional commitments to equity, anti-racism, Indigeneity, and accessibility. The 2025-26 strategic priorities emphasize extending the 2020-25 Strategic Plan 'Inspiring Inclusive Excellence,' experiential and land-based learning, and cross-departmental collaboration driven by the launch of SAMIH, expansion of health sciences, opening of Indigenous House, and new Conservation Centre at Toronto Zoo. A new Expression of Interest process will enable units to submit ideas for future interdisciplinary and health sciences programs. Supporting committees include the UTSC Campus Curriculum Committee (CCC), Health Across Disciplines Working Group (formerly HESA), and UTSC Campus Curriculum Review Working Circle.
documents.gradRelevance: Graduate students will be affected by the new Expression of Interest process and resulting expansion of health sciences and interdisciplinary programming, which may create new graduate programs and research opportunities. The emphasis on experiential learning and land-based learning will shape graduate curriculum design.

documents.title: Review of Academic Programs and Units, UTSC: Specialist in Paramedicine (offered jointly with Centennial College)
documents.summary: This cyclical review examined the joint Specialist in Paramedicine program (since 2003, first in Canada) offering students a Paramedic Diploma (Centennial College) and Honours BSc in Biology (UTSC). The site visit (November 2-3, 2022) found strong outcomes: near 100% employment immediately post-graduation and high success rates on Advanced Emergency Medical Care Assistant exams. However, reviewers identified major issues: inadequate program integration between institutions (described as 'two solitudes'), a 25% attrition rate due to the mandatory 114 kg lift test requirement, course overlaps and conflicts, and insufficient research opportunities. Key recommendations included establishing seamless integration, moving the program from Department of Biological Sciences to Department of Health and Society (implemented July 1, 2023), bringing it under SAMIH, and improving student communication about fitness requirements. Following the transfer, a new 0.5 FTE Program Coordinator was hired in 2024 and a new course HLTB80H3 was approved in 2024-25. A major modification addressing readiness and workload concerns is in development. Next review scheduled no later than Fall 2029.
documents.gradRelevance: No direct graduate student relevance, as this is an undergraduate diploma/BSc joint program with no graduate component mentioned.

documents.title: Review of Academic Programs and Units, UTSC: Department of Health and Society and its Undergraduate Programs
documents.summary: This cyclical review examined the Department of Health and Society (created 2020 from former Health Studies programs in Anthropology) and its four undergraduate programs: Health Humanities (Minor Arts), Health Studies–Health Policy (BA Major/Co-op), Health Studies–Population Health (BSc Major/Co-op), and Health Studies Minor (suspended). The site visit (March 20-21, 2024) found a 'unique and highly valuable blend of courses' providing holistic health perspectives, strong universal design implementation, and engaged teaching faculty. Reviewers recommended strategic directions for Population Health and Health Policy programs, streamlined course modification processes, equitable faculty workload and research space distribution, research space for dry lab researchers, temporary space in advance of SAMIH move, and enhanced focus on Indigenous student recruitment and community building. In response, the Department initiated regular curriculum reviews, modified and renamed two Major programs, introduced new courses including HLTB27H3 (Applied Statistics for Public Health), HLTC53H3 (Creative Research Practices in Aging), and HLTD82H3 (Black Community Health), and launched its first departmental Research Day. The Dean's Office assigned a permanent staff member to support the Certificate in Pathways to Health Professions. Next review scheduled no later than 2031-32.
documents.gradRelevance: This primarily reviews undergraduate programs; however, graduate students may benefit from the Department's expanded research infrastructure, new permanent staff support, enhanced community building initiatives through Research Day, and potential for graduate research opportunities in the identified disciplinary clusters.

documents.title: Review of Academic Programs and Units, UTSC: Department of Physical and Environmental Sciences and its Undergraduate Programs
documents.summary: This cyclical review examined the Department of Physical and Environmental Sciences (DPES) and its 23 undergraduate and graduate programs spanning physics, chemistry, environmental science, and environmental studies. The site visit (March 27-28, 2024) found the Department 'functioning highly effectively' with exceptionally strong research, impressive Co-op programs, high student morale, and significant progress implementing prior review recommendations. Since the 2017 review, DPES added 18 net faculty and submitted 150 course/program proposals; it offers extensive experiential learning and emphasizes EDI initiatives including UDL principles, EDI-focused curriculum innovations, and partnerships with high schools and community organizations. The Department enrolls 50% local students (Scarborough, Markham, Durham) and 30% international students. Reviewers recommended consulting stakeholders on future co-op directions, enhancing graduate student support, prioritizing Physics faculty hiring, strategic planning for leadership transitions, and upgrading laboratory spaces for accessibility and incoming SAMIH cohorts. In response, DPES reassessed TA responsibilities and increased TA hours for courses needing support, reaffirmed EDI commitments, and will collaborate cross-campus to strengthen graduate student community. Next review scheduled no later than 2031-32.
documents.gradRelevance: Graduate students directly benefit from: increased TA hours for courses requiring extra support; enhanced support structures for graduate students; cross-campus collaborations to strengthen graduate community and engagement; laboratory space upgrades to meet accessibility needs; and strategic hiring priorities. The Department offers graduate programs including M.Env.Sc., M.Sc. (Environmental Science), and Ph.D. (Environmental Science).

documents.title: Minor Modifications: Graduate Curriculum Changes, UTSC
documents.summary: This document approves a minor modification to graduate curriculum effective September 2, 2025. The Department of Physical and Environmental Sciences submitted one course modification: EES1125H (Contaminated Site Remediation). The previous description focused on technical implementation of remediation processes (Soil Vapour Extraction, Pump and Treat, Monitored Natural Attenuation, Bioremediation, novel methods). The revised description broadens the course to integrate science, professional judgment, business considerations, and perspectives of project partners and affected/interested parties, emphasizing assessment of multiple remediation methods for site-specific objectives through real-world case studies and interactive discussions, covering in-situ and ex-situ technologies (Bioremediation, Chemical Oxidation/Reduction, Soil Vapour Extraction, Pump and Treat, Excavation, emerging techniques). The revision provides clearer course information for MEnvSc students and aids partner employers in assessing student preparedness for internships. Approved by Grad DCC (July 24, 2025) and VPAP (July 16, 2025); submitted out-of-cycle because the sessional instructor is unfamiliar with regular submission timelines.
documents.gradRelevance: This directly affects Master of Environmental Science (MEnvSc) graduate students enrolled in or considering EES1125H, providing updated content emphasizing stakeholder engagement, real-world application, and complex decision-making in environmental remediation—skills valuable for internship preparation and professional practice.

packages.title: UTSC Academic Affairs Committee - September 11, 2025
packages.summary: The UTSC Academic Affairs Committee met on September 11, 2025 to review quality assurance processes, strategic curriculum priorities, and three academic program reviews. Kevin Mak and Katie Larson presented the University of Toronto Quality Assurance Process (UTQAP) and highlighted 2025-26 curriculum development priorities including culture of care, equity, anti-racism, Indigeneity, accessibility, and expanded experiential learning. The Committee approved graduate curriculum changes effective September 2, 2025, and reviewed three programs: the Specialist in Paramedicine (jointly with Centennial College), which showed near 100% employment rates post-graduation; the Department of Health and Society, which had enhanced its two major programs and introduced new courses since its 2024 review; and the Department of Physical and Environmental Sciences, which reassessed TA responsibilities, co-op pathways, and laboratory accessibility since its March 2024 review. OVPRI announced selection of nominees for four of five Tier 2 Canada Research Chairs across five different departments and reported the Departmental Research Fund application deadline of September 26, 2025.
packages.gradRelevance: The approval of graduate curriculum changes effective September 2, 2025 directly affects graduate students enrolled in UTSC programs. The Committee's discussion of TA responsibilities reassessment in the Department of Physical and Environmental Sciences has direct relevance to graduate student labour. The expansion of experiential learning opportunities and the new 'Health Across Disciplines' Working Group may create additional opportunities for graduate student engagement and research collaboration. However, this meeting contained no discussion of graduate funding, stipends, fee changes, TA/RA wages, housing, or other direct graduate support mechanisms.
keyItems: Graduate curriculum changes for 2025-26 academic year approved effective September 2, 2025 by motion duly moved and carried
Katie Larson outlined 2025-26 strategic curriculum priorities: culture of care, equity, anti-racism, Indigeneity, accessibility, with convergence of multiple institutional priorities
New 'Health Across Disciplines' Working Group launched as active working space for collective development across all departments in health-related fields
All UTSC departments directed to review 'dormant courses' (not taught for 7+ years) for potential retirement

Specialist in Paramedicine program (joint with Centennial College) external review showed near 100% employment rate upon graduation and high success rates on advanced emergency medical care assistant exams; administrative home transferred from Department of Biological Sciences to Department of Health and Society; new part-time Program Coordinator hired in 2024; major modification proposal in development

Department of Health and Society (reviewed March 2024) made changes including modified two major programs for enhanced distinctiveness, introduced new courses, incorporated enrollment data and student feedback into regular curriculum reviews; next review scheduled no later than 2031-32; changes grandfathered for upper-year students

Three program reviews presented: Specialist in Paramedicine (joint with Centennial College) showing near 100% employment rates; Department of Health and Society review from March 2024 detailing curriculum modifications and enhanced distinctiveness of two major programs; Department of Physical and Environmental Sciences review from March 2024 noting reassessed TA responsibilities, co-op pathways, and capacity planning. packages.title: UTSC Campus Affairs Committee - April 28, 2026
packages.summary: On April 28, 2026, the UTSC Campus Affairs Committee will consider five major items: closure of the Centre for Planetary Sciences (EDU:C) effective June 30, 2026, due to inactivity since 2019; a $1.75/session increase (full-time) and $0.50/session increase (part-time) to the WUSC Student Refugee Program fee within the Scarborough Campus Students' Union levy, raising full-time fees from $568.73 to $605.91 per session; approval in principle of the UTSC Field House project spanning 7,194 gross square metres to be funded through the Athletic Centre Facility Levy with construction targeted for April 2027–May 2029; presentation of Campus Safety's 2025 Annual Report highlighting three new Special Constables hired, the Student Ambassador Program launch, and 578 community assistance calls; and information on 307 recognized student groups with 8,874 members.
packages.gradRelevance: The WUSC Student Refugee Program fee increase directly affects graduate students if they are international students or support refugee sponsorship; however, the referendum was limited to undergraduate students. The Field House project is relevant to all students for athletics and recreation but has no specific graduate student funding or TA/RA labour implications. Campus Safety's Community Crisis Response Coordinator and Student Ambassador Program may provide services to graduate students. The Centre for Planetary Sciences closure will not affect graduate students as the centre has been inactive since 2019 and no faculty, staff, or students are currently engaged with it.
keyItems: Centre for Planetary Sciences (EDU:C) closure recommended effective June 30, 2026; centre was established 2013 as tri-campus research hub with ~18 faculty but inactive since 2019, with no identifiable academic, administrative, or outreach work, no active governance, and no staff or budget lines requiring reassignment.

WUSC Student Refugee Program fee increase: $1.75 per session for full-time students (from $0.75 to $2.50) and $0.50 for part-time students (from $0.25 to $0.75), effective Fall 2026 session; referendum held March 9–11, 2026 with 432 Yes (33.9%), 420 No (33.0%), 421 Abstain (33.1%) of 1,273 votes (8.4% turnout of ~15,179 eligible voters).
Total SCSU full-time fee rising from $568.73 to $605.91 per session (6.5% increase, $37.18); part-time fee rising from $63.73 to $66.31 (4.0% increase, $2.58), if all fee proposals approved.

UTSC Field House project scope approved: 7,194 gross square metres (6,332 net assignable square metres) with FIFA-regulation field (91.44m × 45.72m), three baseball cages, changerooms, administration spaces, student hub, and dance/aerobics studio; tensile fabric roof structure selected as most cost-effective; construction targeted April 2027–May 2029 with occupancy May 2029.

Campus Safety 2025 Annual Report presented: three new Special Constables hired, one promoted to Corporal; Student Ambassador Program launched August 2025 as first-of-its-kind tri-campus initiative; 578 community assistance calls, 159 medical assistance calls, 214 Community Crisis Response Coordinator interactions (198 students, 11 faculty, 5 staff); homicide of student in 2025 with CCTV systems supporting prompt arrest.

Campus Safety staff changes: one Special Constable departed for local police career, administrative coordinator role redesigned ahead of Lori Shields' retirement early 2026, one Building Patrol member departed, one hired.

documents.title: Annual Report: Campus Safety, UTSC
documents.summary: 2025 Campus Safety annual report detailing organizational structure, crime statistics, training, committees, and community engagement. Highlights include hiring of three new Special Constables, launch of Student Ambassador Program in August 2025, 578 community assistance calls, 159 medical assistance calls, and Community Crisis Response Coordinator handling 214 interactions. Crime data shows Theft Under $5,000 as most frequent (56 incidents, up 4), Sexual Assaults (2, up 2), Assaults (4, unchanged), Criminal Harassment (4, up 4), Homicide (1, up 1), with significant reduction in alarm calls (567, down 44) and fire alarms (46, down 44). Team comprised of Special Constables under Community Safety and Policing Act, Building Patrol Security, Community Crisis Response Coordinator, and administrative staff.
documents.gradRelevance: Community Crisis Response Coordinator interactions included 198 students and may provide mental health support; Campus Safety's service model and Community Crisis Response role affect student wellbeing. Student Ambassador Program provides employment and experiential learning for student volunteers; however, no specific graduate student stipend, TA/RA, or unionized labour matters are detailed.

documents.title: Campus Safety 2025 Annual Report Presentation
documents.summary: Slide presentation by Tanya Poppleton, Senior Director Campus Safety Operations, presented April 28, 2026. Covers tragic loss of student homicide in 2025 with CCTV support enabling prompt arrest and campus coordination. Highlights 2025 priorities including Culture of Care, Wellness Wednesdays, LEAD mentorship program, and collaborations with EDIO/HWC/ISC/JHSC. Key statistics: 578 Assist Community Members calls, 159 Medical Assistance calls, 214 CCRC interactions; 1,364 hours of training delivered. Forward-looking initiatives include infrastructure and technology enhancements, strengthened law enforcement partnerships, expanded personnel training, community-focused well-being initiatives, and data-driven practice evolution.

documents.title: Closure of the Centre for Planetary Sciences as an Extra-Departmental Unit C (EDU:C) at the University of Toronto Scarborough
documents.summary: Proposal to close Centre for Planetary Sciences (EDU:C) effective June 30, 2026. CPS was established in 2013 as cross-departmental tri-campus research hub with approximately 18 faculty from UTSC, St. George, and UTM spanning astrophysics, planetary dynamics, geophysics, and observational astronomy. Closure rationale: no identifiable academic, administrative, or outreach activity since ~2019; no active governance, advisory structures, or administrative oversight; website unchanged since 2017; last CPS-branded activity was cancelled 2019–20 colloquium. Consultation in November 2025 with Department of Physical and Environmental Sciences and February 2026 with Faculty of Arts & Science and UTM confirmed no active faculty engagement, no staff or student roles affected, and no external partner or funder connections remain. Closure aligns with University Guidelines for EDU:C requiring active programming, clear governance, and demonstrable outcomes. No net budget implications; minimal stakeholder impact as observatory-based teaching continues embedded in DPES courses.
documents.gradRelevance: Centre for Planetary Sciences closure has no direct graduate student impact as the centre has been inactive since 2019 with no current faculty engagement, student involvement, or programming. Graduate students in planetary/astrophysics research continue through departmental and institute structures (DPES, Department of Astronomy and Astrophysics, Dunlap Institute, CITA).

Referendum held March 9–11, 2026 yielded: Yes 432 (33.9%), No 420 (33.0%), Abstain/Spoiled 421 (33.1%), Total 1,273 ballots, 8.4% turnout of ~15,179 eligible voters. Preamble specified funds administered by SCSU, transferred to International Student Centre within OSEW for redistribution to WUSC Student Refugee Program students covering immigration/sponsorship fees, living expenses, settlement, and transitional support. Total SCSU full-time fee rises from $568.73 to $605.91 per session (6.5% increase, $37.18); part-time fee from $63.73 to $66.31 (4.0% increase, $2.58). Detailed fee breakdown table shows all component fees (Health Plan, Dental Plan, Student Centre, CFS, Day Care Subsidy, Orientation, UTSC Sports & Recreation Complex Levy, etc.) with cost-of-living and percentage increases.

documents.title: Capital Project: Report of the Project Planning Committee for the UTSC Field House - Project Scope and Sources of Funding
documents.summary: Project Planning Report for UTSC Field House dated April 10, 2026, recommending approval in principle of project scope totaling 7,194 gross square metres (6,332 net assignable square metres) to be funded through UTSC Athletic Centre Facility Levy. Field House envisions new enclosed FIFA-regulation field (91.44m × 45.72m) divisible into three soccer pitches, plus three baseball cages (21m × 4m × 3.7m), changerooms, washrooms, storage, administration spaces, student hub, food facilities, and dance/aerobics studio on lands adjacent to Toronto Pan Am Sports Centre (TPASC). Feasibility study by Workshop and HCMA Architects (November 2023–August 2025) evaluated three options; Option 2 (tensile fabric roof structure) selected as most cost-effective while providing weather protection, safety, and security. Project site on Morningside Athletic Fields (east of TPASC) is within area of potential influence of former landfill; environmental testing by Pinchin and early site investigations underway with City coordination. Design-build delivery selected; Project Specific Output Specifications (PSOS) completed; contract award targeted July 2026; construction April 2027–May 2029 with occupancy May 2029. Governance path includes UTSC Campus Affairs Committee, UTSC Campus Council, Academic Board, Business Board (financing approval), Executive Committee, and Governing Council. No open-session financial details provided (in-camera documentation contains Total Project Cost and funding sources). Previous approvals: November 2019 (Terms of Reference), April 2023 (feasibility study consultant fees), November 2025 (early site investigation and PSOS stipends).
documents.gradRelevance: UTSC Field House project provides athletics and recreation facilities benefiting all students including graduate students; Department of Athletics and Recreation manages programming within TPASC and will expand offerings. No specific graduate student funding, TA/RA labour, housing, or fee implications are detailed in the public document (total cost and funding sources remain confidential).

documents.title: Annual Report: Recognized Student Groups
documents.summary: Annual report for 2025–26 academic year documenting recognition decisions for UTSC student groups. As of March 10, 2026, 307 student groups have been granted recognition through July 31, 2026, with cumulative UTSC membership of 8,874 and average 29 members per group. 27 applications are under review for total of 334 active applications. Zero groups denied recognition; zero groups had recognition withdrawn. Student Groups Portfolio initiatives include multiple orientation sessions for group leaders, biweekly Club Hub drop-in sessions (80+ groups attended), and piloted tri-campus Clubs Leadership Retreat in August 2025 to be offered annually. Recognition benefits include right to use University name, eligibility for facilities/meeting space discounts or free access, temporary office space eligibility, directory listings, verification letters, and access to other University services. Groups must maintain democratic governance, open membership to all registered U of T students, non-discriminatory practices, and generally non-profit status; however, recognition does not imply University endorsement of group philosophy/beliefs nor legal liability for group activities.
documents.gradRelevance: Recognized student groups provide co-curricular engagement, leadership development, and community integration for all students including graduates. However, the report does not specify graduate-only student groups, graduate membership numbers, or graduate-specific programming. Most listed groups appear to be undergraduate-focused (e.g., pre-med, pre-pharmacy, undergraduate research). No information on graduate student union representation, fees, or services is provided.

packages.title: UTSC Campus Affairs Committee - April 28, 2026
packages.summary: The UTSC Campus Affairs Committee met on April 28, 2026, and approved four substantive items: (1) a recommendation to close the Centre for Planetary Sciences effective June 30, 2026, due to no active programming since 2019; (2) a $1.75 per-session increase ($.50 for part-time) to the WUSC Student Refugee Program fee beginning Fall 2026, approved via March 2026 student referendum, which will triple funding from ~$22,000 to support refugee students; (3) approval of the UTSC Field House project scope (7,194 gross square metres) to be funded through the Athletic Centre Facility Levy, with completion expected May 2029; and (4) approval of total project cost and funding sources for Field House in camera. Campus Safety presented its 2025 annual report documenting 578 community assistance calls, 159 medical calls, and the office's response to the December 2025 homicide of third-year student Shivank Avasthi.
packages.gradRelevance: The WUSC Student Refugee Program fee increase directly affects graduate and undergraduate students' fees: the increase of $1.75 per session will generate triple the current ~$22,000 annual funding to support refugee students beyond their first year and potentially support two students simultaneously rather than one. The Field House project, funded through the student Athletic Centre Facility Levy approved in a 2010 referendum, will provide exclusive indoor facilities for field sports and recreation; student clubs will not be charged space rental fees. Campus Safety's reporting on crisis response and co-op opportunities has marginal relevance. The Centre for Planetary Sciences closure has no documented impact on graduate students or research activities.
keyItems: Centre for Planetary Sciences closure approved effective June 30, 2026, as an Extra-Departmental Unit C with no active teaching, learning, research, or administrative activities since 2019 and no faculty or staff reassignments required

WUSC Student Refugee Program fee increase of $1.75 per session ($0.50 part-time) approved beginning Fall 2026, approved via March 2026 student referendum with 33% abstention rate; will increase annual funding from ~$22,000 to ~$66,000 to support refugee students for multiple years and potentially two students concurrently

UTSC Field House indoor sports facility approved in principle: 7,194 gross square metres (6,332 net assignable), to include field spaces, recreation rooms, administrative offices, student society offices, locker rooms, and rentable spaces; funded through UTSC Athletic Centre Facility Levy; completion targeted May 2029

Field House funded through 2010 student referendum levy originally for Toronto PanAm Sports Centre operations; student clubs will not pay rental fees; facility exclusive to UTSC with private rentals only if capacity allows; addresses 50% student body growth from 10,000 to 15,000 since 2010 and lack of grass field for winter field sports

Campus Safety 2025 annual report presented: 578 community assistance calls, 159 medical assistance calls, 214 Community Crisis Response Coordinator interactions; 1,364 training hours completed; office response to December 2025 homicide of Shivank Avasthi, 20-year-old third-year undergraduate student

Faculty Hub pilot project planned for Highland Hall with Fall 2026 construction start: bookable spaces, café, meeting rooms, soundproof phone booths, seating areas; participating faculty retain designated offices; designed to address isolation of multi-campus appointed faculty and hybrid workers

packages.title: UTSC Campus Affairs Committee - March 11, 2026
packages.summary: On March 11, 2026, the UTSC Campus Affairs Committee will consider SCSU student fee increases totalling up to $35.43 per session for full-time undergraduates (from $568.73 to $604.16) and $2.08 for part-time students, including raises in society fees ($0.69), Student Centre fees ($1.03), CFS fees ($0.23), and conditional Health Plan ($10.98) and Dental Plan ($13.88) increases pending SCSU Board approval. The committee will receive presentations on the 2026-27 UTSC Campus Operating Budget showing a balanced $412 million net expense budget reflecting constrained revenues, international student enrollment shifts, and strategic priorities including implementing an activity-based budget model, operationalizing new facilities (SAMIH health sciences complex, Toronto Zoo Conservation Campus, Indigenous House), and fostering a Culture of Care initiative. Sustainability updates highlight UTSC's Climate Positive 2050 commitment with ongoing geo-exchange field expansion and South Campus decarbonization projects.
packages.gradRelevance: While this meeting primarily addresses undergraduate student fees, graduate students should note that the UTSC budget presentation identifies constrained revenue growth (1.1% in 2026-27) and international student enrollment challenges that may affect institutional resources for graduate programming and support. The fee increases apply only to undergraduate students, not graduate students. The Campus Affairs Committee does not address graduate tuition, TA/RA stipends, or graduate-specific services in this agenda. However, the broader budget context of reduced international revenues (down $43 million projected) may have indirect implications for graduate funding pools and research support moving forward.

documents.title: UTSC Campus Operating Budget Overview: 2026-2027
documents.summary: Budget Report 2026-27 and Long-Range Budget Guidelines 2026-31 present institutional fiscal environment and financial planning landscape. University-wide operating budget of $3.66 billion increasing only 1.1% over prior year, approximately $100 million below previous long-range plan projections. International fee revenues projected to decrease $43 million due to federal PAL restrictions on international student permits (35% reduction 2024, 10% additional 2025, 7% additional 2026). Domestic tuition freeze continues; adjusted for inflation, domestic fees are 30% lower than 2018-19 levels. Provincial operating grant increase of 7% fully phased in 2026-27, providing only modest growth on 20% of operating revenues. Enrolment-related revenues (fees plus grants) represent 85% of operating budget. University Fund allocations include $4.1 million for emerging scholar recruitment through federal talent initiative and $4.2 million for divisional priorities including U of T Co-op and student mental health. Compensation costs account for 65% of expenses with ongoing increases creating budget pressure. Institutional investments in 100 emerging scholar positions, AI-readiness, and climate-positive capital projects.
documents.gradRelevance: Graduate students should note the constrained budget environment ($100 million shortfall from prior plan) and reduced international student revenues (-$43 million), which may impact institutional resources for graduate support, research funding, and TA/RA positions. The $4.1 million allocation for emerging scholar recruitment may create competition for resources. The $4.2 million divisional priority funding for mental health support may benefit graduate students. International enrolment challenges could affect graduate program enrollment and funding.

documents.title: Presentation: Campus Budget and Priorities for the 2026/2027 Fiscal Year
documents.summary: Chief Administrative Officer Andrew Arifuzzaman's presentation on UTSC's 2026-27 budget and strategic priorities. UTSC net expense budget of $412 million with five key strategic priorities: (1) implementing activity-based budget model in academic units with three-phase rollout (Phase 1 Feb-Apr 2026: databooks and dashboards; Phase 2 May-Jul 2026: 1:1 department meetings and model comparison; Phase 3 May 2027: implementation); (2) addressing international student shift with recalibration of enrollment targets; (3) building SAMIH (Myron and Berna Garron Health Sciences Complex) programming bringing together medicine, nursing, and pharmacy faculties; (4) operationalizing new facilities including retail commons and Indigenous House; (5) implementing Culture of Care initiative per Principal Linda Johnston's mandate emphasizing employee and student wellbeing. Undergraduate applications data shows UTSC applications at 6,869 as of March 3, 2026. International students remain significant revenue source. UTSC revenue sources show 63% from international fees, 18% from domestic fees, 15% from operating grants, 4% other in 2026-27 projection.
documents.gradRelevance: Graduate students should be aware of the activity-based budget model implementation which may affect departmental allocations and resource availability. The SAMIH operationalization may create opportunities for graduate research spaces. The Culture of Care initiative could benefit graduate student wellbeing through workplace mental health investments. International student enrollment challenges may affect graduate program demographics and funding.

documents.title: Presentation: UTSC Sustainability Update
documents.summary: Sustainability office presentation by Jeff Miller and Patricia Escobar on UTSC's Climate Positive commitment and progress. UTSC published Climate Positive Plan in December 2025 targeting net-zero-plus GHG emissions reduction of 80% annually by 2050. Current projects include: converting South Campus buildings off steam heating to geo-exchange systems; expanding geo-exchange field in S-Wing Quad completing Spring 2026; energy audits for South Campus institutional buildings and Central Utilities Plant; entrance vestibule glazing improvements in Andrews Buildings. Biking for Community bikeshare program (free since 2023) reports 865 rentals in 2025 and 93 participants at cycling events. Repair Café community engagement series in Meeting Place and Harmony Commons with 110 participants October 2025, 46 in November, 83 in February. Sustainability Office volunteer program recruits 15-20 students annually for 30-40 volunteer hours with co-curricular recognition. Organic waste innovation at Harmony Commons under study by M.Env.Sci placement student. New City of Toronto Bike Share kiosk planned at TPASC.
documents.gradRelevance: Limited direct relevance to graduate students. Sustainability programming offers volunteer opportunities (30-40 hours annually with CCR recognition) potentially available to graduate students. M.Env.Sci work placement studying organic waste and Scope 3 GHG emissions may involve graduate research. Repair Café and bikeshare programs available to all campus community.

packages.title: UTSC Campus Affairs Committee - March 11, 2026
packages.summary: On March 11, 2026, the UTSC Campus Affairs Committee approved fee increases for Fall 2026: SCSU society fee up $0.69/session ($0.04 part-time), Student Centre up $1.03/session ($0.31 part-time), CFS up $0.23/session (full-time only), Health Plan up to $10.98/session (full-time only), and Dental Plan up to $13.88/session (full-time only), pending SCSU Board confirmation. CAO Andrew Arifuzzaman reported that the 2026-27 operating budget decreased despite 15.7% yield growth in domestic students, due to a 50% drop in international student applications from government visa policy changes; UTSC is implementing an activity-based budget model, operationalizing four new facilities (Garron Health Sciences Complex, Retail and Parking Commons, Indigenous House, Toronto Zoo Conservation Campus), and pursuing cost-saving technological innovations. The committee also received reports on UTSC's Climate Positive Plan (published December 2025, targeting 80% emissions reduction by 2050), student housing at 96% occupancy for first-year and near 100% for upper-year but only 82% for graduate students, Academic Advising experiencing 27% demand increase, and renewed Toronto Region Conservation Authority agreements for Highland Creek Village flood prevention.
packages.gradRelevance: Graduate student housing occupancy stands at only 82% compared to 96% for first-year and nearly 100% for upper-year undergraduates, indicating potential availability but also lower demand. The approved student fee increases (Health Plan $10.98/session and Dental Plan $13.88/session for full-time students) directly affect graduate students enrolled in Fall 2026 onwards. Academic Advising and Career Centres reported 27% increased demand but no specific mention of graduate-specific services or staffing. No targeted funding, stipend, TA/RA wage, or unionized labour matters were discussed in this meeting.

packages.title: UTSC Campus Affairs Committee - January 27, 2026
packages.summary: On January 27, 2026, UTSC Campus Affairs Committee will consider multiple fee increases affecting students. Health & Wellness Service Fee will increase 11.30% from $94.70 to $105.40 per full-time student per session ($18.94 to $21.08 part-time); Athletics & Recreation Fee increases 0.79% from $142.05 to $143.17 full-time ($28.41 to $28.63 part-time); and Student Services Fee increases 4.12% from $217.75 to $226.74 full-time ($43.55 to $45.34 part-time). On February 20, 2026, these fees were superseded by electronic ballot with minor adjustments to part-time Health & Wellness charges. Service Ancillaries (housing, parking, food, conference) will have capital budgets of $134.8 million in 2026-27, including $132 million for Retail and Parking Commons parking garage opening early 2026-27. Student Housing proposes increases of 3.0%–16.7% (mostly 3%–5%); Parking proposes 3% permit fee increases; Food Services proposes 3% meal plan increase.
