packages.title: UTSC Campus Affairs Committee - September 10, 2025
packages.summary: On September 10, 2025, the UTSC Campus Affairs Committee heard reports on enrollment, student services, and capital projects. Vice-Principal Karin Ruhlandt reported UTSC welcomed nearly 4,200 new students, bringing total enrollment to almost 15,000, and highlighted success of the Certificate in Health Studies (900 incoming, 1,700 total after 2 years). Dean Tim Tang reported Fall 2025 Orientation drew over 900 participants, the new Course Selection Module was viewed 17,000 times, AccessAbility office intake requests increased 57%, and Athletics saw 848 first-year students in the Athletics Challenge. Chief Administrative Officer Andrew Arifuzzaman updated on capital projects: Indigenous House completion delayed to end of November 2025, the parking structure on track for late 2026 completion, and two new projects (fieldhouse and LAMP Project) expected in the current governance year. The Committee also learned that parking rates would shift from the usual 10% annual increase to a new pricing strategy under development.
packages.gradRelevance: The Committee discussed expansions to academic advising services that will benefit graduate students seeking career guidance, particularly in humanities and social sciences. AccessAbility office intake requests increased 57% and contact points increased 86%, affecting both undergraduate and graduate students requiring accommodations. The delayed Indigenous House completion (now end of November 2025) may affect graduate student housing availability. No specific graduate student fee changes, stipend amounts, TA/RA rates, or unionized-labour matters were discussed or decided in this meeting.
keyItems: UTSC enrolled nearly 4,200 new students in Fall 2025, bringing total student population to almost 15,000, meeting domestic enrollment goals for the first time in many years
Certificate in Health Studies program reached 1,700 total enrollment after only 2 years since launch, with approximately 900 in the incoming cohort
Fall 2025 Orientation milestone: 900+ participants, 66 parents, first-ever UTSC invocation and matriculation ceremony on August 29, 2025 with 1,500 attendees
Course Selection Module viewed over 17,000 times; 'Get Started' initiative supported 1,340 students and nearly 400 parents May–August 2025
AccessAbility office new student intake requests increased 57%; total points of contact (in-person, email, phone) increased 86%
Athletics & Recreation Athletics Challenge event saw 848 first-year participants, over 200 more than previous year

packages.title: UTSC Campus Council - May 25, 2026
packages.summary: On May 25, 2026, UTSC Campus Council will consider six major items: approval to close the Centre for Planetary Sciences (EDU:C) effective June 30, 2026, which has been inactive since 2019 with no teaching, research, or programming; recommendation to approve the UTSC Field House project scope of 7,194 gross square metres (6,332 net assignable) to be funded through the UTSC Athletic Centre Facility Levy, with construction start planned for April 2027 and occupancy May 2029; approval of Scarborough Campus Students' Union fee increases of $1.75 per session for full-time students and $0.50 for part-time students in the WUSC Student Refugee Program, raising full-time SCSU fees from $568.73 to $605.91 per session; receipt of the 2024-2025 Development & Alumni Relations Annual Impact Report highlighting philanthropic support and emergency grants for students; and consent agenda approval of the March 31, 2026 Capital Projects Report showing three active projects including UTSC Retail & Parking Commons (87% complete, $80M spent of $140.5M TPC), UTSC Indigenous House (97% complete), and Myron & Berna Garron Health Sciences Complex (88% complete). Two additional in-camera items regarding Field House total project costs and 2026-27 committee appointments will be discussed in closed session.
packages.gradRelevance: The WUSC Student Refugee Program fee increase directly affects graduate students if they pay SCSU fees (though the referendum and fee structure target undergraduates explicitly). The Centre for Planetary Sciences closure will not impact graduate students as the centre has been inactive since 2019 with no affiliated graduate programming. The Field House project may indirectly benefit graduate students through enhanced campus recreational facilities, though it is primarily an undergraduate athletics initiative. The Development & Alumni Relations report notes emergency grants provide critical stability for students facing financial hardship, though specific graduate allocation data is not detailed.
keyItems: Closure of Centre for Planetary Sciences (EDU:C) effective June 30, 2026: tri-campus research hub established 2013 with ~18 faculty members, inactive since 2019, no teaching/research/programming, no staff or budget lines affected, redundant functions now covered by Department of Physical and Environmental Sciences, David A. Dunlap Department of Astronomy and Astrophysics, Dunlap Institute, and Canadian Institute for Theoretical Astrophysics

UTSC Field House approval in principle: 7,194 gross square metres (6,332 net assignable square metres), FIFA-regulation field 91.44m x 45.72m divisible into three smaller pitches, three baseball cages 21m x 4m x 3.7m, includes changerooms, washrooms, administration, student hub, food facilities, dance/aerobics studio, funded through UTSC Athletic Centre Facility Levy

Field House project schedule: Design Build delivery selected as most cost-effective, construction start April 2027, occupancy targeted May 2029, municipal approvals and building permits required, site subject to former landfill area of potential influence requiring environmental testing

Field House project scope selection: Option 2 (tensile fabric structure with minimal mechanical loads) chosen as most cost-effective while providing weather protection, safety, and security; Option 1 was open-air structure, Option 3 was conventional insulated steel structure

Scarborough Campus Students' Union WUSC Student Refugee Program fee increase: full-time students $1.75 per session ($0.75 to $2.50), part-time students $0.50 per session ($0.25 to $0.75), total SCSU fee rising from $568.73 to $605.91 for full-time (increase of $37.18) and from $63.73 to $66.31 for part-time (increase of $2.58), effective Fall 2026 session
WUSC referendum results (March 9-11, 2026): 432 Yes (33.9%), 420 No (33.0%), 421 Abstain/Spoiled (33.1%), total 1,273 ballots, approximately 8.4% voter turnout of ~15,179 eligible voters

documents.title: Annual Report: Development & Alumni Relations Office
documents.summary: 2024-2025 annual impact report summarizing UTSC Development & Alumni Relations priorities centred on creating communities of care through people-centred storytelling, highlighting diverse stakeholders, and prioritizing representation. Report addresses four key themes: introducing culture of care (new era of leadership centred on care, connection, collective success), highlighting lifelong alumni relationships (meaningful engagement transcends geography, alumni exemplify UTSC's influence globally), people behind progress (emergency grants provide critical stability for students in financial hardship, demand for support continues to grow), and prioritizing accountability (visionary leadership drives progress, donor support foundational for cultivating cultural leaders). Also addresses Tamil language preservation through philanthropic giving and community networks.
documents.gradRelevance: Report indicates emergency grants provide critical stability for students facing sudden financial hardship and emphasizes continued demand for student support requiring sustained commitment, though does not specify graduate student allocation amounts or eligibility criteria.

documents.title: Closure of the Centre for Planetary Sciences as an Extra-Departmental Unit C (EDU:C) at the University of Toronto Scarborough
documents.summary: Proposal to close Centre for Planetary Sciences (EDU:C) effective June 30, 2026, a tri-campus research hub established 2013 with approximately 18 faculty members from UTSC, St. George Campus, and UTM conducting research in astrophysics, planetary dynamics, geophysics, and observational astronomy. Centre has been inactive since approximately 2019 with no identifiable academic, administrative, or outreach work; website unchanged since 2017; last branded activity was cancelled 2019-20 colloquium. Closure rationale: inactivity and loss of core functions (no governance, advisory structures, or administrative oversight), redundancy of mandate now covered by Department of Physical and Environmental Sciences, David A. Dunlap Department of Astronomy and Astrophysics, Dunlap Institute, and Canadian Institute for Theoretical Astrophysics, and misalignment with EDU Guidelines requiring active programming and clear governance. Closure will have minimal impact: no active teaching/research/programming, faculty continue through departmental/institute structures, student learning opportunities remain in DPES courses, no staff reassignment required, no budget implications. Consultation with DPES (November 2025) and Faculty of Arts & Science/UTM (February 2026) confirmed support or no objection.
documents.gradRelevance: No graduate relevance—Centre has been inactive since 2019 with no graduate programming, supervision, or affiliated graduate students. All graduate learning opportunities and research supervision have migrated to permanent departmental units.

documents.title: Capital Project (Level 3): Report of the Project Planning Committee for the University of Toronto Scarborough (UTSC) Field House – Project Scope and Sources of Funding
documents.summary: Project Planning Report for UTSC Field House capital project recommending approval in principle of 7,194 gross square metres (6,332 net assignable square metres) to be funded through UTSC Athletic Centre Facility Levy. Field house includes FIFA-regulation turf field (91.44m x 45.72m) divisible into three smaller soccer pitches via ceiling curtains, three baseball cages (21m x 4m x 3.7m each), changerooms, washrooms, field storage, administration spaces, student hub, food facilities, and dance/aerobics studio. Project uses Design Build delivery methodology with tensile fabric roof structure (Option 2) selected as most cost-effective while providing weather protection, safety, and security. Site located on Morningside Athletic Fields east of Toronto Pan Am Sports Centre, subject to former landfill area of potential influence requiring environmental testing. Construction start scheduled April 2027 via Design Build; occupancy targeted May 2029. Project addresses growing student demand for indoor field programming space during winter/spring months and strengthens community partnerships. Project Planning Committee meetings began March 2020; feasibility study by Workshop and HCMA Architects conducted November 2023–August 2025 evaluating three structural options.
documents.gradRelevance: Field House project may indirectly benefit graduate students through enhanced campus recreational facilities and student employment/mentorship opportunities mentioned in project description, though project is primarily undergraduate athletics initiative. No specific graduate programming or services impact identified.

documents.title: Compulsory Non-Academic Incidental Fees: UTSC Student Societies - Proposals for Fee Increases
documents.summary: Fee increase request from Scarborough Campus Students' Union (SCSU) for WUSC Student Refugee Program: full-time students increase $1.75 per session (from $0.75 to $2.50), part-time students increase $0.50 per session (from $0.25 to $0.75), with annual cost-of-living adjustment provision based on Ontario Consumer Price Index. Referendum held March 9-11, 2026 with results: 432 Yes (33.9%), 420 No (33.0%), 421 Abstain/Spoiled (33.1%), 1,273 total ballots cast, approximately 8.4% voter turnout of ~15,179 eligible voters. If approved, total fall/winter SCSU fee increases from $568.73 to $605.91 for full-time students (up $37.18, or up to 6.5%) and from $63.73 to $66.31 for part-time students (up $2.58, or 4.0%). Fees administered by SCSU and transferred to International Student Centre within Office of Student Experience & Well-being for WUSC Student Refugee Program support including immigration/sponsorship fees, basic living/settlement expenses, and transitional/upper-year support. Office of Vice-Provost, Students confirmed compliance with Policy for Compulsory Non-Academic Incidental Fees and reported no member complaints.
documents.gradRelevance: Fee increase targets undergraduate students explicitly (referendum states UTSC undergraduates), though graduate students paying SCSU fees may be affected if they fall under compulsory fee structure. WUSC Student Refugee Program funds immigration sponsorship fees, basic living expenses, and settlement support—potentially relevant to international graduate students. International Student Centre administers funds for student support regardless of level.

documents.title: UTSC Capital Projects Report dated March 31, 2026
documents.summary: Construction status report for three active UTSC capital projects as of March 31, 2026: (1) UTSC Retail & Parking Commons (P200-15-053): 87% complete, original approval February 2022, construction start February 2024, forecasted completion September 2026, total project cost $140.5M with $116M committed and $80M spent, 42,877 gross square metres, contractors Ellis Don, design by Gh3 and Behnisch, currently installing curtain walls, interior partitions, and photovoltaic panel steel structure with mechanical/electrical/sprinkler work ongoing, low schedule and budget risk; (2) UTSC Indigenous House (P200-19-081): 97% complete, original approval December 2020, construction start May 2022, occupancy granted mid-February 2026, total project cost $25.5M with $20.7M committed and $20.1M spent, 937.4 gross square metres, contractor Magil, design by Formline with LGA, deficiency corrections underway with substantial performance forecast April 2026, low schedule and budget risk with remaining contingency low and pending delay claim from contractor; (3) Myron & Berna Garron Health Sciences Complex/SAMIH (P200-20-130): 88% complete, original approval February 2023, construction start October 2023, forecasted completion July 2026, total project cost $161.6M with $133.9M committed and $106.2M spent, 13,423 gross square metres, contractor EllisDon/DSAI, design by DSAI/MVRDV, exterior photovoltaic cladding and interior millwork/equipment installation ongoing, low schedule and budget risk with potential Scarborough Health Network ground floor implementation challenges.
documents.gradRelevance: UTSC Retail & Parking Commons and SAMIH may provide graduate student facilities (health sciences complex explicitly relevant for graduate health sciences programs). Indigenous House directly relevant if graduate students are residents or access services. Reports provide construction timeline and status updates but do not specify graduate-specific programming or services.

packages.title: UTSC Campus Council - May 25, 2026
packages.summary: On May 25, 2026, UTSC Campus Council held its final meeting of the 2025-26 cycle, with Chair Grace Westcott stepping down after six years and Vice-Chair Aarthi Ashok assuming the role for 2026-27. Council approved the permanent closure of the Centre for Planetary Sciences (EDU:C), effective June 30, 2026, due to absence of administrative, academic, or outreach activities since 2019 and failure to meet University guidelines. Council approved a student society fee increase for the WUSC Student Refugee Program, which was endorsed via student referendum in March 2026 and funds tuition, living expenses, and transition support for refugee students. The Field House project—an indoor facility for field sports with recreation rooms and student spaces, funded entirely by the existing UTSC Athletic Centre Facility Levy established in 2010—was approved in principle with expected completion in May 2029. Development & Alumni Relations reported that UTSC had raised $82 million toward its $75 million target under the university-wide "Defy Gravity" campaign by 2029, including a $3 million Chair in Tamil Studies funded by over 3,800 donors.
packages.gradRelevance: The WUSC Student Refugee Program fee increase directly affects graduate students who are refugees or part of that program, as it funds tuition, living expenses, and transition needs. The Field House project will provide athletic and recreation facilities accessible to graduate students from all three campuses, though it is funded by an undergraduate athletic centre levy rather than graduate fees. The closure of the Centre for Planetary Sciences may affect graduate students in planetary science research, though Council noted minimal impact given no active research or teaching activities have occurred since 2019. No other items in this meeting address graduate student funding, stipends, TA/RA matters, housing, or unionized labour.
keyItems: Permanent closure of Centre for Planetary Sciences (EDU:C) effective June 30, 2026, approved on motion; closure based on absence of identifiable administrative, academic, or outreach activities since 2019 and failure to meet University Guidelines for Extra-Departmental Units

UTSC Development & Alumni Relations raised $82 million toward $75 million campaign target under university-wide 'Defy Gravity' campaign, with specific success of $3 million Chair in Tamil Studies funded by 3,800+ donors giving amounts as low as $25

Field House capital project approved in principle: indoor facility for field sports (cricket, soccer) with recreation rooms, administrative offices, student society spaces, locker rooms, and rentable spaces; funded entirely by existing UTSC Athletic Centre Facility Levy (established 2010 via student referendum); expected completion May 2029; accessible to students from all three campuses

WUSC Student Refugee Program fee increase approved following March 2026 student referendum; funds tuition, living expenses, and transition needs for refugee students through International Student Centre

Field House funding: UTSC student population grew 50% from 10,000 to 15,000 students since 2010 levy approval, increasing levy revenue base to support facility operating costs; no student tuition used; City approval sought for low-temperature regulation expected to reduce carbon footprint by 40%
Grace Westcott concluded six-year tenure as Chair; Aarthi Ashok assumed Chair role for 2026-27 governance year

packages.title: UTSC Campus Council - April 14, 2026
packages.summary: On April 14, 2026, UTSC Campus Council approved increases to Scarborough Campus Students' Union (SCSU) compulsory fees, effective Fall 2026: society fee +$0.69/session (full-time; +$0.04 part-time), Student Centre +$1.03/session (full-time; +$0.31 part-time), CFS +$0.23/session (full-time only), Health Plan up to +$10.98/fall-winter session (full-time only), and Dental Plan up to +$13.88/fall-winter session (full-time only), bringing total full-time fees to up to $604.16/session from $568.73. Council received for information the 2026-27 UTSC Campus Operating Budget presentation showing $412 million net expense budget with priorities including activity-based budgeting implementation, international student recruitment recalibration, Myron & Berna Garron Health Sciences Complex (SAMIH) operationalization, and Culture of Care initiatives. Council also received the Code of Student Conduct review summary reporting 44 hours of consultations with 350+ participants and recommending creation of a centralized Student Conduct Office, updated code structure in two documents (Code and Procedures), expanded scope including online harassment, and a mental health diversion pathway.
packages.gradRelevance: The SCSU fee increases directly affect graduate students who pay compulsory student society fees; full-time grad students will face increased fees of up to $35.43 per session (Health and Dental Plan increases pending SCSU Board confirmation). The 2026-27 UTSC budget allocates $408 million university-wide for student financial support (UTAPS and divisional programs) and mentions $4.2 million for student mental health investment and U of T Co-op support. The Code of Student Conduct review addresses conduct processes applicable to all students including graduate students and proposes mental health diversion alternatives to punitive sanctions.
keyItems: SCSU fee increases approved for Fall 2026: society portion +$0.69/session full-time (+$0.04 part-time); Student Centre +$1.03/session full-time (+$0.31 part-time); CFS +$0.23/session full-time only; Health Plan up to +$10.98/fall-winter full-time; Dental Plan up to +$13.88/fall-winter full-time; total full-time fee rising to up to $604.16 from $568.73 per session.

UTSC 2026-27 Campus Operating Budget of $412 million net expense (projected) with allocation priorities: implementing activity-based budget model in academic units, addressing international student enrollment shift through recruitment diversification and merit scholarships (6% of international undergraduate tuition), and operationalizing new facilities.

SAMIH (Myron & Berna Garron Health Sciences Complex) bringing together Temerty Faculty of Medicine, Lawrence Bloomberg Faculty of Nursing, and Leslie Dan Faculty of Pharmacy; construction 79.3% complete as of December 2025 with July 2026 completion target; $161.6M total project cost approved.
UTSC Retail & Parking Commons project 69% complete as of December 2025 tracking toward July 2026 occupancy; $140.5M approved total project cost.
UTSC Indigenous House project 97% complete as of December 2025; occupancy pending city document review; $25.5M approved total project cost.

Code of Student Conduct review consultation process conducted November 2025-February 2026 with 44 hours of consultation, 350+ participants, and 50+ online submissions; identified gaps including lack of code relevance to university life, behaviors not meeting threshold, decentralized reporting structure, and unclear intersections with mental health policy.

documents.title: UTSC Campus Operating Budget: Overview of the UTSC Campus Operating Budget 2026-2027
documents.summary: Presentation and budget report for UTSC's 2026-27 operating budget of $412 million, presented to Campus Council for information. Document outlines strategic priorities aligned with the 'Inspiring Inclusive Excellence' strategic plan (2020-2025), including resource allocation to differentiate the campus, reorganize structures for recruitment/retention, and build infrastructure for teaching and research. Budget demonstrates balanced allocation with focus on activity-based budgeting implementation, international student diversification in response to federal permit caps, SAMIH operationalization, and Culture of Care implementation.
documents.gradRelevance: Graduate students may be affected by investment priorities in mental health ($4.2 million), Co-op programming ($4.2 million), and faculty recruitment for emerging scholars ($4.1 million). The budget context of constrained international recruitment and activity-based budgeting may impact graduate program funding and TA/RA availability as departments reallocate resources.

documents.title: Presentation: Campus Budget and Priorities for the 2026/2027 Fiscal Year
documents.summary: Andrew Arifuzzaman's (UTSC Chief Administrative Officer) presentation to Council detailing five budget priorities: implementing activity-based budgeting in academic units with Financial Dashboards and Departmental Data Books (phased rollout FY2025-26 through FY2027-28); addressing international student shift through recalibration of enrollment targets and merit scholarships representing 6% of international undergraduate tuition; building SAMIH-related programming with three faculties (Medicine, Nursing, Pharmacy) and new health science certificates; operationalizing new facilities (SAMIH, Retail & Parking Commons, Indigenous House, Toronto Zoo Conservation Campus); and implementing Culture of Care as cornerstone of leadership mandate. UTSC net expense budget growth projected: $335M (2025-26) to $412M (2031-32).
documents.gradRelevance: Activity-based budget model implementation directly affects graduate programs through departmental control of budgets and incentives for revenue generation; SAMIH operationalization may create new graduate research and teaching opportunities in health sciences; Culture of Care framework applies to graduate student well-being and support.

documents.title: Compulsory Non-Academic Incidental Fees – UTSC Student Society Fees, Proposal for Increases
documents.summary: Approval document detailing SCSU fee increases for Fall 2026 session. Society portion increases $0.69/session full-time ($0.04 part-time) based on December 2025 Ontario CPI of 2.1%; Student Centre increases $1.03/session full-time ($0.31 part-time); CFS increases $0.23/session full-time based on December 2024 Canada CPI of 2.4%; Health Plan increases up to $10.98/session and Dental Plan up to $13.88/session based on approved 10% inflationary cap, pending SCSU Board confirmation. Total full-time fee reaches up to $604.16 from $568.73 (up to +6.2%). Detailed breakdown shows all component fees and notes compliance with Policy for Compulsory Non-Academic Incidental Fees following due constitutional process.
documents.gradRelevance: Graduate students enrolled at UTSC who pay compulsory SCSU fees will experience fee increases of up to $35.43 per fall/winter session; Health and Dental Plan increases particularly affect graduate students seeking refundable health/dental coverage; part-time graduate students face smaller increases of $2.08 per session.

documents.title: Report on the Review of the Code of Student Conduct: Summary of Consultation and Feedback
documents.summary: Report on Code of Student Conduct review commissioned by Provost, chaired by Professor Cheryl Regehr. Review occurred November 7, 2025-February 20, 2026 across all three campuses with 44 hours of consultation including 12 open sessions, 30+ staff consultations, 3 student association consultations, and 50+ anonymous online submissions from 350+ participants. Consultation identified four key issues: lack of Code relevance to university life with unclear overly-legal language, concerning behaviors not meeting Code threshold, decentralized reporting/case management creating confusion and inconsistent sanctions, and unclear intersections between Code and mental health policy. Four recommendations proposed: update Code structure into separate Code and Procedures documents; clarify and broaden scope to include online harassment and weapons; create central Student Conduct Office under Vice-Provost, Students oversight; create mental health diversion pathway.
documents.gradRelevance: Code of Student Conduct applies to all students including graduate students; current decentralized structure and mental health intersections affect graduate student conduct proceedings and accommodations; proposed Student Conduct Office centralization and mental health diversion pathway may provide graduate students clearer processes and alternatives to punitive sanctions for conduct concerns.

documents.title: Presentation: Code of Student Conduct Review 2025-26 - Summary of Consultation Feedback & Preliminary Recommendations
documents.summary: Professor Cheryl Regehr's presentation to Council on Code of Student Conduct review findings. Consultation launched November 17, 2025 via Provost memo, website, targeted email blasts to stakeholders, and digital displays on all three campuses. Consultation statistics: 12 open sessions (student/staff/faculty/librarian participants across tri-campus), 30+ staff consultations, 3 student association consultations, 50+ online submissions, 350+ total participants, 44+ hours of consultations. Landscape scan reviewed 20+ Canadian institutions; legal research conducted. Four recommendations detailed: (1) restructure Code into two documents with behavior expectations preamble and tiered offences with alternative sanctions; (2) update offences for online harassment/weapons, create tiers for local vs. central handling, add educative/restorative options; (3) establish centralized Student Conduct Office reporting to Vice-Provost, Students with assessment, triage, and guidance functions; (4) create mental health diversion process balancing accountability and accommodation.
documents.gradRelevance: Graduate students represented in consultation feedback; proposed mental health diversion process and restorative options may provide graduate students alternative accountability pathways; centralized Student Conduct Office may provide clearer processes for graduate student conduct concerns.

documents.title: Capital Projects Report dated December 31, 2025
documents.summary: UTSC capital construction status report as of December 31, 2025 tracking three major projects. (1) UTSC Retail & Parking Commons (P200-15-053): 69% complete, $140.5M approved total project cost, designed by Gh3/Behnisch with CM Ellis Don, tracking 3-week delay due to foundation design changes, forecasted July 2026 occupancy with low schedule and budget risk. (2) UTSC Indigenous House (P200-19-081): 97% complete, $25.5M approved cost, designed by Formline with LGA, occupancy pending city document review, February 2026 substantial performance target, high schedule risk from commissioning and deficiency corrections, low budget risk. (3) Myron & Berna Garron Health Sciences Complex/SAMIH (P200-20-130): 79.3% complete, $161.6M approved cost, designed by DSAI/MVRDV with DB EllisDon/DSAI, July 2026 completion on track, bringing together Temerty Faculty of Medicine, Lawrence Bloomberg Faculty of Nursing, Leslie Dan Faculty of Pharmacy, low schedule and budget risk.
documents.gradRelevance: SAMIH completion (July 2026) will provide new research and teaching facilities for graduate students in health sciences disciplines; new Indigenous House may provide enhanced residence and cultural space for Indigenous graduate students; Retail & Parking Commons operationalization affects graduate student campus experience and parking availability.

packages.title: UTSC Campus Council - April 14, 2026
packages.summary: At the April 14, 2026 UTSC Campus Council meeting, Vice-Principal Linda Johnston reported $3.895 million in successful grant funding across CIHR ($1.9M at 33% success rate), SSHRC partnership ($297K at 50%) and insight grants ($649K at 59%), and NSERC programs ($287K tools/instruments at 33%, $762K discovery grants at 85.7%). The Council approved student fee increases totaling $0.69–$0.23 per session across society, student centre, and CFS portions, with health plan increases up to $10.98 and dental up to $13.88 per session (full-time only) pending SCSU Board confirmation. Professor Cheryl Regehr presented findings from a Code of Student Conduct review (350+ participants, consultation Nov 17, 2025–Feb 20, 2026) recommending splitting the Code into two documents, creating a centralized Student Conduct Office under Vice-Provost Students, adding online harassment and weapons offences, and establishing restorative/educative outcomes. Dean Tim Tang reported strong demand for student services including 500+ residence tour attendees (52% increase), 2,100+ immigration advising appointments (80% increase), 300+ active student clubs (up from 281), and launch of the ConnectYou app pairing isolated students (215 participants to date).
packages.gradRelevance: The fee increases directly affect graduate students: student society fees rise $0.69–$0.23 per session; health and dental plan increases (up to $10.98 and $13.88 per session) apply to full-time students including graduate students, pending SCSU Board approval. The Code of Student Conduct revision affects all students including graduate students through new online harassment provisions, clearer procedures, and a proposed mental health diversion process. The immigration advising expansion (80% increase to 2,100+ appointments annually) benefits international graduate students. Dean Tang's report on housing demand and the townhouses modernization project beginning summer 2026 is relevant to graduate students seeking on-campus housing.
keyItems: Grant funding successes: CIHR $1.9M (33% success rate), SSHRC partnership development $297K (50%), SSHRC Insight $649K (59%), NSERC Tools/Instruments $287K (33%), NSERC Discovery $762K (85.7%) — all above national and tri-campus averages.

Motion carried: UTSC student fees increased effective Fall 2026: society portion +$0.69/session (+$0.04 part-time), Student Centre +$1.03/session (+$0.31 part-time), CFS +$0.23/session (full-time only); Health Plan +up to $10.98/session (full-time), Dental Plan +up to $13.88/session (full-time) subject to SCSU Board confirmation.

Code of Student Conduct review completed with 350+ participants (Nov 17, 2025–Feb 20, 2026): key recommendations include splitting into Code and Procedures documents, creating centralized Student Conduct Office under Vice-Provost Students, adding online harassment and weapons offences, establishing educative/restorative outcomes tier, and addressing mental health diversion.

International student enrollment impact: federal visa policy changes caused significant decline in international applications; international students account for ~60% of operating revenue; new 5-year plan expects domestic enrollment steady, modest international recovery.

Provincial tuition freeze lifted with new funding: University projects $85M additional operating grants + $10M tuition revenue in 2026-27; $68M operating + $5M tuition in 2027-28; expected return to financial stability by 2028-29.

Activity-Based Budget Model implementation: Phase 2 underway, full implementation expected May 2027; Institutional Planning and Research Office providing academic departments with data dashboards for evidence-based budget decisions.

packages.title: UTSC Campus Council - February 23, 2026
packages.summary: At the February 23, 2026 UTSC Campus Council meeting, the university approved a balanced $3.66B operating budget (1.1% increase) with $400M allocated for student financial support, while international enrolment targets were recalibrated due to federal policy shifts on student visas. Council approved three major fee increases: the UTSC Sports & Recreation Complex Levy rising 4% ($8.62 per session for full-time students, $1.73 part-time), the Health & Wellness Service Fee increasing 11.30% to $105.40 per full-time student per session, and the Student Services Fee rising 4.13% to $226.74 per full-time student per session. Research funding grew from $14M to $23M over five years (59% increase), external funding reached just over $22M in 2024-25, and the new Indigenous House residence achieved substantial completion at 97%. Applications to UTSC increased overall by 5%, with a notable 18% increase in joint degree applications with Centennial College, though domestic applications declined 2% while international grew 14%.
packages.gradRelevance: Graduate students should note the three compulsory fee increases that will directly affect their stipends and financial support: Health & Wellness Service Fee rising 11.30% ($10.70 per full-time student per session) and Student Services Fee increasing 4.13% ($8.99 per full-time student per session), both effective Summer 2026. The $400M university-wide student financial support allocation and Project Catalyst cost-reduction initiative may impact graduate funding and TA/RA compensation structures. The substantial completion of Indigenous House (97% done) provides additional on-campus housing that may affect graduate student housing access, and planned Premium Townhouse renovations beginning in 2026-27 could further expand graduate residence options. The recalibration of international student intake targets due to federal visa policy changes will likely affect international graduate student recruitment and funding.
keyItems: Institutional Operating Budget 2026-27 of $3.66B approved, representing 1.1% increase over prior year, with $400M allocated for student financial support university-wide
Total external research funding increased from $14M (five-year baseline) to $23M in 2024-25, representing 59% growth; 2024-25 figure was just over $22M with tri-agency funding steady at ~64% of total
UTSC Sports & Recreation Complex Levy increased 4% effective Summer 2026: $8.62 per session for full-time students and $1.73 for part-time students
Health & Wellness Service Fee increased 11.30% effective Summer 2026 to $105.40 per full-time student per session ($21.08 part-time), an increase of $10.70 for full-time ($2.14 part-time)
Athletics and Recreation Fee increased 0.79% to $143.17 per full-time student per session ($28.63 part-time), an increase of $1.12 for full-time ($0.22 part-time)
Student Services Fee increased 4.13% to $226.74 per full-time student per session ($45.34 part-time), an increase of $8.99 for full-time ($1.79 part-time)

documents.title: Annual Report: Office of the Vice-Principal Research & Innovation
documents.summary: Prof. Alison Mountz presented annual research report showing UTSC research community growth with theme of 'Thrive.' External funding secured increased from just under $20M in 2023-24 to just over $22M in 2024-25. Over five years, total research funding grew from approximately $14M to approximately $23M (59% increase) with tri-agency funding holding steady at about 64% of total. Tenure-stream faculty grew approximately 20%. Notable initiatives include launch of research leaders community of practice, Harvest Walls arrival, funded scholarly prominence clusters, and new 'UTSC Engaged' initiative focused on democratizing knowledge and collaborative problem-solving beyond traditional peer-reviewed publications.
documents.gradRelevance: Graduate students conducting research benefit directly from the 59% growth in total research funding ($14M to $23M) and the $22M+ in external funding available through competitive grants. The new research leaders community of practice and 'UTSC Engaged' initiative may create additional opportunities for graduate research collaboration and funding, while expanded digital platforms for research storytelling may support graduate thesis dissemination.

packages.title: UTSC Campus Council - November 11, 2025
packages.summary: At the November 11, 2025 UTSC Campus Council meeting, the Vice-Provost, Students presented proposed revisions to the Policy on Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment, which underwent community consultation from January–March 2025 (chaired by Professor Faye Mishna) and a second round of feedback September 23–October 8, 2025 that yielded 25 online submissions, six written submissions, and approximately 150 individual comments. Key revisions address clarity in definitions (consent, procedural fairness, reprisals, intimate partner violence), timelines (paragraph 33), respondent supports (paragraphs 40 & 44) and mandatory training (paragraph 42), interim conditions/measures (paragraph 47), and reprisals provisions (paragraphs 52 & 53). The Ombudsperson's 2024–25 annual report showed 357 new requests for assistance, including 88 graduate students (up from 61 in 2023–24), with graduate supervision (14 cases) and graduate funding/awards (12 cases) as leading concerns; the Office noted increases in international student cases (64 vs. 50 prior year) and fees-related complaints (27 vs. 21 prior year) but found no systemic unfairness. UTSC's strategic priorities for 2025–26 include implementing activity-based budgeting in academic units, addressing international student enrollment shifts (targeting 1,428 international undergraduates by 2026), operationalizing the Myron and Berna Garron Health Sciences Complex (SAMIH) with three faculties (Temerty Medicine, Bloomberg Nursing, Dan Pharmacy), and implementing a Culture of Care initiative grounded in Principal Linda Johnston's January 2025 installation address.
packages.gradRelevance: Graduate students directly affected by three items: (1) The proposed Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment policy revisions now include enhanced clarity on respondent supports (paragraphs 40 & 44) and mandatory training (paragraph 42), improving protections for graduate respondents; (2) The Ombudsperson report identifies graduate students as an emerging concern group with 88 assistances in 2024–25 (up 44% from prior year), with 14 cases on graduate supervision and 12 on financial aid/funding/awards—no systemic unfairness found but the Office continues monitoring with School of Graduate Studies and Centre for Graduate Mentorship & Supervision; (3) UTSC's SAMIH operationalization will expand health sciences programming across three faculties, potentially affecting graduate enrolment and program offerings in these areas.
keyItems: Policy on Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment: Second community consultation (September 23–October 8, 2025) received 25 online submissions, six written submissions, and ~150 individual comments; revisions address definitions (consent, procedural fairness, reprisals, intimate partner violence), timelines, respondent supports, mandatory training, interim conditions/measures; governance path includes Governing Council approval December 11, 2025

Ombudsperson 2024–25 Annual Report: 357 new requests for assistance; 88 graduate students assisted (up from 61 in 2023–24); top graduate student concerns were supervision (14 cases) and financial aid/funding/awards (12 cases); 64 international student cases (up from 50); 27 fees-related cases (up from 21); no formal recommendations but Office monitors policy currency, transfer credit (7 cases, up from 2), and petitions/appeals (19 cases)

Professor Faye Mishna appointed University Ombudsperson for three-year term effective July 1, 2025, through June 30, 2028, replacing Professor Bruce Kidd; Office structure includes Ombuds Officers at each campus (Emma Thacker at St. George, Miranda Edwards at UTSC, Cindy Ferencz Hammond at UTM)

UTSC Strategic Priority: Implementing activity-based budget model in academic units with principles of decentralized revenue generation, transparency, incentives for innovation, and flexibility; aimed at greater control for department chairs and predictability in budget allocation

UTSC International Student Enrollment Shift: Domestic undergraduates declining from 2,850 (2025) to 2,498 (2026); international undergraduates increasing from 614 (2025) to 1,428 (2026); international fee revenue projected to rise from 53% (2024–25) to 62% (2025–26) of operating budget

SAMIH (Myron and Berna Garron Health Sciences Complex) Operationalization: Bringing together Temerty Faculty of Medicine, Lawrence Bloomberg Faculty of Nursing, and Leslie Dan Faculty of Pharmacy; includes defining daily operations, delivering technology/support services, establishing new health science certificates and programs

documents.title: Revisions to the Policy on Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment
documents.summary: Document presents proposed revisions to the Policy on Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment (originally approved December 15, 2016; last revised December 15, 2022). The 2025 review was conducted in two phases: broad community consultation January 15–March 28, 2025, chaired by Professor Faye Mishna, plus external legal review by Gillian Hnatiw; a second public feedback period (September 23–October 8, 2025) received 25 online submissions, six written submissions, and ~150 individual comments addressing definitions, procedural fairness, reprisals, intimate partner violence, confidentiality, timelines, respondent supports, mandatory training, and interim conditions. Proposed revisions include clarifications to definitions (consent, facilitated resolution, procedural fairness, reprisal, gender-based violence), updates to timelines (paragraph 33), respondent supports (paragraphs 40 & 44), mandatory training (paragraph 42), interim conditions/measures (paragraph 47), and reprisals (paragraphs 52 & 53). Governance path requires approvals by University Affairs Board (November 25, 2025), Executive Committee (December 1, 2025), and Governing Council (December 11, 2025); no direct operating budget implications.
documents.gradRelevance: Graduate students benefit from enhanced protections and clarity in the revised policy, particularly sections on respondent supports and mandatory training; clarified interim conditions/measures and reprisal provisions provide procedural fairness protections relevant to graduate respondents in sexual violence and harassment investigations.

documents.title: Presentation: 2025 Review of the Policy on Sexual Violence & Sexual Harassment
documents.summary: Presentation by Professor Sandy Welsh, Vice-Provost, Students, summarizing community feedback from September 23–October 8, 2025 consultation on proposed policy revisions. Documents 25 online submissions, six written submissions yielding ~150 individual comments from 47% staff, 34% students, 13% faculty, and 6% staff/students across all three campuses. Key feedback addressed suggestions for improved clarity in definitions, procedural fairness, reprisals, intimate partner violence, confidentiality, timelines, respondent supports, mandatory training, and interim conditions/measures. Substantive improvements include updated language on timelines (paragraph 33), respondent supports (paragraphs 40 & 44), mandatory training (paragraph 42), interim conditions/measures (paragraph 47), reprisals (paragraphs 52 & 53), multi-policy engagement (paragraph 89), and determinations that reports do not fall under the policy (paragraph 93).
documents.gradRelevance: Graduate students contributed to community feedback; revisions specifically address respondent supports and training requirements that directly affect graduate respondents in sexual violence investigations.

documents.title: Report of the University Ombudsperson for the period July 1, 2024 - June 30, 2025 and Administrative Response
documents.summary: Annual report from Bruce Kidd, outgoing Ombudsperson (term ending June 30, 2025), documenting 357 new requests for assistance (RFAs) from July 1, 2024–June 30, 2025. Office structure includes Ombudsperson (Bruce Kidd), three campus-based Ombuds Officers (Emma Thacker, St. George; Miranda Edwards, UTSC; Cindy Ferencz Hammond, UTM), and one support staff member. Report notes no formal recommendations but highlights ongoing monitoring of policy currency (reviews underway for Code of Behaviour on Academic Matters, Policy on Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment, Statement on Prohibited Discrimination); international students (64 cases, up from 50; 9 International Fee Exemption cases vs. 4 prior years); civility (27 cases, down from 46); transfer credit (7 cases, up from 2); petitions/appeals (19 cases); and fees/financial aid/awards/funding (27 fee cases up from 21; 25 financial aid/funding/awards cases up from 15). For graduate students specifically: 88 cases (up from 61 in 2023–24, 78 in 2022–23), with 77 from St. George, 8 from UTM, 3 from UTSC; leading concerns were supervision (14 cases) and financial aid/funding/awards (12 cases). Professor Faye Mishna appointed as Ombudsperson effective July 1, 2025, for three-year term through June 30, 2028.
documents.gradRelevance: Directly relevant: 88 graduate students sought Ombudsperson assistance in 2024–25 (up 44% from 61 prior year), with graduate supervision (14 cases) and graduate funding/awards (12 cases) as leading concerns; increased cases on financial aid/funding/awards (25 total, up from 15) and fees (27 total, up from 21) affect graduate student financial circumstances; Office notes School of Graduate Studies provides formal complaint resolution for complex graduate funding matters and commends Centre for Graduate Mentorship & Supervision efforts.

documents.title: Status Report on UTSC Campus Strategic Priorities
documents.summary: Status report and presentation by Andrew Arifuzzaman (Chief Administrative Officer), Karin Ruhlandt (Vice-Principal, Academic and Dean), and Linda Johnston (Vice-Principal and Principal) detailing UTSC strategic priorities for 2025–26: (1) Implementing activity-based budget model in academic units with principles of decentralized revenue generation, transparency, incentive structures, and flexibility; (2) Addressing international student enrollment shift from 614 actual (2025) to 1,428 target (2026–2029) international undergraduates, with domestic undergraduates declining from 2,850 (2025) to 2,498 (2026); international fee revenue projected to increase from 53% (2024–25) to 62% (2025–26) of operating budget; (3) Building SAMIH (Myron and Berna Garron Health Sciences Complex) programming integrating Temerty Faculty of Medicine, Lawrence Bloomberg Faculty of Nursing, Leslie Dan Faculty of Pharmacy, with new health science certificates, programs, and pathways; (4) Operationalizing new facilities including Retail & Parking Commons (technology systems, bookstore operations), Indigenous House (director hiring, staffing), Toronto Zoo Conservation Campus (classrooms, labs, research spaces); (5) Implementing Culture of Care initiative grounded in Principal Johnston's January 2025 installation address emphasizing support for faculty, staff, librarians, and students with evidence-based tools and success metrics, creating psychological safety, equity, and joy in work/learning.
documents.gradRelevance: Graduate programs may expand through SAMIH operationalization (medicine, nursing, pharmacy graduate offerings); activity-based budget model affects graduate unit resource allocation and financial stability; international student enrollment shift (targeting 1,428 international undergraduates by 2026) impacts graduate program composition and funding sources; Culture of Care initiative aims to support graduate students as part of broader community wellbeing priority.

packages.title: UTSC Campus Council - November 11, 2025
packages.summary: On November 11, 2025, UTSC Campus Council approved the final revised Policy on Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment for submission to Governing Council in December after incorporating 150+ items of feedback from 31 submissions received between September 3–October 8, 2025; heard that the Office of the Ombudsperson received 357 cases in 2024–25 (45 from UTSC), including 25 cases concerning graduate student financial aid, with notable increases in international student visa and fee exemption complaints; reported UTSC's five strategic priorities including transition to activity-based budgeting, addressing international student enrollment decline (469 students below target), building SAMIH programming, operationalizing new facilities including Indigenous House and the Garron Health Sciences Complex, and implementing Culture of Care, Healthy Campus, and Inspiring Inclusive Excellence initiatives; and noted that the graduate student seat on Council remained vacant after no nominations were received in the September 15, 2025 by-election, with general elections scheduled for January 2026.
packages.gradRelevance: The Ombudsperson report explicitly identified 25 cases concerning graduate student financial aid received in 2024–25 and noted that the University must become more sensitive to students' economic challenges as circumstances worsen; the revised Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment Policy will apply to all students including graduate students and emphasizes procedural fairness and respondent supports; the activity-based budget model may affect departmental resources available for graduate funding and TA/RA positions; and critically, no graduate students nominated for the vacant graduate student seat on UTSC Campus Council in the September 2025 by-election, leaving graduate student representation absent on this governance body.
keyItems: Final revised Policy on Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment approved for Governing Council submission in December 2025, incorporating 150 individual feedback items from 31 submissions (25 online forms, 6 emails) collected September 3–October 8, 2025; substantive improvements include updated language on process timelines, respondent supports, clarified procedural fairness and jurisdiction, with many additions enshrining existing practices into formal policy

Office of the Ombudsperson received 357 total cases in 2024–25 (9% increase over prior year), including 45 cases from UTSC; 25 cases specifically concerned graduate student financial aid; complaints about international student fee exemptions doubled; cases concerning incivility dropped significantly from 56 (2022–23) to 46 (2023–24) to 27 (2024–25)

Vice-Principal Johnston reported Culture of Care webpage launched October 21, 2025 at UofT.me/CultureofCare with 'In Their Words' video series; Listening Tour underway with focus group sessions; collaboration with Professors Julie McCarthy and Alan Saks on organizational care research

President Melanie Woodin highlighted federal government's $1.6 billion talent recruitment fund announcement as result of University advocacy and noted institutional focus on recruiting international talent to capitalize on this opportunity

UTSC enrolled 4,016 new students (largest incoming class in UTSC history) against target of 4,431; overall enrollment reached 15,450 (highest in UTSC history); campus fell 469 spaces short on international student intake target, mirroring shortfalls across all three major divisions; 37% of UTSC students enrolled in co-op programs; 10% increase in Bachelor of Science enrollment with corresponding decline in Bachelor of Arts

Five strategic priorities approved: (1) transition to activity-based budget model providing incentives for revenue generation and cost savings; (2) address international student enrollment decline; (3) build SAMIH programming with focus on enrolment demand and tailored supports; (4) operationalize Garron Health Sciences Complex (housing medicine, nursing, pharmacy), Retail & Parking Commons, Toronto Zoo Conservation Campus, and Indigenous House with recruitment of new Director and support staff; (5) implement Culture of Care, Healthy Campus, and Inspiring Inclusive Excellence initiatives

documents.title: Report Number 71 of the UTSC Campus Council – November 11, 2025
documents.url: N/A – this is the primary meeting report
documents.summary: Complete meeting report documenting presentations by Vice-Principal Johnston on Culture of Care initiative, President Woodin's remarks on 'meeting the moment' and $1.6 billion federal talent recruitment fund, final revised Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment Policy incorporating 150 feedback items, Ombudsperson Bruce Kidd's report of 357 cases (45 from UTSC, 25 graduate student financial aid cases), and strategic priority updates including enrollment data (4,016 new students, 15,450 total, 469 below international target) and operationalization of new facilities and SAMIH programming.
documents.gradRelevance: Documents 25 graduate student financial aid cases handled by Ombudsperson in 2024–25; notes policy improving sexual violence protections applicable to graduate students; identifies resource allocation pressures from international student enrollment shortfall; tracks vacant graduate student Council seat.

packages.title: UTSC Campus Council - October 06, 2025
packages.summary: On October 6, 2025, UTSC Campus Council received a presentation on the 2025 review of the University of Toronto's Policy on Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment, chaired by Professor Emeritus Faye Mishna. The review included consultations with over 200 community members (held 45 hours over 10 weeks January–March 2025) and an external legal review by Gillian Hnatiw, a leading Canadian gender-based violence lawyer. Draft revisions were posted publicly September 23–October 8, 2025 for final feedback. Key proposed changes include adding new definitions (gender-based violence, intimate partner violence, tech-facilitated sexual violence, gender identity/expression), expanding the Vice-Provost Students' decision-making authority to impose sanctions in student Respondent cases without expulsion/suspension, renaming and broadening non-adjudicative resolution processes, adding timelines throughout the Policy, creating online navigation tools, and implementing mandatory trauma-informed training for key staff roles. The Policy was last revised December 15, 2022 (effective January 1, 2023); the 2025 review addresses compliance with Ontario's Bill 132 (Sexual Violence and Harassment Action Plan Act, 2016) and the Policy's requirement for three-year reviews.
packages.gradRelevance: Graduate students are directly affected by this Policy as Members of the University Community eligible to file Reports or Disclosures. The Policy revision explicitly names UTGSU's formal submission during consultations, indicating graduate student input was sought. The expanded non-adjudicative resolution process and clarified supports for Respondents (para 41) are relevant to graduate students accused of sexual violence. The mandatory trauma-informed training for staff (para 42) will affect how Case Managers support graduate student Complainants and Respondents. Online navigation tools will help graduate students understand reporting and resolution options. The new definition of intimate partner violence and expanded scope (para 20) applies to graduate students experiencing or accused of such conduct.
keyItems: Policy review launched January 15, 2025; consultations January 15–March 28, 2025 chaired by Professor Emeritus Faye Mishna; over 200 community members provided input across 45 hours of consultation over 10 weeks; UTSU and UTGSU submitted formal submissions
External legal review conducted by Gillian Hnatiw (Toronto-based lawyer, expert in gender-based violence, administrative law, employment law) with final report submitted August 21, 2025

Draft Policy revisions posted publicly September 23–October 8, 2025 for community feedback; governance path includes UTM Campus Council, UTSC Campus Council, Academic Board, University Affairs Board, Executive Committee, and Governing Council (final approval November 6, 2025)

New definitions added: gender-based violence, intimate partner violence, tech-facilitated sexual violence, gender identity, gender expression, procedural fairness, reprisal; consent definition clarified to address Criminal Code standards
Vice-Provost Students' decision-making authority expanded to impose sanctions directly in student Respondent cases where expulsion and suspension are not warranted (para 110)

Non-adjudicative resolution process renamed and expanded with new language on purpose, structure, processes, and outcomes (paras 120–138); Section V newly added covering safety, reprisals, interim conditions, interim measures, and safety planning

documents.title: Update on the Review of the Policy on Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment
documents.summary: Official presentation document to UTSC Campus Council outlining the 2025 review mandate and process. Details the two-part review structure: 1) community consultations led by Professor Emeritus Faye Mishna involving over 200 participants across 45 hours of sessions (January 15–March 28, 2025) with email solicitation to student societies, engagement sessions with administrators and staff, open meetings at each campus targeting racialized, Indigenous, 2SLGBTQ+, and disability communities, and an anonymous online form; 2) external expert review by Gillian Hnatiw (submitted August 21, 2025). Documents summary of consultation input organized into five themes (Clarity & Transparency, Process & Communication Improvements, Timelines, Scope, Education/Training/Awareness) and five categories of external legal recommendations (Definitions, Statement of Commitment, Reporting avenues, Non-adjudicative Resolution, Timelines, Decision-making authority). Notes that draft revisions were posted September 23–October 8, 2025 for final public feedback and provides governance pathway through Governing Council for November 6, 2025 approval.
documents.gradRelevance: Confirms UTGSU submitted a formal submission during the consultation process (January–March 2025), indicating graduate student voice was incorporated into the 2025 review. Graduate students affected by the Policy would benefit from the proposed online navigation tools and clarified processes.

documents.title: Presentation Slides: 2025 Review of the Policy on Sexual Violence & Sexual Harassment
documents.summary: Visual presentation by Vice-Provost Sandy Welsh summarizing the 2025 review progress and proposed Policy revisions. Outlines the two-part mandate (assess impact of 2022 changes; identify further improvements), community consultation themes, and external expert recommendations. Details proposed revisions across all major Policy sections: new definitions (gender-based violence, intimate partner violence, tech-facilitated sexual violence, gender identity/expression), renamed Statement of Commitments with added commitments to trauma-informed process and accommodations (paras 5, 10), formalized practices re: Reports from former members (para 16) and intimate partner violence response (para 20), moved Confidentiality and Privacy section with new intimate image/document protocols (para 25–26), new Section V on Safety, Reprisals, Interim Conditions and Measures (paras 48–54), expanded Vice-Provost Students decision-making authority (para 110), expanded Facilitated Resolution section (paras 120–138), added investigation timelines and trauma-informed investigator requirement (paras 96, 98), clarified Complainant options post-Report (para 85), and added response timeline of 90 days for Assessment (para 90). Also lists planned responses: online navigation tools, internal process review, and trauma-informed case management enhancements.
documents.gradRelevance: Presentation clarifies that trauma-informed training will be mandatory for key staff roles who interact with graduate students experiencing sexual violence. Expanded Vice-Provost decision-making authority affects graduate student Respondents. New Facilitated Resolution process details (paras 120–138) provide potential alternative resolution pathways for graduate student disputes.

documents.title: Policy on Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment (Current Policy, Approved December 15, 2022)
documents.summary: Full text of the currently operative Policy on Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment, effective January 1, 2023. Defines key terms (Member of University Community includes students, faculty, librarians, post-doctoral fellows, and all employees; Complainant; Respondent; Sexual Violence including Sexual Assault and Sexual Harassment; Consent with explicit Criminal Code language; Disclosure; Reporting; Non-Adjudicative Resolution). Provides framework for Application and Scope, Education/Support/Resources, Principles Respecting Process, Confidentiality and Privacy, Disclosure and Reporting procedures, Assessment and Investigation, Decision-making and Appeal processes, Non-Adjudicative Outcomes, University obligations when Complainant requests no investigation or chooses not to participate, University obligations regarding third-party or anonymous Reports, and Annual Reporting requirements. Policy requires three-year reviews per Section VIII, with previous versions approved December 15, 2016 (initial), December 12, 2019, December 16, 2021, and December 15, 2022.
documents.gradRelevance: Graduate students are explicitly Members of the University Community subject to this Policy as Complainants, Respondents, or participants in Disclosures and Reports. Current Policy defines Consent with Criminal Code standards directly relevant to graduate student conduct. Definition of Non-Adjudicative Resolution and current investigation and appeal procedures affect how graduate student sexual violence allegations are handled.

packages.title: UTSC Campus Council - October 06, 2025
packages.summary: At the October 6, 2025 UTSC Campus Council meeting, Vice-Dean Katie Larson presented findings from the 2025 Curriculum Review Working Circle Milestone report, noting that approximately $2.5 million had been allocated to support over 250 campus initiatives since the initial 2022 Report's 56 recommended actions, with nearly half of almost 400 new courses introduced since 2022 linked to the Review. Professor Sandy Welsh presented draft revisions to the Policy on Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment (in effect since 2017, subject to mandatory 3-year review under Ontario law), with proposed changes including trauma-informed language, updated definitions, formalized confidentiality provisions, and new process timelines, available for community feedback through October 8, 2025. CAO Andrew Arifuzzaman highlighted major construction projects (Indigenous House opening mid-to-late October 2025, SAMIH, Parking Commons) and three high-priority partnerships (Toronto Zoo, EARTH Hub, Golden Mile transformation), while noting the campus Strategic Plan was undergoing a fifth-year refresh to align with new President Melanie Woodin's vision. Dean Tim Tang reported that OSEW programs are student-fee funded and serve all students, though an institution-wide work-study funding reduction was impacting employment opportunities. Dean Karin Ruhlandt announced a proposal for a new PhD in Applied and Interdisciplinary Biology for Fall 2026 entry, aligned with SAMIH's opening.
packages.gradRelevance: The work-study funding reduction across tri-campus directly affects graduate student employment and financial support, with Dean Tang confirming institution-wide cuts though noting co-op, internships, and casual positions remain unaffected. The proposed new PhD in Applied and Interdisciplinary Biology (Fall 2026 entry) creates a new graduate program at UTSC. The Policy on Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment revision affects all students including graduate students, with trauma-informed approaches and enhanced supports mentioned, though specific grad-focused provisions are not detailed in this report. No specific graduate student funding, stipend amounts, TA/RA provisions, or housing matters for graduate students were discussed.
keyItems: 2025 Curriculum Review Working Circle Milestone report presented: $2.5 million allocated to support 250+ campus initiatives; nearly 50% of ~400 new courses since 2022 linked to Review priorities; notable increases in cross-disciplinary and indigenous/black knowledge incorporation; challenges identified include uneven uptake, project-based funding sustainability, and tensions between inclusive pedagogy and academic expectations.

Policy on Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment revision: draft policy available for community feedback September 3–October 8, 2025; proposed changes include new/updated definitions, trauma-informed approach commitments, formalized confidentiality/privacy provisions, and new process timeline language; extensive consultations January–March 2025 with 200+ students, faculty, librarians; external legal review completed; 16-person support team across three campuses with full-time UTSC office conducted 17,000+ prevention outreach initiatives in prior year.

Principal Linda Johnston announced Culture of Care webpage launch forthcoming on Principal's Office website, with evaluation metrics and evidence-based resources; Indigenous House construction progressing with occupancy planned mid-to-late October 2025; Community Partnerships and Engagement Office transition from CAO's office to Vice-President & Principal's office completed, with CPE Planning Process to begin Fall-Winter 2025-26.

CAO Andrew Arifuzzaman's portfolio overview: 2025-26 budget includes capital projects and institutional partnerships; three high-priority partnerships are Toronto Zoo, Environmental and Related Technologies Hub (EARTH), and Golden Mile transformation; major construction projects include Indigenous House, Scarborough Academy of Integrated Health and Medicine (SAMIH), and Parking Commons; approximately 650 geothermal wells currently on campus with expansion strategy to be presented to governance; SAMIH and Parking Commons designed with solar panel facades for electricity generation; all UTSC students to receive free Toronto Zoo memberships; TTC transit route negotiations underway with potential charter bus interim solution.

Dean Tim Tang, Office of Student Experience and Wellbeing: portfolio primarily funded by student fees; services include student orientation, career services, work-study, mentorship programs, accessibility services; increased student demand for accessibility services noted; institution-wide reduction in work-study funding confirmed across tri-campus with multi-factor causes including post-pandemic shifts; approximately 1,500 students currently in residence with first-year housing guarantee met plus ~200 upper-year students accommodated; Harmony Commons opening in prior year enabled meeting demand; about 50% of residence occupants are international students.

Dean Karin Ruhlandt, Vice-Principal Academic & Dean: oversees 16 academic departments; priorities include innovations in exam invigilation to counter cheating technologies, cross-departmental collaboration, growth of health sciences certificate and complementary programs, and promotion of humanities/social sciences facing declining enrolment; external review on experiential learning underway with updates to follow.
